Course goals are to develop student skills in visual perception, cultural awareness, historical geography and chronology, and to enhance their skills as effective visual navigators and communicators in the global world today.

title: Independent Study
description: The privilege of independent study is possible for students who are prepared to undertake it based on student need or interest with approval of the Chair of the department, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. The study must be in an area not covered by a regularly scheduled course. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. The maximum number of units that can be earned in one semester by independent study is 4; a total of up to six units of independent study may be applied to the degree.

title: Museums of the World
description: What's in a museum and why? Find out in this short course introducing you to some of the world's most wonderful (and weird) museums. This course uses the platform Moodle. Students need reliable internet access. Recommended for returning students or new students who are comfortable working independently. A fully asynchronous course.

title: Global Visual Cultures: Seeing the World
description: An enticing, wide-ranging introduction to the "visual arts" and "visual literacy," via emphasis on historical, social, and interpretive issues relevant to the critical analysis of artistic production, meaning, and reception of the material/visual arts produced by diverse world cultures from the past to present. Course coverage includes works of art and architecture from multiple world regions via focus on global "World Heritage Sites" (as designated by UNESCO.) Course goals are to develop student skills in visual perception, cultural awareness, historical geography and chronology, and to enhance their skills as effective visual navigators and communicators in the global world today.

title: Photographic Seminar
description: This course is designed for students working photographically at an advanced level. Coursework will be based on projects of personal interest and refining technical abilities. Intensive discussion and critique will help ground students discovery and application of a variety of approaches, and expand the range of possibilities in their photographic pursuits. **Additional fee applies.**

title: World Art 2: Renaissance-Contemporary
description: A chronological and thematic introduction to diverse world cultures and civilizations from the fifteenth century to the contemporary period. Artistic monuments presented within the contexts of social concerns, religious beliefs, political circumstances and cultural values. Fundamental concepts for the analysis and interpretation of works of art, the development of skills in visual literacy. A core required course for Art and Art History majors and minors.

title: Renaissance and Renaissances in the Global World
description: Course focuses on the concept and creation of "renaissances" in art, history, literature, religion, and philosophy from the past to the present. The arts of the "Italian" and "Northern" Renaissances in 16th century Europe, and the renaissance of arts of diverse Asian cultures within diverse cultural/social frameworks. Course investigates the concept of a "renaissance" â and the social/political agendas this designation represents.

title: Visual Cultures of Asia: History, Traditions, and the Contemporary
description: Course offers students a focused study of the arts of diverse Asian cultures, including China, Japan, India, and other Asian regions. Historical and contemporary art of these regions. Pending interest and need, this course could be replaced with Latin American Visual Cultures. Topics and focus can vary.

title: Art Crime
description: An overview of the varieties of crimes against art from socio-historical, legal, political, and psychological perspectives. One of the top three largest criminal enterprises worldwide, art crime involves: theft, forgery, antiquities looting, deliberate destruction of monuments of cultural heritage, etc.

title: Seeing Here Now: Movements in Modernism
description: Course introduces students to the diversity of "modern" art movements in the 19th, 20th, and 21st centuries in the global context. Modernist and postmodernist movements, including environmental art, photography, video, installation, etc. Options: Language in the Visual Arts (a wide-ranging investigation of text/image relationships in the arts of both western and Asian cultures.)

title: Public Art: The Politics of Presentation
description: Public art is defined as "original works of art in any medium for temporary or permanent placement in outdoor/indoor settings and accessible to the publicâ¦" Course examines specific works and key concepts to question assumptions about the value/role of art in public spaces. Who is "the public" for which the art is made? Why do some works cause great controversy? What is the history of "public art?" What role can it play in urban revitalization?

title: Indigenous World Art
description: The arts produced by traditional cultures in diverse world regions. Attention to the collecting/display of "ethnographic" art, the commercialization/appropriation of artistic styles/motifs, and the complex definitions of "Indigenous peoples" in a variety of global areas. Challenges involved with the preservation of indigenous cultures and the importance of indigenous cultural practitioners â including contemporary artists, athletes, dancers, writers, musicians, and performers. The opportunity to meet collectors, visit local museums, work with actual materials from the Dominican University collection, and meet several contemporary artists working to preserve/expand the definitions of Indigenous arts in the world today.

title: Antiquity, Archaeology and the Modern World
description: How do we assess, understand, and preserve the cultural heritage of the past in the present? This course focuses on the complex issues and politics of archaeological/visual studies of ancient eras from a global perspective: "Who owns Antiquity?" Course involves not only a detailed study of ancient civilizations and their continued influence on the modern world but also an investigation of how modes of preserving the past can influence the future.

title: Art in Focus: Modern Architecture of the San Francisco Bay Area
description: This course focuses on selected highlights of Bay Area architecture beginning with the late 19th century work of architect Bernard Maybeck to the present. We will explore the social, political, and environmental factors that have influenced the architecture of the San Francisco Bay area and examine the regional interpretations of a variety of commercial, institutional, and domestic buildings. A field trip/site visit of significant buildings will be included.

title: Modern Art: 20th Century
description: Cubism, expressionism, surrealism, and related movements and theories. Contemporary American painting and the visual arts and the direction of art in the 21st century.

title: Structures of Faith
description: Architectural forms associated with world religions. Classical temples, Gothic cathedrals, Hindu, Buddhist, Jewish, and Islamic religious architecture.

title: Indigenous World Art
description: The arts produced by traditional cultures in diverse world regions. Attention to the collecting/display of "ethnographic" art, the commercialization/appropriation of artistic styles/motifs, and the complex definitions of "Indigenous peoples" in a variety of global areas. Challenges involved with the preservation of indigenous cultures and the importance of indigenous cultural practitioners â including contemporary artists, athletes, dancers, writers, musicians, and performers. The opportunity to meet collectors, visit local museums, work with actual materials from the Dominican University collection, and meet several contemporary artists working to preserve/expand the definitions of Indigenous arts in the world today.

title: Capstone I
description: Senior Visual Studies Majors complete their degree by writing a senior thesis/capstone project. Individual preparation and progress take place on a directed study and consultation basis.

title: Teaching Assistant
description: The privilege of serving as a teaching assistant is available to students who are interested with the approval of the Department Chair, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. A contract between the student and the instructor to determine goals, specifics on project contents/materials, and evaluation process will be required at time of request. Course may be taken for 1-4 units.

title: Thesis Continuation
description: If after taking ARTH 4910 & 4920 a student has not completed the senior project, s/he enrolls in this course in order to complete. The reader must be consulted prior to enrolling to make sure s/he will be available during the semester in which the student wishes to complete. Please note that most readers are unavailable during the summer. Also note, students who do not complete the project during thesis continuation are required to re-enroll in ARTH 4910 for 3 units at the full course rate. This course has a flat fee. Check with Business Services for the current fee.

title: Research Assistantship
description: Research Assistant opportunities in Art History partner a student with new or ongoing faculty research projects with approval from the Department Chair, the instructor concerned and the Dean of the School. Students become involved in either library research, data collection, analysis, presentation or other aspects of faculty scholarly research. Course may be taken for 1-4 units.

title: Internship: Art History
description: Internship opportunities in art history include placement in Bay Area art galleries, museums, and various arts organizations. Fieldwork in art appraisal, sales, exhibitions, slide curatorship, art conservation, research, and art editing. Internships are arranged on an individual basis for qualified students. Course may be taken for .5-4 units.

title: Presence of the Past in the Present: Medieval Period/Medievalism
description: The enduring appeal of the medieval period from a global perspective. Options for course material under this designation: Structures of Faith: The Art and Architecture of World Religions (a visually-based introduction to the major faith traditions of the world from ancient times to the present), and The Arts of Islamic Cultures: Religion, Geography, Politics (an introduction to the arts and cultures of the Islamic Worlds.)

title: Introduction to Sport and Performance Psychology
description: This course introduces students to the history, research, and main theoretical approaches relevant to sport and performance psychology. The course provides an overview of the principles and methods of sport and performance psychology as well as the cognitions, emotions, behaviors, and resulting outcomes of team and individual participants. It introduces students to the concept of theory to applied practice with demonstrations of mental skills training and techniques.

title: Advanced Sport and Performance Psychology
description: This culminating course will deepen studentsâ skills, knowledge of research, and theoretical approaches. The course will challenge students to think critically about research and how it impacts their work as practitioners, including debates on various topics. Case conceptualizations will examine more complex issues to cultivate emotional, cultural, and contextual intelligences. Students will explore the interconnectedness of concepts, theories, and techniques that go into an integrated consulting approach. Future directions of the field will also be covered.

title: Museums of the World
description: What's in a museum and why? Find out in this short course introducing you to some of the world's most wonderful (and weird) museums. This course uses the platform Moodle. Students need reliable internet access. Recommended for returning students or new students who are comfortable working independently. A fully asynchronous course.

title: Applied Mental Skills Training
description: Building on the foundation of mental skills learned in Introduction to Sport and Performance Psychology, this course will advance studentsâ knowledge of mental skills for performance and give them opportunities to experience and apply the interventions and concepts to a wide range of performers and teams. Students will practice, teach, and reflect on a variety of mental skills techniques from evidence-based perspectives and address how to bridge theory to practice. Students will also examine how the efficacy of the techniques change based on the cultural contexts of the performance arenas and performers.

title: Counseling Skills in Sport and Performance Psychology
description: This course introduces the student to foundational counseling skills for applied practice in Sport and Performance Psychology. These skills include appropriate use of self, empathy, reflective practice, attending and confronting with special attention to the impact of diversity on the counseling relationship. ASPP 203 will introduce the practice of assessment, and action planning with emphasis on Rogerian and Motivational Interviewing approaches within the SPP consulting relationship. The course will address AOD, co-occurring disorders, suicidality, crisis management and legal and ethical considerations for SPP practitioners. Topics of collaboration with community resources, psychoeducation, referrals and advocacy will also be introduced. Methods of service delivery will be discussed. A didactic experiential approach will be utilized, including group processes. This course will incorporate hands-on experience with role-playing and active practice of counseling skills for practice in applied settings.

title: Psychopathology
description: Students will be introduced to assessment, diagnosis, and treatment of the major mental disorders, including severe mental illness, co-occurring disorders, AOD, and consequences of disaster and trauma; recovery-oriented care approaches to treatment of psychopathology; psychopharmacology; how issues of diversity, socio-economic position, human sexuality, and stress affect mental health.

title: Capstone II
description: Continuation of Capstone 1. Senior Visual Studies Majors complete their degree by writing a senior thesis/capstone project. Individual preparation and progress take place on a directed study and consultation basis.

title: Professional Ethics and Standards in Sport and Performance Psychology
description: This course provides an introduction and exploration of the range of ethical and legal issues that professionals may encounter within the field of sport and performance psychology. Students will examine the theoretical constructs of ethical decision-making and explore issues such as the impact of personal biases on decision-making, transference and countertransference, confidentiality, dual relationships, technology, social media, and boundaries of competence.

title: Assessment in Sport and Performance Psychology
description: This course encompasses the purpose, implementation, and utility of initial and ongoing assessment strategies with individuals, teams, and groups. Topics include: structured, semi-structured, and unstructured interviews, performing behavioral observations, theoretical foundations of assessment measures, suicidal assessment, test construction and the administration, scoring, and interpretation of objective psychological measures applicable to sport and performance settings. This course will emphasize synthesizing information gleaned from multiple methods to assist in accurate initial assessment, intervention planning, and evaluating changes in performance.

title: Group Dynamics
description: This course will focus on the unique dynamics that occur within groups and systems and will address how these dynamics impact group cohesion and performance. Students will examine the theory and research of group dynamics and explore topics such as group formation and the stages of development, communication, leadership, trust, and group behavior. Additionally, the course will address the ways in which group members arrive at roles, norms, and identities and learn to identify cultural and ethical issues in working with groups.

title: Diversity in Sport and Performance Psychology
description: This course provides students with an examination of the historical and present-day topics regarding diversity in sport and performance. Students will engage in critical self-reflection to uncover their own implicit biases and assumptions as they relate to their future work as practitioners. The course will explore the concept of cultural competence regarding ethnicity, race, gender, sexual orientation and identity, religion, age, socioeconomic status and other areas of cultural considerations. Students will learn to think critically by enhancing their awareness of intersectionality, microaggressions, diversity, inclusion, social justice, and equity and how these concepts may impact performance and well-being.

title: Research Methods in Sport and Performance Psychology
description: This course explores research methods and design, assisting students in becoming critical consumers of research. Course topics include qualitative and quantitative research assumptions, statistical analysis, multi-cultural considerations, and ethical application of research. Upon course completion, students will recognize the importance of evidence-based research within Applied Sport and Performance Psychology.

title: Independent Study
description: The privilege of independent study is possible for students who are prepared to undertake it based on student need or interest with approval of the Chair of the department, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. The study must be in an area not covered by a regularly scheduled course. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. The maximum number of units that can be earned in one semester by independent study is 4; a total of up to six units of independent study may be applied to the degree.

title: Applied Performance Project II
description: The Applied Performance Project is a program-long sequence of courses that students take each semester in order to produce a final project. Elements may include development of the following: an extensive literature review, a portfolio of academic and applied work, a philosophy of practice presentation, elevator speeches, a resume, social media profiles, templates of professional practice documents, and a Creative element that may include a podcast, workbook or development of a website. The project will be presented to a panel of experts who will also conduct a mock job interview. This is a Credit/No Credit course.

title: Advanced Mental Skills Training
description: Building on the knowledge and skills gained in the Applied Mental Skills Training course, this course will further advance studentsâ understanding of how to integrate ethical practice, theory, and research into their applied work. Students will explore interventions for both short-term, problem-focused work, as well as continual needs assessment and support for the evolution of the consulting process for long-term clients. The course will explore formulating case conceptualizations, bespoke consultation and interventions, reflective practice applications, personal consulting style, and deepening cultural, emotional, and contextual intelligence.

title: Applied Performance Project I
description: The Applied Performance Project is a program-long sequence of courses that students take each semester in order to produce a final project. Elements may include development of the following: an extensive literature review, a portfolio of academic and applied work, a philosophy of practice presentation, elevator speeches, a resume, social media profiles, templates of professional practice documents, and a Creative element that may include a podcast, workbook or development of a website. The project will be presented to a panel of experts who will also conduct a mock job interview. This is a Credit/No Credit course.

title: Psychology of Human Development
description: This course covers the study of human development that includes social, cultural, biological and psychological issues of conception, prenatal life, infancy, childhood, adolescence, adulthood and aging/death. Psychosocial development, brain development/neuropsychology, youth sports, self-concept, language, personality, moral reasoning, and culture are emphasized. This course focuses primarily on the theories and application of normal development using a systems approach.

title: Applied Performance Placement I: Intensive Placement Preparation
description: This course is designed for student-trainees to prepare presentations, activities and other materials for the upcoming Intensive Placement Camp. Students will be guided by supervisors on elements that require preparation and will typically work in pairs to complete materials. This is a Credit/No Credit course.

title: Applied Performance Project IV
description: The Applied Performance Project is a program-long sequence of courses that students take each semester in order to produce a final project. Elements may include development of the following: an extensive literature review, a portfolio of academic and applied work, a philosophy of practice presentation, elevator speeches, a resume, social media profiles, templates of professional practice documents, and a Creative element that may include a podcast, workbook or development of a website. The project will be presented to a panel of experts who will also conduct a mock job interview. This is a Credit/No Credit course.

title: Applied Performance Placement II
description: This course is the second in a series of three Applied Performance Placement experiences students will engage in over the course of the program. This placement experience provides the student with direct experiential work as trainee-practitioners with a population of their choosing in the sport and performance psychology realm, supervised by a Certified Mental Performance Consultant of the Association for Applied Sport Psychology (AASP) for both individual and group supervision. This is a Credit/No Credit course. **Additional fee applies.**

title: Applied Performance Placement III
description: This course is the third in a series of three applied performance placement experiences students will engage in over the course of the program. This placement experience provides students with direct experiential work as trainee-practitioners in the sport and performance psychology realm. Students will be supervised by a Certified Mental Performance Consultant (CMPC) of the Association for Applied Sport Psychology (AASP) for both individual and group supervision. Opportunities for placements within the University’s Athletic Department may be offered to qualified students. This is a Credit/No Credit course.**Additional fee applies.**

title: Applied Performance Placement III
description: This course is the second in a series of three Applied Performance Placement experiences students will engage in over the course of the program. This placement experience provides the student with direct experiential work as trainee-practitioners with a population of their choosing in the sport and performance psychology realm, supervised by a Certified Mental Performance Consultant of the Association for Applied Sport Psychology (AASP) for both individual and group supervision. This is a Credit/No Credit course.**Additional fee applies.**

title: Performance Masterclass
description: The Performance Masterclass series provides students the opportunity to learn from some of the very best in the field of Applied Sport and Performance Psychology. We invite leading academics, practitioners, coaches, and performers to share their trade secrets, working methods, and expertise. The masterclasses are typically 2 or 3-hours in length. Students will be able to engage with the presenters and ask questions to gain insight into the expertsâ work. This is a Credit/No Credit course.

title: The Business of Consulting
description: This course aims to provide students with the fundamentals of starting and operating a business as mental performance practitioners. Students will cover the basics of entrepreneurship, understanding their value, owning their expertise, and securing work. Topics include networking, marketing, sales, branding, negotiating, setting fees, creating proposals, and developing contracts.

title: Speak Like a Champ
description: As a mental performance consultant, a primary aspect of a practitioner’s role is to teach. However, prior to content delivery, the consultant must focus on engagement and connection with the individual or group. In this professional development workshop, the student will learn how to develop content (planned or impromptu) and how to deliver it effectively, so the audience walks away with the intended take-home message. The heart of this workshop will be learning about and practicing essential presentation skills, for example, effective voice volume, pace, eye contact, physical stance, and gestures as well as managing one's own performance anxiety. This is a Credit/No Credit course.

title: Team Building
description: This workshop will introduce students to the concepts of team building, team building activities (TBAs), and icebreakers focusing primarily on the application of these activities. We will also cover the differences between icebreakers and team building activities, important considerations when leading group activities, and the keys to effective debriefing, all through a theoretical lens. This is a Pass/No Pass course.

title: APA Writing Workshop
description: This professional development workshop focuses on helping students develop foundational skills in reading and writing at the graduate level, including reading and analysis of scholarly literature, APA style, and the basic mechanics and structure of literature reviews. Workshop sessions and assignments occur during non-consecutive weeks at various points throughout the semester, providing supplemental guidance and instruction on skills needed for assignments in ASPP 5200 Introduction to Sport and Performance Psychology. This is a Credit/No Credit course.

title: Applied Performance Placement I: Intensive Placement Camps
description: This 2-week in-residence experiential learning program is typically the first applied experience in the program. The student-trainees will receive on-site supervision of their work in a sport camp setting. Student-trainees will typically work in pairs, and with other members of their cohort, in delivering the mental performance elements of a summer camp athletic program. This course also entails post-camp supervisory meetings. This is a Credit/No Credit course. **Additional fee applies.**

title: Introduction to Executive Coaching
description: This course will enable a student to manage an executive coaching engagement. Students will understand the mindset required to coach an executive without experience of the clientsâ situation or business. By being client focused the students will be able to build long-lasting and impactful relationships with their clients. The course will cover the fundamentals of executive coaching, the culture of working within this population, and will include some of the effective frameworks that are used by practitioners while also distinguishing the field of executive coaching from traditional sport and performance psychology. The course will cover the basics of building an executive coaching practice, including how to attract and retain clients. The students will be encouraged to think about their own framework and approach.

title: Professional Development Workshop
description: Professional development workshops provide opportunities for students to further their education beyond program-specific coursework. Each workshop offers valuable, hands-on training and tools to support career development. These workshops may also cover aspects from the curriculum in more depth and/or from different perspectives. This is a Credit/No Credit course.

title: Exit Meeting
description: The Exit Meeting is the last required element of the MA and Certificate programs. It should be completed once all other requirements are met. This involves meeting with the Program Director to reflect on time in the program, specific courses, instructors, etc. and have an opportunity to give feedback about any element of the program and their time at HNU. Students will be encouraged to articulate the ways in which they wish to be involved with the program as alumni and also how they can help promote the program and HNU as ambassadors. We will also cover what benefits they have as alumni of the university and program and what HNU benefits they may not have access to any more once they graduate. We will discuss what happens post-graduation and various elements to consider as they step into the field, for example, becoming certified as a Certified Mental Performance Consultant (CPMPC), Continuing Education requirements, journal subscriptions, and professional liability insurance, etc. Other elements include reviewing and editing the studentâs CV/resume, discussing the importance of peer consultation groups, promoting themselves in the field, their future plans and ideas, networking and introductions to various professionals in the field. Finally, we will discuss the importance of service and giving back. Professional development workshops provide opportunities for students to further their education beyond program-specific coursework. Each workshop offers valuable, hands-on training and tools to support career development. Potential workshops may include how to read research articles, writing skills for graduate school, job searches, interviewing, and other topics within professional development. This series will also facilitate preparation for the final Comprehensive Exam and the post-masters CMPC exam. This is a Credit/No Credit course.

title: Introduction to Applied Performance Placements
description: This course is designed to orient students to the logistics and expectations of working as mental skills trainee-practitioners in their applied performance placements. The course provides in-depth details regarding trainee roles and responsibilities, and the opportunity to plan for their first applied placement experience (Intensive). This course will assist the students with instruction and content development in order to set them up for success in their applied placements. It will also introduce students to debriefing skills and the supervisory experience.

title: Applying Mindfulness in Sport and Performance Psychology
description: This course is designed to provide students with an introduction to mindfulness as a practice and theoretical framework from which they can assist athletes and other performers. Drawing from mindfulness literature and research, students will learn experientially by engaging in their own mindfulness practice while gathering evidence supporting a mindfulness approach. Students will have the opportunity to create their own meditation scripts, practice application, and learn the differences between a mindfulness approach and CBT frameworks.

title: Mental Performance in Athletic Coaching
description: This course provides students with theories, research, and applications for integrating mental performance concepts into the athletic coaching realm. Students will consider how to apply interventions within team and individual sports and examine how to use mental performance techniques in collaboration with the coach. The course will help students understand the dynamics of the coach-athlete relationship and explore how to navigate ethical challenges in working with coaches.

title: Mental Performance in eSports
description: In this course, students will examine the esports industry and the application of mental performance coaching techniques with esports teams and individuals. Students will gain an understanding of how to operate a mental performance curriculum within an esports organization. The course will assist students in areas including industry communication practices, integrating into an organization, and working around esports media. How to network and search for opportunities within the esports industry will be explored during the course.

title: Mental Skills Training: Consulting Through a Season
description: This course will examine the experience of being a Mental Performance Consultant on an athletic team for the duration of an entire season (including pre-season, mid-season and postseason). We will explore the social and psychological setting of multiple sports, with special emphasis on the individual and collective mental skills related to team success. Through the use of the main text and case studies, various situations the consultant may encounter throughout a season will be analyzed. A basic understanding of the collegiate sport model, as well as conditions related to mental skills training with a team, will be explored by the end of the course.

title: Neurocognitive Technology Applications in Sport and Performance Psychology
description: This course will focus on how mental performance practitioners can integrate neurocognitive training technologies. Students will examine the science, theory, and practice of leveraging technology to train cognitive and neurocognitive skills. Additionally, the course will cover the basics of evaluating, selecting, and applying new technologies within a consulting role. Topics include biofeedback, performance vision, and perceptual-cognitive training as well as best practices for introducing technologies in applied settings.

title: Performance Psychology in the Perf Arts
description: To help someone develop a new skill or movement one must understand motor learning and control. This course will cover key fundamentals of motor learning and control that will help students understand what is going, why it is going on, and how to structure activities that will lead to efficient development. In this course students will learn to develop a knowledge framework to address problems, how to structure activities for individuals and groups, which learning processes are the most effective and why, and how to organize and communicate effectively with clients (and oneself).

title: Motor Learning and Control
description: To help someone develop a new skill or movement one must understand motor learning and control. This course will cover key fundamentals of motor learning and control that will help students understand what is going, why it is going on, and how to structure activities that will lead to efficient development. In this course students will learn to develop a knowledge framework to address problems, how to structure activities for individuals and groups, which learning processes are the most effective and why, and how to organize and communicate effectively with clients (and oneself).

title: Independent Study: Applied Sport & Performance Psychology
description: The privilege of independent study is possible for students who are prepared to undertake it based on student need or interest with approval of the Chair of the department, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. The study must be in an area not covered by a regularly scheduled course.

title: History and Foundations of Art Therapy
description: This course provides an overview of the history and foundations of art as healing. It begins with an exploration of early images, sacred sites, and the role they have played in physical and mental healing. The discovery of art of the mentally ill and how Freud and Jung blended art and psychology are also addressed. The major focus of the course is on contributions made by Art Therapy pioneers and current leaders in the field to the development and practice of art therapy in diverse settings and with diverse populations.

title: Introduct to Clinical Art Therapy II
description: This course incorporates presentations by licensed therapists and art therapists who have been working in the field and covers the counseling processes and psychotherapeutic techniques of practice used by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society.

title: Final Comprehensive Examination
description: The Final Comprehensive Exam is offered in Spring and Fall each year and taken in the studentsâ final semester. It consists of two separate exams, one written and one oral. The written exam includes questions from all the core classes taken up until the studentsâ final semester. Upon passing the written exam, the student is then eligible to take the oral exam (usually 1-2 weeks later). The oral exam includes presenting a case conceptualization, teaching a mental skills technique and responding to an ethical vignette. Preparation for the final exam is given in a Professional Development Workshop in the same semester in which the student takes the exam. This is a Credit/No Credit course.

title: Introduction to Clinical Art Therapy I
description: This course explores the history and theory of art therapy, the counseling process, and psychotherapeutic techniques of practice used by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists in a culturally diversesociety.

title: Communication and Counseling Skills
description: This course examines the counseling and psychotherapeutic theories and techniques of practice used by professional marriage and family counselors and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society. The course provides an orientation to wellness and prevention, counseling theories to assist in selection of appropriate counseling interventions, models of counseling consistent with current professional research, and practice and development of a personal model of counseling. This course teaches students to be familiar with a broad range of matters that may arise within marriage and family relationships and prepares them to be able to apply a variety of effective psychotherapeutic techniques and modalities to improve, restore, and maintain healthy individual, couple, and family relationships. Examination of areas such as selfawareness, feelings, self-concepts, implicit and explicit communication, and perception assists in developing a greater understanding of interpersonal skills needed in therapeutic relationships.

title: Mental Performance in Military Populations
description: This course will focus on the unique mental challenges experienced by military populations. Students will examine how sport and performance psychology skills can be tailored to fit this specific performance population. Throughout the course, students will learn about the culture of the military, team dynamics within a military unit, distinctive performances that impact their career and mission, as well as the overall psychological components of combat. Additionally, students will gain knowledge on the profession of performance consulting with the military as an occupation.

title: Principles of Marriage & Family Therapy
description: This course examines the counseling and psychotherapeutic theories and techniques of practice used by professional marriage and family counselors and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society. This course is an orientation to wellness and prevention and introduces students to a variety of family systems, counseling theories, and models of counseling used to assist in selection of appropriate counseling interventions that are consistent with current professional research and practice. The course prepares students to be familiar with the broad range of matters that may arise within marriage and family relationships and trains them in the application of marriage and family systems theories, relationship counseling principles, and methods. Students apply a variety of effective psychotherapeutic techniques and modalities that may be utilized to improve, restore, or maintain healthy individual, couple, and family relationships.

title: Advanced Human Development
description: 'This course examines the counseling and psychotherapeutic theories and techniques of practice used by professional marriage and family counselors and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society. Course content focuses on human growth and development across the lifespan, normal and abnormal behavior, developmental crises, disability, psychopathology, and situational and environmental factors that affect both normal and abnormal behavior. Varieties of effective psychotherapeutic techniques and modalities that may be utilized to improve, restore, or maintain healthy individual, couple, and family relationships are explored. The techniques taught include the counseling process in a culturally diverse society, an orientation to wellness and prevention, counseling theories to assist in selection of appropriate counseling interventions, models of counseling consistent with current professional research and practice, and the development of a personal model of counseling. Prerequisite: An undergraduate-level course in Human Development from an accredited institution.'

title: Psychopathology
description: 'This course covers the principles of psychopathology and the diagnostic processes used in a culturally and socially diverse society by providing students with an integrated course of study that trains students in the diagnosis, assessment, prognosis, and treatment of mental disorders. Topics include differential diagnosis and the use of current diagnostic tools such as the current edition of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual; the impact of cooccurring substance use disorders or medical psychological disorders; established diagnostic criteria for mental or emotional disorders; and behavioral and psychosocial models of change. Students examine the development of treatment modalities and placement criteria within the continuum of care. Prerequisite: An undergraduate-level course in Abnormal Psychology from an accredited institution.'

title: Methods of Group and Family Therapy
description: This course examines the counseling and psychotherapeutic theories and techniques of practice used by professional marriage and family counselors and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society. Course content focuses on applying counseling principles of marriage and family systems and relationships. Students receive training in a variety of effective psychotherapeutic techniques and modalities that may be utilized to improve, restore, or maintain healthy individual, couple, and family relationships. Course content also includes group counseling theories and techniques, including principles of group dynamics, group process components, developmental stage theories, therapeutic factors of group work, group leadership styles and approaches, pertinent research and literature, and group counseling methods. The course teaches students to develop and analyze clinical applications to family and group assessment and formulate treatment planning methods.

title: Optimal Performance Recovery
description: This course will focus on implementing micro and macro recovery routines and techniques to support optimal physical and mental performance. Students will examine research on the benefits of purposeful recovery and evidence-based recovery practices. Methods, which sport and performance psychology professionals can use to teach recovery within individual and group settings, will be explored using cognitive, behavioral, emotional, and/or spiritual strategies and techniques. Each student will develop their own personal recovery protocol. Additionally, students will choose a specific performance domain to develop a micro recovery training plan and implementation strategy using evidence-based psychology methods.

title: Psychological Assessment II
description: This course is a continuation of ATM 5220. This course is an art therapy assessment survey in psychological testing where students are trained in applying variables in a culturally and socially diverse society. Topics covered include psychological assessment; appraisal; testing of individuals, including basic concepts of standardized and nonstandardized testing; assessment techniques; norm referenced and criterion-referenced assessment; statistical concepts; social and cultural factors related to assessment and evaluation of individuals and groups; ethical strategies for selecting, administering, and interpreting assessment instruments; and techniques in counseling. Students develop the knowledge and breadth of specific treatment and assessment issues relevant to special populations, clinical interventions, psychopathology, and other clinical topics.

title: Cross Cultural Issues
description: This course covers the multicultural counseling and psychotherapeutic theories and techniques of practice used by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists and studies counselorsâ roles in developing cultural self-awareness and identity development while promoting cultural social justice. Students learn individual and community strategies for working with and advocating for diverse populations and explore counselorsâ roles in eliminating biases and prejudices. Other topics include cultural competency, which includes understanding the processes of intentional and unintentional oppression and discrimination, including experiences of race, ethnicity, class, spirituality, sexual orientation, gender, disability, and their incorporation into the psychotherapeutic process. Students learn to integrate and understand how cross-cultural mores and values impact the individual in society while developing a familiarity with a wide range of racial and ethnic backgrounds common among Californiaâs population, including, but not limited to, Blacks, Hispanics, Asians, and Native Americans. The effects of socioeconomic position on how poverty and social stress affect the individualâs mental health and recovery are also explored.

title: Human Sexuality
description: This course covers counseling and psychotherapeutic theories and techniques of practice used by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists and studies counselorsâ roles in developing cultural self-awareness and sexual identity development while promoting sexual and cultural social justice. Students learn individual and community strategies for working with and advocating for diverse populations and counselorsâ roles in eliminating biases and prejudices. Other topics include analyzing normal and psychosexual dysfunction; gender identity with attention to diagnosis of sexual problems; and major approaches to sex therapy using both verbal and nonverbal modalities. This course identifies experiences of race, ethnicity, class, and spirituality in the context of sexual orientation, gender, and disability and how these experiences are incorporated into the psychotherapeutic process.

title: Psychopharmacology
description: This course addresses counseling and psychotherapeutic theories, the counseling process, and techniques of practice used by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society. Students are provided with an overview and working knowledge of current evidence based practices that utilize psychopharmacological and clinical interventions relevant to psychopathology and are shown how practices impact the clinical counseling relationship and treatment.

title: Domestic Violence: Assessment & Treatment
description: This course covers counseling processes and psychotherapeutic techniques of practice used by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society. Students learn how to work with bilingual clients and special populations, the impact of cultural factors, how to understand spousal or partner abuse assessments, and same-gender abuse dynamics. Students develop knowledge of detection and intervention strategies, community resources, assessment and treatment planning, specific treatment issues, and application of counseling constructs.

title: Child Abuse
description: This course covers counseling processes and psychotherapeutic standards of practice and utilization of art therapy assessments by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society. Students are provided training in the history and theory of art therapy and in the application of art therapy with people in different treatment settings. Other topics include group work, art therapy assessment, legal and ethical issues related to art therapy practice, process for reporting child abuse, child abuse coverage, and assessment and treatment using a family systems approach.

title: Professional Ethics and Law I
description: This course covers application of Californiaâs laws and professional ethics governing counseling processes and psychotherapeutic standards of practice used by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society. Students receive an orientation to the profession and study the ethical standards and legal considerations of counseling. Topics covered include licensing law and process; regulatory laws that delineate the professionâs scope of practice; counselor-client privilege; confidentiality; what to do if the client is dangerous to self or others; treatment of minors with or without parental consent; the relationship between a practitionerâs sense of self and human values; functions and relationships with other human service providers; strategies for collaboration; and advocacy processes needed to address institutional and social barriers that impeded access, equity, and success for clients.

title: Starting a Private Practice
description: This elective course is offered periodically. This elective course is for students who wish to open a private psychotherapy practice. Participants of this course learn about the personal, professional, and ethical considerations to confidently opening the doors to a private MFT/Art Therapist psychotherapy practice. Topics covered include regulations for record keeping, how to provide service to a variety of populations, how to advertise aprivate practice, setting up an office space, benefits of joining a consultation group, using interns, payment scales, and how to handle taxes.

title: Special Topics in Art Therapy
description: Special Topics courses are 1-3 units and course topics vary each semester. Students are required to take a 1-unit class in Special Topics.

title: Eldercare in the 21st Century
description: This course is an examination of biological, spiritual, social, emotional, and psychological changes that impact aging and long-term care in a culturally diverse society. Students explore the myths of aging, assumptions about lifestyles, and the impact of societal views about care, application of counseling interventions, and treatment during the end of life. Students are provided with tools and information to work with an expanding aging population to better assess the issues faced by aging people at different stages of old age.

title: Substance Abuse: Thry Treatmt Assessmnt
description: In this course, students learn about the major approaches and evidence-based best practices of identification, evaluation, treatment, and instruction in the definition of substance use disorders. Students learn to apply the mental health recovery program of prevention of substance use disorders and addiction. Other topics include dual-diagnosis, substance abuse, co-occurring disorders, the medical aspects of substance use disorders, the effects of psychoactive drug use, current theories of the etiology of substance abuse and addiction, the role of persons and systems that support or compound substance abuse, and addiction in the treatment of children and adults. Students learn how to understand and evaluate high-risk populations with regard to substance use disorders and co-occurring disorders and how to refer and provide community resources that offer screening, assessment, treatment, and follow-up for the affected person and family.

title: Art Therapy Techqs: Special Populations
description: This is an experiential course, integrating and applying art therapy techniques with special populations in a variety of treatment settings. Course content covers counseling processes and psychotherapeutic standards of practice used by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society.

title: Art Therapy Techniques: Group Experience
description: This is an experiential course integrating art therapy techniques and practices of group work. Course content covers counseling processes and psychotherapeutic standards of practice used by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society. Other topics include group counseling theories and techniques, principles of group dynamics, group process components, developmental stage theories, therapeutic factors of group work, group leadership styles and approaches, pertinent research and literature, group counseling methods, and evaluation of effectiveness.

title: Digital Art Therapy
description: This is an experiential course, integrating art therapy techniques and digital and virtual reality technology. Course content covers an introduction to art making digital technology using 3D and virtual computers. Class covers psychotherapeutic standards in using technology in the therapeutic setting by art therapy, safety standards of individual and group counseling using digital media, pertinent research and literature, access for under-served and poor communities, and evaluation of effectiveness.

title: Psychological Assessment I
description: This is a survey course in psychological testing where students are trained in applying variables in a culturally and socially diverse society. Topics covered include psychological assessment; appraisal; testing of individuals, including basic concepts of standardized and nonstandardized testing; norm-referenced and criterion referenced assessment; statistical concepts; social and cultural factors related to assessment and evaluation of individuals and groups; ethical strategies for selecting, administering, and interpreting assessment instruments; and techniques in counseling. Students develop the knowledge and breadth of specific treatment and assessment issues relevant to special populations, clinical interventions, psychopathology, and other clinical topics.

title: Metaphors in Psychopathology: Children
description: This is an experiential course integrating aspects of child development from a systems perspective and explores issues related to normal and abnormal development and attachment and the impact of trauma on a child. Course content covers counseling processes and psychotherapeutic standards of practice used by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society.

title: Art Ther Techqs: Integrtng Creative Arts
description: This is an experiential course emphasizing the integration of creative arts therapies. Course content covers counseling processes and psychotherapeutic standards of practice used by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society.

title: Practicum I
description: 'Practicum is a 700-hour nonresident, supervised clinical fieldwork experience, which provides the student with an opportunity to work with clients in an institutional or community setting within the licensing organizationsâ scope of practice. Students are able to work with clients onhealth promotion and illness prevention while integrating ethical and legal practices under the supervision of a licensed professional. Course content covers counseling processes, psychotherapeutic techniques, and standards of practice used by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society. The supervised practicum is in applied psychotherapeutic techniques, art therapy and psychological assessment, diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment. Art therapy techniques are applied in a variety of settings with a variety of dysfunctional and healthy-functioning relationship types, which include premarital, couple, family, and child relationships. Prerequisite: Completion of all prerequisites in art and psychology, 30 hours of fieldwork, and 15 semester units of graduate course work, including ATM 5255 and ATM 5260. Corequisite: ATM 5365.'

title: Metaphors in Psychopathology: Adults
description: This is an experiential course integrating aspects of adults from a systems perspective, issues related to developmental growth and adaptation, and the impact of trauma on the adult. Course content covers counseling processes and psychotherapeutic standards of practice used by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society.

title: Career Counseling
description: This course explores the basic tenets of career counseling for vocational and occupational careers in the mental health profession. Students learn about career development theories and techniques, including career development decision-making models and the interrelationships among work, family, and other life roles and factors. Special consideration is given to career education, career exploration, career choice, the role of multicultural issues in career development, and career counseling. Testing instruments, including appropriate achievement tests; interest inventories; aptitude tests; personality tests; and valued tests are reviewed.

title: Professional Ethics and Law II
description: This course covers counseling and psychotherapeutic standards of practice and utilization of art therapy assessments by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society. Students learn the ethical and legal issues related to the practice of art therapy. This course is an adjunct to Professional Ethics and Law I and addresses aspects of ethics and the law as they relate to the Art Therapy profession.

title: Practicum Extension
description: 'This course covers counseling processes, psychotherapeutic techniques, and standards of practice used by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society. The supervised practicum is in applied psychotherapeutic techniques, art therapy and psychological assessment, diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment and integrates ethical and legal practices under the supervision of a licensed professional. Students work in a supervised clinical placement, which provides supervised fieldwork experience within the licensing organizationsâ scope of practice. Focus is on application of art therapy techniques in a variety of settings and with variations in patient age, practice setting, and type of intervention to provide for specialization in competency areas. Students work to promote health and provide illness prevention while working with dysfunctional and healthy-functioning relationships, which may include couples, families, and parent and child relationships. Students must have both individual and group supervision. Prerequisite: ATM 5355. Corequisite: ATM 5370.'

title: Seminar I
description: 'This small-group supervision seminar course provides students with opportunities to present cases from their practicum field experience and receive feedback from a faculty supervisor and other students. Students review and apply legal and ethical standards of practice, develop and prepare weekly theoretical treatment plans, and deliver an oral presentation. Corequisite: ATM 5350.'

title: Explorations in Sandplay
description: This course covers the basic tenets of Sandplay and its use in psychotherapy. The work of founder Dora Kalff is reviewed and discussed. There is a study of Sandplay figures and their meaning, especially in the sequencing of trays, with a variety of patients. Experientials and the deepening of symbols are included.

title: Seminar Extension
description: 'This small-group supervision seminar course provides students with opportunities to present cases from their practicum field experience and receive feedback from a faculty supervisor and other students. Students review and apply legal and ethical standards of practice, develop and prepare weekly theoretical treatment plans, and deliver an oral presentation. Corequisite: ATM 5355.'

title: Explorations in Art Therapy Literature
description: This Special Topic course is offered periodically. An independent study review of classic and recently published literature in the field of art therapy.

title: Metaphors in Psychopathology: Adolescents
description: This is an experiential course addressing different treatment settings and group work while integrating aspects of adolescent development, issues related to normal and abnormal development and attachment, and the impact of trauma on an adolescent in a family system. Course content covers counseling processes and psychotherapeutic standards of practice used by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society.

title: Trauma II: Trauma & Art Therapy Community Engagment
description: This course explores counseling and psychotherapeutic theories and techniques, including the counseling process in a multicultural society. It serves as orientation to wellness and prevention and introduces counseling theories used to assist in selection of appropriate counseling interventions; models of crises or trauma counseling consistent with current professional research and practice; development of a personal model of counseling; and multidisciplinary responses to crises, emergencies, and disasters. This community engagement class incorporates the student in research, discussion, observation, and reflection about a traumatized population, including formulation of best practice treatments incorporating art therapy specific to the needs of the community being served which is related to their trauma and homelessness status. The impact of acculturation, assimilation and trauma related systemic problems result from racism, oppression, and violence towards the vulnerable population. Today the world is filled with individuals, communities and nations who live with trauma or traumatic memories. Their traumatic experiences exacerbate feelings that interfere with the thinking part of their brains. Untreated, trauma affects an individualâs capacity to form trusting relationships and to find meaning in a chaotic world. With untreated dissociative and violent memories, individuals, communities, and countries continue the cycle of violence. This course is the second of two elective Trauma classes, (2 units of 3), as students must have taken the 1-unit foundation Trauma and Art Therapy Treatment course, GPY 4400 before taking this class. This class builds upon the first trauma class, with its lectures, group work, videos, ongoing reflection, and art experiences which will review best practices and equip professionals in treating traumatized individuals in diverse settings using both graphic and narrative languages. Additionally, this community engagement class provides students with a learning opportunity to focus on reciprocity, assets, having the community identify their needs, and learning from community members. Once the community has been identified and the instructor has collaborated with the community leaders to find out their needs and desires about this collaboration, the initial two Saturday classes will have the students meet the community to help further identify areas of collaboration and exploration. The agency will lead and dictate the needs of the engagement. After reaching a consensus, which meets the needs of the agency, the students will further research the identified community, their cultural history, the specific types of traumas incurred and to identify best practice treatments. This community engagement (CE) course will implement 12-15+ hours of community service, concluding with an art show. This class will include: several small student groups researching history and victimization in the communities, acculturation and assimilation of complex and compound traumas; the culturally relevant clinical treatments appropriate to support this community; as well as art therapy practices to employ within a short-term treatment model of community engagement.

title: Seminar II
description: 'This small-group supervision seminar course provides students with opportunities to present cases from their practicum field experience and receive feedback from a faculty supervisor and other students. Students review and apply legal and ethical standards of practice, develop and prepare weekly theoretical treatment plans, and deliver an oral presentation. Corequisite: ATM 5355.'

title: Focusing-Oriented Art Therapy
description: This Special Topic course is offered periodically. The Focusing-Oriented Art Therapy (FOAT) course uses a mindfulness-based approach, which integrates psychologist Eugene Gendlin's Focusing with Art Therapy. This course covers basic approaches of FOAT, reflective listening, and application to a variety of clinical populations.

title: Internatl & Multicultural Art Therapy
description: 'This is a course involving volunteer service for an international project, which gives participants a multicultural learning experience providing art therapy to avariety of diverse populations. Participants work as a team in an international setting under the direction and supervision of Art Therapy faculty to implement art therapy interventions with individuals and families in need. Participants embody the Dominican mission statement goals by providing service activities that empower and strengthen communities, increase social justice by actively partnering with local organizations, and foster a peaceful collaborative partnership while working collectively. Prerequisite: Advancement to candidacy and approval of the Program Director.'

title: Art as a Way of Knowing: International Ancient Sites and Living Art
description: This course provides an art therapy method, Living Art painting, as a way to explore cross-cultural experiences within a group setting. The course involves visiting ancient British sites of mystery and antiquity, as well as modern day London museums, responding with the art process of Living Art and journaling, as well as living, traveling, and interacting with the British cultures - all to enhance a growth in understanding art and culture as a way of knowing. The practice of response art making involves gaining insight, expanding one's vision, working and interacting mindfully during the five-day course. Students summate their experiences with a final art project, as well as a debriefing five-page paper done to integrate personal and academic meaning of their work.

title: Practicum II
description: 'This course covers counseling processes, psychotherapeutic techniques, and standards of practice used by marriage and family therapists, professional counselors, and art psychotherapists in a culturally diverse society. The supervised practicum is in applied psychotherapeutic techniques, art therapy and psychological assessment, diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment and integrates ethical and legal practices under the supervision of a licensed professional. Students work in a supervised clinical placement, which provides supervised fieldwork experience within the licensing organizationsâ scope of practice. Focus is on application of art therapy techniques in a variety of settings and with variations in patient age, practice setting, and type of intervention to provide for specialization in competency areas. Students work to promote health and provide illness prevention while working with dysfunctional and healthy-functioning relationships, which may include couples, families, and parent and child relationships. Students must have both individual and group supervision. Prerequisite: ATM 5355. Corequisite: ATM 5370.'

title: Research Project
description: This course prepares students for research and evaluation by exploring studies that provide an understanding of research methods, statistical analysis, and the use of research to inform evidence-based practices. The importance of research in advancing the profession of counseling and the use of statistical methods in conducting research, needs assessment, and program evaluations are also covered. Students learn the application of diversity factors relevant to research.

title: Research: Thesis Completion
description: This course prepares students for research and evaluation by exploring studies that provide an understanding of research methods, statistical analysis, and the use of research to inform evidence-based practices. The importance of research in advancing the profession of counseling and the use of statistical methods in conducting research, needs assessment, and program evaluations are also covered. Students learn the application of diversity factors relevant to research. Students produce a final Thesis or Grant Proposal as the culminating project.

title: Supervised Field Experience
description: Supervised practicum hours for MFT licensure that are not part of the Practicum/Seminar sequence. This course is not a program requirement. Approval is required.

title: Thesis Extension
description: The Thesis Extension segment is offered as an alternative to students who do not complete their thesis in the previous two segments. NOTE: An Incomplete grade is not permitted for this extension course. Students who fail to complete the thesis within the extension semester must reenroll in the extension course.

title: Trauma & Art Therapy Treatment
description: This Special Topic course is offered periodically. This course explores counseling and psychotherapeutic theories and techniques, including the counseling process in a multicultural society. It serves as orientation to wellness and prevention and introduces counseling theories used to assist in selection of appropriate counseling interventions; models of counseling consistent with current professional research and practice; development of a personal model of counseling; and multidisciplinary responses to crises, emergencies, and disasters.

title: Art as a Way of Knowing III
description: Developing and designing experiential approaches introduced in using art as a way of knowing others: Theory, practice, reflection, documentation, and focus on art-based assessments; create art as a way of knowing; attend to reflections on experiences in the creation of art, and in the relationship with the completed art; work in self-directed and faculty-directed approaches. Address neuropsychological components of art as a way of knowing.

title: Art as a Way of Knowing II
description: Theory and practice of art as a way of knowing self through art-based assessments: Create art as a way of knowing; attend to reflections on experiences in the creation of art and in the relationship with the completed art; work in self-directed and faculty-directed approaches. Address neuropsychological components of art as a way of knowing.

title: Art as a Way of Knowing V
description: Development of a cumulative creative project which addresses art as a way of knowing self and others: Development of a documentation of the learning experience related to art as a way of knowing.

title: Research Methodology
description: This course prepares students for research and evaluation by exploring studies that provide an understanding of research methods, statistical analysis, and the use of research to inform evidence-based practices. The importance of research in advancing the profession of counseling and the use of statistical methods in conducting research, needs assessment, and program evaluations is also covered. Students learn the application of diversity factors relevant to research.

title: Philosophical Foundations
description: Exploration of historical and anthropological practices in the use of art as a therapeutic process: Includes the development of the professional field, current status, and strategic plan for future.

title: Art as a Way of Knowing IV
description: Art as a way of knowing community and culture: Theory, practice, reflection, and documentation; create art as a way of knowing; attend to reflections on experiences in the creation of art, and in the relationship with the completed art; work in self-directed and faculty-directed approaches.

title: Art Therapy Practices I
description: Theoretical exploration with emphasis on practice implications: Overview of art therapy practices and emphasis on specific practices; documenting and communicating art therapy best practices.

title: Art Therapy Practices II
description: Theoretical integration of art therapy practices with traditional therapeutic practices: Art therapy practices with different populations and needs and future development in art therapy practices; multi-cultural variables and practices.

title: Practicum II
description: Defense of Community Engagement Proposal: Implementation of Community Engagement Proposal; defending and implementing community engagement proposal. Proposed plan will include objectives as well as a plan for measuring effectiveness of the intervention and recommendations for future development.

title: Practicum I
description: Developing and Designing Community Engagement Proposal: Students research, develop, and design an art therapy community engagement proposal. The community engagement proposal includes identification of specific goals, implementation strategies, and effectiveness measurements. Students develop understanding and practice of establishing community with emphasis on the role of the arts in this process. Research is conducted on potential community partners and a plan is developed that is inclusive of community partner mission, Dominican's mission, and the goals and objectives of the proposed intervention.

title: Art Therapy Practices III
description: Art Therapy practices with special populations, current trends in art therapy practices and development and innovation in art therapy practices: Experiencing, understanding, documenting, and communicating art therapy practices; plans for development of a cohort edition of on-line journal including use of digital media.

title: Practicum III
description: Implementation of Community Engagement Proposal: Completion of Community Engagement Project, the project evaluation, and development of the proposal report.

title: Practicum V
description: Defense of Practicum Program: Implementation of Practicum Program. Each student defends, and upon successful completion, implements Practicum Program.

title: Practicum IV
description: Development of Individual Practicum Program: Each student develops emphasis for their practicum work. Student works with faculty advisors in developing practicum proposal, including individualized learning objectives and service objectives, as well as plans for measuring effectiveness in the following areas: clinical skills, community service, international service, and teaching.

title: Practicum VI
description: Implementation of Practicum Program: Students complete implementation of practicum program and defense of completed practicum.

title: Practicum Extension
description: Students must be registered each semester for this class until they complete their practicum.

title: Art as a Way of Knowing I
description: Overview of the concept and practice of art as a way of knowing: Theory and experiential approaches in using art as a way of knowing self and others; art-based assessment procedures, pedagogical practices for reviewing literature and research, and engaging in experiential approaches to working with art as a means of understanding and communicating.

title: Research II
description: Quantitative methodology and analysis: Quantitative methods of identifying, organizing, and communicating information in research in art therapy and related fields; course addresses strengths and weaknesses of the use of quantifying strategies in understanding art therapy and its relationship to the human experience.

title: Research III
description: Implementation of Community Engagement Proposal: Completion of Community Engagement Project, the project evaluation, and development of the proposal report.

title: Research I
description: Methodological approaches meta-analysis of literature: Review of current research in the field of art therapy; trends in research in the field and contrast/compare of research results; focus on trends in methodological procedures used in art therapy studies, evaluating patterns in literature emphasis as well as areas that need additional research and theoretical study.

title: Theoretical Approaches
description: Theoretical integration of art therapy practices with traditional therapeutic practices and integration of creative art therapy approaches: Compare and contrast theoretical approaches to the use of art in therapy; research, reflection, and dialogue on the process/product balance of emphasis, the role of interpretation, and other-directed and client-directed approaches; interface of art therapy and major verbal therapy approaches and creative art therapy approaches.

title: Art Therapy Practices IV
description: Current trends in art therapy practices and the development and innovation in art therapy practices: Plans for development of a cohort edition of on-line journal including use of digital media will be outlined and a strategy implemented.

title: Research IV
description: Dissertation Proposal Development: Writing the dissertation proposal; design and development of the research question and claims and evidence that serve as the basis of the proposed methodology for inquiry.

title: Research V
description: Dissertation Proposal Defense: Continuation of writing dissertation proposal, culminating in the defense of the dissertation proposal.

title: Research VII
description: Dissertation Completion: Final completion and defense of the dissertation research.

title: Research VI B
description: Dissertation Research Implementation II: Conducting the dissertation research.

title: Dissertation Extension
description: Students must be registered each semester for this class until they complete their dissertation.

title: Cell and Developmental Biology
description: This course introduces life on the planet emphasizing cell biology, molecular genetics and development and physiology using plant, animal and microbial model systems throughout. Students may utilize microscopic techniques, physiological recording equipment, and techniques of spectrophotometry - 3 lecture hours.
