Recommended only for students for whom this is a required course and those interested in a pre-health professional program. BIO 1100 / BIO 1105 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: Biological Foundations
description: An introduction to the basic principles of life, leading to broader understanding of our biological environment and our place within it. Explores issues where science and technology impact society, such as the genetic modification of food and humans, drug-resistance in microbes, and the human impact on biodiversity. Origin of life, structure and function of cells and organisms, metabolism, reproduction, evolution, biotechnology, the ecosystem, biodiversity, and adaptation are discussed - 3 lecture hours. Recommended for non-Biological Sciences or non-Chemistry majors. Does not count toward the degree for Biological Sciences majors. BIO 1400 / BIO 1405 meet the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirements. BIO 1400 / BIO 1405 are designed to meet all requirements for the Life Science portion of the California Multiple Subject Teaching Credential when taken in combination with PHYS 1000 / PHYS 1005 and PHSC 2200 / PHSC 2205 .

title: Organismal Biology Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 1000 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. BIO 1000 / BIO 1005 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: Cell and Developmental Biology Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 1100 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. BIO 1100 / BIO 1105 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: Biological Foundations Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 1400 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. BIO 1400 / BIO 1405 meets the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirements. BIO 1400 / BIO 1405 are designed to meet all requirements for the Life Science portion of the California Multiple Subject Teaching Credential when taken in combination with PHYS 1000 / PHYS 1005 and PHSC 2200 / PHSC 2205 .

title: Slithering, Slimy, Scaly, and Sticky Animals " Herpetology 101"
description: Do you have an affinity for exotic pets? Especially cold-blooded ones? Or maybe you are interested in learning more about amphibians and reptiles? This course will give you an introduction to herpetology (the study of amphibians and reptiles), captive animal care, and a few techniques that herpetologists use to study these animals. Possible topics include: an introduction to the diversity of amphibians and reptiles of the world, exploration of the natural history of California amphibians and reptiles, captive animal care and enclosure design (zoos and pets), common ailments of captive and wild animals, and a broad overview of surveying techniques (with a focus on frog call surveys). This course uses the platforms Moodle, Zoom, and YouTube and is taught in eight synchronous lessons. Students need reliable internet access. Recommended for new and returning students.

title: Research VI A
description: Dissertation Research Implementation I: Conducting the dissertation research.

title: Human Anatomy
description: Morphology of organ systems and integration of the anatomical systems will be emphasized - 3 lecture hours. Recommended only for students for whom this is a required course. Does not count toward the degree for Biological Sciences majors. BIO 2500 / BIO 2505 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: Career Paths for Science Majors
description: Have you considered majoring in science, but arenât sure if itâs right for you? This course is designed for students interested in pursuing a range of careers in science and healthcare fields. The focus of the course is to outline the roles, responsibilities, and educational requirements for select careers in scientific and environmental research and health care professions to help you understand career opportunities available with a degree in biology or chemistry. This course uses the platforms Moodle, Zoom, and YouTube and is taught in eight synchronous lessons. Students need reliable internet access. Recommended for new and returning students

title: Human Anatomy Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 2500 - 3 lab hours. A microscopic presentation of the tissue types in the body and examination of the individual organ systems and their components. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. BIO 2500 / BIO 2505 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: Global Wildlife Ecology
description: This course will take you virtually to different parts of the world to learn about global biodiversity, conservation and anthropogenic impacts. Case studies will be used to illustrate the diversity of life on the planet, its importance, what are the unique and common challenges to conserve wildlife, and how local action contributes to global change. This course uses the platforms Moodle and Zoom and is a blend of four synchronous sessions and asynchronous learning. Students need reliable internet access. Recommended for new and returning students.

title: Organismal Biology
description: This course investigates the evolutionary history of biodiversity with an emphasis on plant and animal form and function. Students will utilize microscopes as well as live organisms to perform experiments - 3 lecture hours. Recommended only for students for whom this is a required course and those interested in a pre-health professional program. BIO 1000 / BIO 1005 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: Human Physiology Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 2600 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. BIO 2600 / BIO 2605 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: Genetics and Molecular Bio Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 2800 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Genetics and Molecular Biology
description: Topics to be studied will include: Mendelian genetics, gene linkages, sex determination, chemistry and structure of genes, chemical nature of mutations, population genetics, molecular genetics and biotechnology - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: BIO 1100 / BIO 1105 with a grade of âC-â (1.7) or better.

title: Ecology and Evolution
description: Topics to be studied will include: micro-macro evolution, adaptation, speciation, population growth and regulation, competition, predator-prey interactions, symbiosis, succession, energy and nutrient flow in ecosystems, food webs, conservation, and environmental impacts - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: BIO 1000 / BIO 1005 with a grade of âC-â (1.7) or better. BIO 2900 / BIO 2905 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: Ecology and Evolution Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 2900 . This course uses the natural history of California to illustrate topics from lectures and teach quantitative methods. Subject material will be learned through on-campus exercises and off-campus field trips (reached by carpool). 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. BIO 2900 / BIO 2905 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: Medical Terminology
description: Medical terminology is the foundation of medical science. Students will learn how to decipher this new language by breaking down the root words, prefixes and suffixes. Additionally, they will learn the common abbreviations and acronyms used in the medical industry.

title: Advanced Cell Biology
description: In-depth study of the structure, organization, and biochemistry of the cell. Areas of study include: chemical components of a cell; metabolism of major cellular constituents; molecular organization of the cell; enzymatic catalysts and metabolic processes; mechanisms of heredity; and cell growth and differentiation - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: BIO 2800 / BIO 2805 or consent of the instructor - 3 lecture hours.

title: Invertebrate Biology
description: Evolutionary relationships of invertebrate animals, including anatomy, physiology, classification, development, and ecology - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: BIO 2900 / BIO 2905 .

title: Advanced Cell Biology Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 3000 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Advanced Genetics
description: In-depth study of genetics. Selected topics to be included: gene linkage, chromosome mapping, chemical nature of mutations, protein synthesis and its regulation, transposable genetic element, and gene chemistry, cloning and sequencing - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: BIO 2800 / BIO 2805 or consent of the instructor.

title: Advanced Genetics Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 3200 - 3 lab hours. Techniques of DNA analysis including recombination, restriction mapping and polymerase chain reactions will be used to study gene expression and regulation in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic organisms. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Biological Research Methodology I
description: This is a research based laboratory course designed to introduce methods for researching the literature and writing a research proposal in the fields of field biology, organismal biology, molecular cell physiology, biochemistry, biotechnology, or environmental sciences - 3 lab hours.

title: Invertebrate Biology Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 3150 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Human Physiology
description: Processes and mechanisms underlying the normal functioning of the body: cellular metabolism and processes. Special emphasis on the functioning of the nervous, endocrine, urinary, respiratory, immune, digestive and reproductive systems - 3 lecture hours. Recommended only for students for whom this is a required course. Does not count toward the degree for Biological Sciences majors. BIO 2600 / BIO 2605 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: Nutrition for Disease Prevention and Treatment
description: This course covers fundamental aspects of human nutrition and metabolism from a nursing perspective including the basic biochemical and physiological functions of protein, carbohydrate, fat, vitamins, minerals and phytonutrients and how they are processed and metabolized by the human body. Students will consider nutrient needs throughout the lifespan from pregnancy and infancy through the older adult, and explore nutrition for disease prevention and treatment. Prerequisites: CHEM 1600 / CHEM 1605 or higher and BIO 2600 / BIO 2605 or BIO 4600 / BIO 4605 . Recommended only for students for whom this is a required course.

title: Physical Education for Schools
description: This course explores theoretical topics from lectures related to movement knowledge and skills, self-image and personal and social development will be addressed through active exploration and reflection. Topics include: basic movement skills, movement forms, health and physical fitness, physical and psychological development, and social, cultural and historical aspects of movement forms - 1 lecture hour or alternative/compressed timeframe. Concurrent enrollment with BIO 3300 required, or taken separately with Instructor approval. Required for Education Studies majors, non-majors require instructor approval.

title: Health and Wellness for Schools
description: This course explores the various dimensions of health, health education and health promotion for schools. Priority school health issues identified by the Centers for Disease Control will be addressed including: nutrition and physical activity; growth, development and sexual health; safety and violence and injury prevention; alcohol, tobacco and other drugs; mental, emotional and social health; and personal and community health. This course introduces students to skills-based health education and provides a foundation for planning and implementing effective health education and promotion programs - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: BIO 1000 / BIO 1005 or higher, or consent of the instructor. Concurrent enrollment with BIO 3320 required, or taken separately with Instructor approval. Does not count toward the Biological Sciences major. Required for Education Studies majors, non-majors require instructor approval.

title: Nutrition for Disease Prevention and Treatment Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 3455 - 3 lab hours. This course provides students an opportunity to apply concepts learned in lecture through case studies and the development of practical skills.

title: Independent Study: Biology
description: Directed reading on specialized topics or development of a set of special laboratory skills. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. BIO 4999 is also available for upper division credit. Contract required.

title: Microbiology for Health Sciences
description: Classification, morphology, growth, and metabolism of bacteria, non-bacterial microorganisms, and viruses. Physical and chemical methods for determination and control of microorganisms; human defense mechanisms and immunology - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: CHEM 1600 / CHEM 1605 or equivalent or consent of the instructor. Recommended only for students for whom this is a required course. Does not count toward the degree for Biological Sciences majors.

title: Science Seminar
description: Lecture on current research and career opportunities in biology through the seminar format - 1 lecture hour. Requirement of Biological Sciences Majors. May be repeated for credit. Offered pass/no pass.

title: General Microbiology
description: In-depth study of cell structure, cell metabolism, and genetics of microorganisms. Also, microbial pathogenesis and host defense mechanisms are included. Emphasis is placed on molecular microbiology including the study of bacterial gene expression, DNA replication, and recombinant DNA mechanisms - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: BIO 2800 / BIO 2805 and BIO 2900 / BIO 2905 or consent of the instructor.

title: Microbiology for Health Sciences Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 3600 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Medical Microbiology Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 3620 . Laboratory sessions provide an in-depth study of microscopic organisms of medical importance, with particular emphasis on bacterial pathogens. Laboratory will introduce students to clinical methods of handling, isolating, and identifying microorganisms. In addition, students will be taught various physical and chemical methods used to control the growth and spread of pathogenic microorganisms - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: General Microbiology Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 3610 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Comparative Anatomy
description: Vertebrate ancestry and phylogeny; structure and evolutionary trends in the integumentary, skeletal, muscular, digestive, respiratory, circulatory, urogenital, nervous, and endocrine systems of vertebrates in relation to function and evolution - 3 lecture hours.

title: Comparative Anatomy Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 3700 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Immunology
description: Topics to be studied include: innate and adaptive immunity, antigen and antibody structure, mechanisms of immunoglobulin gene rearrangements, antibody function, antigen presentation to T-cells, lymphocyte development and activation, B-cell and T-cell mediated immunity, diseases of the immune system, including allergy, autoimmunity, AIDS and cancer immunology - 3 lecture hours.

title: Field Biology
description: A field class applying principles and techniques from natural history to the natural environment. An original research project and report will result from a field trip to selected localities in California and Nevada. Prerequisites: BIO 2800 / BIO 2805 and BIO 2900 / BIO 2905 . Course may be taken for 1-4 units. Course may be repeated for credit, not to exceed a total of 4 units. Duration of trip is usually one week. Trip fee variable.

title: Immunology Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 3750 - 3 lab hours. Uses antibodies as a research and diagnostic tool. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Anatomy and Kinesiology for Dance
description: Anatomical study of the human muscular-skeletal system and the scientific principles of movement as related to dance. To include introduction to anatomical and physiological concepts that form the foundation of dance kinesiology. Analysis of structural considerations to understand muscle function, somatic awareness, and dance injury prevention - 3 lecture hours. Recommended only for Dance majors. Does not count toward the degree for Biological Sciences majors. BIO 3810 / BIO 3815 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: Anatomy and Kinesiology for Dance Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 3810 . Movement will be studied to increase joint function, alignment, and biomechanical efficiency of fundamental dance motions - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. BIO 3810 / BIO 3815 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: Advanced Scientific Communication
description: This is a research based course designed to develop advanced techniques for oral and written presentations of scientific information. The focus is on higher level scientific literacy, using detailed article summaries and critical analyses. Primary literature will be used to write scientific research papers and develop informational oral presentations. A variety of topics may be investigated, including field biology, organismal biology, molecular cell physiology, biochemistry, biotechnology, and environmental sciences - 3 lecture hours. Spring semester. Prerequisite: BIO 3990 or consent of instructor.

title: International Field Biology
description: In this field-based class students learn about global biodiversity and sustainability studying natural environments overseas. Research projects may be conducted and the results will be analyzed and discussed in a written report. Students may enroll for 1 to 4 units. Course can be taken more than once for credit. Duration of trip usually ten to twelve days. Trip fee variable.

title: Neuroscience
description: This course investigates the nervous system at the molecular, cellular and system level. Topics covered include historical foundations of neuroscience, neurophysiology, neurochemistry, neural development, various sensory and motor systems, the neurological basis of behavior, memory and learning, and the consequences of neuronal disease and trauma - 3 lecture hours.

title: Neuroscience Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 3850 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Medical Parasitology
description: A basic course which considers parasitism as one of the most prevalent biological interactions. The principles discussed are structural, evolutionary, and physiological adaptations to parasitism as well as host specificity, immunity, parasitic life cycles. Living and fixed representative species are examined in the laboratory. The course primarily focuses on parasites of biomedical and veterinary interest - 3 lecture hours.

title: Animal Physiology
description: This course deals with the functional mechanisms that underlie the life processes in animals. Particular focus will be on energy utilization, gas transport, kidney function, and muscle function of organisms from diverse habitats - 3 lecture hours.

title: Medical Parasitology Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 4220 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Animal Behavior Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 4300 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Medical Microbiology
description: Lecture sessions provide an in-depth study of microscopic organisms of medical importance, focusing specifically on those viruses, bacteria, fungi, protozoa, and helminths involved in pathogenic processes. Lectures will cover prokaryotic and eukaryotic cell structure, microbial growth and metabolism, microbial classification, virology, microbial pathogenesis, and host defense mechanisms - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: BIO 2800 / BIO 2805 and BIO 2900 / BIO 2905 or consent of the instructor.

title: Animal Physiology Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 4110 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Advanced Ecology
description: Advanced concepts in ecology will be studied in this course such as modeling of competition and predation, and factors influencing community structure. The lecture concentrates on ecosystem energies, population dynamics, community structure and physiological adaptations - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: BIO 2900 / BIO 2905 .

title: Introduction to Scientific Communication
description: This is a research-based course designed to introduce techniques used for oral and written presentations of scientific information. The initial focus is on general scientific literacy using article summaries and critical analyses. Primary literature will be used to write short scientific research papers and develop informational oral presentations. A variety of topics may be investigated including field biology, organismal biology, molecular cell physiology, biochemistry, biotechnology, and environmental sciences - 3 lecture hours. Fall semester.

title: Advanced Ecology Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 4310 - 3 lab hours. Investigates population and community problems in specific environments. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Developmental Biology
description: Gametogenesis and fertilization; ovulation and uterine cycles, cleavage, gastrulation, determination of primary organ rudiments; embryonic adaptations; development of ectodermal, endodermal, and mesodermal structures; cellular differentiation; metamorphosis - 3 lecture hours.

title: Molecular Cell Biotechnology Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 4410 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Molecular Cell Biotechnology
description: A lecture and project-based course that focuses on the molecular and genetic tools used to analyze and modify genetic material and their applications in biotechnology, medicine, and drug development. Topics will include sequencing techniques, PCR, cloning, the manipulation of gene and protein expression, DNA and protein microarrays, RNAi silencing and other current technology used in the study of molecular biology - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: BIO 2800 / BIO 2805 .

title: General Human Anatomy Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 4500 - 3 lab hours. An examination of the individual organ systems and their components using models, preserved organ dissections, and interactive virtual dissections. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. Recommended for Biological Sciences and Chemistry students applying to health professional programs including pharmacy, veterinary, and physician assistant programs.

title: General Human Physiology Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 4600 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. Department Chair approval required for non-Biological Sciences majors.

title: General Human Physiology
description: Processes and mechanisms underlying the normal functioning of the body: cellular metabolism and processes. Special emphasis on the functioning of the nervous, endocrine, urinary, respiratory, immune, digestive and reproductive systems - 3 lecture hours. Recommended only for students for whom this is a required course. Department Chair approval required for non-Biological Sciences majors.

title: Histology
description: This course studies the microscopic anatomy which comprises the structural basis of normal physiology. Students are expected to identify the specialized cells, tissue and organs, and understand the structural basis of their function - 3 lecture hours.

title: Animal Behavior
description: This course studies animal behavior with an emphasis on the evolution and adaptive significance of behavior. Areas to be studied will include genetics and development of behavior, communication, feeding and predatory behavior, mating and reproductive behavior, and social behavior. Open to both majors and non-majors - 3 lecture hours.

title: Histology Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 4810 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Computational Chemistry and Biology Lab
description: Laboratory component for BIO 4900 - 3 lab hours.

title: Computational Chemistry and Biology
description: This course provides an introduction to computational chemistry and biology. Topics include bioinformatics, biostatistics, molecular dynamics, and quantum mechanics - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: CHEM 2000 / CHEM 2005 , CHEM 2100 / CHEM 2105 , BIO 2800 / BIO 2805 .

title: Biological Research Methodology II
description: Research based laboratory course designed to introduce students to the methods of biological research including sampling plan, data collection, data analysis and discussion in terms of the practical process of scientific experimentation in the fields of organismal biology, molecular cell physiology, biochemistry, biotechnology, or environmental sciences - 6 lab hours each. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. Prerequisite: BIO 2990 or equivalent.

title: Developmental Biology Lab
description: Lab component for BIO 4510 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Biological Research Methodology III
description: Research based laboratory course designed to introduce students to the methods of biological research including sampling plan, data collection, data analysis and discussion in terms of the practical process of scientific experimentation in the fields of organismal biology, molecular cell physiology, biochemistry, biotechnology, or environmental sciences - 6 lab hours each. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. Prerequisite: BIO 4990 or equivalent.

title: General Human Anatomy
description: This course will focus on human organ systems and the integration of the anatomical systems will be emphasized - 3 lecture hours. Recommended for Biological Sciences and Chemistry students applying to health professional programs including pharmacy, veterinary, and physician assistant programs.

title: Internship: Biology
description: This course provides opportunities for experiencing the practical and experimental application of biological principles. Students will be involved in volunteer or paid work experience in biological research projects conducted by agencies and institutions outside the university - for example, state parks, zoos, private or public biological industries, and universities. Course may be taken for 1-3 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Prerequisite: consent of the Internship Faculty Advisor. Contract required. Course may be repeated for credit.

title: Directed Research: Biology
description: Experimental research project in various fields of biology, carried out by students under the guidance of an instructor. This involves literature searching, developing research project, performing research, collection and analyzing of data, oral and poster presentation of research at a peer forum, and a written submission of the work in a thesis format. This course has an additional $25 Fee to cover lab materials. Course may be taken for 1-6 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. Contract require.

title: Teaching Assistant: Biology
description: This course provides teaching experience at the undergraduate level. Students will assist faculty members in preparing, setting up, delivering, and deconstructing laboratory (and/or discussion section) instructional units in biology courses, conducting review sessions and tutoring students under the direct supervision of a faculty mentor. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Prerequisites: a grade of 'B' or higher, an overall GPA of 3.0 or higher, and consent of the instructor. Contract required. Course may be repeated for credit.

title: Graduate Seminar III
description: This seminar discusses scientific and professional knowledge for graduate students in the sciences. It includes methods of scientific presentation, scientific writing, critical thinking and logical organization for clarity and accuracy, and techniques of interpreting primary scientific literature. Students make and evaluate technical and scientific presentations with an emphasis on journal articles on current literature in their field of interest. Students also participate in presentations and discussion of major papers in their field with their peers. Seminars are taken in numeric sequence each semester of enrollment.

title: Independent Study: Biology
description: Directed reading on specialized topics or development of a set of special laboratory skills. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. BIO 2999 also available for lower division credit. Contract required.

title: Graduate Seminar I
description: This seminar discusses scientific and professional knowledge for graduate students in the sciences. It includes methods of scientific presentation, scientific writing, critical thinking and logical organization for clarity and accuracy, and techniques of interpreting primary scientific literature. Students make and evaluate technical and scientific presentations with an emphasis on journal articles on current literature in their field of interest. Students also participate in presentations and discussion of major papers in their field with their peers. Seminars are taken in numeric sequence each semester of enrollment.

title: Graduate Research Methodology
description: This course explores standard, acceptable and ethical research practices in advanced biological sciences, with an emphasis on record keeping, authorship, peer review, mentoring, and participation in research that may utilize human or animal subjects. Additional topics may include: public policy, medical ethics, conflicts of interest, ownership of data and intellectual property, and other potential problems stemming from use of data from experiments involving humans and animals. The course includes a component focusing on the writing of scientific papers, culminating into their thesis progress report - 3 lecture hours.

title: Graduate Seminar IV
description: This seminar discusses scientific and professional knowledge for graduate students in the sciences. It includes methods of scientific presentation, scientific writing, critical thinking and logical organization for clarity and accuracy, and techniques of interpreting primary scientific literature. Students make and evaluate technical and scientific presentations with an emphasis on journal articles on current literature in their field of interest. Students also participate in presentations and discussion of major papers in their field with their peers. Seminars are taken in numeric sequence each semester of enrollment.

title: Advanced Biochemistry
description: This course is designed to provide students the opportunity to study advanced topics in biochemistry, including cell signaling, protein function and regulation of gene expression. Discussion of techniques and technologies used to study biochemical problems at molecular, sub-cellular and cellular levels will be emphasized - 3 lecture hours.

title: Advanced Molecular Biotechnology
description: This course will cover research techniques and technologies used in molecular biology. It is designed to study more advanced concepts and how they may be applied in biological sciences. Topics include but are not limited to: PCR techniques, recombinant DNA technology, site-directed-mutagenesis, epigenetics, gene silencing, microarrays, and gene expression systems. The course will also include a component focusing on the writing of scientific papers - 3 lecture hours.

title: Graduate Special Topics: Biological Sciences
description: This course is designed to give graduate students fundamental knowledge of the multiple disciplines, concepts, models and techniques that are crucial for a well-rounded career path in the biological sciences. Topics may include: stem cell research, molecular and cellular biology of aging, orphan diseases, cancer and other age-related pathologies. The course will also emphasize the complexities and controversies in these topics, and the increasing need to incorporate multiple approaches and disciplines into research projects - 3 lecture hours. Topics vary each semester. Course may be repeated for credit provided topics differ.

title: Biological Research Methodology IV
description: This is a research based writing laboratory course designed to introduce students to the methods of presentations and writing of scientific topics, audience analysis and adaptation, techniques of support and visualization, organization for clarity and accuracy, and techniques of interpreting and answering questions. Students make and evaluate technical and scientific presentations with an emphasis on seminar reports and professional conference papers. Course may be taken for 1-2 units. 1 unit equals 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. Prerequisite: BIO 4991 or equivalent.

title: Graduate Research II
description: Experimental research projects in various fields of biology, carried out by students under the guidance of an instructor. Consulting with their graduate advisor, students will design a reading plan from current and/or seminal literature in their field of interest. This will involve literature searching, developing research projects, performing research, collection and analysis of data, oral and poster presentation of research at a peer forum, and a written submission of the work in a thesis format. Research internships are taken in numeric sequence each semester of enrollment. Course may be taken for 2-5 units. Spring Semester.

title: Environmental Chemistry
description: This course is a study of chemical principles as applied to ecosystems, soil, water and water management, pest control, sediments, nutrients, sewage and air pollution from hazardous chemicals and solar and other renewable energy courses - 3 lecture hours.

title: International Field Biology
description: In this field-based class students learn about global biodiversity and sustainability studying natural environments overseas. Research projects will be conducted and the results will be analyzed and discussed in a written report. Duration of trip usually ten to twelve days. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. Trip fee variable. Course may be repeated for credit.

Consulting with their graduate advisor, students will design a reading plan from current and/or seminal literature in their field of interest. This will involve literature searching, developing research projects, performing research, collection and analysis of data, oral and poster presentation of research at a peer forum, and a written submission of the work in a thesis format. Research internships are taken in numeric sequence each semester of enrollment. Course may be taken for 2-5 units. Fall Semester.

Research internships are taken in numeric sequence each semester of enrollment. Summer Semester.

description: This is the culmination of the masterâs research experiences producing a masterâs thesis. This is a written final thesis and an oral presentation. Approval and successful defense of the thesis presentations are required to complete the program. May be repeated for credit.

Consulting with their graduate advisor, students will design a reading plan from current and/or seminal literature in their field of interest. This will involve literature searching, developing research projects, performing research, collection and analysis of data, oral and poster presentation of research at a peer forum, and a written submission of the work in a thesis format. Research internships are taken in numeric sequence each semester of enrollment. Summer Semester.

title: Exploring the World of Business: BSB Summer Experience
description: This development seminar focuses on exploring teamwork through real time team activities and competitions. Team performance and achievement is discussed each session. This course uses the platforms Moodle and Zoom. Students need reliable internet access. For new Barowsky School of Business (BSB) freshman and BSB transfer students only.

title: Economics
description: The course introduces the fundamental theories of microeconomics, macroeconomics, and environmentalÂ economics. The first part of the course focuses on microeconomic analysis including, the behavior of consumers and firms, demand and supply, opportunity cost, market structures, and decision making for efficient results. The second part moves on to macroeconomic concepts such as national production,Â economicÂ growth, employment, inflation, the role of government regulation, monetary policy, and fiscal policy. The last portion of the course deals with techniques that relate to depletion and degradation of natural systems and their components, including land, energy, air water, and biodiversity.

title: Master's Thesis Continuation
description: Continuation of Master's thesis process. May be repeated, as needed.

title: Exploring the World of Business: Discovering the World of Business and You I
description: This personal/professional development seminar explores contemporary business issues and interactions with society, both domestically and globally. While practicing critical thinking through inâclass discussions and written reflections of current events depicted in the news, students consider personal values and beliefs. Examining business functions, inter-related disciplines and engaging with faculty and professionals, students consider optimal matches for a major linked to career by exploring their values, interests, talents, skills, strengths and self-efficacy. Dominicanâs and Barowsky Business Schoolâs core values and educational programs are reviewed Experience foundations of listening, networking at campus events and field trips to San Francisco Bay Area businesses. Required for all business majors.

title: Excel 1 - Section 1
description: In this introductory course, you will learn what you can do using Excel. In a very short period of time, you will master the basics of Excel that will provide a solid foundation for more advanced Excel functionalities. Upon completion of the course, you will be able to: - Learn genesis of spreadsheet - Understand the Excel interface and document structure - Enter data in an Excel worksheet - Work with formulas and the most common Excel functions - Format your data to make it more presentable - Create charts to visually present data - Develop an Excel template for easy reuse This course uses the platforms Moodle and Zoom and is taught in four synchronous sessions. Students need reliable internet access and Microsoft Excel.

title: Introduction to Sport Management
description: This course provides a general introduction to the sports management field. There are three main goals associated with this course: (a) to provide a perspective of the sports and recreation industry, (b) to explore the wide variety of exciting career opportunities in an expanding array of sport and recreation delivery systems such as parks and recreation, health clubs, university and professional sport, and (c) to focus on some of the most important issues impacting the sport and recreation field today.

title: Graduate Research VII
description: Experimental research projects in various fields of biology, carried out by students under the guidance of an instructor. Consulting with their graduate advisor, students will design a reading plan from current and/or seminal literature in their field of interest. This will involve literature searching, developing research projects, performing research, collection and analysis of data, oral and poster presentation of research at a peer forum, and a written submission of the work in a thesis format. Research internships are taken in numeric sequence each semester of enrollment. Fall Semester.

title: OL: StoryTelling and Data Visualization with Tableau
description: In this introductory course, students will learn the basics of Storytelling and Data Visualization. Upon completion of the course, students will be able to: (1) Understand the Tableau interface (2) Create visuals, dashboards, and stories with Tableau (3) Create and use dynamic and static visuals with Tableau (4) Understand and apply basic rules of the visual creation This course is to be designed for incoming and returning students who are interested in this topic.

title: Accounting
description: This course introduces accounting transactions, financial statements, and the use of accounting information from a management perspective, while connections between financial and managerial accounting are established through the learning of topics from accounting concepts to tools and principles. A thorough study of the accounting cycle emphasizes how information moves through an accounting system and how information could be produced and used in controlling, planning, budgeting, reporting, and decision-making processes.

title: Exploring the World of Business: Building Your Business Career Foundation I
description: This personal/professional development seminar prepares business sophomores to align their interests within business and related fields. Using critical thinking techniques, students conduct a SWOT gap analysis of their knowledge and skills, focusing on developing oneâs "personal brand" and self-efficacy leading to career and personal well being. Â Studentsâ explorations seek to align personal interests with business education and career paths, developing a preliminary action plan for building their career foundations. Workshops include preparation for internships, study abroad, examining employment settings, and interactions with the faculty and business communities to facilitate educational goals linked to career decision-making. Required for all business majors.

title: Graduate Research V
description: Experimental research projects in various fields of biology, carried out by students under the guidance of an instructor. Consulting with their graduate advisor, students will design a reading plan from current and/or seminal literature in their field of interest. This will involve literature searching, developing research projects, performing research, collection and analysis of data, oral and poster presentation of research at a peer forum, and a written submission of the work in a thesis format. Research internships are taken in numeric sequence each semester of enrollment. Spring Semester.

title: Business Communications and Critical Thinking
description: This course helps students to develop sound business writing and presentation skills while honing critical thinking/analytic skills. Writing concepts include planning strategies, composing efficiently, revising effectively and quickly, providing constructive coaching to another writer, producing successful documents with enhanced image and effect, improving managerial review skills, recognizing nuances of written communication, and the effective use of âheadlinesâ. Presentation concepts include planning a presentation strategy, designing the appropriate use of media, identifying audience need and characteristics, packaging the appropriate message for the audience, and using the body and voice effectively so as to develop a âstageâ presence.

title: Exploring the World of Business: BSB Summer Camp
description: This development seminar focuses on exploring teamwork through real time team activities and competitions. Team performance and achievement is discussed each session. This course uses the platforms Moodle and Zoom. Students need reliable internet access. For all returning Dominican students and transfer students only.

title: Innovation and Design Thinking
description: In a hands-on laboratory setting, students will learn about how firms produce innovations today using the revolutionary concept of design thinking. Students will learn the process of ideation and generate creative ideas. They will use their creative thinking skills to learn to build rapid prototypes and design human-centered solutions that are desirable, feasible and viable. They will learn how to innovate by balancing resource, time and technological constraints. Students will participate in a class trip to Silicon Valley to visit an innovation lab and network with innovators and entrepreneurs. A trip fee will be assessed.

title: Exploring the World of Business: Building Your Business Career Foundation II
description: This personal/professional development seminar prepares business sophomores to align their interests within business and related fields. Using critical thinking techniques, students conduct a SWOT gap analysis of their knowledge and skills, focusing on developing oneâs "personal brand" and self-efficacy leading to career and personal well being. Â Studentsâ explorations seek to align personal interests with business education and career paths, developing a preliminary action plan for building their career foundations. Workshops include preparation for internships, study abroad, examining employment settings, and interactions with the faculty and business communities to facilitate educational goals linked to career decision-making. Required for all business majors.

title: Excel 1 - Section 2
description: In this introductory course, you will learn what you can do using Excel. In a very short period of time, you will master the basics of Excel that will provide a solid foundation for more advanced Excel functionalities. Upon completion of the course, you will be able to: - Learn genesis of spreadsheet - Understand the Excel interface and document structure - Enter data in an Excel worksheet - Work with formulas and the most common Excel functions - Format your data to make it more presentable - Create charts to visually present data This course uses the platforms Moodle and Zoom and is taught in four synchronous sessions. Students need reliable internet access and Mirosoft Excel. For transfer and returning students.

title: Exploring the World of Business: Discovering the World of Business and You II
description: This personal/professional development seminar explores contemporary business issues and interactions with society, both domestically and globally. While practicing critical thinking through inâclass discussions and written reflections of current events depicted in the news, students consider personal values and beliefs. Examining business functions, inter-related disciplines and engaging with faculty and professionals, students consider optimal matches for a major linked to career by exploring their values, interests, talents, skills, strengths and self-efficacy. Dominicanâs and Barowsky Business Schoolâs core values and educational programs are reviewed Experience foundations of listening, networking at campus events and field trips to San Francisco Bay Area businesses. Required for all business majors.

title: Information Technology and Operations Management
description: Analyze the advanced management methods utilized by modern enterprises in the operations and supply chain functions to gain competitive advantage in the global marketplace. Evaluate the integration of information technology (IT) in operations management (OM) and supply chain management (SCM) fields. Utilize medium to upper level spreadsheet tools to provide solutions for OM and SCM problems in the current business world. Introduce the project management body of knowledge that identifies the best practices to ensure the success of projects. Understand the significance of corporate social responsibility and sustainability issues in operations and supply chain management.

title: Exploring the World of Business: Developing Your Business Career Network I
description: This seminar is designed to help students expand their career potential through internship and career network development. Additionally, students learn more about preparing (personally) for life after university. Students hone networking skills through attending an etiquette dinner, a local business chamber networking event, the BSB career fair, and alumni panel/networking receptions. Support for obtaining internships takes place through business communications refresher seminars, mock interviews, and informational interviews. The studentâs personal development includes learning the importance of credit and how to build a sound credit score, and an introduction to building wealth through 401K/403B/profit sharing participation. Required for all business majors.

title: Exploring the World of Business: Developing Your Business Career Network II
description: This seminar is designed to help students expand their career potential through internship and career network development. Additionally, students learn more about preparing (personally) for life after university. Students hone networking skills through attending an etiquette dinner, a local business chamber networking event, the BSB career fair, and alumni panel/networking receptions. Support for obtaining internships takes place through business communications refresher seminars, mock interviews, and informational interviews. The studentâs personal development includes learning the importance of credit and how to build a sound credit score, and an introduction to building wealth through 401K/403B/profit sharing participation. Required for all business majors.

title: Sustainability Driven Business Innovation
description: Sustainability is a lens for the next wave of the frontier of innovation and entrepreneurial activity. The course provides sustainability as a fair and equitable approach to making the world a better place for the present and future generation. The course uses student-centered and a pragmatic business perspective to engage students with in-depth independent research into company operations.

title: Corporate Finance
description: This course introduces financial and management in terms of its most important functionsâraising capital at a minimum cost and risk, allocating those funds between competing short and long-term uses and managing working capital. Some of the topics include financial statement analysis, time value of money, valuation of bonds and stocks, capital budgeting, cost of capital, risk and return, cost of capital and asset pricing models, and the capital asset pricing model (CAPM) are also included. Social and ethical issues in the field of finance will also be explored. Prerequisite: BUS 2003 or equivalent.

title: Marketing
description: Understanding, creating, communicating, and delivering customer value and satisfaction are at the very core of modern marketing thinking and practice. The marketerâs role is to build a portfolio of products or services that bring value to targeted customers, to be able to assess that value and price accordingly, and to plan for the effective distribution of the goods. This course is designed to introduce students to core marketing concepts and functions in modern firms. Discussions will involve the role of marketing and business environment in which firms face their primary challenges and opportunities; strategic considerations including segmentation, targeting, and positioning; and specific marketing mix decisions to help execute a marketing strategy effectively. Case study and simulation will be used to bridge the gap between theory and praxis, emphasizing the application of marketing concepts and theories. Emphasis will also be placed on describing the marketing process and the implications of these activities for society. Prerequisite: BUS 2030 . Students may take concurrently with Instructorâs approval.

title: Business Statistics and Data Analytics
description: Utilizes statistical tools and hone analytical skills to prepare for make well-informed decisions in todayâs complex business world. Statistical methods to apply to business scenarios include measures of central tendency and dispersion, probability distribution, sampling estimation, hypothesis testing, analysis of variance, correlation, multiple regression and business/economics statistical forecasting. Computer software packages commonly utilized in business will be applied to facilitate the data analysis.

title: Leadership in Action
description: Through a leadership practice project, serve as an effective team leader, or team member, implementing a change project guided by an approved plan with an organization, mentored by a faculty member. LeaderShape Blueprints, Leadership Practicum Projects continuations, or projects by student government officers and clubs, and the Torch Program are included among the options. LeaderShape Institute graduates are eligible to earn one-unit, which may be completed within two semesters following completion of the Institute. LeaderShape On-Site Coordinators are eligible for an additional one-unit for each year of service. Minimum 45 hours per unit, plus a learning contract and reflection paper.

title: Sport Marketing
description: This course entails the application of modern marketing principles in the sports industry. Promotions and public relations, sport consumer behavior, strategic market planning, marketing information management, sport marketing research, marketing communications, social media, branding, sponsorship, and endorsement are among the topics covered.

title: Spreadsheet Modeling
description: Students will learn to translate managerial decision scenarios into mathematical models and find optimal solutions using Excel. Upon completion of this hands-on course, students will enhance their problem-solving and spreadsheet skills. Topics include linear programming, integer linear programming, nonlinear programming, forecasting, queuing models, and simulation.

title: Exploring the World of Business: Launching Your Business Career II
description: This seminar focuses on preparing graduating seniors for their career start. A business communication refresher is completed early in the year and then the focus turns to additional networking opportunities (business chamber mixer and the BSB Career Fair) and evaluating career options. Cost of living and earning differentials throughout the United States are explored, and students learn how to evaluate an offer package (healthcare benefits, salary, commission compensation, and other benefits). Additional sessions include discussions on debt management and building wealth. Required for all business majors.

title: Exploring the World of Business: Launching Your Business Career I
description: This seminar focuses on preparing graduating seniors for their career start. A business communication refresher is completed early in the year and then the focus turns to additional networking opportunities (business chamber mixer and the BSB Career Fair) and evaluating career options. Cost of living and earning differentials throughout the United States are explored, and students learn how to evaluate an offer package (healthcare benefits, salary, commission compensation, and other benefits). Additional sessions include discussions on debt management and building wealth. Required for all business majors.

title: Finance & Economics of Sports
description: This course entails the application of modern financial and economic principles to the field of sports management. Financial resource management, economic impact analysis, financing major sport events, venue financing, and economic pillars underlying the professional sports leagues are among the topics that will be covered in this course.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent research may be conducted on a topic of special interest to a student and a supervising faculty member. This course is available on a limited basis by request, and subject to the approval of the Director of the Undergraduate Business Program and the Dean. Offered as needed.

title: Investment Principles
description: This course investigates the valuation of financial assets and their optimal allocation in a portfolio. Other topics include macroeconomic and industry analysis, securities markets, the relationship between risk and return, portfolio performance evaluation, active and passive investing strategies, and behavioral finance. Prerequisite: BUS 3013 or equivalent.

title: Marketing Research
description: This course is developed to provide a fundamental understanding of marketing research methods and analytical techniques as employed by contemporary marketers. This course incorporates formulating critical managerial problems, developing relevant hypotheses, conducting data analysis, and making actionable marketing decisions. Students will be exposed to a broad number of both quantitative and qualitative methodologies; including survey, experiments, focus group, and etc. Prerequisite: BUS 3024

title: Financial Statement Analysis
description: This course is recommended for students who wish to pursue careers in accounting, consulting, banking, and investment management. Students will learn how firms communicate through financial statements and how investors assess firm value. Accounting standards and financial theories are the prerequisites, but the integration of accounting and financial fundamentals advances students from the role of information producer to the level of decision makers in the arena of investment where interpreting financial reports and valuing firm equities are musts. Prerequisite: BUS 2003 .

title: Intermediate Accounting 2
description: An evaluation of the sources of debt and equity capital for the business is the primary focus of this class. Implications of the time value of money on debt-financing decisions; lease versus purchase options for assets; preparation and interpretation of the statement of cash flows; and the computation and analysis of earnings per share and its importance in merger and acquisitions are key topics. Prerequisite: BUS 4030 .

title: Internet Marketing
description: The internet has revolutionized business practices, bringing unprecedented transparency and opportunities for customer engagement. Businesses are creating virtual communities of interest and support. This has implications throughout organizations, including for product development, operations, customer service, and investor relations. Marketing practices are being fundamentally reoriented around the new reality of instant, on demand access to information. This hands-on course engages students in the strategic challenges and opportunities businesses face in the internet age. An emphasis is placed on internet marketing strategies and practices, including website development, SEO/SEM, and the responsible use of various channels of social media to achieve organizational objectives. Prerequisite: BUS 3024

title: Introduction to Accounting Technology
description: This course uses technology to introduce students to basic accounting principles and skills necessary in the workplace. It utilizes a commonly-used accounting software package such as QuickBooks to enable students learn skills related to bookkeeping, tax preparation, accounts payable and accounts receivable. Students will work on the software hands-on in the classroom doing assignments and complete other course deliverables.

title: Taxation: A Business Perspective
description: Students will determine Federal individual and corporate tax liabilities and prepare Federal individual and corporate tax returns. The tax implications of investment, retirement, and business decisions are covered. The class will study the taxation of property dispositions, partnerships and S corporations, accounting for income taxes, state and local taxes and compute a corporationâs earnings and profits and a shareholderâs dividend income. Students may apply their knowledge of taxation in a service-learning opportunity. Prerequisite: BUS 2003 .

title: Global Business
description: Examine how the global business environment impacts the competitiveness of firms and nations in the global marketplace using international business theories and tools. Explore how domestic firms, multinationals and countries need to respond to, or lead, in an environment of global competitive pressures. Through case analysis and in-class discussions, students learn concepts and theories of international trade, investment, foreign market entry and culture, business ethics in international business, as well as models of business-government relations. Students then devise an appropriate global business strategy for an organization that seeks to operate in a foreign market.

title: Auditing
description: This course will study concepts and problems in the substantiation of financial and related information, including ethical, legal and other professional issues, evolution of auditing trends, and current issues are covered. Application of auditing standards, methods and procedures of auditing, sampling methods, control systems, control design, and control evaluation are covered. Prerequisite: BUS 2003 .

title: Financial Markets and Institutions
description: This course examines the transfer of funds in the economic system through financial intermediaries. Topics include the flow of funds, capital markets, debt, liquidity, nature of money in the US economy, the innovations and interrelationships of institutions within the changing financial services industry, government regulations, and agencies, and the impact of public policy on economic transfers. Prerequisite: BUS 3013 or equivalent.

title: Leadership and Teams in Organizations: Theory and Practice
description: Examines theories and practices of leadership, teams and change in organizations. Consider organizational structures and governance in profit and not-for profit settings and variables that create constructive organizational cultures. Examine leadership versus management; groups versus teams; impact of leadership styles and emotional intelligence; ethical influence of power and motivation of leader and team member behaviors effecting change; inspiring shared vision; setting measurable change planning objectives achieving innovative business solutions; employ conflict resolution and facilitation skills to accomplish effective group and team decision-making. Apply these theories as leaders and team members in a Leadership Practice Consulting Project with a client organization.

title: Personal Selling
description: Examines theory and practice in the principles and art of selling. Topics include planning, organizing, leading, evaluating and controlling of sales force activities.

title: Digital Marketing
description: Recent digital technologies and platforms have dramatically changed the way companies communicate with, listen to, and learn from consumers. This course examines the digital channels and platforms for marketing communications and provides an overview of how modern organizations use them to reach and interact with their target market. Students will learn about planning, implementing, managing, and assessing the commonly used digital marketing applications. Prerequisite: BUS 3024 .

title: Global Innovation & Entrepreneurial Practicum
description: Students will analyze models of global innovation and entrepreneurship using case studies and a hands-on approach. They will learn how business ecosystems, public policy, and the global business climate interact to shape business strategy and drive the creation of entrepreneurial ventures. Students will travel on a 10-day international business trip to meet with businesses and immerse themselves into a foreign business culture. A non-refundable trip fee will be assessed.

title: International Marketing
description: The overall objective is to enhance studentsâ understanding of principles and applications of marketing in the international environment. This course involves (1) risk analysis (political, economic, socio-cultural, technological, and legal) and (2) globally vs. locally customized marketing efforts. Prerequisite: BUS 3024

title: Advanced Management Practices and Negotiations
description: This course focuses on the advanced management practices necessary to succeed in a global business environment. Building on applied theory, students will research and apply management skills in: on-boarding, planning and budgeting, project management, meeting planning, interviewing prospective employees, conflict resolution and negotiations, peer coaching, managing virtual teams, and innovation and creativity. Prerequisite: BUS 4046 or instructorâs permission. Course fee may be assessed.

title: Entrepreneurship
description: This course focuses on developing successful business ideas; moving from an idea to an entrepreneurial firm and then managing and growing the firm. Students will learn the process of planning, starting, and positioning new businesses which link directly to customer requirements through understanding unmet market opportunities or customer needs. Students will develop a business plan for a start-up firm. Topics related to social entrepreneurship, legal, and IP issues will also be explored. Students in the Global Innovation and Entrepreneurship (GIE) minor will participate in a start-up pitch and business plan competition. It is recommended that students first take BUS 2059 if completing the GIE Minor.

title: Managing Not-For-Profit Organizations
description: This course will examine the independent non-profit sector and the topics affecting the governance and management of nonprofit organizations. Readings and discussions will cover a wide range of themes including types of nonprofits (e.g. social welfare, charitable nonprofits, foundations), size and composition of the board of directors, grant writing, volunteer recruitment and management, fundraising, and strategic planning and execution.

title: Intermediate Accounting I
description: This course presents an in-depth study of financial accounting principles and financial statement preparation. Accounting for cash and receivables, fixed assets, intangible assets, current liabilities, long-term liabilities, equity, investments, income taxes, pension and postretirement benefits, and leases are covered. Students also learn how time value of money is applied to assets and liabilities through accounting works. Prerequisite: BUS 2003 .

title: Socially and Environmentally Responsible Management
description: This course examines socially and environmentally responsible management. These topics include new ways of thinking about business and economy involving environmental and social interaction. Students explore current developments in sustainability across the functional areas of businesses, and learn to critically evaluate social and environmental problems through a holistic managerial lens. Topics ranging from sustainability frameworks, stakeholder management, corporate social responsibility, the triple bottom line, cross-sector collaborations, environmental management systems, social entrepreneurship, fair trade, and sustainability reporting will be covered. Global perspectives are emphasized.

title: Data Mining
description: This course is an introductory course on data mining. It introduces the basic concepts, principles, methods, implementation techniques, and applications of data mining, with a focus on two major data mining functions: (1) pattern discovery and (2) cluster analysis.

title: ADC: Economics for Business
description: An accelerated course in macro and micro economics, explains the international and domestic factors which affect the performance of national economies, including fiscal and monetary policy, balance of payments, and inflation. The behavior of specific industries, firms, and consumers is analyzed in economic terms of supply and demand, profit maximization, and imperfect competition. This theoretical foundation course should be completed early in the program. Prerequisite: MATH 1350 or equivalent with a grade of "C" (2.0) or better, or appropriate placement test score.

title: ADC: Information Technology for Managers
description: This course will introduce students to an overall understanding of information technology (IT) including customer databases, financial systems, and communication networks. Topics covered include how information technology supports managerial decision-making and the development of an information technology system from needs assessment through to implementation.

title: Managing Global Human Resources
description: This course comprehensively examines global human resource management. Students will develop intercultural competencies and awareness; identify trends in the globalized workforce; and evaluate policies, practices, and roles in global human resource management. Topics include global staffing, training, compensation, evaluation, motivation, international corporate responsibility and employee health and safety.

title: Consumer Behavior
description: This course is developed to provide a fundamental understanding of online/offline consumer behavior. Its goals are to (1) provide theoretical understanding of consumer behavior; (2) cultivate analytical capabilities in using consumer behavior research; and (3) enable students to apply learned theories and skills to marketing management decisions. Prerequisite: BUS 3024

title: ADC: Business Analytics & Quantitative Reasoning
description: Learn quantitative and qualitative approaches to analyzing data in business. They will apply logical reasoning and statistical methods to business scenarios for management and decision-making. Topics include calculating and interpreting descriptive statistics, data analytics for business decision making, data mining from market research data, estimating probabilities, and performing predictive analysis including forecasting and evaluating business and economic data.

title: Statistics and Research/Decision Making
description: This course introduces managers to the statistical techniques and research methodology most applicable in making management decisions. Topics include interpreting descriptive statistics and organizing data from market research, probability and forecasting and understanding the research methodology available in both qualitative and quantitative design. This course should be completed early in the program. Prerequisite: MATH 1350 or equivalent with a grade of "C" (2.0) or better, or appropriate placement test score.

title: ADC: Managerial Accounting for Managers
description: The course broadens studentsâ understanding of complex financial decision-making skills including budgeting, project evaluation, acquisition valuation, break-even analysis, and return on investment. The use of net present value, real options theory, and payback period will be practiced within the context of real life and academic case studies. Prerequisite: BUS 4120 or permission of the instructor.

title: ADC: Budgeting for Managers
description: Prepares entry and middle level managers to develop skills required to prepare and manage budgets. Students will learn how to read and interpret accounting reports such as income statements and balance sheets, cost-volume-profit relationships, master budgeting and variance analysis.

title: Financial Management & Analysis
description: Introduction to capital markets, understanding the sources, costs, and ramifications of short-term and long-term funding. Topics also include capital budgeting, cost of capital, debt and equity financing and overall financial structure. Prerequisite: BUS 4115 and BUS 4120 .

title: ADC: Business Communications for Managers
description: Today's business environment has an inherent need to communicate across cultures and on a global scale. Students apply communication strategies appropriate to the audience and purpose of the message. Writing includes planning strategies, composing efficiently, revising effectively and quickly, providing constructive coaching to another writer, producing successful documents with enhanced image and effect, improving managerial review skills, recognizing nuances of written communication, and effective use of âheadlinesâ. Presentations include planning a presentation strategy, designing appropriate use of media, identifying audience need and characteristics, packaging appropriate messages for an audience and using body and voice effectively to develop a âstageâ presence.

title: ADC:Organizational Behavior & Human Resources
description: Examine theories and practices of organizational behavior and structures to understand human behavior and social processes that effectively organize, motivate and lead people in multicultural and/or global business settings. Challenges of managing people in a geographically dispersed or virtual organization and designing organization structures to support rapidly evolving eÂ­commerce business models are explored. Examine ethical human resource practices key to creating healthy organizational cultures and the importance of cultural intelligence in todayâs workplace. Explore coaching for performance and development, high-performing teams and legal issues in personnel management.

title: ADC: Marketing for Management
description: Integrates marketing theory and practice with everyday examples of marketing in the for-profit and non-profit workplaces focusing on product/service, price, placement and promotion. Examine consumer behavior and determine customer-focused marketing strategies, value pricing and distribution. Understanding role of market research in marketing strategy and target marketing opportunities, using digital and social media techniques, and satisfying them thereafter in a decisive way, is an essential component of marketing theory.

title: ADC: Bus Communications: A Cross-Cultural Perspective
description: Effective communication is vital to adapting to a work place which is constantly changing due to globalization and increasing diversity within the workplace. In today's business environment there is an inherent need to communicate and negotiate across cultures and on a global scale. Students will apply communication strategies appropriate to the audience and purpose of the message. Further, students will develop an understanding of the role of gender, race, and cultural values, beliefs, customs, and attitudes in verbal and non-verbal communications patterns in presentation with and without media, design and delivery. Emphasis will also be placed on understanding the importance of cultures in management decisions, communicating across cultures, and communication patterns.

title: ADC: Financial Accounting for Managers
description: This course will build an understanding of how accounting rules for reporting activity in the accounts receivable, accounts payable, purchasing, and order entry functions relate to and build an organizationâs general ledger. Students will identify the links between the general ledger and standard financial reports (such as income statements, balance sheets, and cash flow projections) and teach analysis tools for diagnosing and comparing the financial performance of current or proposed businesses. This financial foundation course should be completed early in the program. Prerequisite: MATH 1350 or equivalent with a grade of "C" (2.0) or better, or appropriate placement test score.

title: ADC: Leadership & Teams: Theory and Practice
description: Examine leadership and team theories applied to business to achieve shared organizational goals. Consider the fundamentals of organizational change processes to effect innovative and continuous change for business success. Key topics include: Management versus leadership practices; leadership traits, skills and behaviors across situations and cultures; roles of power and ethics in effective leadership behaviors; team development and follower theories/practices; creating and communicating a shared organizational vision; aligning individuals and teams in planning and implementation; harnessing conflict, promoting collaboration and accountability, building trust and facilitating agreement for creative decision making. Complete and reflect upon oneâs leadership effectiveness assessment.

title: ADC: Organization Development and Leading Change
description: Examine factors impacting change in the workplace from multiple perspectives. Explore processes for leading organizational change, and foundations of the field of Organization Development (OD) effecting system-wide improvements to achieve goals and organizational effectiveness. Engage in various activities to illustrate the skills involved in assessing, planning and implementing organizational change. Experiment using intervention tools to assess organizational systems programs and implement change efforts that garner support of organization members. Practice leadership facilitation skills including interpersonal awareness, personal power and influence, and process consultation capabilities to be an effective change agent.

title: ADC: Leadership and Project Management (Capstone)
description: Going beyond project managers to being project leaders, apply a blend of technical competencies and skills for planning and controlling projects while understanding interpersonal variables that drive successful team project outcomes. Examine the project management life cycle, defining project parameters, matrix management challenges, effective project management tools and techniques, and the role of a project manager as an organizational leader, with or without authority, coaching members and leveraging the combined talents of group members to function as high-performing project teams. Apply ethical principles, creating a motivating team atmosphere to successfully lead and manage a project on time and on budget.

title: ADC: Leadership & Change Management
description: Leadership and organizational change theories and applications for achieving clarity of vision and aligning organizational capacity for continuous change leading to competitive advantage will be examined and experienced.
