The topics explored include leadership traits, behaviors and styles demonstrated in ethically effective organizations, leadership in diverse and international settings, differences between management and leadership, corporate social responsibility and strategies for leading, inspiring and motivating individuals and teams in planning and implementation, resolving conflict, facilitating agreement for decision making and promoting collaboration to achieve innovative change.

title: ADC: Strategic Management: Capstone Project
description: This course focuses on strategic management topics, linking together the various disciplines of study into a capstone course, incorporating accounting, finance, marketing, production, human resources and information technology. Students will be introduced to the many variables that must be addressed when managing a business, focusing on the unique challenges in a rapidly changing and highly competitive global environment. Using a variety of medium, students will focus on the total enterprise, the industry and the competitive environment in which it operates. Students will delve into historical business challenges, current management methodologies, assessment of their own ability to manage strategically, and will look into the future to anticipate and prepare for emerging trends. A minimum grade of âCâ is required to pass. This course is open to Senior students only.

title: ADC: Corporate Social Responsibility, Law, Ethics
description: Explore the interface between legal requirements governing businesses and the emerging discipline of Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR). Examine history of the idea of CSR and how it has emerged simultaneously with a new generation of legal requirements imposing quasi-legal requirements on industry: Product disclosure and labeling laws; organic and similar green certifications; carbon neutral supply chain requirements; green claims regulation; transparency and reporting regulation; and related emerging rules. Explore industry-specific codes of conduct that operate as de facto legal requirements within the electronics, pharmaceutical, and automotive sectors to drive responsible corporate conduct.

title: Senior Seminar
description: This seminar requires that students meet with a thesis advisor to develop a topic of interest for a culminating research paper/project. After a topic is selected and approved, each student will submit an outline of proposed steps and a timetable for the completion of each step. At the discretion of the advisor, students meet individually or in small groups.

title: Special Topics in Business
description: A variety of topics may be introduced on an ongoing basis.

title: Special Topics: Accounting
description: A variety of topics may be introduced on an ongoing basis. Courses will generally address current developments in accounting. Topics may include: Auditing; Cost Accounting; Advanced Accounting, Accounting Research. Offered as needed.

title: Special Topics: Finance
description: A variety of topics may be introduced on an ongoing basis. Courses will generally address current developments in finance. Topics may include: Financial Modeling; Money and Financial Institutions. Offered as needed.

title: ADC: Organizational Behavior and Structure
description: A review of the classic literature on organizational behavior and structures, and the new tasks, roles, and structures that have been enabled by communication and office automation technology. Topics include the challenges of managing people in a geographically dispersed or virtual organization and designing organization structures to support rapidly evolving e-commerce business models.

title: Special Topics: International Business
description: A variety of topics may be introduced on an ongoing basis. Courses will generally address current developments in International Business. Topics may include: Multinational Corporations; Import/Export and International Trade. Offered as needed.

title: Sports Management Internship
description: Students must have completed all lower division prerequisites before being accepted into the Internship Program. Internships set in a variety of sports and recreation settings help students build professional competencies and a network of industry contacts. Managerial placements in the Conlan Recreation Center and in professional and intercollegiate sports, health and fitness clubs, sport marketing and management firms provide students with real âon-the-jobâ working experiences in the sport industry. Students may apply a maximum of 3 Internship units to the minor. Spring, Fall Semesters, Summer Sessions. Students may not register for this class online. They must first meet with faculty sponsor, complete and file paperwork to Registrar for course registration.

title: Special Topics: Management
description: A variety of topics may be introduced on an ongoing basis. Courses will generally address current developments in management. Topics may include: Staffing and Benefits; Conflict Resolution. Offered as needed.

title: Special Topics: Marketing
description: A variety of topics may be introduced on an ongoing basis. Courses will generally address current developments in marketing. Topics may include: Marketing in the Music Industry; Strategic Brand Management; Advertising and Promotion. Offered as needed.

title: Strategic Management
description: This capstone course for seniors integrates functional knowledge acquired throughout the major (economics, accounting, finance, marketing, management, etc.). This course introduces the concept of strategic management and considers the basic direction and goals of an organization, the general, industry, and competitor environments, and organizational resources and capabilities. The focus is on creating sustained success through strategy formulation and execution in different types of firms and industries. Topics include: strategic planning, industry analysis, diversification, as well as ethics, social responsibility, and sustainability in strategic decision-making. A minumum grade of 'C' is required to pass. Prerequisite: Senior standing.

title: ADC: Global Business and Cultural Management
description: Examines major trends of globalization and their impact on the economy and business at the local, national and global levels. Focuses on the importance of intercultural communications in multiple aspects of business and management. The roles of trade agreements, international financial institutions, and Foreign Direct Investment are examined. The effect of demographics on business, human rights and environmental issues are explored.

title: Research Fellow
description: High achieving students (CGPA of 3.5+) are eligible to apply and be considered for a BSB research fellowship. The research fellowship is designed to provide high achieving students with a way to customize their education while providing support to faculty conducting research related to scholarly/curricular pursuits. This independent form of study/research must receive the Deanâs approval and will require a fellowsâ contract similar to the independent study ( BUS 4999 ) contract. Course requirements will be determined by the faculty mentor and research fellows will be awarded a stipend by the Barowsky School of Business.

title: Internship in Business
description: The ability to work off campus will provide students with the opportunity to develop an awareness of business topics and skills in an actual work setting outside the classroom. Getting the opportunity to observe business related practices in the workplace and being able to connect this experience to the classroom theories are a particular emphasis. Students will meet the academic requirements determined by the faculty sponsor. A student may complete up to six units of internship for the degree. A maximum of three units of internship may count toward any single concentration. Internships may also be taken for elective credit. Business students present the results of their internships at the Internship Presentation Event held near the end of each semester.Students may not register for this class online. They must first meet with faculty sponsor, complete and file paperwork to Registrar for course registration.

title: Seminar
description: The seminar focuses on engaging students with successful entrepreneurs, business professionals, and leaders from non-profit organizations. Emphasis is placed on having students interact, question, and learn from the experiences of these guest speakers. Previous iterations of this seminar have led to successful outcomes for seminar participants in terms of internships and job placement.

title: Teaching Assistant/Tutoring
description: This course provides teaching experience at the undergraduate level and allows students to gain experience working with students both in a classroom and on an individual basis. Permission of the Department is required. Fall, Spring Semesters.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent research may be conducted on a topic of special interest to a student and a supervising faculty member. This course is available on a limited basis by request, and subject to the approval of the Department is required. Offered as needed.

title: Topics in Community Action and Social Change
description: Selected topics that provide the student with additional breadth or depth in relation to community action and social change. Some learning community courses might be issue-based and others might be process-based. Some examples of critical and creative pedagogy in these courses: Social Biographies; Legislative Policy Analysis; School to Prison Pipeline; Courage to Lead.

title: Theory and Practice for Community Action and Social Change
description: This course provides foundational frameworks for analyzing oppression, power and privilege. Through readings, social engagement and case studies, students examine larger contexts and structures that impact individuals and diverse communities. Students learn about key theories in community engagement literature and participate directly with community to build civic skills and learn about social advocacy from local leaders. Students reflect on their own identities and civic roles in the larger society.

title: Community Engaged Capstone 2
description: ' Students refine, implement, and complete their community-engaged signature work or capstone with guidance from a faculty member with both Social Justice disciplinary and community engagement expertise. Students present their work at the universityâs Scholarly and Creative Works conference. ' Prerequisite: CASC 4901

title: Topics in Community Action and Social Change IV
description: Selected topics that provide the student with additional breadth or depth in relation to community action and social change. Some learning community courses might be issue-based and others might beprocess-based. Some examples of critical and creative pedagogy in these courses: Social Biographies; Legislative Policy Analysis; School to Prison Pipeline; Courage to Lead.

title: Community Engaged Capstone 1
description: This course provides students an opportunity to develop a culminating community-engaged signature work or capstone. The course provides a community of practice where students with shared interests and commitments come together with a faculty member to guide and support research, organizing, advocacy, and action steps that meet community-identified interests. Students will also explore career options and postgraduate goals. Prerequisite: CASC 3405

title: Topics in Community Action and Social Change II
description: Selected topics that provide the student with additional breadth or depth in relation to community action and social change. Some learning community courses might be issue-based and others might be process-based. Some examples of critical and creative pedagogy in these courses: Social Biographies; Legislative Policy Analysis; School to Prison Pipeline; Courage to Lead.

title: Leadership Internship
description: The ability to work off campus will provide students with the opportunity to develop an awareness of leadership topics and skills in a setting outside the classroom. Students will meet the academic requirements determined by the faculty sponsor. Students may not register for this class online. They must first meet with faculty sponsor, complete and file paperwork to Registrar for course registration.

title: Community-Engaged Internship
description: Opportunity for students to deepen knowledge, skills, and commitment to addressing social justice issues through work with specific community partner organizations and schools. The internship is arranged with the Center for Community Engagement or Service-Learning program, in cooperation with the Career Development office to complete required paperwork. Internship may be 1-4 units.

title: Community of Practice and Signature Work
description: A faculty-led learning community for students in community-engaged internships, research, and arts-based practice to critically reflect on civic identity, develop signature work, and cultivate dialogue about systemic issues that impede the well-being of all.

title: Independent Study
description: The privilege of independent study is possible for students who are prepared to undertake it based on student need or interest with approval of the Chair of the department, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. The study must be in an area not covered by a regularly scheduled course.

title: Conceptual Chemistry Lab
description: Lab component of CHEM 1100 . Laboratories complement the lecture discussions of the theory behind the fundamentals of chemistry with hands-on experiments that demonstrate chemical fundamentals and applications - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. CHEM 1100 / CHEM 1105 meet the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirements.

title: Community Engaged Research Methodology and Project Design
description: This course will introduce students to the research methodologies, effective best practices, and principles associated with the continuum of community-based participatory research (CBPR) approaches in which stakeholders collaborate to meet community-identified interests. Students will be guided to ask questions and gather community knowledge centered around a specific, researchable issue identified with partners and residents Prerequisite: CASC 3400 or Service Learning Director permission.

title: Conceptual Chemistry
description: This course enables students to understand and appreciate chemistry in the context of their own lives and that of the significant issues faced by science and the world today: global warming, efficacy of alternate fuels, translational public health, and genetic engineering, among others. Students will examine the fundamentals of chemistry, such as thermodynamics, atomic and molecular structure, kinetics, and equilibrium. The fundamentals will be illustrated using applications in the physical world, which will provide students with the knowledge and vocabulary to make informed decisions in the future - 3 lecture hours. CHEM 1100 / CHEM 1105 meet the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirements.

title: Chemistry for Health Sciences
description: This course is designed to expose students in the health sciences to chemical principles important to human physiology. Topics include: an introduction to organic compounds and functional groups; biochemistry of carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, nucleic acids and enzymes; protein synthesis and metabolic pathways - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: students must complete one of the following before they are eligible to enroll into CHEM 1600 : 1) SAT II Chemistry score equivalent to a 'B' (3.0); 2) CHEM 1500 / CHEM 1505 at Dominican or a general or introductory chemistry course from another college within the past two (2) years with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or better; 3) CLEP General Chemistry subject examination with a passing score of 50 or higher; or 4) course challenge or 5) placement based on appropriate scores on Mathematics and Chemistry placement exams. Recommended only for students for whom this is a required course. Does not count toward degree for Chemistry majors. CHEM 1600 / CHEM 1605 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: Introduction to Chemistry Lab
description: Lab component for CHEM 1500 - 3 hours lab. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Chemistry for Health Science Lab
description: Lab component of CHEM 1600 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. CHEM 1600 / CHEM 1605 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: General Chemistry I Lab
description: Lab component of CHEM 2000 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. CHEM 2000 / CHEM 2005 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: Career Paths for Science Majors
description: Have you considered majoring in science, but arenât sure if itâs right for you? This course is designed for students interested in pursuing a range of careers in science and healthcare fields. The focus of the course is to outline the roles, responsibilities, and educational requirements for select careers in scientific and environmental research and health care professions to help you understand career opportunities available with a degree in biology or chemistry. This course uses the platforms Moodle, Zoom, and YouTube and is taught in eight synchronous lessons. Students need reliable internet access. Recommended for new and returning students

title: Topics in Community Action and Social Change III
description: Selected topics that provide the student with additional breadth or depth in relation to community action and social change. Some learning community courses might be issue-based and others might be process-based. Some examples of critical and creative pedagogy in these courses: Social Biographies; Legislative Policy Analysis; School to Prison Pipeline; Courage to Lead.

title: General Chemistry II
description: This course is a continuation of General Chemistry I. Topics include introduction to chemical analysis, gas and solution equilibria, acid-base chemistry, metals and non-metals, oxidation-reduction, electrochemistry, radiochemistry, introductory organic and biochemistry - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: CHEM 2000 / CHEM 2005 with a grade of âC-â (1.7) or better.

title: General Chemistry II Lab
description: Lab component for CHEM 2100 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Organic Chemistry I
description: Introduction to the fundamental concepts of organic chemistry focusing on chemical structures, bonding, stereochemistry and chemical reactivity. This course covers the nomenclature and reactivities of simple organic molecules such as alkanes, alkenes, alkynes, aromatics, and alkyl halides - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: CHEM 2100 / CHEM 2105 with a grade of âC-â (1.7) or better.

title: Chemistry Research Methodology I
description: Directed reading on specialized topics or development of a set of special laboratory skills. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Prerequisite: consent of the Program Chair. Contract required. Course may be repeated for credit. CHEM 4999 also available for upper division credit.

title: Independent Study: Chemistry
description: Directed reading on specialized topics or development of a set of special laboratory skills. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. CHEM 4999 also available for upper division credit. Contract required.

title: Organic Chemistry II
description: This course is a continuation of Organic Chemistry I. Topics include structure, naming and chemical reactivity of alcohols, ethers, aldehydes, ketones, carboxylic acids, amines, phenols, aryl halides and heterocyclic compounds. This course also includes spectroscopy and biomolecules such as fats, carbohydrates, lipids, steroids, proteins and nucleic acids - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: CHEM 3800 / CHEM 3805 with a grade of âC-â (1.7) or better.

title: Introduction to Chemistry
description: This course is designed to introduce students to a variety of fundamental concepts in chemistry while developing a set of core mathematical and problem solving skills necessary for success in subsequent chemistry courses. Topics include: measurements, energy and matter, atoms and elements, compounds and their bonds, chemical quantities and reactions, solutions, and gas laws - 3 lecture hours.

title: General Chemistry I
description: This course is focused on the fundamental principles and theories of chemistry with special emphasis on chemical calculations. Topics include atomic structure, gases, kinetic-molecular theory, periodicity and bonding, chemical thermodynamics etc. - 3 lecture hours. Recommended only for students for whom this is a major or minor requirement. CHEM 2000 / CHEM 2005 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: Organic Chemistry II Lab
description: Lab component of CHEM 3900 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Advanced Scientific Communication
description: This is a research based course designed to develop effective techniques used for oral and written presentations of scientific information. The focus is on higher level scientific literacy using detailed article summaries and critical analyses. Primary literature will be used to write scientific research papers and develop informational oral presentations. A variety of topics may be investigated including field biology, organismal biology, molecular cell physiology, biochemistry, biotechnology, chemistry, and environmental sciences - 3 lecture hours. Spring semester.

title: Quantitative Analysis
description: This course focuses on gravimetry, acid-base, redox, complex-formation equilibria, and their applications in volumetric titrations. Techniques including ion exchange and chromatography separation techniques will be discussed along with selected topics in instrumental analysis - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: CHEM 2100 / CHEM 2105 with a grade of âC-â (1.7) or better.

title: Introduction to Scientific Communication
description: This is a research based course designed to introduce techniques used for oral and written presentations of scientific information. The initial focus is on general scientific literacy using article summaries and critical analyses. Primary literature will be used to write short scientific research papers and develop informational oral presentations. A variety of topics may be investigated including field biology, organismal biology, molecular cell physiology, biochemistry, biotechnology, chemistry, and environmental sciences - 3 lecture hours. Fall semester.

title: Quantitative Analysis Lab
description: Lab component of CHEM 4000 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Spectroscopic Analysis Lab
description: Lab component of CHEM 4200 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Biochemistry II
description: Nucleic acid biochemistry and molecular biology, chemistry of nucleotide metabolism, chromosome and chromatin structure, DNA structure and replication, RNA transcription and gene expression, protein synthesis and regulation, signal transduction, and other advanced topics in Biochemistry - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: CHEM 4100 / CHEM 4105 with a grade of âC-â (1.7) or better.

title: Physical Chemistry I
description: This course provides an introduction to thermodynamics basis of equilibrium processes, electrolyte and nonelectrolyte solutions, thermochemistry and membrane potentials - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: CHEM 3900 / CHEM 3905 ; PHYS 2100 / PHYS 2105 ; and MATH 1700 each with a grade of âC-â (1.7) or better.

title: Organic Chemistry I Lab
description: Lab component of CHEM 3800 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Biochemistry II Lab
description: Lab component of CHEM 4500 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Physical Chemistry I Lab
description: Lab component of CHEM 4600 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Physical Chemistry II
description: This course provides an introduction to chemical kinetics, enzyme kinetics and theories of reaction rates, quantum theory, atomic and molecular structure and spectroscopy - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: CHEM 4600 / CHEM 4605 with a grade of 'C-â (1.7) or better.

title: Spectroscopic Analysis
description: This course provides an introduction to theoretical and experimental methods used to study molecular structure and bonding, with an emphasis in spectroscopic methods - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: CHEM 3900 / CHEM 3905 with a grade of âC-â (1.7) or better.

title: Physical Chemistry II Lab
description: Lab component of CHEM 4700 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Computational Chemistry and Biology Lab
description: Laboratory component for CHEM 4900 - 3 lab hours.

title: Teaching Assistant: Chemistry
description: This course provides teaching experience at the undergraduate level. Students will assist faculty members in preparing and delivering laboratory (and/or discussion sections). Students may also conduct review sessions and tutor students under the direct supervision of a faculty mentor. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Prerequisites: a grade of 'B' (3.0) or higher, an overall GPA of 3.0 or higher, and consent of the instructor. Contract required. Course may be repeated for credit.

title: Chemistry Research Methodology II
description: A research based laboratory course designed to introduce students to the methods of research including sampling plan, data collection, data analysis and discussion in terms of practical process of scientific experimentation in the field of chemistry - 6 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. Prerequisite: CHEM 2990 .

title: Biochemistry I
description: An introduction to Biochemistry. Chemistry of carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, nucleic acids, vitamins, and hormones, with major emphasis on biochemical processes in human cells. This class also covers enzyme kinetics, energetics of metabolic reactions and molecular biochemistry - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: CHEM 3800 / CHEM 3805 with a grade of âC-â (1.7) or better.

title: Chemistry Research Methodology IV
description: This is a research based writing laboratory course designed to introduce students to the methods of presentations and writing of scientific topics, audience analysis and adaptation, techniques of support and visualization, organization for clarity and accuracy, and techniques of interpreting and answering questions. Students make and evaluate technical and scientific presentations with an emphasis on seminar reports and professional conference papers. Course may be taken for 1-2 units. 1 unit equals 3 hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. Prerequisite: CHEM 4991 .

title: Chemistry Research Methodology III
description: A research based laboratory course designed to introduce students to the methods of research including sampling plan, data collection, data analysis and discussion in terms of practical process of scientific experimentation in the field of chemistry - 6 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. Prerequisite: CHEM 4990 .

title: Directed Research: Chemistry
description: In this course students will carry out experimental research projects in various fields of chemistry under the guidance of an instructor. This involves literature searching, developing research project, performing research, collection and analyzing of data, oral and poster presentation of research at a peer forum, and a written submission of the work in a thesis format. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. Course may be taken for 1-6 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. Contract required.

title: Internship: Chemistry
description: This course provides opportunities for experiencing the practical and experimental application of chemical principles. Students will be involved via volunteer or paid work experience in chemical research projects conducted by agencies and institutions outside the university; for example, hospitals, private or public chemical industries, and universities. Course may be taken for 1-3 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Prerequisite: consent of the Internship Faculty Advisor. Contract required. Course may be repeated for credit.

title: Independent Study: Chemistry
description: Directed reading on specialized topics or development of a set of special laboratory skills. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. CHEM 2999 also available for lower division credit. Contract required.

title: Biochemistry I Lab
description: Lab component of CHEM 4100 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials

title: Audio Story Workshop
description: This entry-level course introduces students to the fundamental concepts of sound recording. Students learn the basics of acoustics, hone listening skills, and explore contemporary recording technology. They gain hands-on experience with essential recording equipment and produce a short audio piece. **Additional fee applies**

title: Independent Study
description: The privilege of independent study is possible for students who are prepared to undertake it based on student need or interest with approval of the Department Chair, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. The study must be in an area not covered by a regularly scheduled course. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. The maximum number of units that can be earned in one semester by independent study is 4; a total of up to six units of independent study may be applied to the degree.

title: Principles of Communication Studies
description: This course explores the historical and foundational concepts, theories, and principles of communication studies. Students develop the skills necessary to communicate effectively in face-to-face and digital environments. Ethical principles, in both private and civic arenas, are emphasized. Students critically examine the role of mass and digital media in our world and explore how interpersonal, intercultural, small group and organizational communication patterns influence and shape how we perceive and understand the world.

title: Computational Chemistry and Biology
description: This course provides an introduction to computational chemistry and biology. Topics include bioinformatics, biostatistics, molecular dynamics, and quantum mechanics - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: CHEM 2000 / CHEM 2005 , CHEM 2100 / CHEM 2105 , BIO 2800 / BIO 2805 .

title: Environmental Communication
description: This course explores how culture and communication shape and are shaped by our relations with the natural world. We look critically at how cultural and communication processes and contexts produce perceptions of and practices toward nature. Field work is a key course component.

title: Video Story Workshop
description: This entry-level course introduces students to the fundamental concepts of video recording. Students learn strategies for planning and producing stories. They are introduced to the fundamentals of digital camera operation and gain hands-on experience when they create a short video.

title: Radio 1
description: Study of the history and present state of the radio industry and practice of the skills required for professional broadcasting. Students write broadcast news scripts and study the use of the microphone and their voice as an instrument. They develop skills of timing and magazine format segmenting. Students develop and present on-air programs on Dominicanâs Penguin Radio. **Additional fee applies**

title: Radio 2
description: Students build on the skills learned in Radio 1 and 2, developing and presenting professional quality on-air programs for Dominican's Penguin Radio. They assume station management responsibilities, inlcuding events, playlist and sophisticated broadcast projects. Students curate production work to aid in professional development. Prerequisite: CMS 3322 . **Additional fee applies**

title: Digital Journalism 1
description: Advanced study and practice of the fundamentals of journalism. Students enrolled serve as staff members of thedominicanbeat.com, Dominican Universityâs online school newspaper. Students identify, research, investigate, and write news stories focusing on news from campus, the surrounding community and across the globe. We evaluate news sources, learn how to identify points of view, and document sources. Student work demonstrates journalistic style, editing, headline writing, and layout. Students will have the opportunity to meet communications professionals who guest lecture. Corequisite: CMS 3436 .

title: Podcasting
description: Learn to podcast " Weâll cover theory and recording techniques to create broadcast-worthy podcasts. Discover what makes a good radio story subject and where to find sources for story ideas. Find your personal voice and style. Learn to use audio equipment

title: Introduction to Radio
description: Learn the basics of developing your own radio show, using your voice as an instrument, and how to run a radio station. This course uses the platforms Moodle and Zoom and is synchronous. Students need reliable internet access. Recommended for new and returning students.

title: Broadcast Field Study: TV Sports
description: In this course, students learn how to prepare broadcast for TV. Students are in the field, covering live events for broadcast (on and off campus) and assist in the preparation and development of content televised through local broadcast media. Students learn the technologies involved as well as the steps necessary for preparing newsworthy events for televising including video, audio and editing. **Additional fee applies**

title: Storytelling for Sports Television
description: In this course students will learn the essentials of on-air broadcasting as it relates to the media. Subjects covered will include the necessary preparation for live broadcasts including research, voice, pace, and the necessary tempo and fell of various sporting events. Students will work to prepare gameday scripts as well as learning the nuances of calling live sporting events. **Additional fee applies.**

title: Digital Storytelling
description: Digital technology has changed the way we communicate with each other and has reshaped our narrative practices. The goal of this course is to foster effective communication strategies across media platforms. With an emphasis on transmedia storytelling, students integrate theory with practice as they develop content in digital contexts. Prerequisite: CMS 1315 or instructor permission. **Additional fee applies**

title: Communication Theory
description: This course is a critical introduction to the role theory plays in the study of communication and in everyday life. Students are introduced to different ways of thinking about communication and begin to examine how communication shapes cultural practices, interpersonal relationships, personal values and beliefs. Students apply theories to communication events and experiences in a variety of contexts. Through study and practice, students develop an awareness and appreciation of cultural diversity.

title: Broadcast Field Study: Performing Arts
description: Students explore and participate in the process by which performances are recorded and packaged for TV and radio broadcast. Students attend performances, recording audio and video, then edit and complete video and audio content for local broadcast on radio and TV. Fieldwork is a key course component. **Additional fee applies**

title: Organizational Communication
description: This course addresses the theories and principles that help organizations develop effective strategic thinking. Students explore the role of communication in shaping and understanding the complex systems and cultures that constitute organizations. They critically examine the ethical issues and potential problems important in an organizationâs communication practices. Group dynamics, leadership, decision-making and culture are some of the focal points studied in an evolving communication environment.

title: Public Relations & Marketing 1
description: Students study basic public relations processes and develop the skills necessary to orchestrate and execute a persuasive campaign. Close examination of public relations tasks including writing clear and compelling copy for press releases, targeting media outlets for specific audiences, persuading media outlets to grant media exposure, grooming the client, and controlling negative publicity.

title: Interpersonal Communication
description: This course introduces students to the principles, theories, and key research in interpersonal communication. Students critically examine the communication process as it pertains to interpersonal relationships in various contexts (i.e., intercultural, workplace, romantic, family, etc.) and explore how culture and gender shapes how we perceive and relate to others.

title: Sportscasting 1
description: This class explores the skill of audience excitement by building tension through the professional method of broadcasting play-by-play sports. This course focuses on the entertainment factor included in the art of creating sound pictures of sports activities and the subtleties of describing an event as it happens.**Additional fee applies**

title: Intercultural Communication
description: This course explores the historical and political conditions that make intercultural communication possible, the barriers that exist to effective intercultural communication, and possible solutions to the problem of intercultural misunderstanding. We examine examples of differences in communication styles between and within cultures, with particular attention to the interactions of values, traditions and identities within specific cultures. Issues of race, nation, class, gender, religion, immigration, and sexual orientation will be of significant concern.

title: Gender, Race, and Class
description: This course is designed to explore the centrality of communication in constructing gender, race, and class as they intersect with other social forces. Students investigate the conceptual and research literature on the interplay of gender, race, and class and communication in relational and media contexts.

title: Sportscasting 2
description: This class further advances the skill of audience excitement by building tension through the professional method of broadcasting play-by-play sports. This course focuses on the entertainment factor included in the art of creating sound pictures of sports activities and the subtleties of describing an event as it happens. Prerequisite: CMS 2722. **Additional fee applies**

title: Digital Journalism Lab 1
description: Production and publication of thedominicanbeat.com, Dominican Universityâs online school newspaper. Students develop and practice cooperative teamwork and fundamental team journalism, update the website, and design and deploy social media strategy to promote their stories. Students create a portfolio from work published in the Dominican Beat. Corequisite: CMS 3432 . **Additional fee applies.**

title: Advanced Cinema Production
description: This course offers experience in the principles and processes of creative fictional digital filmmaking, providing a framework for understanding cinema as an art form by studying elements such as camera composition and movement, lighting, color, direction of actors, editing, sound effects and music. Students apply their learning by producing short (@ 15 minute) fictional video productions in small teams. **Additional fee applies**

title: Sportscasting 3
description: Building on the skills learned in Sportscasting 1 and 2, students manage complex projects in sports broadcasting. They deliver professional quality work using industry-standard software, gear and methods. In addition, students valuate critically content and delivery of peer and online shows and podcasts. They curate producation work to aid in professional development. Prerequisite: CMS 3723 . **Additional fee applies**

title: Internship: Communication and Media Studies
description: Students apply the principles, knowledge, and skills from their communication major to a work experience position in the communications industry and earn units for their work. The internship is arranged in cooperation with a supervising faculty member and the Director of Career Services.

title: Strategic and Media Writing
description: In this course students acquire the practical knowledge and skills needed to write professionally in media and in strategic communication environments. Students gain hands-on experience writing for print, social, and broadcast media. They learn AP style and critically examine the production and consumption side of media. Students create a variety of strategic content for public relations, advertising, marketing, and business communication, which they archive in their digital portfolio.

title: Research Methods
description: An introduction to communication research methods with an emphasis on constructing a clear argument, this course provides students with an understanding of the relationship between theory, data collection and analysis. This primary understanding serves as the foundation for assessing and criticizing communication research methodologies. Students will acquire the knowledge and skills necessary to perform research appropriately in an academic or organizational context.

title: Teaching Assistant
description: The privilege of serving as a teaching assistant is available to students who are interested with the approval of the Department Chair, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. A contract between the student and the instructor to determine goals, specifics on project contents/materials, and evaluation process is required at time of request. Course may be taken for 1-4 units.

title: Teaching Assistant
description: The privilege of serving as a teaching assistant is available to students who are interested with the approval of the Department Chair, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. A contract between the student and the instructor to determine goals, specifics on project contents/materials, and evaluation process will be required at time of request. Course may be taken for 1-3 units.

title: Consumerism and the Media
description: This class will focus on the various forms of research and paradigms upon which consumer activities and product choices are based. Students will learn the various ways in which consumer preferences are determined and targeted, the role those preferences play in product development and marketing and the role the media plays in the success of products.

title: Independent Study
description: The privilege of independent study is possible for students who are prepared to undertake it based on student need or interest with approval of the Department Chair, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. The study must be in an area not covered by a regularly scheduled course. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. The maximum number of units that can be earned in one semester by independent study is 4; a total of up to six units of independent study may be applied to the degree.

title: Public Relations & Marketing 2
description: Students practice advanced skills including branding and promoting products and services through varied industry channels and media. Students develop promotional campaigns that compel them to think "outside of the box."

title: Fundamentals of Clinical Practice: Individual Psychotherapy
description: An introduction to the practice of individual psychotherapy. Topics covered include: professional identity and role development, accomplishing the tasks of psychotherapy, cultural identities and diversity, factors that enhance the therapeutic alliance, ways to address therapeutic obstacles, clinical documentation, attachment and neuroscience, and evidence- based practice. Psychodynamic, cognitive-behavior therapy (CBT), and humanistic theoretical perspectives are presented along with practice in skills developed in each perspective. Students not enrolled in the Counseling Psychology program must have recommendations from current professors and apply to the Department Chair who in consultation with the instructor may provide approval.

title: Advanced Counseling Techniques: Assessment and Treatment of Children
description: Evaluation and treatment of children and adolescents in diverse cultural and socio-economic groups, including the study of disorders diagnosed during childhood and adolescence. Special attention will be paid to referral questions, case management in collaboration with family members, schools, and community agencies. Training in social skills will address social stress and encourage resilience. Theoretical orientations include recovery oriented, brief and long-term models, psychodynamic, expressive arts, play therapy, sand tray, behavioral, and cognitive behavioral. This course is restricted to students in the Counseling Psychology program.

title: Crisis Intervention and Trauma
description: This course provides an overview of crisis management and treatment of trauma. Students will learn current strategies for working with individuals and families affected by crisis and traumatic events. Interventions will be taught to reduce the consequences of trauma utilizing clientsâ resilience. This course is restricted to students in the Counseling Psychology Graduate Program.

title: Advanced Counseling Techniques: Psychodynamic
description: Explores traditional and recent developments in psychodynamic theory and technique, including Ego Psychology, Object Relations, Self-Psychology, Control Mastery, Intersubjective, and Relational theories. Students develop the ability to think psychodynamically, work with transference and countertransference and to utilize relevant, supportive and long-term techniques with adolescents, adults and seniors by integrating theory, self- understanding and case examples from clinical practice. Prerequisite: CP 5231 Phases of Human Development or consent of instructor. This course is restricted to students In the Counseling Psychology Graduate Program.

title: Advanced Counseling Techniques: Client Advocacy and Collaborative Care
description: Students will learn the basic techniques of brokerage and clinical case management and how to apply these skills when working with adults, youth, and families. Specific case management, engagement, and treatment approaches for addressing the needs of "complicated" consumers with severe, persistent mental illness, trauma histories, and co-occurring mental health and substance abuse disorders will also be addressed. Additionally, students will learn how to engage in collaborative treatment with various systems of care, including, but not limited to: community behavioral health; alcohol and drug service providers; human service agencies; law enforcement/criminal justice; primary care; and academic/vocational services. Throughout the course, essential issues regarding resilience, illness prevention, health promotion, human diversity, and legal/ethical concerns will be highlighted and discussed. This course is restricted to students in the Counseling Psychology program.

title: Domestic Violence: Assessment, Reporting and Treatment
description: The study of spousal or partner abuse assessment, detection, and intervention strategies, including knowledge of community resources, cultural and human diversity factors, and same gender abuse dynamics. Specific interventions and assessment strategies will be presented. This course is restricted to students in the Counseling Psychology program.

title: Capstone
description: This Capstone course acts as the culminating experience of your academic career as a student in Communication and Media Studies at Dominican University. The Signature Project is intended to be a transformative educational experience in student-centered learning, wherein students are expected to apply intensive research, higher-order critical thinking, and effective writing and use of media for an intensive learning activity. The purpose of the thesis is to integrate your knowledge of this field of study by exploring a topic of interest to you in considerable detail.

title: Child Abuse: Assessment, Reporting and Treatment
description: Overview of identification, assessment, and reporting of child abuse and neglect. This course will include discussion of initial response and ongoing treatment for children, adolescents and adult survivors including available community resources. There will be a speaker from Marin County Child Protective Services. Course meets BBS requirement for training in child abuse and neglect for marriage and family therapists. This course is restricted to students in the Counseling Psychology Graduate Program.

title: Professional Ethics and Law
description: Review and analysis of statutory and regulatory licensing law and licensing process relating to the practice of psychotherapy. Differences in legal and ethical standards for different types of work settings will be explored. Topics include: psychotherapist-patient privilege; confidentiality; exceptions to confidentiality; duty to warn; family law and child therapy; assessment; detection and intervention with dangerousness and suicide; HIPAA; dual relationships; advertising; scope of practice; elder and dependent abuse reporting; child abuse reporting; online therapy; insurance reimbursement; termination of therapy; standards of care; therapist disclosure to patients; and record keeping. A variety of ethical issues and ethical dilemmas are examined by using hypothetical situations and recent court cases. This course is restricted to students in the Counseling Psychology program.

title: Alcohol and Substance Abuse
description: An integrative biopsychosocial model for the etiology, assessment and treatment of substance abuse. Topics include: commonly abused substances and trends in drug use; medical aspects of drug use; harm reduction; relapse prevention; legal issues; the relationship of social stress to drug use; co-occurring mental health and substance abuse disorders; and the effect of substance use on the individual, family, and community members. Best practice for evaluation and treatment are studied as applied to diverse cultural and socio-economic groups with an emphasis on at risk populations. In order to further health, healthy functioning and resilience, common resources available for treating alcohol and drug abuse will include speakers from community agencies and consumers of mental health services. Students not enrolled in the Counseling Psychology program must have recommendations from current professors and apply to the Department Chair who in consultation with the instructor may provide approval.

title: Geriatrics and Long-Term Care
description: Overview of psychological, biological, social, and interpersonal issues during older adulthood. Topics include: biopsychosocial assessment of functioning and psychopathology in the elderly; therapeutic issues; treatment modalities and interventions; caregiving and long-term care and reporting elder abuse. Course meets BBS requirement for training in aging and long-term care for marriage and family therapists. This course is restricted to students in the Counseling Psychology program.

title: Research in Human Systems and Treatment Outcomes
description: This course will survey the philosophical, epistemological and methodological components necessary to conduct research and articulate treatment outcomes within the field of Counseling Psychology. Qualitative and quantitative research designs will be covered along with their methods of data analysis and concomitant ethical guidelines. Students will utilize the library databases to examine current research and formulate a research design. The Publication Manual of the American Psychological Association (6th ed.) will be utilized as a guideline for communicating research findings. Students not enrolled in the Counseling Psychology program must have recommendations from current professors and apply to the Department Chair who in consultation with the instructor may provide approval.

title: Human Diversity
description: Exploration of issues of human diversity and mental health will be included in this course. Topics include: human diversity as it relates to socio-economic, social stress and society, culture, ethnicity, age and development, gender and sexuality, spirituality, DSM diagnoses, and other cultural mores, competencies, sensitivities, values and linguistic differences. There will be a special focus on the implications of human diversity issues on therapeutic and interpersonal relationships. Utilization of appropriate community resources will be addressed. (The following questions will be addressed: What stereotypes, perceptions, and beliefs are held about culturally different groups? What influence does this have on how you relate to others? What are the worldviews you may bring to the clinical encounter and to your role as a professional counselor? Without such an awareness and understanding, psychotherapists may inadvertently assume that everyone shares their worldview. When this happens, clinicians may become guilty of cultural oppression, imposing values on their culturally different clients.) This course is restricted to students In the Counseling Psychology Graduate Program.

title: Psychological Assessment
description: This course introduces students to a conceptual and practical understanding of psychological assessment via the clinical interview and use of psychometric measurements and instruments. Assessment via the clinical interview includes assessment of mental status, risks/crises, anxiety management, cognitive impairment, affect, defensive structure, DSM-5 diagnosis and prognosis and report writing. This course provides pragmatic clinical skills to conduct a clinical interview, assessment and diagnosis. An overview of projective and objective psychometric instruments, assessment techniques and styles will be provided. Students will be introduced to over 75 psychological assessment tools and measurements. Additionally, students learn how to write initial psychological reports. Exploration of issues of human diversity and mental health will be included in this course. This course is restricted to students In the Counseling Psychology Graduate Program.

title: Advanced Counseling Techniques: Brief and CBT Therapies
description: Presentation and practice of Brief and CBT theories and interventions applied to consumers and their families, diverse cultural and socioeconomic groups, and HMOâs to promote resilience, health, and healthy functioning. Contemporary evidence-based models in CBT, Solution-Focused, and Narrative Therapies address behavioral issues, co-occurring mental health and substance abuse disorders, personality disorders and other DSM 5 categories as appropriate. The course will include instruction on how to write the Individual Case Study and Treatment Plan that the CP Program and other professional settings use. This course is restricted to students in the Counseling Psychology Graduate Program.

title: Psychopathology and Treatment Planning
description: An introduction to the use of diagnosis in assessment and treatment. Topics include: concepts of health and psychopathology, history of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM), appropriate use of diagnosis, cultural and cross-cultural factors involved in diagnosis, and developing a treatment plan based on diagnosis. The DSM-5 is the primary text, and all diagnostic categories are covered in the course. First-person memoir, film depictions, and case vignettes supplement the primary text. This course is restricted to students In the Counseling Psychology Graduate Program.

title: Psychopharmacology
description: This course is intended to provide students with an understanding of the foundational knowledge of psychopharmacology, including the basics of neurobiology and how psychotropic medications work, the various classes of psychotropic drugs and their application among symptom patterns, how to talk to clients about medications, and the role of the clinician in building a collaborative team including the client and prescribing professionals. The course also focuses on social and cultural factors that impact the experience of psychotropic medication. This course is restricted to students in the Counseling Psychology Graduate Program.

title: Fundamentals of Clinical Practice: Group Psychotherapy
description: Theoretical and practical approaches to working with groups are discussed and practiced. Topics include: setting goals and objectives, group observation and assessment skills, leadership styles, process techniques, developmental stages, cultural and socio-economic diversity, social stress, and termination. Recovery oriented applications are made to psychotherapy groups, support groups, organizational development, schools, managed care, in-patient hospitalization, community mental health, and social services. Group structure is assessed for self-help groups, task groups, and time limited groups. Acquiring present-centered skills promotes a balance of structure and process that facilitates insight, compassion, integrity, and flexibility. The course content is relevant for both the beginner and experienced student and group leader. This course is restricted to students in the Counseling Psychology Graduate Program.

title: Phases of Human Development
description: Overview of psychological, biological, social, and cognitive concepts of development as applied to the entire lifespan from pre- and perinatal to death and dying. Research findings related to the understanding of human behavior within the context of cultural and socio-economic status will be discussed and interpreted through the perspectives of a number of developmental theorists. These findings will include health issues and counseling strategies involving geriatrics and long term care with diverse populations. Students not enrolled in the Counseling Psychology program must have recommendations from current professors and apply to the Department Chair who in consultation with the instructor may provide approval.

title: Practicum Seminar
description: Three Seminars are required for the MFT/PCC concentration (a total of 9 units). Practicum Seminars focus on the application, development, and refinement of assessment, counseling, consultation, case management, and client centered advocacy skills in approved practicum sites. Seminars include the applied use of evidence-based treatments in working with children, adolescents, adults, couples, and families from culturally and socio-economically diverse populations. Documentation skills and resource utilization will be emphasized. The mode of instruction is designed to foster integrity, sensitivity, flexibility, insight, compassion, and professional presence. Students will develop written case studies that include assessment with a mini-mental status exam, family history, previous treatment history, medical history, case conceptualization, differential diagnosis, treatment planning, summary documenting the course of treatment, legal and ethical issues, human diversity issues, outcome report, prognosis, and community referrals. Supervision is provided by the on-site supervisor, and the Practicum Seminar provides group consultation and liaison between the University and the on-site supervisor. Students are required to locate their own practicum placements by consultation with the Practicum Director, use of the Practicum Database maintained by the Department of Counseling Psychology, attendance at the Practicum Information Meetings and at the Fieldwork Fair held each year. Prerequisites: 24 units of Counseling Psychology courses including CP 5217 , CP 5201 , CP 5208 , CP 5237 , CP 5221 . This course is restricted to students In the Counseling Psychology Graduate Program.

title: Clinical Aspects of Human Sexuality
description: Examines diverse views on contemporary issues regarding human sexuality and gender identity. Various physiological, psychological, and socio-cultural perspectives will be highlighted. Study of assessment and treatment of sexual dysfunction includes a review of contemporary best practice and evidence-based counseling approaches. This course is restricted to students in the Counseling Psychology program.

title: Advanced Counseling Techniques: Family Therapy
description: Systems concepts and terminology will be presented. Various theories and their applications (especially Bowenian) with diverse socio-economic populations and cultures will be discussed. Topics will include: families as systems, the family life cycle, how to understand family dynamics clinically, challenges faced when working with families, genograms, traditional and non-traditional approaches to working with families (both evidenced-based and beyond), family assessments, family consultations, blended families, parenting and step-parenting issues, LGBTQ families, socially and economically disadvantaged families, divorcing families, resilience in families. Students will explore theory-based treatment planning formats used in response to family vignettes. Students will view and assess family dynamics in operation from popular media as well as professional videos with expert family practitioners. Students will practice recognized family therapy techniques via role plays. Students will generate their own personal genograms (traditional and postmodern), will present on diverse family issues (as reflected in the California/San Francisco Bay area population) and will create relationally-oriented diagnostic criteria for both challenging and strengthening families. This course is restricted to MFT/PCC students in the Counseling Psychology Graduate Program.

title: The Criminal Justice System
description: This course provides a detailed examination of the workings of the criminal justice system, including the roles played by police, judges and other court officials, corrections and parole officers, lawyers, therapists and other advocates.

title: GMPP Seminar
description: The General Masterâs Proficiency Project (GMPP) provides an opportunity for those students who wish to obtain a General Masterâs in Counseling Psychology to integrate, apply and demonstrate their knowledge through the development of a Masterâs Project pertaining to a special field of interest within Counseling Psychology. The project may comprise research, experience, design, service learning or other contributions to the field of Counseling Psychology, which can be documented or presented. Project proposals are approved and monitored by the GMPP Advisor and are presented in progress within the required two semesters of GMPP Seminars. A final presentation of the project is made to an appropriate academic or community group during the semester of planned graduation. Prerequisites: 24 units of Counseling Psychology Courses including, CP 5217 , CP 5201 , CP 5208 , CP 5237 , and CP 5221 . This course is restricted to students in the Counseling Psychology Graduate Program.

title: Introduction to Criminology
description: This course introduces students to the sociological and psychological study of crime and criminal behavior. The historical roots of criminology as well as current controversies will be studied. An overview of criminal law and the criminal justice system are discussed, as well as issues of punishment, rehabilitation, prevention and social reform.

title: Juvenile Delinquency and Juvenile Justice
description: An examination of the various theories of delinquent behavior and an introduction to the field of juvenile justice practice. This course explores the social, historical and legal context within which delinquency and juvenile justice occur.

title: Career Counseling
description: Utilizing current theories and models for career development, assessment and guidance for career transitions across the lifespan is applied to diverse populations and settings. Written and digital information resources will target career issues such as job seeking skills, interviewing practices, job maintenance, strategic planning, retirement, severance choices, secondary education, and the work/life balance. Educational degrees, professional licensing, and certification issues are covered along with decision-making and goal setting strategies. Individuals will gain clarity about their own career choices as well as learn counseling skills to help clients determine a meaningful career path. This course is only open to students in the Counseling Psychology Program.

title: Theoretical Images of Crime, Deviance and Social Control
description: This course provides a detailed overview of ten major theoretical traditions for understanding crime and deviance, as well as the policy implications and social control methods recommended by each theoretical perspective. Some attention will be given to empirical studies to see how well the theories meet the test of material reality.

title: Forensic Psychology
description: This course explores the various facets of the field of forensic psychology, including models, practices and procedures. Psychological constructs in forensic psychology will be reviewed, including developmental, behavioral, cognitive, social, and biological models of understanding human behavior. The course will cover landmark legal cases relevant to the practice of forensic psychology, potential careers in psychology including expert testimony, legal consultation, and forensic assessment in the criminal justice system. The course also reviews ethical principles and dilemmas in the practice of forensic psychology, correctional psychology and working in the juvenile justice system. Relevant case law will be covered to provide a historical perspective of the increasing important role of forensic psychology in our country.

title: Senior Capstone
description: The senior capstone course provides students an opportunity to develop a culminating research project during their senior year. Students develop a research project that demonstrates the ability to synthesize, analyze, and evaluate major questions within the discipline. Students will also explore career options and postgraduate goals.

title: Advanced Counseling Techniques: Couple Therapy
description: Multiple theories (traditional and non-traditional) and their application to partnered and married clients from a variety of diverse cultural and socio-economic populations will be presented. Topics will include: couple diversity (including LGBTQ), current research on couples, communication patterns and myths, the effects of stress/trauma on couples, stages of development in securing a couple identity, building successful partnerships, the de-escalation of conflict cycles (including drug and alcohol-induced), differentiation processes, attachment theory and its integration with systems theory, acceptance and change strategies during clinical interventions, and the neurobiology of couple relationships. EFCT, the Gottman Method, and the Developmental Approach to working with couples will be emphasized. Students will formulate theory-based treatment plans in response to specific couple vignettes and will practice couple therapy techniques during role plays. Students will critique clinical cases as presented by a variety of expert couple therapists, will determine a viable approach to use when working with a couple in popular media, and will develop a modified Couple Therapy Handbook as a resource for future practice. This course is restricted to MFT/PCC students in the Counseling Psychology Graduate Program.

title: Internship: Criminology
description: This course provides an opportunity for students to integrate theory and practice through an on-campus seminar and a supervised off-campus internship at an organization related to the field of criminology. In the seminar, students reflect upon and share their internship experiences and link those experiences to research and theories within the major. Students are responsible for finding their own internship and must have the internship approved by the instructor before the start of the course.

title: Residency 3
description: Workshops, lectures, panel discussions, and readings broaden and deepen each studentâs knowledge of diverse artistic sensibilities. Scheduled activities address the craft of writing as well as larger issues related to a writerâs vocation. Students form a literary community who will sharpen their skills with peer review, encouragement, and criticism. Invited guests educate students about publishing and editing. Students work with their mentors to prepare the curriculum and goals for the upcoming semester.

title: Mentorship 1: Poetry
description: Mentorship 1 places American poetry of the 21st century in its historical context in order to inform the studentsâ stylistic and aesthetic choices for his or her poetry. Students are expected to become familiar with the American poetry movements since 1914. Students will read selected poems along with essays that discuss the craft of poetry. Regular writing exercises that arise from the readings are aimed towards generating new poems for the end-of-semester portfolio. Every four weeks students will submit original creative writing to the mentor for feedback. Students will also engage in monthly online discussion/ workshops with their peers.

title: Mentorship 2: Poetry
description: Mentorship 2 continues the study of poetry from a more historical perspective. Students are expected to become familiar with the English roots of the poetic tradition. Students will read selected poems along with essays that discuss the craft of poetry. Regular writing exercises that arise from the readings are aimed towards generating new poems for the end-of-semester portfolio. Every four weeks students will submit original creative writing to the mentor for feedback. Students will also engage in monthly online discussion/ workshops with their peers.

description: Directed reading and/or independent research on a topic of special interest to student and faculty member. Course may be taken for 1-3 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit, not to exceed a total of 6 units.

Regular writing exercises that arise from course materials are aimed towards generating new works for the end-of-semester portfolio. Every four weeks students will submit original creative writing to the mentor for feedback. Students will also engage in monthly online discussion / workshops with their peers.

title: Publish Your Writing
description: A hands-on look at the business of publishing creative writing, from market research, query letters, and copyright laws to the issues of multiple submissions and print vs. online publications. Through practical activities, students learn strategies for submitting work successfully. Students also learn digital literacy skills and develop an online presence for their work.

Every four weeks students will submit original creative writing to the mentor for feedback. Students will also engage in monthly online discussion / workshops with their peers.

title: Coding and Building Websites
description: In this course students build static and dynamic web pages. They use industry-standard web development tools, become familiar with the fundamentals of how web browsers and the internet work, and add their own custom website to their digital portfolio. This course uses an innovative project-based approach to implement technology with attention to ethics, equity and social justice.

title: Mentorship 2: Creative Nonfiction: Narrative Medicine
description: Mentorship 2 continues the study of Narrative/Poetic Medicine. Students will decide upon their chosen genre and read selected works along with essays that discuss literary craft. Regular writing exercises that arise from the readings are aimed towards generating new works for the end-of-semester portfolio. Every four weeks students will submit original creative writing to the mentor for feedback. Students will also engage in monthly online discussion/ workshops with their peers.

title: Teaching Practicum
description: This course gives students an opportunity to develop teaching and leadership skills. Students are guided through the process of devising and leading a creative writing workshop. Activities include creating a lesson plan thoughtful to group dynamics, writing a short craft talk, devising appropriate support materials (handouts and writing prompts).

title: Publishing Practicum
description: This course gives students an opportunity to develop editorial and leadership skills specific to literary fields. Students learn through producing Dominican’s literary and arts journal, the Tuxedo. Activities include soliciting submissions, engaging in a group editing process, communicating with authors, designing and publishing the online journal.

title: Mentorship: Performance Writing
description: Focuses on developing the student's unique aesthetic in craft and voice. Readings are selected to reflect and enhance the student's work. Regular writing exercises that arise from the readings are aimed towards generating new works for the end-of-semester portfolio. Every four weeks students will submit original creative writing to the mentor for feedback. Students will also engage in monthly online discussion/workshops with their peers.

title: The Fundamentals of Computer Science and Algorithms
description: Description: This course covers the fundamentals of programming, software development, core data structures and algorithms. Students will master top-level concepts in software development and the foundation for coding in any language. Key topics include data types, control flow, conditionals, loops, lists, pseudocode, using functions to process input into output, technical project planning, and diagramming. (Mac required)

title: Coding and Building Websites II
description: This course focuses on the tools and patterns used to build servers and use databases. Students learn resourceful architecture for organizing server code and data. Students will learn how to authenticate and authorize web application users, and manage the dynamic rendering of HTML pages. Students will complete various tutorial projects including a clone of a social news aggregation and online forum, as well as their own custom dynamic website. (Mac Required)

title: Intro to Data Science: Visualization, Analysis, and Modeling
description: In this course, students learn the foundational skills of data science, including data collection, scrubbing, analysis, and visualization with modern tools and libraries. Students gain a grounding in statistical concepts and techniques to practice the science and art of data representation to tell stories with data-driven insights. Students are exposed to machine learning and its applications to data science. (Mac Required)

title: Mentorship 4: Performance Writing
description: Mentorship 4 focuses on revising and assembling student writing into a cohesive manuscript that may be a series of short narratives intended for live performance, a screenplay, a playscript, or a hybrid genre. Every four weeks students will submit the manuscript to the mentor for feedback. Students will also engage in monthly online discussion / workshops with their peers.

GYROTONICÂ®/GYROKINESISÂ® courses are held twice weekly for one and one half hours. Eight semesters required.

description: In this course, participants will build their own mobile apps in the Apple ecosystem. Students learn to build applications using Appleâs software development platform Xcode to build iPhone apps with mobile best practices. Key concepts include implementing sleek mobile user interfaces, navigation, and transitions as well as communicating with remote servers and external services. (Mac Required)

title: Dance and Spiritual Expression
description: This course demonstrates the power of dance to open human beings to knowledge and experience that they would not otherwise have. In case studies drawn from around the world, we see dancing bodies as instruments of devotion, and as vehicles for traveling through different realms of reality. We see moving bodies as catalysts for spiritual change, and as vessels for religious meaning. We see women and men finding in dance a medium for expressing and becoming their highest ideals. In a thimble, we will come to see dance as nothing less than a vital art â as the currency (literally, "condition of flowing") of life â precisely because of the role dancing has played in the evolution of human nature and culture.

title: Dance History I (World)
description: This two-part historical survey course traces the theory and development of dance from its anthropological origins to the contemporary performance practices of the 21st century. Dance History I covers dance as ritual, dance as spiritual practice, dance of the African Diaspora in both concert and vernacular forms, and the origins of ballet. Special emphasis is given to experiential learning in non-Western dance techniques.

description: This two-part historical survey course traces the theory and development of dance from its anthropological origins to the contemporary performance practices of the 21st century. Dance History II covers a detailed chronology of ballet and modern dance in the United States, beginning in the late 19th century. Major socio-political shifts are contextualized in analysis that centralizes the power of the dancing body to challenge, negotiate, and/or affirm dominant cultural values.

title: Performance
description: Senior dance majors shall commission a solo from a choreographer, to be performed during senior year.

title: Senior Project I
description: The culmination of the creative work of the program. Building on the knowledge and skills developed throughout the program, students, with the support of faculty, will develop and complete a work of choreography to be presented to the community. Emphasis will be placed on the creation of a highly personal, layered work, with all theatrical elements such as location, score, lighting, and costuming considered. Included in the Senior Project is a written explanation of the origins, impulses, and choices made, as well as an oral presentation that may include a question/answer session with viewers.

title: Independent Study
