description: The privilege of independent study is possible for students who are prepared to undertake it based on student need or interest with approval of the Chair of the department, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. The study must be in an area not covered by a regularly scheduled course. Course may be taken for 1-3 units. The maximum number of units that can be earned in one semester by independent study is 3; a total of up to six units of independent study may be applied to the degree.

title: Theater of the Oppressed
description: Theater of the Oppressed (TO) is a form of popular education that uses theater as a vehicle for fundamental social transformation. Forged in the crucible of revolutionary movements, it uses the dynamized human body and the charged theatrical space as laboratories for exploring power, transforming oppression, and finding collective solutions to the fundamental problems of conflict, exploitation, violence, and human suffering.

title: Theater Workshop I
description: In this practice-based workshop, students will develop performance techniques and hone their skills in communication, creativity, and teamwork. Through improvisational games, we will explore vocal and physical expression, spontaneity and presence, clarity and commitment. The course emphasizes participation over performance expectations. No previous theater experience required. All students are welcome in this course.

title: Theater Workshop II
description: In this practice-based workshop, students will develop performance techniques and hone their skills in communication, creativity, and teamwork. Through improvisational games, we will explore vocal and physical expression, spontaneity and presence, clarity and commitment. The course emphasizes participation over performance expectations. No previous theater experience required. All students are welcome in this course.

title: Theater Workshop III
description: In this practice-based workshop, students will develop performance techniques and hone their skills in communication, creativity, and teamwork. Through improvisational games, we will explore vocal and physical expression, spontaneity and presence, clarity and commitment. The course emphasizes participation over performance expectations. No previous theater experience required. All students are welcome in this course.

title: Page to Stage
description: Exploration of language and the process of bringing dramatic literature to life on stage through scenes and monologues. Proceeding from essential Stanislavsky-based acting techniques, students learn how to play the intention of a scene through actions and objectives while incorporating the rich subtext of a character.

title: Workshop
description: Each semester of Workshop will contain the creation of an original or restaged dance work for students by a faculty or guest choreographer, to be performed for the community. At least one semester each year will include study of the choreography of Alonzo King, either in the form of movement studies or the recreation of LINES Ballet repertory. Woven between these two main components is a progressive sequence of workshops exploring creativity, expression and critical analysis. Workshop includes extensive studies in improvisation and composition. Workshop may also include, pointe & variations, contemporary and classical partnering, and critical theory studies. Eight semesters required.

title: Stand & Deliver
description: Based on techniques derived from both Theatre and Sports, this course focuses on communication and confidence building. Students explore vocal technique and movement and learn the value of rehearsal and how to active greater levels of performance under pressure. Students raise their credibility through learning and practicing techniques for effective communication. Stand and deliver includes: role-play, theater games, improvisations, warm-ups, and storytelling.

title: Introduction to Acting
description: Based in the stagecraft of theater, this course develops the actorâs skill through monologue and character development. Studying and rehearsing speeches from early modern, modern, and contemporary drama, students will practice objective setting, improvisation, movement, and vocal technique. This course uses the platforms Moodle and GSuite.Students need reliable internet access. Students must have access to a recording device (i.e. mobile phone with built-in camera). Recommended for new and returning students.

title: Microeconmics & Personal Finance
description: This course constructs the beginning of a strong foundation of economics and personal finance in order to empower students to function effectively as consumers, workers, investors, entrepreneurs, and active citizens. It begins with a basic introduction to supply and demand and microeconomic concepts. It then builds on economic concepts that will help students interpret the daily news, understand the interdependencies of the worldâs economies, and understand how markets operate within the United States. On a personal level, students develop critical thinking skills about their own economic choices and consumer decisions directly affect their likelihood of future economic success.

title: Senior Project II
description: The culmination of the creative work of the program. Building on the knowledge and skills developed throughout the program, students, with the support of faculty, will develop and complete a work of choreography to be presented to the community. Emphasis will be placed on the creation of a highly personal, layered work, with all theatrical elements such as location, score, lighting, and costuming considered. Included in the Senior Project is a written explanation of the origins, impulses, and choices made, as well as an oral presentation that may include a question/answer session with viewers.

title: Navigating College: Part I
description: In this 0.5-unit course, you will receive a warm welcome to Dominincan as you begin to build community with other new students. Together you will learn about the following topics from a variety of Integrative Coaches and dynamic DU faculty: Digital Portfolio, The Art and Discipline of Writing and Reflecting, Global Citizenship, Navigating Dominican Systems. You will reflect on your transition from high school to college and you will be prepared to navigate DU systems and learning platforms. Synchronous meetings will be held in Zoom and facilitators will provide introductions to using Moodle. This course uses the platforms Moodle and Zoom and is a blend of synchronous and asynchronous learning. Students need reliable internet access. For new first-year students only.

title: Institutional Macroeconomics
description: This course focuses on understanding the ways to measure an economy by examining topics such as economic growth, productivity, labor markets, monetary and fiscal policies, and international trade. It introduces basic models of macroeconomics and illustrates principles with the experience of the United States and other economies.

title: Academic Mentoring Program
description: The enrolled student receives one-on-one weekly academic mentoring from a graduate student at Dominican. The student may request help with any personal or academic skill that will support success in college.

title: Navigating Colleg II
description: Navigating Colleg II is the 0.5-unit course that includes the content from the second half of the 1-unit Navigating College course. Develops the skills necessary for academic success in college. The student explores the purposes of higher education and his/her potential role as a participant in college. Emphasis is placed on self-assessment, self-management, goal-directed behavior, and effective study habits such as time management, note taking, the comprehension of academic materials, test taking strategies, and relevant information concerning college resources. Individual and group learning experiences identify and work with the student's dominant learning style to increase the ability to engage in critical thinking, the ability to explore and resolve issues relative to stress and anxiety in academic settings and activities, and personal wellness. The course will require the creation of a personal Success Plan. (For first-semester freshmen and transfers only).

title: Introduction to Acting
description: Based in the stagecraft of theater, this course develops the actorâs skill through monologue and character development. Studying and rehearsing speeches from early modern, modern, and contemporary drama, students will practice objective setting, improvisation, movement, and vocal technique. This course uses the platforms Moodle and GSuite.Students need reliable internet access. Students must have access to a recording device (i.e. mobile phone with built-in camera). Recommended for new and returning students.

title: Developing an E-Portfolio
description: Students learn the principles and practices of individual electronic portfolio construction, including how to create e-portfolios online, gathering academic artifacts and examples of personal growth, and submission of reflective commentary on their learning. All artifacts must demonstrate studentsâ understanding of course content equivalent to a summative assessment.

title: Internship
description: 5-15 hours a week in a work situation which constitutes a valid educational experience relevant to an academic discipline. Candidates work under the direction of an on-site supervisor. Units are counted toward graduation and towards some majors; a maximum of six (6) internship units can be counted toward a degree. Grading is Pass/Fail. Candidates must be recommended by their department advisors. Fall, Spring Semesters.

title: Career Jump Start
description: This course focuses on principles, methods, and practice of career management during and after college. There will be an emphasis on the exploration of job opportunities, identification of strengths and weaknesses, resume development, cover letter composition, interviewing, networking, and the use of technology and social media to develop a personal brand. Students will complete a preliminary career plan for their digital portfolio. Recommended for Sophomores & Juniors.

title: Introduction to Teaching
description: An opportunity for students interested in becoming elementary school teachers to explore the nature and purpose of schooling. Students examine their motivation for teaching, the diversity inherent in the student population, and the characteristics of effective teaching. Students participate in five hours of observation in local schools, observe teachers in action, and learn to utilize educational technology while building their own professional websites. Students create a lesson plan using the California History/Social Science Framework. Fall semesters.

title: Navigating College
description: This is the full 1-unit Navigating College course that includes the content from the 0.5-unit courses Navigating College: Part I and Navigating College: Part 2. Develops the skills necessary for academic success in college. The student explores the purposes of higher education and his/her potential role as a participant in college. Emphasis is placed on self-assessment, self-management, goal-directed behavior, and effective study habits such as time management, note taking, the comprehension of academic materials, test taking strategies, and relevant information concerning college resources. Individual and group learning experiences identify and work with the student's dominant learning style to increase the ability to engage in critical thinking, the ability to explore and resolve issues relative to stress and anxiety in academic settings and activities, and personal wellness. (For first-semester freshmen and transfers only).

title: Complexities of Teaching in Schools 1
description: Students preparing to be teachers investigate the complexities of teaching and learning in the 21st century. This course also prepares students to begin: integrating multiple subjects, embedding educational technologies, and proactively supporting students with special needs when planning lessons. Students continue constructing the "Becoming a Teacher" website and focus on specific TPEs while actively engaged in 20 hours of fieldwork in classrooms. Fall semesters.

title: Complexities of Teaching in Schools 2
description: Students preparing to be teachers examine: classroom management strategies and current issues related to social justice, equity, and diversity. Students continue constructing the "Becoming a Teacher" website and focus on specific TPEs while actively engaged in 20 hours of fieldwork in classrooms. This course also prepares students to teach History/SS in the school using Californiaâs academic frameworks and standards and to integrate multiple subjects, including educational technology. Spring semesters. Prerequisite: EDU 3000 : Complexities of Teaching in Schools 1 or instructor approval.

title: Mentor Training
description: This course examines the fundamentals of effective mentoring by exploring student development theory and considering how students learn and adapt to college life. Topics include learning styles, study and life skills, trust and confidentiality, ethics, and interpersonal and cross-cultural communication techniques. Students who complete this course may apply to be a peer mentor for the following academic year. Open to all students.

title: Teaching Science
description: This course prepares students to teach science in elementary school. Students are prepared to deliver an equitable instructional program through active construction of pedagogical knowledge and skills. Students learn developmentally appropriate strategies to teach students the Californiaâs Science Framework and Content Standards, including English learners and those with learning differences. Students integrate multiple subjects to create instructional units, planning for inclusive learning environments. Students continue constructing the "Becoming a Teacher" website and focus on specific TPEs while actively engaged in 20 hours of fieldwork in elementary classrooms. Fall semesters.

title: Observation
description: Teacher candidates observe and participate in partner elementary and secondary schools with a focus on the essential knowledge and skills required for effective teaching and learning as defined by the California Teaching Performance Expectations (TPEs). The Field Placement Coordinator establishes the school site in cooperation with a supervising faculty member. **Additional Fee Applies**

title: Early Literacy & Literature
description: This course provides students with the theoretical foundations for the development of literacy (reading, writing, listening, & speaking) in young children. Students learn the social, cultural, and linguistic factors that affect literacy development. Students study childrenâs literature including important authors and illustrators and uses of childrenâs literature for teachers and others working with young children. This course includes language structures and linguistics, including the basics of early reading (e.g., phonemes, phonemic awareness, phonics, and sound-symbol relationships). A fieldwork practicum tutoring reading in a culturally and linguistically diverse early childhood classroom is required. Spring semesters.

title: Teaching for Equity
description: Teaching for Equity is a course that examines principles of educational equity and diversity and their implementation in curriculum content and school practices. Candidates examine their own beliefs, attitudes, biases, and expectations about educational equity, so that they may be better prepared to create equitable classrooms. Candidates also explore the history of schooling in the United States and ways to include cultural traditions in instructional programs. Candidates will learn how to maximize the academic achievement for all students. Candidates participate in fieldwork throughout the semester.

title: Differentiating Instruction for Inclusive Classrooms
description: The purpose of this course is to develop skills and competencies in addressing the needs of diverse learners within K-12 classrooms, specifically English Language Lerners and students with disabilities. Candidates will learn how to assess students and implement effective instructional strategies to provide inclusive, collaborative educational experiences to all students. Additional topics include language and literacy development, federal and state mandates for serving diverse students, and the psychological, social, political and cultural factors affecting learning. Candidates will participate in focused fieldwork in general and special education classrooms and during Designated English Language Development classes.

title: Elementary Preparation for Student Teaching
description: This course integrates previous and on-going coursework and prepares candidates for supervised teaching within an elementary school. Candidates observe and participate in a variety of elementary grades, including in classrooms with English learners and students with special needs. Observations focus on engaging and supporting all students and creating and maintaining effective environments for learning.

title: Classroom Climate & Curriculum
description: This course introduces Californiaâs curriculum frameworks and academic content standards for science, history-social science, visual and performing arts, physical education, and health. Candidates utilize a backwards design approach to create integrated, thematic lessons and units for the elementary grades. Candidates learn to engage and support all students in learning through the development and maintenance of a caring, responsive classroom environment. Concurrently, candidates participate in fieldwork experiences to apply their developing understandings of teaching and learning.

title: Teaching Reading in Elementary School
description: Provides substantive, research-based instruction that prepares students to teach Reading/Language Arts in elementary school. Emphasis on organizing systematic instruction in reading, writing, listening and speaking to meet the needs of the full range of learners, including struggling readers, students with special needs, English learners, and advanced learners based on California's Standards for English Language Arts and Literacy. Includes fieldwork where appropriate Reading instruction is modeled.

title: Explorations in Teaching
description: An opportunity for students interested in becoming elementary school teachers to further explore the nature and purpose of schooling. Students participate in fifteen hours of observation in local schools, observe exemplary teachers in action, and learn to utilize educational technology while building their own professional websites. Students create a lesson plan and present it to their peers based on the California Next Generation Science Standards. Students also learn basic presentation skills through introduction to acting activities. Spring semesters.

title: Applied Instructional Technology
description: This course offers credential candidates an opportunity to apply current instructional technologies within their content area(s). Incorporating ISTE standards, emphasis is on making significant changes in teaching and learning. Focus is on utilizing a range of instructional technologies to create and maintain effective environments for student learning, including student engagement, assessment, assistive technology, and reflection.

title: Teaching Mathematics in Elementary School
description: Prepares students to teach mathematics in elementary school. Students are prepared to deliver a balanced instructional program through active construction of pedagogical knowledge and skills. Students learn developmentally appropriate strategies to teach students Californiaâs Standards for Mathematics. Includes fieldwork where appropriate mathematics instruction is modeled.

title: Elementary Supervised Teaching Seminar
description: In seminar fashion, candidates discuss their teaching performance related to curriculum, instruction, assessment, and working with colleagues. Candidates draw upon and apply their learning from ongoing and previous coursework as they learn to effectively manage issues and challenges related to teaching and learning.

title: Literacy Across the Curriculum
description: All secondary teachers share the responsibility of helping students develop the literacy skills that they will need to further their education. Participants taking this course will learn how to support literacy in their specific content areas, with emphasis on academic language abilities, critical thinking, and twenty-first century skills. Concurrently, candidates participate in fieldwork experiences to apply their developing understandings of teaching and learning.

title: Student Teaching
description: Teacher candidates in partner elementary and secondary schools apply their knowledge from ongoing and previous coursework as they learn to effectively manage issues and challenges related to teaching and learning. The Field Placement Coordinator establishes the school site in cooperation with a supervising faculty member. **Additional Fee Applies**

title: Program Design
description: Focuses on Individual Education Plan (IEP) development and curriculum planning and instruction for students with mild/moderate learning challenges. Candidates will write a complete IEP including transition plans. Candidates will learn how to make adaptations to general education curriculum in order to ensure student success. Knowledge of ethical standards related to laws and regulations that provide equity for students with learning challenges is emphasized.

title: Internship
description: 5-15 hours a week in a work situation which constitutes a valid educational experience relevant to an academic discipline. Students work under the direction of an on-site supervisor. Units are counted toward graduation and towards some majors; a maximum of 4 (4) internship units can be counted toward a degree. Grading is Pass/No Pass. Students must be recommended by their department advisors. Fall, Spring Semesters.

title: Special Education Supervised Teaching Seminar
description: Candidates participate in and reflect on a variety of activities representing different roles of special educators, including interactions with parents, and discuss other responsibilities of full-time teachers and service providers. Candidates teach in general and special education classrooms during regular school sessions daily. Students begin to plan for their professional clear credential.

title: Explorations in Equity
description: Explorations in Equity examines principles of equity and diversity and their implementations in schools and institutional settings. Students examine their own beliefs, attitudes, biases, and expectations about equity, so that they are better prepared for their future careers. With the goal of developing comfort with difference through heightened self-awareness, students participate in community engagement projects, integrating course content through cultural immersion.

title: Internship Teaching
description: Special Education teacher candidates in partner elementary and secondary schools apply their knowledge from ongoing and previous coursework as they learn to effectively manage issues and challenges related to teaching and learning. The Field Placement Coordinator establishes the school site in cooperation with a supervising faculty member.

title: Student Teaching
description: Teacher candidates in partner elementary and secondary schools apply their knowledge from ongoing and previous coursework as they learn to effectively manage issues and challenges related to teaching and learning. The Field Placement Coordinator establishes the school site in cooperation with a supervising faculty member. **Additional Fee Applies**

title: Early Literacy & Literature
description: This course provides students with the theoretical foundations for the development of literacy (reading, writing, listening, & speaking) in young children. Students learn the social, cultural, and linguistic factors that affect literacy development. Students study childrenâs literature including important authors and illustrators and uses of childrenâs literature for teachers and others working with young children. This course includes language structures and linguistics, including the basics of early reading (e.g., phonemes, phonemic awareness, phonics, and sound-symbol relationships). A fieldwork practicum tutoring reading in a culturally and linguistically diverse early childhood classroom is required. Spring semesters.

title: Internship Completion
description: This course is intended for intern students who require continued enrollment for credit beyond the Fieldwork component.

title: Independent Study
description: Permission of faculty supervisor. Fall, Spring Semesters.

title: TPA
description: This course is for students who require continued work toward the Teacher Performance Assessment (TPA) after all other credential courses are completed.

title: Classroom Climate & Curriculum
description: This course introduces Californiaâs curriculum frameworks and academic content standards for science, history-social science, visual and performing arts, physical education, and health. Candidates utilize a backwards design approach to create integrated, thematic lessons and units for the elementary grades. Candidates learn to engage and support all students in learning through the development and maintenance of a caring, responsive classroom environment. Concurrently, candidates participate in fieldwork experiences to apply their developing understandings of teaching and learning.

title: Behavior Intervention and Support
description: This course will provide candidates with an introduction to positive behavioral approaches for individuals with mild/moderate disabilities, including those on the autism spectrum. Candidates are required to conduct Functional Behavior Assessments and develop Positive Behavioral Intervention Plans and participate effectively in individual, classroom and school-wide behavior support processes using appropriate positive behaviorally-based teaching strategies. Candidates will learn that careful observation of behavior and attention to cultural differences are considered basic to the understanding of student behavior. Concurrently, candidates participate in fieldwork experiences to apply their developing knowledge of and skills with teaching and learning.

title: Teaching for Equity
description: Teaching for Equity is a course that examines principles of educational equity and diversity and their implementation in curriculum content and school practices. Candidates examine their own beliefs, attitudes, biases, and expectations about educational equity, so that they may be better prepared to create equitable classrooms. Candidates also explore the history of schooling in the United States and ways to include cultural traditions in instructional programs. Candidates will learn how to maximize the academic achievement for all students. Candidates participate in fieldwork throughout the semester.

title: Using Technology in Classrooms
description: This course offers credential candidates with an introductory overview of current computer-based technologies used in a variety of educational settings within and across curricular content areas. Candidates will develop the skills necessary to navigate technology for the purpose of leveraging it as an instructional tool.

title: Applied Instructional Technology
description: This course offers credential candidates an opportunity to apply current instructional technologies within their content area(s). Incorporating ISTE standards, emphasis is on making significant changes in teaching and learning. Focus is on utilizing a range of instructional technologies to create and maintain effective environments for student learning, including student engagement, assessment, assistive technology, and reflection.

title: Elementary Preparation for Student Teaching
description: This course integrates previous and on-going coursework and prepares candidates for supervised teaching within an elementary school. Candidates observe and participate in a variety of elementary grades, including in classrooms with English learners and students with special needs. Observations focus on engaging and supporting all students and creating and maintaining effective environments for learning.

title: Teaching Math in Elementary Schools
description: Prepares students to teach mathematics in elementary school. Students are prepared to deliver a balanced instructional program through active construction of pedagogical knowledge and skills. Students learn developmentally appropriate strategies to teach students Californiaâs Standards for Mathematics. Includes fieldwork where appropriate mathematics instruction is modeled.

title: Observation
description: Teacher candidates observe and participate in partner elementary and secondary schools with a focus on the essential knowledge and skills required for effective teaching and learning as defined by the California Teaching Performance Expectations (TPEs). The Field Placement Coordinator establishes the school site in cooperation with a supervising faculty member. **Additional Fee Applies**

title: Pedagogical Preparation I
description: This course explores instructional methodology, classroom management, and basic concepts of the secondary school curriculum. It includes strategies and activities designed to increase awareness and skill in dealing with diversity in the classroom as well as integrating differentiated instruction. Concurrently, candidates participate in fieldwork experiences to apply their developing understandings of teaching and learning.

title: Literacy Across the Curriculum
description: All secondary teachers share the responsibility of helping students develop the literacy skills that they will need to further their education. Participants taking this course will learn how to support literacy in their specific content areas, with emphasis on academic language abilities, critical thinking, and twenty-first century skills. Concurrently, candidates participate in fieldwork experiences to apply their developing understandings of teaching and learning.

title: Pedagogical Preparation II
description: This course is designed to support you during your student teaching or intern teaching. We will integrate educational theory with classroom reality to create standards based units while using a variety of instructional strategies. We will also discuss and evaluate assessment methods, student accommodation and modification plans, diversity issues in the classroom, the role of parents and the community in the learning process, and how to use various resources in a meaningful and effective manner. Concurrently, candidates participate in fieldwork experiences to apply their developing understandings of teaching and learning.

title: Elementary Supervised Teaching Seminar
description: In seminar fashion, candidates discuss their teaching performance related to curriculum, instruction, assessment, and working with colleagues. Candidates draw upon and apply their learning from ongoing and previous coursework as they learn to effectively manage issues and challenges related to teaching and learning.

title: Pedagogical Preparation III
description: This pedagogy course is designed to be taken while obtaining a second single subject credential. The course may be taken during the final semester of supervised teaching, or after the completion of earning a first preliminary credential. Candidates meet with a content coach in the added content area who will provide support in planning and implementation of a UbD plan. Candidates incorporate educational theory with classroom experiences to create a standards-based unit using a variety of instructional strategies. In addition, the candidate, content coach and instructor will discuss and evaluate assessment methods, student accommodation and modification plans, diversity issues in the classroom, the role of parents and the community in the learning process, and how to use various resources in a meaningful and effective manner.

title: Differentiating Instruction for Inclusive Classrooms
description: The purpose of this course is to develop skills and competencies in addressing the needs of diverse learners within K-12 classrooms, specifically English Language Learners and students with disabilities. Candidates will learn how to assess students and implement effective instructional strategies to provide inclusive, collaborative educational experiences to all students. Additional topics include language and literacy development, federal and state mandates for serving diverse students, and the psychological, social, political and cultural factors affecting learning. Candidates will participate in focused fieldwork in general and special education classrooms and during Designated English Language Development classes.

title: Program Design
description: Focuses on Individual Education Plan (IEP) development and curriculum planning and instruction for students with mild/moderate learning challenges. Candidates will write a complete IEP including transition plans. Candidates will learn how to make adaptations to general education curriculum in order to ensure student success. Knowledge of ethical standards related to laws and regulations that provide equity for students with learning challenges is emphasized.

title: Secondary Supervised Teaching Seminar
description: This course explores alternative solutions to perplexing problems related to teaching and learning. Includes reflection on the use of teaching and assessment strategies and continued development as a professional educator. Students develop and write Educational Philosophy Statements and prepare for the job search and hiring process. Concurrently, candidates participate in fieldwork experiences to apply their developing understandings of teaching and learning.

title: Behavior Intervention and Support
description: This course will provide candidates with an introduction to positive behavioral approaches for individuals with mild/moderate disabilities, including those on the autism spectrum. Candidates are required to conduct Functional Behavior Assessments and develop Positive Behavioral Intervention Plans and participate effectively in individual, classroom and school-wide behavior support processes using appropriate positive behaviorally-based teaching strategies. Candidates will learn that careful observation of behavior and attention to cultural differences are considered basic to the understanding of student behavior. Concurrently, candidates participate in fieldwork experiences to apply their developing knowledge of and skills with teaching and learning.

title: Informal & Formal Assessment
description: Focuses on responsible assessment practices that take into account the diverse backgrounds and needs of students with mild/moderate disabilities. Formal assessment topics include the relationship between assessment and teaching practices, statistical interpretation of standardized assessment instruments, and interpretation and use of standardized data to write appropriate goals for IEPs. Informal assessment topics include the use and interpretation of informal measures such as writing portfolios, curriculum-based assessment, student self-assessment and observational data collection. Addresses appropriate transition planning for students with learning challenges.

title: Advanced Curriculum
description: Candidates receive advanced training in assessment and instruction that address the individual needs of students with mild/moderate disabilities. Emphasizes advanced reading, mathematics, and writing curriculum that incorporates more sophisticated assessment, lesson plans, instructional modifications, and collaboration between relevant stakeholders. Prerequisite: EDU 4165 /5165 Teaching Reading in Elementary Schools.

title: Special Education Supervised Teaching Seminar
description: Candidates participate in and reflect on a variety of activities representing different roles of special educators, including interactions with parents, and discuss other responsibilities of full-time teachers and service providers. Candidates teach in general and special education classrooms during regular school sessions daily. Students begin to plan for their professional clear credential.

title: Teaching Reading in Elementary Schools
description: Provides substantive, research-based instruction that prepares students to teach Reading/Language Arts in elementary school. Emphasis on organizing systematic instruction in reading, writing, listening and speaking to meet the needs of the full range of learners, including struggling readers, students with special needs, English learners, and advanced learners based on California's Standards for English Language Arts and Literacy. Includes fieldwork where appropriate Reading instruction is modeled.

title: Characteristics of Infants, Toddlers, and Preschoolers with Special Needs
description: This course lays the foundation for understanding the unique needs and challenges faced by infants, toddlers, and preschoolers with special education needs. Candidates will demonstrate knowledge of the characteristics of young children, including children with developmental, physical, cognitive, communication, and socioemotional delays and disabilities and will examine implications for program planning and service delivery. Concurrently, candidates participate in fieldwork experiences to apply their developing knowledge of and skills with teaching and learning.

title: Secondary Preparation for Supervised Teaching
description: This course develops an understanding of school culture and adolescent development while preparing candidates for the demands of a teaching career. It explores the essential knowledge and skills required for effective secondary teaching and learning as defined by the California Teaching Performance Expectations (TPEs). Concurrently, candidates participate in fieldwork experiences to apply their developing understandings of teaching and learning.

title: Assessment and Instruction in Early Childhood Special Education
description: This course provides students with an overview of current methods of assessment and instruction in early childhood special education. Focal topics include standardized methods of assessment, observation-based and play-based measures of assessing infants, toddlers and young children. Candidates learn the importance of linking assessments with California Preschool Curricular Frameworks, instructional program planning, embedding strategies into daily routines and activities, and using assistive technology when appropriate. Also included are related policy issues and implications for current research and practice in ECSE assessment and instruction, including issues pertaining to young children at risk and English learners. Concurrently, candidates participate in a minimum of 20 hours of fieldwork to apply their developing knowledge of and skills with teaching and learning.

title: Special Education Supervised Teaching Seminar
description: This course is designed to provide practical experience teaching students with mild/moderate disabilities in public and private school (K-12) settings. Candidates learn about the special education process including IEP and BIP development, assessment, instructional planning, and Universal precautions. Each candidate is required to participate in and reflect on a variety of activities representing different roles of special educators including interactions with parents, and to assume other responsibilities of full-time teachers and service providers for up to one semester. Credential candidates work in general and special education classrooms during regular school sessions daily. Students begin to plan for their second level or professional clear credential. Concurrently, candidates participate in fieldwork experiences to apply their developing knowledge of and skills with teaching and learning.

title: Supervised Fieldwork in Early Childhood Special Education
description: This course is designed to provide practical teaching experience to students eligible for special education in public and private school (preschool) settings. Candidates learn about the special education process including IEP, BIP development, assessment, instructional planning, and Universal precautions to support preschool aged students.. Each candidate is required to participate in and reflect on a variety of activities representing different roles of special educators including interactions with parents, case management responsibilities of an early childhood education preschool special education teacher. Credential candidates work in inclusion models, self-contained, and special day classrooms during regular school sessions daily. Students begin to plan for their second level or professional clear credential. Concurrently, candidates participate in fieldwork experiences to apply their developing knowledge of and skills with teaching and learning.

title: Seminar in Family and Professional Partnerships and Practices in Early Childhood Special Education
description: This course provides students with an overview of the Family Systems Model and highlights the importance of collaborating with families from culturally relevant and inclusive practices. Candidates will learn the importance of family engagement in the IEP/IFSP process to ensure fair and equitable participation within the program. Candidates will engage in self reflection, dialogue, and empathy as essential tools to support families. Along with ensuring candidates are demonstrating skills to be culturally and linguistically appropriate interactions with families to develop a positive relationship with families. Candidates will learn to educate families about their rights, advocacy, and navigate through available resources they may need to support their child within the home setting. Candidates will help families learn how to develop daily routines within the daily living skills and pre academics to promote independent learning.

title: Current Trends: Child and Adolescent Development
description: This course will introduce major themes and current issues in child and adolescent development. Specific topics vary by semester and will include issues of both historical relevance and contemporary debate. Topics are drawn from current research and theoretical foundations in social, cognitive, physical, and psychological development with an in-depth study of selected topics. Content includes theory and research on psychological dimensions of children as they participate in various contextual settings. Students will be introduced to the importance of research based knowledge to analyze and investigate these issues, in addition to think critically about information and sources of information as they develop skills in evaluating research.

title: Approaches to Educational Research
description: This course introduces the components and issues related to conducting research in education. Emphasis is placed on: developing skills using qualitative and quantitative research methods; critiquing othersâ research; designing a research study in a particular area of interest; and preparing and presenting a research proposal (including a review of the academic literature and description of the research methodology).

title: Culminating Project
description: This course prepares students to undertake the journey to becoming a teacher, school administrator, or leader in the field of education. Structured activities provide opportunities for students to explore their personal and professional values, their motivations for pursuing a career in education, and their concerns or reservations about undertaking a new career path. Students synthesize their coursework from the academic year to design a culminating project of their choice. Projects must include attention to equity, social justice, and quality of outcomes for diverse students.

title: Current Trends: Policy & Change
description: This course explores the history of American education through the lens of federal, state, and local politics and policies. Policy development, and its effects on school districts and schools, will be examined, with an emphasis on understanding educational reform, accountability, and funding. The contributions made by policy and school reform initiatives on quality and equity will be considered.

title: Current Trends: Special Education
description: This course provides a comprehensive overview of theory, research, practice, and policy and provides a solid research background in special education which includes educational equity for students with exceptionalities from diverse cultural, ethnic, gender and language backgrounds. A variety of research methods are explored, including group experimental, single subject, and qualitative research designs that address current issues in special education. Research studies are evaluated in terms of their methodological strengths and weaknesses in order to determine the role of convergent research evidence used for determining practice and policy. Course is designed so that research can be integrated into related coursework and practice.

title: Current Trends: Data Analysis
description: This course is designed for students currently in the process of data collection and analysis. Using a grounded theory approach, students are guided through their initial exploration of data and coding to develop the primary findings of their research. Continued exploration of academic literature allows students to situate their findings and develop a theoretical framework that contributes to new understandings in education.

title: Independent Study
description: The privilege of independent study is possible for students who are prepared to undertake it based on student need or interest with approval of the Chair of the department, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. The study must be in an area not covered by a regularly scheduled course. Course may be taken for 1-3 units. The maximum number of units that can be earned in one semester by independent study is 3; a total of up to six units of independent study may be applied to the degree.

title: Thesis Continuation
description: If after taking EDU 5915 a student has not completed the thesis, s/he enrolls in this course in order to complete it. Students who do not complete the thesis during thesis continuation are required to re-enroll in EDU 5915 at the full course rate. EDU 5992 has a flat fee and is taken for no units. Check with Business Services for the current fee.

title: Latin American Literatures
description: Study of major works and movements of Latin arts. The materials for this course are drawn from across or within major geographical regions of Latin America and are focused on specific literary periods varying from pre-contact indigenous works to 21st century postmodernism; they cover a wide spectrum of prose, poetry, and, at times, narrative film that exemplifies national and regional differences. Works and writers may include "Popol Vuh," "Quetzalcoatl," Garcia Marquez, Allende, Borges, Neruda, and Paz.

title: English as a Second Language
description: The first of a two-semester course for students whose first language is not English and whose English Placement Test places them into ESL. Emphasis on the writing of grammatical, well-structured sentences and the development of paragraphs in clear, idiomatic English. Grades: Pass/Fail/Satisfactory Progress.

title: English as a Second Language
description: The second of a two-semester course for students whose first language is not English and whose English Placement Test places them into ESL. Emphasis on the writing of grammatical, well-structured sentences and the development of paragraphs in clear, idiomatic English. Grades: Pass/Fail/Satisfactory Progress.

title: Bringing Theory to Practice
description: This course provides students with the opportunity to explore current academic research, theoretical frameworks, and historical trends in the field of education. Students select a specific educational issue or controversy of their choice for in-depth study. After completing a synthesis of the literature, students consider the implications of current academic research for designing educational policies and practices in their chosen career path.

title: SPED: Observation of Teaching
description: Explores essential knowledge and skills required for special education teaching and learning as defined by the Teaching Performance Expectations (TPE's). Includes observations in classrooms with diverse student populations, special needs students, and English learners. Leads to supervised teaching assignments. Required for non-SB2042 credential holders only. Fall, Spring Semesters.

title: Explorations in Equity
description: Explorations in Equity examines principles of equity and diversity and their implementations in schools and institutional settings. Students examine their own beliefs, attitudes, biases, and expectations about equity, so that they are better prepared for their future careers. With the goal of developing comfort with difference through heightened self-awareness, students participate in community engagement projects, integrating course content through cultural immersion.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Beowulf to Milton
description: Study of early developments in English literature from Old English epic through the medieval period and into the Restoration Period. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Writing and Rhetoric: Effective Communication I
description: Course teaches college level written and oral communication skills, with an emphasis on critical analysis and thinking, summary, synthesis, and research. Students learn to effectively read and create diverse forms of oral, digital, and visual communication, in addition to rigorously engaging the process of drafting, feedback and revision in order to produce their own well-designed, carefully researched essays for different audiences and purposes. With an emphasis on the conventions of reflective and critical modes of writing and public speaking, this course provides students with a foundation for communicative success in writing and speech in their college careers and beyond. The course may be offered as Service-Learning. Students must complete this course with a 'C' (2.0) or higher to fulfill the CORE requirement in Effective Communication.

title: Flash Fiction: Writing the very short, short story
description: Flash or sudden fiction is a genre that grows in popularity as attention spans shorten. Are you still reading? Students review, practice, and workshop all the basics found in longer works (plot, dialogue, characterization, setting, and conflict) and learn to hone and compress. Useful application for writing very short stories, graphic novels, one-act plays, blog narratives, and the like. Short and sweet. This course uses the platforms Moodle and Zoom and is a blend of synchronous and asynchronous learning. Students need reliable internet access. Recommended for new and returning students.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Literary Theory
description: What is literature and what are its uses? This course introduces students to foundational works of literary criticism and explores key theories that drive the study of literature, exploring the mediations between self and other, humans and nonhumans, technologies, and environments, as well as social inequities on the bases of race, gender, sexuality, class, and ability. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Victorian Gothic
description: A study of how Victorian literature, culture, and aesthetics have persisted into the present through an exploration of Victorian fiction and Neovictorian film. Topics include scientific progress and its underside, the fascination with the gothic and the occult, the ghosts of empire, the fear of Darwinism, and the entrapment of domestic interiors. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing: 19th Century US Literature
description: Major works and literary movements of the United States from the colonial era, inclusive of Native American literatures, to the Gilded Age, and featuring such authors as Equiano, Longfellow, Hawthorne, Beecher Stowe, Poe, Douglass, Melville, Whitman, Dickinson, Twain, and Zitkala-Sa. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing: American Modernism
description: Major works and movements of American literature from modernism to the present. May include authors such as Wharton, Pound, H.D., Eliot, Hughes, Stein, Hemingway, Faulkner, Hurston, Brooks, Ginsberg, Plath, O’Hara, Baraka, Baldwin, and Morrison. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Queer Literature & Film
description: Representations of queerness in both American and British contexts through literature, film, and theory. Foundational texts in queer theory, including those by Foucault, Sedgwick, and Bersani, inform the reading of works across historical periods—from Renaissance to Victorian, modern to contemporary—and by authors including Rossetti, Forster, Gonzalez, Woolf, Isherwood, Machado, Walker, Baldwin, Moraga, Islas, Winterson, and Kureishi. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: African Literature
description: In-depth exploration of African and African diaspora literature, spanning regions from the U.S. to the Caribbean, Brazil, and Central America. We will study works by classic authors like Amos Tutuola and Chinua Achebe, as well as contemporary voices such as Nalo Hopkinson, focusing on themes of colonialism, postcolonialism, and identity. Emphasis will be placed on Afrofuturism, Black Joy, the role of language, and the often overlooked experiences of the African diaspora in Latin America. Through readings, film, and arts analysis, we will explore literature as a medium for resistance and memory. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: China & Japan
description: Introduction to premodern literary traditions in China and Japan and an examination of how these forms live in present day writing. All works will be read in translation and examined through aesthetic, religious, and cultural contexts. Authors may include: Li Bai, Du Fu, Wei Ying-wu, Murasaki Shikibu, Sei Shonagon, Basho, Buson, Issa. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: AAPI Literatures
description: Introductory studies of the writings by Asian American authors from a diversity of national origins: Chinese, Filipinx, Hawaiian, Japanese, Korean, Pakistani, Vietnamese, and others. In drama, fiction, memoirs, and poetry, the course examines Asian American themes relating to historical, cultural, and societal issues, such as immigration, social conflicts, family, gender, identity. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Shakespeare
description: An introduction to Shakespeare’s plays, including history, comedy, tragedy, and romance, as well as his poetry. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Litratures of California
description: Starting with the Indigenous oral literatures of the North American West Coast and ending with the birth of ethnic studies on Bay Area campuses, this course is an introduction, via literature, to the diversity of our state and the ongoing social, cultural, and eceonomic challenges that we continue to face in achieving social justice and repairing historical harms. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: African -American Literatures
description: Introductory studies to the writings by African American authors from early to contemporary times. In drama, essays, fiction, and poetry, the course examines African American themes relating to historical, cultural, and societal issues, such as folk tradition, language, the blues, slavery, gender, family and ancestors, identity. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Native American Literatures
description: Introductory studies to the writings by Native American authors from a diversity of North American tribes: Cherokee, Chickasaw, Creek, Kiowa, Laguna, Mohawk, Navajo, Sioux, and others. From oral tradition to contemporary forms of literature, the course examines Native American themes relating to historical, cultural, and societal issues, such as identity, spirit world, earth, and language. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Postcolonialism
description: Study of literature produced by cultures that developed in response to colonial domination, using the theoretical frameworks of postcolonial, decolonial, and anti-colonial thinkers. Introduces students to "Third Worldist" thinking agross the Global South, including the work of Edward Said, Gayatri Spivak, Eduoard Glissant, Sylvia Wynter, Aime Cesaire, Djamila Ribeiro, Walter Mignolo, Marisol De La Cadena, and Rita Segato. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Toni Morrison
description: Study of Toni Morrison's oeuvre, the first African American and second American woman to win the Pulitzer Prize. Short fiction, plays, novels, criticism, and an opera are covered. African American themes relating to historical, cultural, and societal issues, such as folk tradition and her most succesful novels - The Bluest Eye, Sula, Beloved, Song of Solomon, Jazz, and others - are featured. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Transcendent Literatures
description: Influenced by German philosophers and British romantic poets, nineteenth-century New England writers developed a uniquely American concept of theology and nature that has endured to this day. This course will explore how the writings of Emerson, Thoreau, Whitman, and Fuller not only inspired the revolutionary spiritualism of the Beat poets, but remain a touchstone for today’s ecological and political movements. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Children's Literature
description: Study of literature for children through adolescence, from global genres such as the fairy tale and selections from nineteenth century children’s literature to young adult genres of the present. Welcomes all majors, including education majors and future teachers, creative writers, and literary scholars. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Retelling Myths
description: Study of contemporary poems and stories that retell Greek myths. Texts will be read through the lens of archetypal criticism and feminist theory. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Writing the Body
description: This course looks at the body as a form of text, exploring how gender and identity are represented in writing. Students explore both historical and contemporary views on the body, including nineteenth-century female writers’ treatment of illness and modern memoirs of illness. Through reading and writing exercises, students will explore how writing shapes and is shaped by the body. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Politics of Body
description: This course is an introduction to the field of performance studies, in which we discuss how bodies—both their kinesthetics and aesthetics—are read and politicized. Select topics in dance history intersect with social science and cultural anthropology as we study issues of power, visibility, and representation both on and off-stage. We pay particular attention to how dance as a form of embodied knowledge reflects and creates cultural values and identity, honoring movement both as a mirror to the culture that created it, and as an instigator of cultural change. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Literary Monsters
description: Study of monsters and monstrous bodies in literature and film from early American legends to contemporary cyborgs. Examination of how monsters and patchwork bodies negotiate cultural anxieties about science and technology as well as gendered and raced embodiment. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Romantic Literature
description: Poetry and prose of the English Romanticist period including such authors as Wordsworth, Keats, Blake, Mary Shelley, and Jane Austen. Topics include: revolution, reform, empire, feminist thought, and ecocritical perspectives on nature and human technology. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Film & Drama
description: Formal study of the performance genres of film and drama. Covers how to analyze elements of film such as visual effects, sound, composition, and mise en scène, and how to analyze elements of drama such as acting, stagecraft, and theatricality. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Environmental Literature
description: This course explores climate change, environmental justice, and identity through interdisciplinary and multicultural perspectives. We will examine how environmental degradation, displacement, and efforts toward sustainability shape both individual and collective identities. Key questions include: How does the environment influence who we are and where we belong? How do human actions impact the places we inhabit, and how do those changes, in turn, reshape us? What are the effects of environmental disconnection or over-exploitation on communities and ecosystems? Through close readings of essays, literature, poetry, and philosophical texts, we will analyze how stories of place, identity, and environmental justice are told and understood. Various lenses, including cultural, ecological, and geographical theory, will guide our inquiry. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Poetry & Fiction
description: Formal study of the genres of poetry and fiction. Covers how to analyze poetic elements such as rhyme, meter, lineation, stanzaic structure, and shape, and how to analyze elements of fiction such as characterization, narrative form, and plot. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Fiction Studies
description: Formal study of the genre of fiction, with analysis of its literary elements (point of view, characterization, and narrative structure), covering selected authors and forms from classical fables to twentieth-century novels.

title: Drama Studies
description: Formal study of the genre of drama, with analysis of its literary and performance elements (acting, theatricality, and stagecraft), covering selected playwrights and forms from classical Greek tragedy to contemporary avant-garde theater.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Sci-Fi & Fantasy
description: Science fiction and modern fantasy both emerged in response to an increasingly industrialized and standardized world, offering imaginary alternative worlds with different social orders, different moralities, and new ways of thinking about what it means to be human in the face of the truly alien and fantastic.  Course will span the seminal works of the 19th century to the most recent literature and film. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Global Hollywood
description: Film study of recent, global blockbuster hits through the lens of understanding how Hollywood film genres and popular culture adapt to changes in the flow of global capital and markets. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Autobiography
description: The introduction to the art of writing the personal essay and biographical sketch through readings, exercises, and workshopping, including writing about personal and/or fictionalized experience.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Comparative Literature
description: An exploration of the theoretical perspectives comparative literature brings to literature and the arts.  This interdisciplinary and global approach relates literary texts to the visual worlds of paintings, photographs, film, and dance. Topics will include the shifting constructions of identity, theories of difference, and the various states of language. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Literature & Psychoanalysis
description: Study of literature and other media including film and theater through the lens of psychoanalytic theory. Course considers how major psychoanalytic concepts such as the unconscious, melancholia, transference, and projection can be applied to the study of literary art forms. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Literary Theory
description: What is literature and what are its uses? This course introduces students to foundational works of literary criticism and explores key theories that drive the study of literature, exploring the mediations between self and other, humans and nonhumans, technologies, and environments, as well as social inequities on the bases of race, gender, sexuality, class, and ability. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Beowulf to Milton
description: Study of early developments in English literature from Old English epic through the medieval period and into the Restoration Period. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing and Research: Effective Communication II
description: Instruction in advanced principles of expository writing, critical thinking, oral and visual communication, and in-depth mastery of reflective, critical and academic modes of writing, and research across the disciplines within and beyond the University. Designed to enhance student awareness of and ability to navigate as effective communicators in todayâs poly-modal world. Course builds upon the skills developed in Effective Communication 1, including writing and speech, with an additional emphasis on academic research, evaluating source material, and synthesizing concepts for advanced argumentation in academic writing and presentations. Students consolidate advanced skills in integrating research, using appropriate citation and documentation. Students must complete this course with a 'C' (2.0) or higher to fulfill the second CORE requirement in Effective Communication. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Shakespeare
description: An introduction to Shakespeare’s plays, including history, comedy, tragedy, and romance, as well as his poetry. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Victorian Gothic
description: A study of how Victorian literature, culture, and aesthetics have persisted into the present through an exploration of Victorian fiction and Neovictorian film. Topics include scientific progress and its underside, the fascination with the gothic and the occult, the ghosts of empire, the fear of Darwinism, and the entrapment of domestic interiors. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: The Middle Ages to the Renaissance
description: Study of major works of English literature from the Middle Ages through the Restoration, including such authors as Bede, Marie de France, Chaucer, Malory, Spenser, Donne, Lanyer, Crashaw, and Milton.

title: United States Literature before 1914
description: Major works and literary movements of the United States from the colonial era, inclusive of Native American literatures, to the Gilded Age, and featuring such authors as Equiano, Longfellow, Hawthorne, Beecher Stowe, Poe, Douglass, Melville, Whitman, Dickinson, Twain, and Zitkala-Sa. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Romantic to Pre-War Literature
description: Poetry and prose of the English Romanticist period including such authors as Wordsworth, Keats, Blake, Mary Shelley, and Jane Austen. Topics include: revolution, reform, empire, feminist thought, and ecocritical perspectives on nature and human technology. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Performance Studies
description: This course fosters community-engaged learning and bridges the study of literature with the restorative practices of performing arts. Students will work with various community partners–performing arts organizations that focus upon the fight for equity and justice for marginalized communities. Students analyze select works across different periods and contemporary performance art in order to examine the role of performance in social critique. Students will explore issues of social justice, especially at the intersection of race, class, disability, sexuality, and gender, in plays, works of choreography, and relevant literary texts. This course only satisfies the CORE CKE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: Introduction to Creative Writing
description: Introduction to many forms of creative writing, including poetry, fiction, and drama writing. Study of key terms and concepts in the writing process and practice in peer workshops.

title: American Modernism to the Present
description: Major works and movements of American literature from modernism to the present. May include authors such as Wharton, Pound, H.D., Eliot, Hughes, Stein, Hemingway, Faulkner, Hurston, Brooks, Ginsberg, Plath, O’Hara, Baraka, Baldwin, and Morrison. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Linguistics
description: Introduction to linguistics: the scientific inquiry into human language, its structures and uses and the relationship between them, as well as into the development and acquisition of language.

title: Literature by Women
description: Examination of the evolution and revolution of literature by women and their tradition in English. Study of womenâs writings in English from the Middle Ages to the present and from geographically and culturally diverse areas. Exploration of feminist literary criticism, which considers the impact of gender upon writing and reading.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Queer Literatures & Film
description: Representations of queerness in both American and British contexts through literature, film, and theory. Foundational texts in queer theory, including those by Foucault, Sedgwick, and Bersani, inform the reading of works across historical periods—from Renaissance to Victorian, modern to contemporary—and by authors including Rossetti, Forster, Gonzalez, Woolf, Isherwood, Machado, Walker, Baldwin, Moraga, Islas, Winterson, and Kureishi. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Feminist Disability Ethics in Literature
description: This course examines nineteenth to twenty-first century American literature that prompts debate about ethical issues regarding gender and disability. Applying a feminist methodology that includes intersectional analysis and an ethics of care, students will analyze narrative plots to form ethical arguments regarding sexism and ableism.

title: Western Classics
description: Study of major works of Western literature from the time of its beginnings through the Renaissance, including mainly pieces composed or written in languages other than English, such as the Old and New Testaments, The Epic of Gilgamesh, Homerâs Iliad, Virgilâs Aeneid, Danteâs Divine Comedy. Selected works may vary from semester to semester.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: China & Japan
description: Introduction to premodern literary traditions in China and Japan and an examination of how these forms live in present day writing. All works will be read in translation and examined through aesthetic, religious, and cultural contexts. Authors may include: Li Bai, Du Fu, Wei Ying-wu, Murasaki Shikibu, Sei Shonagon, Basho, Buson, Issa. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Adv Writing & Research: AAPI Literatures
description: Introductory studies of the writings by Asian American authors from a diversity of national origins: Chinese, Filipinx, Hawaiian, Japanese, Korean, Pakistani, Vietnamese, and others. In drama, fiction, memoirs, and poetry, the course examines Asian American themes relating to historical, cultural, and societal issues, such as immigration, social conflicts, family, gender, identity. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Native American Literatures
description: Introductory studies to the writings by Native American authors from a diversity of North American tribes: Cherokee, Chickasaw, Creek, Kiowa, Laguna, Mohawk, Navajo, Sioux, and others. From oral tradition to contemporary forms of literature, the course examines Native American themes relating to historical, cultural, and societal issues, such as identity, spirit world, earth, and language. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Myth and Ancient Literatures
description: An in-depth study of myth and ancient literature, including such works as the Old Testament, Enuma Elish, The Epic of Gilgamesh, Lucretiusâ De Rerum Natura, Hesiodâs Theogony, Homerâs Iliad, Virgilâs Aeneid, Aesopâs Fables, Sapphoâs lyrics, Sophoclesâ Theban plays, and Ovidâs Metamorphoses.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: African-American Literature
description: Introductory studies to the writings by African American authors from early to contemporary times. In drama, essays, fiction, and poetry, the course examines African American themes relating to historical, cultural, and societal issues, such as folk tradition, language, the blues, slavery, gender, family and ancestors, identity. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: British Modernism to the Present
description: Major works and movements of English literature from the 1920s to the present, including such authors as Eliot, Joyce, Forster, Woolf, Yeats, Beckett, Selvon, Rushdie, Winterson, and Ishiguro.

title: Pacific Islander American Literature
description: Writings of Pacific Islander American authors from a diversity of island heritages: Hawaiian, Samoan, Guamanian/Chamorro, Tahitian, Tongan, Mariana Islander and others.
