Covering prose and poetry, the course examines themes relating to historical, cultural, and social issues such as family, gender, identity island life, mainland life, acculturation, and colonialism.

title: Hispanic American Literatures
description: Study of the writings by Hispanic American authors from a diversity of national origins: Cuban, Mexican, Puerto Rican. From early chronicles and oral traditions to contemporary forms of literature, the course examines Hispanic American themes relating to historical, cultural, and societal issues, such as sense of place, oral tradition, family, love, migration and exile, gender, identity.

title: Filipina/o American Literatures
description: The oral and literary traditions from a diversity of Filipina/o cultures: indigenous languages to Western, colonial languages such Spanish and English. With a particular focus on writings by Filipina/os in America (Pinoys) â short stories, poems, memoirs, and essays â the course examines themes of family, communities, work, war, diaspora, migration, and identity.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Postcolonialism
description: Study of literature produced by cultures that developed in response to colonial domination, using the theoretical frameworks of postcolonial, decolonial, and anti-colonial thinkers. Introduces students to "Third Worldist" thinking agross the Global South, including the work of Edward Said, Gayatri Spivak, Eduoard Glissant, Sylvia Wynter, Aime Cesaire, Djamila Ribeiro, Walter Mignolo, Marisol De La Cadena, and Rita Segato. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Latinx Literatures
description: Study of significant works by Latin American writers, including both well-established and emerging authors. The course focuses on social and historical movements, framing Latin America through a hemispheric perspective and emphasizing the embodied experience of colonialism across diverse territories such as the US, Mexico, Guatemala, and Brazil. It covers artistic movements such as the feminist literary wave in Mexico, the intersection of disability and poetry, and queer Chicanx literature in the US, through an eclectic mix of prose, poetry, and film.

title: African American Narratives
description: Study of writings of African American twentieth and twenty-first-century literary and cinematic narratives. The course examines African American themes relating to historical, cultural, and societal issues in contemporary forms and genres, such as Afrofuturism, horror, superhero fiction, romantic comedies, fantasy, comedies and historical romance.

title: Latina/o American Literature
description: Study of writings of Latina/o authors from a diversity of national origins and newer immigration from Central and South America: Dominican, El Salvadorian, Guatemalan, Venezuelan, Nicaragua, and others. The course examines contemporary forms of literature, Latina/o themes relating to historical, cultural, and societal issues, such as sense of place, oral tradition, family, love, migration and exile, gender, identity.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: African Literatures
description: In-depth exploration of African and African diaspora literature, spanning regions from the U.S. to the Caribbean, Brazil, and Central America. We will study works by classic authors like Amos Tutuola and Chinua Achebe, as well as contemporary voices such as Nalo Hopkinson, focusing on themes of colonialism, postcolonialism, and identity. Emphasis will be placed on Afrofuturism, Black Joy, the role of language, and the often overlooked experiences of the African diaspora in Latin America. Through readings, film, and arts analysis, we will explore literature as a medium for resistance and memory. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Toni Morrison
description: Study of Toni Morrison's oeuvre, the first African American and second American woman to win the Pulitzer Prize. Short fiction, plays, novels, criticism, and an opera are covered. African American themes relating to historical, cultural, and societal issues, such as folk tradition and her most succesful novels - The Bluest Eye, Sula, Beloved, Song of Solomon, Jazz, and others - are featured. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Womenâs Mythmaking
description: The myths of Ancient Greece recur throughout our literature because poets, writers, and artists continue to discover new meanings in archetypes; they rejuvenate our cultural images and can redefine who we are as individuals (our personal and spiritual quests), as we live within communities. In this course modern stories and poems are read and analyzed that retell cultural myths from new and unexpected points of view. Readings include writings about mythology and culture by Carl Karenyi, Alicia Ostriker, and others, with a particular focus on gender and identity.

title: Southern Writers
description: Advanced seminar on literature from the Southern United States, and the literary and cultural perspectives offered by these texts. Considerations of race, post-war identity, regionalism may be examined. Authors may include Welty, Faulkner, OâConnor, Hurston, and contemporary authors Butler and Johnson.

title: The Bard's Beasts
description: A study of animals in Shakespeare's plays to explore human-nonhuman relationships and the ways in which they entangle issues of race, religion, geography, gender and sexuality in the English Renaissance. Students will adopt a variety of interdisciplinary approaches from science, eco-centrism, postcolonial and critical race studies.

title: Queer Lives
description: Twenty-first-century American science, society, gender, and sex knowledge transformations are in renaissance. This course looks at these (trans)formations and (inter)sections of social and personal identities through the prose/poetic creative writings of both cisgender and transgender American authors, as well as through the prisms of cultural, gender, queer, literary and trans studies/theories.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Retelling Myths
description: Study of contemporary poems and stories that retell Greek myths. Texts will be read through the lens of archetypal criticism and feminist theory. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Contemporary Spanish Literature
description: The 20th century constitutes a second golden age in the history of Spanish letters and the first for its cinema. Students will read/view, discuss, and write analyses of selected works by major writers/directors from the Generation of 1898, Generation of 1927, Civil War (1936-1939), dictatorship, and democracy periods. Works are in their original Spanish with English translations.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Writing the Body
description: This course looks at the body as a form of text, exploring how gender and identity are represented in writing. Students explore both historical and contemporary views on the body, including nineteenth-century female writers’ treatment of illness and modern memoirs of illness. Through reading and writing exercises, students will explore how writing shapes and is shaped by the body. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Environmental Literature
description: This course explores climate change, environmental justice, and identity through interdisciplinary and multicultural perspectives. We will examine how environmental degradation, displacement, and efforts toward sustainability shape both individual and collective identities. Key questions include: How does the environment influence who we are and where we belong? How do human actions impact the places we inhabit, and how do those changes, in turn, reshape us? What are the effects of environmental disconnection or over-exploitation on communities and ecosystems? Through close readings of essays, literature, poetry, and philosophical texts, we will analyze how stories of place, identity, and environmental justice are told and understood. Various lenses, including cultural, ecological, and geographical theory, will guide our inquiry. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Literary Monsters
description: Study of monsters and monstrous bodies in literature and film from early American legends to contemporary cyborgs. Examination of how monsters and patchwork bodies negotiate cultural anxieties about science and technology as well as gendered and raced embodiment. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Postmodernism
description: An in-depth and challenging exploration into the postmodern tradition in literature after 1945. This course grapples with theory, definition, and creative works as it seeks to comprehend the vast range of literary innovation since the end of World War II on both sides of the Atlantic.

title: Poetry Studies
description: Formal study of the genre of poetry, with analysis of its visual, sonic, and structural elements (rhyme, meter, lineation, stanzation, and shape), covering selected poets and forms from the medieval period to the present.

title: Film Studies
description: Formal study of the genre of film, with analysis of filmic elements (including visual effects, sound, composition, framing, mise en scÃ¨ne, point of view, character, and dialogue), covering filmmakers and forms from the 20th century to the present.

title: Drama Studies
description: Formal study of the genre of drama, with analysis of its literary and performance elements (acting, theatricality, and stagecraft), covering selected playwrights and forms from classical Greek tragedy to contemporary avant-garde theater.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Transcendent Literatures
description: Influenced by German philosophers and British romantic poets, nineteenth-century New England writers developed a uniquely American concept of theology and nature that has endured to this day. This course will explore how the writings of Emerson, Thoreau, Whitman, and Fuller not only inspired the revolutionary spiritualism of the Beat poets, but remain a touchstone for today’s ecological and political movements. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Fiction Studies
description: Formal study of the genre of fiction, with analysis of its literary elements (point of view, characterization, and narrative structure), covering selected authors and forms from classical fables to twentieth-century novels.

title: Writing the Graphic Novel
description: In this introductory course, students enter the exciting world of graphic novels as creators - both authors and artists - by developing complex characters and compelling narratives and then adapting these narratives into the comic book form. Students create dynamic page layouts and intricate text-to-image relationships that transform their words into modern sequential art. Co-requisite ART 3308 .

title: Literature in Translation
description: Selected readings from writers across the globe, viewed through the lens of comparative literary theories; some practice reading in the original language in parallel text format.

title: Expressive Writing
description: Expressive Writing allows students to explore the techniques of expressive writing as a healing tool. A premise of the course is that, through the creative process, students will gain empathy for others and a deeper understanding of their emotional lives. Students will both practice expressive writing and study the work of Dr. James Pennebaker (Writing to Heal) and others who have published their research on the connections between expressive writing, stress relief, and improvement in overall health.

title: Writing Popular Genres
description: An advanced creative writing workshop that helps generate and revise work as well as research publications in popular genres, including mystery, detective, paranormal romance, fantasy, science fiction.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Global Hollywood
description: Film study of recent, global blockbuster hits through the lens of understanding how Hollywood film genres and popular culture adapt to changes in the flow of global capital and markets. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Poetry Writing
description: Study of and extensive practice in the elements and patterns of poetry. Students compile a portfolio of poems and have training in oral presentation of poems.

title: Creative Nonfiction Writing
description: Introduction to the art of creative nonfiction writing including autobiography, biography, personal essay, and feature article through readings, exercises, and workshops.

title: Fiction Writing
description: Introduction to the craft of short fiction through study of exemplary models and exercises in such elements of form as point of view, narrative modes, dialogue, imagery, etc. Students critique one anotherâs work and complete a number of stories for a final portfolio.

title: Advanced Creative Writing
description: Advanced work in creative writing, including poetry, fiction, and hybrid forms. Reading of published authors and study of key concepts in the writing process and practice in peer workshops.

title: Literary Magazine 3
description: See description for 3426.

title: Spoken Word
description: This course explores storytelling and spoken word poetry. In addition to compositional aspects of performance texts such as image and character, we will consider issues of live performance such as improvisation and focus. Readings and course materials will include examples from local and international performance writing movements. Devising original creative work is a central focus of this course. Students will write, workshop and perform their own original poems and stories.

title: Acting and Stagecraft
description: Immersed in the study of dramaturgy, scenic language, and mise en scÃ¨ne, a student company of actors and technicians mount scenes or a full production of a theatrical work. Exploring the social, political, and cultural histories of a given script, students spend a semester connecting text to context by designing, staging, and rehearsing for mainstage production. Throughout the production process, students critically reflect on how meaning is transmitted through performance in the translation from page to stage. **This course may have specific scheduling needs to accommodate the rehearsal process.**

title: Advanced Writing Seminar
description: Practice in the art of writing within the studentsâ chosen genres; weekly readings and in-class exercises will supplement class discussion. Students critique each otherâs writing and complete a final portfolio.

title: Literary Magazine 4
description: See description for 3426.

title: Fiction Writing 2
description: Advanced creative work in the genre of fiction. Prerequisite: ENGL 3403 .

title: Drama Writing
description: Introduction to the art and craft of writing for the stage. Special emphasis will be given to the technical elements of playwriting, the vocabulary of the playwright, and the nature of the writing experience.

title: Writing in the Workplace
description: Practice in the type of writing commonly required in the workplace: letters, emails, memos, forms, mission statements, and writing for the web. Course will address writing etiquette and style for successful communication.

title: Critical Inquiry & Reflective Writing
description: Provides the context for the exploration of lifelong learning. Preparation of the Experiential Learning Portfolio that documents, describes, analyzes, synthesizes, and evaluates prior experience in terms of university level learning outcomes essays. This Portfolio, consisting of three or more experiential learning essays, may be submitted to the appropriate academic departments for evaluation and the possible awarding of additional university credit.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Comparative Literature
description: An exploration of the theoretical perspectives comparative literature brings to literature and the arts.  This interdisciplinary and global approach relates literary texts to the visual worlds of paintings, photographs, film, and dance. Topics will include the shifting constructions of identity, theories of difference, and the various states of language. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Portfolio Redevelopment
description: Provides the opportunity for a student to work within a structured course environment to develop the second portfolio submission. This submission may include revisions to the original essays (as recommended by the previous evaluators) and/or new essays and supporting documentation. Prerequisite: ENGL 3442 .

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Autobiography
description: The introduction to the art of writing the personal essay and biographical sketch through readings, exercises, and workshopping, including writing about work and other experience. ADC students may elect either to complete a Creative Writing portfolio or an Experiential Learning Portfolio that documents, describes, analyzes, synthesizes, and evaluates prior experience in terms of university level learning outcomes essays. Course serves as the pre-requisite for ENGL 3443 : Portfolio Redevelopment, which supports the completion of additional Experiential Learning essays and the possible awarding of additional university credits. Satisfies the Effective Communication 2 Core area with a 'C' (2.0) or higher. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or an equivalent transfer First Year Writing course with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: STA: Dickens Universe
description: **Study Travel Course** Dickens Universe is a week-long, Summer immersive experience held at the University of California, Santa Cruz at the end of July / beginning of August. Students attend and participate in an intensive seminar course centered on a Dickens novel and Victorian culture. As part of the course, students also attend evening lectures and other community events at the 'universe,' as well as complete a culminating research paper / project that articulates the relevance of course themes to the present day. The 'universe' is a diverse gathering of faculty, high school teachers, graduate students, undergraduates, high school students, and community members in the spirit of Dickensian community and collegiality.

title: STA: Creative Arts at the Finca
description: **Study Travel Course** Creative Arts at the Finca offers a study abroad opportunity for students who seek a sustained period of reflection for writing poetry or prose. Exploration in other creative genres is encouraged; professors may emphasize special themes. Students stay at a retreat center in northern Spain, where they attend workshops and craft lectures with the goal of producing a portfolio inspired by local culture and natural beauty.

title: Capstone 1: Thesis
description: The first semester of a two-semester course to produce the Senior Thesis. Normally enrolling two semesters before graduating, students choose a subject and literary works, develop a thesis idea, working bibliography of primary and secondary sources, and a provisional outline of the Senior Thesis.

title: Capstone 2: Thesis
description: The second semester of a two-semester course to produce the Senior Thesis. Students draft the Senior Thesis, submit the completed paper, and present it orally to the University community. Pre-requisite: ENGL 4201 .

title: Capstone 1: Project
description: The first semester of a two-semester course to produce the Senior Project. Normally enrolling two semesters before graduating, students develop a portfolio of creative writing in a given genre or genres (e.g., drama, fiction, film, nonfiction prose, poetry) together with an introductory essay discussing their project.

title: Internship: Literature & Language
description: An internship of 1 unit is a requirement for the majors in English and in English with a Writing Emphasis. Speak with academic advisor for details on internship opportunities in Literature & Language. Grades: Pass/Fail. Course is available for 1-3 units.

title: Advanced Creative Writing
description: Introduction to many forms of creative writing, including poetry, fiction, and drama writing. Study of key terms and concepts in the writing process and practice in peer workshops.

title: Thesis Continuation
description: If after taking ENGL 4201 & 4202 or ENGL 4203 & 4204 a student has not completed the senior project, s/he enrolls in this course in order to complete. The reader must be consulted prior to enrolling to make sure s/he will be available during the semester in which the student wishes to complete. Please note that most readers are unavailable during the summer. Also note, students who do not complete the project during thesis continuation are required to re-enroll in ENGL 4201 or 4203 for 3-units at the full course rate. This course has a flat fee. Check with Business Services for the current fee.

title: Teaching Assistant
description: The privilege of serving as a teaching assistant is available to students who are interested with the approval of the Department Chair, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. A contract between the student and the instructor to determine goals, specifics on project contents/materials, and evaluation process will be required at time of request. Course may be taken for 1-2 units.

title: Fiction Writing
description: Introduction to the craft of short fiction through study of exemplary models and exercises in such elements of form as point of view, narrative modes, dialogue, imagery, etc. Students critique one anotherâs work and complete a number of stories for a final portfolio.

title: Independent Study
description: The privilege of independent study is possible for students who are prepared to undertake it based on student need or interest with approval of the Chair of the department, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. The study must be in an area not covered by a regularly scheduled course. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. The maximum number of units that can be earned in one semester by independent study is 4; a total of up to four units of independent study may be applied to the degree.

title: Advanced Writing & Research: Literature & Psychoanalysis
description: Study of literature and other media including film and theater through the lens of psychoanalytic theory. Course considers how major psychoanalytic concepts such as the unconscious, melancholia, transference, and projection can be applied to the study of literary art forms. Prerequisite: passing Effective Communication 1 or its equivalent with a grade of 'C' (2.0) or higher.

title: Environmental Sciences
description: Investigation of ecological principles involved in human relationship to and interaction with the environment. Emphasis is given to political and economic aspects involved in the solution of environmental problems - 3 lecture hours. Recommended for non-Biological Sciences or non-Chemistry majors. ENSC 2000 / ENSC 2005 meet the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirements.

title: Birds & the Environment
description: This course is an introduction to the study of birds, emphasizing the roles birds play in ecosystems and how we have come to understand these roles. Birds as early warning indicators of ecological disasters such as climate change will be stressed in the course. Weekly field trips to nearby locations will help students recognize and identify local bird species. Students will also conduct field observations on these field trips to help them appreciate the collection and analysis of field data and how these data can be used to appreciate the dynamics of bird populations - 3 lecture hours. Recommended for non-Biological Sciences or non-Cheistry majors. ENSC 4100 is available for upper division credit with approval by Department Chair. ENSC 2100 / ENSC 2105 meet the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirements.

title: Poetry Writing
description: Study of and extensive practice in the elements and patterns of poetry. Students compile a portfolio of poems and have training in oral presentation of poems.

title: Anatomy Theater
description: This course centralizes both interior and exterior technologies of the body in performance-based media. Exploring, at first, the history of the anatomy theater in Renaissance Europe, students will then examine diverse forms of embodiment and bodily contact in art, theater, photography, and film through conceptual frames in the medical humanities, disability studies, critical race, gender, and sexuality studies.

title: Disease Ecology
description: Disease ecology is designed to provide upper-level undergraduate students with an understanding of the ecological and evolutionary complexity inherent to host-pathogen interactions. The course will draw heavily on case studies representing a diverse array of systems (e.g., plants, animals, aquatic systems, terrestrial systems). It introduces parasite and pathogen diversity, core concepts in disease ecology, host-pathogen coevolution, community ecology of host-pathogen interactions, and the importance of pathogens in conservation and management. The course will use a combination of lectures supplemented with readings from the primary literature, guest lectures by researchers at DUC and beyond, breakout discussion sections, case studies, documentary films, and writing assignments. This course is reading and writing intensive - 4 lecture hours.

title: Environmental Chemistry
description: This course is a study of chemical principles as applied to ecosystems, soil, water and water management, pest control, sediments, nutrients, sewage and air pollution from hazardous chemicals and solar and other renewable energy courses - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: CHEM 2000 / CHEM 2005 or equivalent.

title: Environmental Chemistry Lab
description: Lab component of ENSC 3000 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30Fee to cover lab materials.

title: Capstone 2: Project
description: The second semester of a two-semester course to produce the Senior Project. Students complete their draft of the Senior Project, submit the completed Portfolio, and present it orally to the University community. Pre-requisite: ENGL 4203 .

title: Environmental Sciences Lab
description: Lab component of ENSC 2000 - 3 lab hours. A limited number of laboratory/field problems are studied. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. ENSC 2000 / ENSC 2005 meet the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirements.

title: Birds & the Environment Lab
description: Lab component of ENSC 2100 - 3 lab hours. ENSC 4105 is available for upper division credit with approval by Department Chair. ENSC 2100 / ENSC 2105 meets the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirements.

title: Advanced Environmental Science Lab
description: Lab component of ENSC 3300 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials.

title: Marine Biology
description: This course includes the study of the physical and biological aspects of the marine environment with an emphasis on how marine plants and animals have adapted to the challenges of their environment - 3 lecture hours.

title: Conservation Biology
description: Biological principles will be applied to the conservation of species with an emphasis on the maintenance of biodiversity. The discussion on conservation of game, non-game and endangered species will be included. Field trips to biological preserves, habitat restoration sites, and captive breeding facilities will be included - 3 lecture hours.

title: Conservation Biology Lab
description: Lab component of ENSC 4000 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials.

title: Birds & the Environment Lab
description: Lab component of ENSC 4100 - 3 lab hours. ENSC 4105 requires approval by Department Chair. ENSC 4100 / ENSC 4105 meets the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirements.

title: Toxicology
description: This course provides an in depth study of the effects of various classes of toxins including heavy metals and persistent synthetic organics, with a focus on susceptible biochemical/cellular processes of the central nervous, immune, hepatic and renal target organ systems. Mechanisms of function including dose-response relationships, biotransformation, and biochemical processes underlying toxicity are included - 3 lecture hours.

title: Marine Biology Lab
description: Lab component for ENSC 4200 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials.

title: Independent Study: Environmental Sciences
description: Directed reading on specialized topics or development of a set of special laboratory skills. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. ENSC 4999 also available for upper division credit. Contract required.

title: Teaching Assistant: Environmental Sciences
description: This course provides teaching experience in ecology and environmental sciences at the undergraduate level. Students will assist faculty members in preparing, delivering, and tearing down laboratory (and/or discussion section) instructional units in ecology and environmental sciences courses, conducting review sessions and tutoring students under the direct supervision of a faculty mentor. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Prerequisites: a grade of 'B' or higher, an overall GPA of 3.0 or higher, and consent of the instructor. Contract required. Course may be repeated for credit.

title: Toxicology Lab
description: Lab component of ENSC 4300 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials.

title: Geographic Information Systems Lab
description: Lab component of ENSC 4500 - 3 lab hours.

title: Geographic Information Systems
description: Study of spatial database design and spatial data analysis with Geographical Information Systems (GIS) as applied to the natural sciences - 3 lecture hours.

title: Advanced Environmental Science
description: Investigation of advanced ecological principles involved in human relationship to and interaction with the environment. Emphasis is given to interdisciplinary aspects involved in the solution of environmental problems - 3 lecture hours.

title: Internship: Environmental Sciences
description: This course provides opportunities for experiencing the practical and experimental application of environmental principles. Students will be involved via volunteer or paid work experience in environmental studies research projects conducted by agencies and institutions outside the University, for example state parks, zoos, private or public chemical industries, and universities. Course may be taken for 1-3 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Prerequisite: consent of the Internship Faculty Advisor. Contract required. Course may be repeated for credit.

title: Independent Study: Environmental Sciences
description: Directed reading on specialized topics or development of a set of special laboratory skills. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Prerequisite: consent of the Program Chair. Contract required. Course may be repeated for credit. ENSC 2999 also available for lower division credit.

title: Introduction to Public Health
description: This course applies a multidisciplinary approach to understanding the global to local outcomes, determinants, and solutions to population health issues that transcend national boundaries. The core tenants of global public health are examined using a hands-on approach, including insights into the fields of epidemiology, health behavior, education, and promotion, environmental health, biostatistics, and health policy. This course also includes a survey of the health professions through a series of guest speakers, including epidemiologists, community health workers, researchers, physicians, policy makers, physician assistants, and others. Professional development activities will include exploring professional interests, developing informational interview skills, and presentation skills - 4 lecture hours. This course is open to students from all disciplines.

title: Applied Biostatistics
description: Statistics are everywhere, in the media, the sciences, and in life, but beyond the theory lies the application of these tools. This course offers a practical, hands-on approach to working with open-sourced data, selecting appropriate statistical tests, data interpretation and visualization. Applied statistical models will range from basic descriptive statistics to more advanced multivariate regression analyses, calculated using statistical software packages. Prerequisite for students in the major: PSY 3187 or consent of instructor - 4 lecture hours.

title: Birds & the Environment
description: This course is an introduction to the study of birds, emphasizing the roles birds play in ecosystems and how we have come to understand these roles. Birds as early indicators of ecological disasters such as climate change will be stressed in the course. Weekly field trips to nearby locations will help students recognize and identify local bird species. Students will also conduct field observations on these field trips to help them appreciate the collection and analysis of field data and how these data can be used to appreciate the dynamics of bird populations - 3 lecture hours. ENSC 4100 requires approval by Department Chair. ENSC 4100 / ENSC 4105 meet the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirements.

title: Principles of Global Health
description: This course examines public health infectious, chronic and emerging diseases and conditions that transcend national borders. A multidisciplinary approach is applied. Included are topics on demography, data measurement, water access and sanitation, maternal and child health and much more. This course is open to students from other majors - 4 lecture hours.

title: Health Education and Promotion
description: This course covers theoretical and practical perspectives of health education and promotion interventions including the role of health behavior theories, principles of health communication, and the role of public health professionals - 4 lecture hours. This course is open to students from other disciplines.

title: Determinants of Health
description: This course covers the basic underlying biological processes of infectious and chronic diseases; individual risk and susceptibility to disease; the social, behavioral, and environmental aspects of diseases, and the changing patterns of human diseases globally. An overarching theme of the course will be how social factors that adversely affect health are equitably distributed, contributing to marked health disparities. Mixed teaching methods will be used, such as small and large group discussions, student presentations, and lectures - 4 lecture hours. Prerequisite: GPH 1000 , or instructor consent.

title: Epidemiology Lab
description: Lab component for GPH 3340 - 3 lab hours. This course is offered in both fall and spring semesters.

title: Directed Research: Environmental Sciences
description: Experimental research project in various fields of ecology and environmental sciences, carried out by students under guidance of an instructor. This involves literature searching, developing research projects, performing research, collection and analyzing of data, oral and poster presentation at a peer forum, and a written submission of the work in a thesis format. This course has an additional $30 Fee to cover lab materials. Course may be taken for 1-6 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. Contract required.

title: Global Environmental Health
description: This interdisciplinary course will be an exploration of the various ways that the natural, built, and social environments coalesce to impact health outcomes at local, regional, and global levels. Example topics include climate change, emerging infectious diseases, natural and man-made disasters, air quality, and food security. A special emphasis will be placed on exploring these through the application of Geographic Information Systems (GIS) and spatial analytical tools. This course is open to students from other disciplines. Prerequisites in the major: GPH 1000 or instructor consent - 4 lecture hours.

title: Epidemiology
description: This course is an overview of population-based public health including epidemiological concepts, descriptive epidemiology, and measurements (statistics) of morbidity and mortality. The major analytic study designs will be explored, demonstrated, and critiqued in the public health and medical literature - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: PSY 3187 or equivalent, or consent of instructor. This course is offered in both fall and spring semesters.

title: Professional Development Seminar
description: This course will develop studentsâ professional competencies by guiding resume writing, practicing interview skills, articulating career goals, and analyzing workplace dynamics. Attention will be paid to modeling interpersonal skills, self-reflection, and professionalism - 1 lecture hour.

title: Preparation for a Public Health Career
description: Students will focus on professional development in preparation for a career in global public health, to include managing their digital identities, polishing professional competencies, mapping academic skills acquired to career skills including statistical analysis, program evaluation, and advocacy skills, exploring available career opportunities and graduate programs within the field, and pursuing, applying, and positioning themselves as ideal candidates - 1 lecture hour. Recommended for students of senior standing.

title: Planetary Health Perspectives from Costa Rica
description: This course examines the linkages between human health and the natural environment. Emerging health issues related to how humans have disrupted earthâs natural systems and how these changes are impacting the global burden of disease are covered. Topics explored during the course will include climate change, emerging infectious diseases, natural and manmade disasters, air quality, and food security. Students learn through real-life examples and field site visits in Costa Rica, a country hosting 5% of global biodiversity and a wide range of ecosystems.

title: Global Health Issues in Violence and Social Justice
description: There has been an explosion of academic, policy, and media interest in international issues of violence and social justice. Applying a public health approach to exploring the effects of violence on the health and well-being of individuals and populations globally, this course will also emphasize the interdisciplinary nature of violence prevention. Topics reviewed may include war and other political violence; interpersonal violence such as gang violence, intimate partner violence, gender-based violence and child abuse and neglect; indigenous peoplesâ and civil rights - 4 lecture hours. Course is open to students from other majors.

title: Women's Health
description: This course is an introduction to issues in womenâs health and wellness that will investigate patterns of disease, social and policy implications, and public health interventions and approaches. The course will utilize an equity lens with attention to intersectional identities as it explores health topics such as: reproductive and sexual health, maternal health, mental health, body image, diseases that disproportionately impact women, violence, barriers in the healthcare system, population, climate change and global health issues. This course will consider the experiences of both cisgender and transgender women, and gender expansive peoples assigned female at birth - 4 lecture hours.

title: Senior Capstone I
description: This course is the culminating experience in the major. Students pursue research questions of their choosing in the form of an applied research project that demonstrates a synthesis of concepts, methods, and experiences gained in the program. The course meets once per week for three hours. The additional unit assigned to this course is comprised of individual meetings to address project-specific student needs throughout both semesters. Course limited to students of senior standing. Prerequisite: Satisfactory completion of GPH 3350 and GPH 3340 / GPH 3345 .

title: Comparative Health Policy
description: This course examines the structure and roles of the health care and public health systems in the U.S. and globally. Health care policy in the U.S. will be compared to other developed and developing countries.The U.S. Constitutional authority for public health actions such as quarantine are discussed, as well as the current politics impacting health policy and the role of advocacy organizations in health policy and systems change. Course is open to students from other majors - 4 lecture hours.

title: Evidence-Based Program Planning and Evaluation
description: Examination of program planning and evaluation in public health. This course will cover all stages of the program planning process including needs assessment, utilizing research to inform program theory and design, intervention development, and evaluation. Students will apply learning by constructing their own program plan and brief evaluation - 4 lecture hours. Prerequisite: GPH 3300

title: Research Assistant: Global Public Health
description: Research Assistant opportunities in global public health partner a student with new or ongoing faculty research projects with approval from the Department Chair, the instructor concerned and the Dean of the School. Students become involved in either literature review of the peer-reviewed literature, data collection, analysis, presentation or other aspects of faculty scholarly research. Prerequisite: consent of the Department Chair. Contract required. Course may be taken for 1-4 units.

title: Senior Capstone II
description: This course is the culminating experience in the major. Students pursue research questions of their choosing in the form of an applied research project that demonstrates a synthesis of concepts, methods, and experiences gained in the program. The course meets once per week for three hours. The additional unit assigned to this course is comprised of individual meetings to address project-specific student needs throughout both semesters. Course limited to students of senior standing. Prerequisite: GPH 4360 .

title: Health Research Methods
description: A hands on application of study design theory to health research methodologies and the role of evidence-based data collection and interpretation. Includes quantitative and qualitative designs and methods, such as interviews, focus groups, observations, systematic reviews and meta-analyses, pre/post tests, cross-sectional investigations, and randomized controlled trials. The course explores the peer reviewed literature in order to prepare students for their senior capstone experience - 4 lecture hours. Prerequisite: BIO 3340 and BIO 3345.

title: Global Health in Film
description: The course includes films and documentaries that discuss topics critical to global health study, including but not limited to the AIDS epidemic, gun violence, mental health, poverty, environmental change, world hunger, the tobacco and pharmaceutical industries, struggles in power, structural violence, and imbalances in equity. Personal reflections, communal discussions, and selected readings on the above topics will supplement the films - 4 lecture hours.

title: Internship: Global Public Health
description: Students become knowledgeable about a public health provider and workplace dynamics. Internship opportunities include: county public health departments, local human service agencies, healthcare organizations, non-profits, and various community sites. Course may be taken for 1-3 units, 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Consent of the Global Public Health Internship Coordinator or Program Chair/Director. Contract required.

title: Independent Study: Global Public Health
description: Directed reading/study on specialized topics. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. Contract required.

title: Emerging Challenges in Global Public Health
description: This course covers the latest developments in the field of global public health. Designed to prepare students to enter an ever-changing world, the latest global to local health issues will be covered, including emerging infections, latest controversies (such as the opioid crisis), upcoming areas and current trends in research, vulnerable populations, future directions of the field, and coverage of global public health in the news - 4 lecture hours.

title: HY: Healthcare Economics: Markets and Delivery Systems
description: Covers applying the tools of economic analysis to the problems of healthcare finance and delivery. It provides a comprehensive review of the American healthcare systems and their counter parts in other countries. This course will review and critically examine the available economic tools, and their strengths and weaknesses in examining health care systems and their delivery in the US and internationally. Cross national perspectives will be compared and analyzed.

title: HY: Healthcare Policies, Laws, & Regulation
description: Introduces the legal foundations of healthcare delivery in America. The course covers a broad range of legal issues in health care including autonomy, privacy, liberty and proprietary interests. Topics include laws related to physical and biological interventions to control infectious diseases, regulation of medical services, medical professionals and standards of care. Students will assess current healthcare policies and legislation, case laws and statutory laws, and governmental regulations applicable to the healthcare industry in the US and internationally.

title: HY: Healthcare Analytics & Continuous Quality Improvement
description: Covers fundamentals of healthcare analytics and strategies to drive change and improvement through data driven improvements in healthcare. In the healthcare industry, using and leveraging analytics through continuous quality improvement activities improves healthcare data accuracy, patient care, and financial performance. It provides the framework for prescriptive analytics that are available at the point of care to improve patient specific outcomes based upon population outcomes. This course will demonstrate the application of CQI and its achievement of improved quality of care while lowering costs and enhancing clinician and patient satisfaction.

title: Cultural Anthropology
description: The study of human culture and social organization from a cross-cultural perspective. Topics include kinship, religion and belief systems, cultural transmission, power and social control, racism, the environment, and social change. This course includes a focus on ethnography, the primary methodology of anthropological research, and explores the use of applied anthropology to advance civic knowledge and promote community engagement. Community-engagement may occur during scheduled class meetings or may be integrated into the overall homework expectation and course load. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course. Meets nursing requirements.

title: Sociology
description: Overview of the principles and basic concepts of sociology as a social science. Topics that will be examined include the study of groups, collective behavior, socialization, culture, social institutions, class and caste systems, stratification, community, social roles, social planning, and social control. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: Politics of the Body in Motion
description: This course is an introduction to the field of performance studies, in which we discuss how bodiesâboth their kinesthetics and aestheticsâare read and politicized. Select topics in dance history intersect with social science and cultural anthropology as we study issues of power, visibility, and representation both on and off-stage. We pay particular attention to how dance as a form of embodied knowledge reflects and creates cultural values and identity, honoring movement both as a mirror to the culture that created it, and as an instigator of cultural change.

title: Adulting
description: This course focuses on developing the key skills students will need as they approach graduation and begin to navigate a series of critical life decisions. In order to succeed in the world beyond the classroom, students need access to a basic body of knowledge in four key personal arenas: finance, health, career, and relationships. In a lab-type structure emphasizing hands-on, in-class activities and coaching, this course focuses on translating classroom skills into life and workplace; developing awareness of oneâs own strengths and aptitudes; as well as practical information and strategies for approaching major life issues. Recommended for seniors.

title: Humanities of Science
description: A critical examination of the humanity behind the scientific quest, this course focuses on the writings and personal narratives of some of the major and lesser known figures in the Western scientific tradition and includes historical accounts of their contributions to society. As we read these works, we will pay close attention to the yearnings, passions, strengths, foibles, doubts, struggles, faith, and artistic endeavors of these men and women. With these exemplars as guides, students also will examine and reflect upon their own personal quest for knowledge and discovery.

title: Health Humanities Seminar
description: The Health Humanities Seminar is an integrative course in which students contemplate the roles that science, arts, and humanities play in the process of illness and healing. Looking at illness and healing via a narrative lens is the focus of this course; memoir and other non-fiction narratives spotlight mental and physical illness from the perspective of the doctor and/or the patient's lived experience.

title: Foundations: American Pluralism
description: An interdisciplinary examination of the multicultural fabric of American society and the ideas that shape our nation. While honing essential skills for academic success, this course critically examines the central socio-cultural issues of American Cultural Pluralism through close reading of historical documents, essays, narratives, speeches, and poems. Student research, textual analysis, essay writing, journaling, oral presentations and in class discussions sharpen critical thinking skills and foster reflection upon the role an educated individual plays in our society. This course reinforces Dominicanâs liberal arts tradition and four educational ideals of study, reflection, community, and service. Prerequisite or Corequisite is ENGL 1003. (Adult Degree Completion Program only).

title: Adulting: Part II
description: Adulting: Part II is the 0.5-unit course that includes the content from the second half of the 1-unit Adulting course. This course focuses on developing the key skills students will need as they approach graduation and begin to navigate a series of critical life decisions. In order to succeed in the world beyond the classroom, students need access to a basic body of knowledge in four key personal arenas: finance, health, career, and relationships. In a lab-type structure emphasizing hands-on, in-class activities and coaching, this course focuses on translating classroom skills into life and workplace; developing awareness of oneâs own strengths and aptitudes; as well as practical information and strategies for approaching major life issues. Recommended for seniors.

title: Adulting: Part I
description: A unique course that supports your path into adulthood, guides you through coping with the changes in our current lives and teaches concrete adulting skills to help you navigate these unusual times. We will touch on the four critical components of adulting: work/managing money, play/hobbies, love/relationships and physical/mental health in a time of social distancing. How can you approach finding a job or internship in this current job climate? How are you managing money in a time of quickly changing financial situations? What are ways to manage physical and mental health? You are encouraged to complete the second .5 unit in the fall semester to continue to build on these skills and design the post-college life that you want. This course uses the platforms Moodle and Zoom and is mostly a synchronous course. Students need reliable internet access. For transfer or returning students only.

title: Capstone 1: Thesis
description: The first semester of a two-semester course to produce the Senior Thesis. Normally enrolling two semesters before graduating, students choose a subject and literary works, develop a thesis idea, working bibliography of primary and secondary sources, and a provisional outline of the Senior Thesis.

title: Capstone 2: Project
description: The second semester of a two-semester course to produce the Senior Project. Students complete their draft of the Senior Project, submit the completed Portfolio, and present it orally to the University community. Pre-requisite: HCS 4203 .

title: Technology and Equity
description: This course will investigate the relevance of gender, race and sexuality to understanding equity in technology and the development of new technologies. We will consider a range of questions including: What are some barriers in equity in Computer Science education? And how does an organization promote or impede inequality? In addition, this course will utilize methodologies which promote engagement with local communities to enhance civic knowledge and improve student civic skills.

title: HY: Healthcare Innovation and Informatics
description: Discusses leadership in healthcare innovations and the key drivers for transforming healthcare delivery through the creation and integration of healthcare informatics. This course provides an in-depth investigation of the applications of technology, and the essential elements and current trends in the delivery of patient-centered care demonstrating the refinement of care through applications of health informatics and health information management.

title: Teaching Assistant: Global Public Health
description: The course provides teaching experience at the undergraduate level. Students will assist faculty members in preparing and delivering course content and assessing student performance by, for example, providing feedback on assignments such as rough drafts or laboratory assignments. All tasks will be performed under the direct and close supervision of faculty members. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. Prerequisites: Consent of the instructor. Contract required.

title: Humanities Seminar II
description: Through careful analysis and interpretation of traditional and contemporary works in translation from around the world, we consider global responses to fundamental questions about the human condition and the quest for meaning. We also consider the ways our worldview expands through engagement with translated works to include the experience of those whose worldviews are unlike our own. A variety of texts (written, musical, visual, etc.) and genres are explored in this discussion-based interdisciplinary seminar.

title: Explorations in Equity
description: Explorations in Equity examines principles of equity and diversity and their implementations in schools and institutional settings. Students examine their own beliefs, attitudes, biases, and expectations about equity, so that they are better prepared for their future careers. With the goal of developing comfort with difference through heightened self-awareness, students participate in community engagement projects, integrating course content through cultural immersion.

title: Capstone 2: Thesis
description: The second semester of a two-semester course to produce the Senior Thesis. Students draft the Senior Thesis, submit the completed paper, and present it orally to the University community. Pre-requisite: HCS 4201 .

title: Capstone 1: Project
description: The first semester of a two-semester course to produce the Senior Project. Normally enrolling two semesters before graduating, students develop a portfolio of creative writing in a given genre or genres (e.g., drama, fiction, film, nonfiction prose, poetry) together with an introductory essay discussing their project.

title: Humanities Seminar I
description: An interdisciplinary course closely examining the ways self-knowledge, democracy, equity, sustainability, and/or the natural world are conceptualized and addressed in classic and contemporary literary, philosophical, musical, and artistic works. While works from other countries may be addressed, the course primarily covers culturally diverse American perspectives. A variety of texts and genres are explored in this discussion-based seminar.

title: Politics of the Body in Motion
description: This course is an introduction to the field of performance studies, in which we discuss how bodiesâboth their kinesthetics and aestheticsâare read and politicized. Select topics in dance history intersect with social science and cultural anthropology as we study issues of power, visibility, and representation both on and off-stage. We pay particular attention to how dance as a form of embodied knowledge reflects and creates cultural values and identity, honoring movement both as a mirror to the culture that created it, and as an instigator of cultural change.

title: Interdisciplinary Thinking
description: This course provides a foundation in the history and construction of academic disciplines within the humanities and practice in the principles of interdisciplinary thinking. Students will analyze visual and written texts using various disciplinary approaches and then integrate knowledge and methods from these approaches in a culminating project.

title: Humanities Seminar I
description: A Western Great Books course exploring the seminal texts of Western culture and examining the fundamental questions of what it means to be human. The book list may vary from semester to semester, but will likely include Genesis, Mark, or John and works by such authors as Plato, Luther, Darwin, Thoreau, Whitman, Shakespeare, Woolf, DuBois, Freud, Marx and Engels. Music and art are also analyzed for their contribution to and reflection of Western culture. Required of all Humanities and Cultural Studies majors; open to non-majors.

title: Bugs and Mickey Go to War: WWII Censorship and Propaganda
description: World War II is well-known for technological innovation, fascism, global diplomacy, and military conflicts. However, it also serves as an important historical example for censorship and propaganda. Cartoon characters, including those from Disney, Looney Tunes, and even Dr. Suess, were at the center in this effort. This course carefully studies censorship and propaganda across the globe during the war and asks students to critically analyze the propaganda pieces within their historical context. This course uses the platforms Moodle and Zoom, and is a blend of synchronous and asynchronous learning. Students need reliable internet access. Recommended for new and returning students.

title: The Making of Today: Contemporary World History
description: This course strives to create a foundational understanding of the historical conditions under which the contemporary world came into being, with an emphasis on the recent past. The course is designed to provide context for interpreting global news and promote broader perspectives on world history. The course pays particular attention to political, social, cultural, and economic themes that link multiple regions together. In addition, this course will utilize methodologies which promote engagement with local and/or global communities to enhance civic knowledge and/or improve student civic skills. Meets nursing requirements.

title: East Asia: Past and Present
description: A survey of the origins of modern East Asia, with particular emphasis on the political, social, cultural, and economics of China, Japan, and Korea. This course will utilize methodologies which promote engagement with local and/or global communities to enhance civic knowledge and/or improve student civic skills relevant to East Asia. While a chronology is utilized to frame the course, we will also study the region thematically to highlight linkages among East Asian societies and the Western world. In this way, the course is designed to create a foundational understanding of East Asia, both historically and in present-times.

title: Interdisciplinary Thinking
description: This course provides a foundation in the history and construction of academic disciplines within the humanities and practice in the principles of interdisciplinary thinking. Students will analyze visual and written texts using various disciplinary approaches and then integrate knowledge and methods from these approaches in a culminating project.

title: Europe: Past and Present
description: This course examines the most important events, people, institutions, and developments in Europe from the late 18th through the 20th century. Looks at the challenges and the continuities in European history. Engages with such crucial events as the French Revolution and the two World Wars and examines the relationship between Europe and the rest of the world over the course of the modern era. Meets nursing requirment.

title: Latin America: Past and Present
description: A survey of the origins and development of Latin America designed to introduce students to major ideas and events from Latin Americaâs past, including an understanding of its earliest indigenous civilizations to the present day, while synchronously connecting how these forces have shaped the people, politics, economics, and culture in these places today. This course will utilize methodologies which promote engagement with local and/or global communities to enhance civic knowledge and/or improve student civic skills relevant to Latin America. Since Latin America is often overlooked or misrepresented in the U.S. media, this course seeks to provide a foundational contextualization and promote understanding of the region and its people. Meets nursing requirements.

title: Africa: Past and Present
description: A survey of the origins and development of early modern and modern Africa designed to create a foundational understanding of contemporary Africaâs history, politics, economics, and culture. In addition, this course will utilize methodologies which promote engagement with local and/or global communities to enhance civic knowledge and/or improve student civic skills relevant to Africa. Much of what is reported about Africa in todayâs media is rooted in western stereotypes and reveals a dearth in contextual understanding. In order to better understand this continent, three general time periods will be emphasized: pre-European contact, the colonial period, and the post-colonial (or nationalization) period. Key themes include, but are not limited to African political and environmental history, the impact of Islam and Christianity, trade, and the impact of the slave trade.

title: Internship/Career Exploration: Humanities & Cultural Studies
description: Students gain practical, hands-on training in their particular area of career or volunteer interest by working with an organization of their choosing, in consultation with their academic advisor and the Career and Internship office. After meeting with the academic advisor, students meet with the Career and Internship office to fill out the necessary paperwork and, then, after securing all necessary signatures, submit an add/drop form along with the internship paperwork to the Registrar. This course cannot be accessed via the Self-Service registration cart. 1-3 Units.

title: Latin American Survey
description: Covers the colonial history of Central and South America beginning with pre-colonization and up to the present. Examines independence movements, the formation of the 19th Century nations, and contemporary economic, political, and cultural crises of Latin America.

title: Independent Study
description: The privilege of independent study is possible for students who are prepared to undertake it based on student need or interest with approval of the Chair of the department, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. The study must be in an area not covered by a regularly scheduled course. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. The maximum number of units that can be earned in one semester by independent study is 4.

title: 20th Century Sports as Political and Social Discourse
description: Sports in the 20th century have generated some of the most politically charged moments in world history. Issues of race, gender, and ethnicity; foreign policy intrigue, economics, and even direct political statements from rival nations have made modern sport a critical component of world discourse.

title: Special Topics in Women's History
description: This course provides an overview of womenâs experiences in different historical periods and in various geographical locations; examines the roles assigned to women and at the selected experiences of women; and studies the writings about and by women. The course considers the lives of various historical women who contributed to the disciplines of art, literature, philosophy, politics, and society; the geographical focus will vary. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course. (Research Designated) Meets nursing requirements.

title: History Lab: U.S. History from 1890s to Present
description: This course examines U.S. history from the late 19th century up through the present-day. This includes the U.S. involvement in the Spanish-American War, World War I, World War II, and the Cold War; but also includes social, cultural and economic features such as the Progressive Movement, the Great Depression, the Harlem Renaissance, the Civil Rights Movement, and the Rise of Conservatism. since the contested nature of the historical narrative intersects with issues of race, class and gender in American society. This course includes a community-based learning project.

title: Research Methods in Social Science
description: The study of social science requires attention to development of advanced research, critical thinking, argumentation, and writing skills. This course will challenge the student to reflect upon and utilize various research methods, writing formats, and styles of argumentation and critique within the context of the various sub-fields of the study of social science.

title: History Lab: U.S. History to the 1890s
description: This course explores United States history of the pre-Columbian and colonial eras; the American Republic before and after the Civil War; and the impact of the Industrial Revolution on United States society throughout the 19th century to the dawn of the 20th century, with special attention to the contested nature of the historical narrative when it intersects with issues of race, class and gender. This course includes a community-based learning project; meets nursing requirements.

title: California
description: Looks at the indigenous, colonial, and modern history of the thirty-first state, with special attention to the history of San Francisco and Los Angeles as cultural, economic, and political centers of the state. Meets nursing requirements.

title: The Globe Part 2: Imperialism, Colonization, and Post-Colonialism
description: A study of the history, cultural geography, and physical geography of the worldâs empires and colonies, with special emphasis on 1500 to the present-day. This course discusses political, economic, and social developments throughout the past five centuries emphasizing patterns of authority, the individual, nature, society, and technology as they relate to the historical principles of "Empire," "Colony," and "Post-Colonial". This course will also explain the geographic diversity of natural landscapes and human societies and the relationship between culture and the physical environment. This course is linked to HIST 3002 : The Globe Part 1.

title: Biographical Approach to History: Eleanor Roosevelt
description: Focuses on Eleanor Roosevelt's background prior to her husband, Franklin Roosevelt, becoming president in 1933, and then on her public role thereafter as First Lady as an advocate for women, civil rights, education, and other causes.

title: History and Politics in Film
description: This course examines the relationship between politics and film along with the influence films have on culture, ideas, beliefs, and perceptions of history, politics and the political system. Does film hide the multiple interpretations of historical events and the political process? Does the need to present a story visually impact how people consume history and politics? Has film created a taste for history as entertainment or sensation and distorted accuracy and complexity? Students will critically review and analyze films and explore the way images of political theories and concepts are depicted. The course will have alternating themes exploring historical events, power, justice, ideology, the nature of political leadership, theories of decision making, electoral politics, domestic and international political institutions and political processes.

title: The Globe Part 1: Ancient to Early Modern Times
description: This course explores how early human cultures of ancient foraging societies were transformed into complex agrarian civilizations, primarily in Afro-Eurasia and the Americas, from 3500 BCE â 1500 CE, eventually emerging as early modern civilizations before the Industrial Revolution of the mid-18 th century. This course will incorporate the tools and perspectives of geographic study in order to stress how world history includes the study of the geographic diversity between natural landscapes and physical environments with various world cultures and human societies. This course will stress the California state history standards for Education and History Majors and is linked to HIST 3003 : The Globe Part 2.

title: History and Politics of the Middle East
description: This course introduces students to contemporary politics in the Middle East. The course is designed to answer the following questions: What explains the dynamics of this region known as the Middle East? Why is political development so difficult to achieve in the Middle East? What are the factors contributing to the various conflicts in the region? What are the future prospects for peace in the Middle East? In order to answer these questions, students review the relevant social science theories and methodologies. First part of the course examines the role of religion, nationalism and the different political systems as well as governments found in the region to better understand the 21st Century Middle East. The second part covers the various conflicts, the Gulf Region, and U.S. involvement in the Middle East to assess the possibility for long-lasting democracy, prosperity, and peace. The course culminates with a research project to be presented at an undergraduate academic conference. (International Politics and Policy Designated, Research Designated)

title: Big History Lens on the Evolution of Human Cultures
description: "Big History" takes an immense voyage through time: from the first moments of the Universe fourteen billion years ago with the birth of stars and galaxies to the emergence of our Solar System some nine billion years later. Using a biocultural approach, the course will first examine how life biologically evolved over billions of year as living organisms successfully extracted energy from Earthâs rich environmental resources. The course will then focus on the emergence of human beings and their cultural ability to extract energy by adapting effective food-getting patterns: first in hunter-gatherer cultures, to agrarian multicultural civilizations, and to todayâs complex industrial civilizations. The students will also work with local community organizations on a project. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course. Meets nursing requirements.

title: Disease and Disasters in World History
description: This course considers disease and disasters as historical artifacts. How did people explain and cope with inexplicable disasters? How did disease (e.g., cholera, small pox, syphilis, HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis) ravage societies and how did measures in response both aid and oppress people, oftentimes women, the poor, the elderly, and other marginalized people. We learn how diagnoses and "cures" are shaped by cultural norms.

title: History through Biography
description: This course examines the life and times of selected historical figures in history. Through consideration of the social, political, and cultural issues of the era students assess the importance of the figure by evaluating the consequences of achievements and defeats of the historical figures under study. Readings draw upon primary and secondary sources.

title: Europe: Past and Present
description: This course examines the most important events, people, institutions, and developments in Europe from the late 18th through the 20th century. Looks at the challenges and the continuities in European history. Engages with such crucial events as the French Revolution and the two World Wars and examines the relationship between Europe and the rest of the world over the course of the modern era.

title: Latin America: Past and Present
description: A survey of the origins and development of Latin America designed to introduce students to major ideas and events from Latin Americaâs past, including an understanding of its earliest indigenous civilizations to the present day, while synchronously connecting how these forces have shaped the people, politics, economics, and culture in these places today. This course will utilize methodologies which promote engagement with local and/or global communities to enhance civic knowledge and/or improve student civic skills relevant to Latin America. Since Latin America is often overlooked or misrepresented in the U.S. media, this course seeks to provide a foundational contextualization and promote understanding of the region and its people.

title: Ethnicity and Race in United States History
description: This course explores 500 years of American history by examining the stories and contributions of non-Anglo and non-European migrations, including but not limited to the perspective Native Americans of pre Columbian times; from the perspective of African-Americans from the 17th â 20th centuries; from the perspective of Asian peoples such as the Chinese-Americans and Japanese Americans of the 19th â 20th century migrations; and how this reflects the cultural diversity of the United States today. Meets nursing requirements. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: East Asian Survey
description: In this survey of East Asian societies, we will focus on the cultural, social, and political history of China, Japan, Taiwan, Vietnam, and Korea. While a chronology is utilized to frame the course, we will also study the region thematically to highlight the interconnections and interactions between and among East Asian societies and the Western world. These themes include imperialism and colonization, ethnicity, rural vs. urban, materialism, war, rebellions, and family structure.

title: Africa: Past and Present
description: A survey of the origins and development of early modern and modern Africa designed to create a foundational understanding of contemporary Africaâs history, politics, economics, and culture. In addition, this course will utilize methodologies which promote engagement with local and/or global communities to enhance civic knowledge and/or improve student civic skills relevant to Africa. Much of what is reported about Africa in todayâs media is rooted in western stereotypes and reveals a dearth in contextual understanding. In order to better understand this continent, three general time periods will be emphasized: pre-European contact, the colonial period, and the post-colonial (or nationalization) period. Key themes include, but are not limited to African political and environmental history, the impact of Islam and Christianity, trade, and the impact of the slave trade.

title: Latin American Survey
description: Covers the colonial history of Central and South America beginning with pre-colonization and up to the present. Examines independence movements, the formation of the 19th Century nations, and contemporary economic, political, and cultural crises of Latin America.

title: 20th Century Sports as Political and Social Discourse
description: Sports in the 20th century have generated some of the most politically charged moments in world history. Issues of race, gender, and ethnicity; foreign policy intrigue, economics, and even direct political statements from rival nations have made modern sport a critical component of world discourse. (Research Designated)

title: War and Remembrance
description: This course focuses on the interaction of war and people, their societies, literature, art, technologies, thought, and gender to consider how war transforms societies and people. How did people respond to visions of victory and the traumatic consequences? How did the rebuild their worlds. How did the memory of the war contribute to that new world? How was the war commemorated in public space?

title: Video Games as History
description: Over the past few decades, video games have portrayed real-life historical events in a similar means of movies and radio-dramas before them. The representation of past events through video games offers unique opportunities and challenges; but, to be certain, their impact on youthâs perception of past historical events is only growing. This course seeks to understand the historical representations in various video games, the positive and negative elements founds in its historical representation, andâsimilarly as with filmâoutline theories of how history fictionalization is realized in video games.

title: Biographical Approach to History: Women in the Wild West
description: Explores the lives of trailblazing women who made their mark in the American West.
