description: Lab component for NURS 2002 . Provides opportunities for the student to learn, practice, and demonstrate basic nursing skills in a simulated clinical setting - 3 lab hours. Successful completion of both the theory and lab components of this course with a letter grade of 'C+' (2.3) or higher is necessary to pass the course.

title: Internship: Nursing
description: Internship with a focus on the nursing profession. Course may be taken for 1-3 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Prerequisite: consent of the Program Chair. Contract required.

title: Independent Study: Nursing
description: Directed study of an area of interest in the field of nursing or health care under the supervision of a selected nursing faculty member. Course may be taken for 1-3 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. NURS 2999 may be taken for lower division credit. Contract required.

title: Teaching Assistant: Nursing
description: Opportunity to assist Nursing faculty in teaching of NURS classes. Course may be taken for 1-3 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. Prerequisites: consent of the instructor. Contract required.

title: Directed Research: Nursing
description: Individual guidance in implementation and presentation of the Senior Project in nursing. A letter grade of âC+â (2.3) or higher is necessary to pass the course. Prerequisites: Successful completion of NURS 4500 and eligible for senior 2 level standing in the Nursing program. Prerequisite; NURS 4500 .

title: Kaiser Workstudy/Nursing Student Internship
description: This course provides senior nursing students with an opportunity to practice previously acquired nursing theory and skills under the guidance of an RN preceptor. The student becomes an employee of Kaiser Permanent while working in the role of a nursing student. Students earn a Kaiser paycheck (comparable to that of a nursing assistant) while learning in a supervised environment. Tuition for the 1-unit course is paid for by Kaiser. Instructor approval is necessary before registering into course. Schedule is TBA.

title: Health Assessment Across the Lifespan Lab
description: Lab component for NURS 2014 . Provides opportunities for the student to learn, practice, and demonstrate health history interviews and physical assessments of patients across the lifespan - 3 lab hours. Successful completion of both the theory and lab components of this course with a letter grade of 'C+' (2.3) or higher is necessary to pass the course. This course has an additional $750 Fee to cover clinical expenses.

title: Nursing Research and Senior Thesis
description: This course provides an introduction to nursing research and its role in the study and solution of nursing problems, the improvement of health care, and the development of skills in the analysis/critique of published research, and application to practice. Students will develop a project relevant to a clinical nursing problem or practice issue - 4 theory hours. A letter grade of ‘C+’ (2.3) or higher is necessary to pass the course. Prerequisites: Successful completion of the previous semester’s Nursing course work with a ‘C+’ (2.3) or higher and successful completion of or concurrent enrollment in eligible for senior 1 level standing in the Nursing program.

title: Geriatric Foundations Lab
description: Clinical component for NURS 2150 - 90 clinical hours. Successful completion of both the theory and clinical components of the course with a letter grade of âC+â (2.3) or higher is necessary to pass the course. This course has an additional $750 Fee to cover clinical expenses.

title: Medical-Surgical Nursing I Lab
description: Clinical component for NURS 2050 - 90 clinical hours. Successful completion of both the theory and clinical components of the course with a letter grade of âC+â (2.3) or higher is necessary to pass the course.

title: Psychiatric and Mental Health Nursing Lab
description: Clinical component for NURS 3106 - 90 clinical hours. Successful completion of both the theory and clinical components of the course with a letter grade of âC+â (2.3) or higher is necessary to pass the course. This course has an additional $750 Fee to cover clinical expenses.

title: Maternal-Newborn and Women's Health Nursing Lab
description: Clinical component for NURS 3100 - 90 clinical hours. Successful completion of both the theory and clinical components of the course with a letter grade of âC+â (2.3) or higher is necessary to pass the course.

title: Medical-Surgical Nursing II Lab
description: Clinical component for NURS 3105 - 90 clinical hours. Successful completion of both the theory and clinical components of the course with a letter grade of âC+â (2.3) or higher is necessary to pass the course.

title: Palliative and End of Life Care
description: This course will present an overview of issues pertinent to end-of-life care. It explores cultural aspects of death and dying, palliative care in all settings, and grief management of those left behind. It is designed for any level of nursing student. NURS 2250 also available for lower division credit. The Friday evening class is online, the Saturday class is on campus.

title: Occupation and Others
description: Study of humans as occupational beings, using a lifespan developmental approach. Use of occupational narratives, film, video, observations, and interviews to develop a deeper understanding of occupational patterns, occupational choices, and the role of occupational balance in promoting health. Continued development of a student learning community for occupational therapy majors - 1 lecture hour. Open to all students.

title: Community Health and Nursing Lab
description: Clinical component for NURS 4150 - 90 clinical hours. Successful completion of both the theory and clinical components of the course with a letter grade of âC+â (2.3) or higher is necessary to pass the course. This course has an additional $750 Fee to cover clinical expenses.

title: Leadership and NursingCare Management Lab
description: Clinical component for NURS 4200 - 135 clinical hours total. Successful completion of both the theory and clinical components of the course with a letter grade of âC+â (2.3) or higher is necessary to pass the course. This course has an additional $750 Fee to cover clinical expenses.

title: Occupation and Community
description: An examination of social and cultural influences on occupation and on community as an essential element of human occupation. Use of field visits, community experiences, and various print and media sources to gain a deeper understanding of the social nature of occupation. Continued development of a student learning community for occupational therapy majors. Professional writing skills are also developed - 1 lecture hour. Open to all students.

title: Foundations of Occupational Therapy
description: An introduction to the knowledge, skills, and attitudes necessary to function as an occupational therapist. History, philosophy and critical theories of the profession along with concepts of occupation and occupational therapy, clinical reasoning, the Occupational Therapy Practice Framework, and official documents of the profession are studied - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: Acceptance into the accelerated MS in OT program. (Exam average policy in effect for this course.)

title: Occupation as Therapy
description: Provides an introduction to occupational therapy as a profession, including an introduction to key professional organizations and traditional and emerging practice settings. Continued development of a student learning community for occupational therapy majors. Professional writing skills, use of APA, and medical terminology are also covered - 1 lecture hour. Open to all students.

title: Occupations of Children and Adolescents I
description: Occupations of the child from infancy through childhood and adolescence. Developmental theories and typical child development are reviewed. Approaches to working with families and pediatric service delivery systems are introduced. Pediatric conditions treated by OT's in varied practice settings are studied - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 5012/OTL 5012 and OT 5014/OTL 5014. (Exam average policy in effect for this course.)

title: Culture, Diversity and Ethics in Occupational Therapy
description: An exploration of cultural and diversity issues in occupational therapy. Consideration of larger social and global issues related to democracy and equity’s influence on health and delivery of healthcare services. Designed to foster cultural competency and humility, social responsibility, and advocacy skills as critical competencies for occupational therapists - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 4012 / OTL 4012 or OT 6012 / OTL 6012 .

title: Human Movement Analysis
description: Study of kinesiology and biomechanics related to human motion and occupation, and principles of joint and muscle action related to activity, function, and dysfunction of the moving body. Study and application of principles of activity/task analysis and the relationship to functional performance - 2 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 3012 / OTL 3012 . (Exam average policy in effect for this course.)

title: Child and Family Nursing Lab
description: Clinical component for NURS 3101 - 90 clinical hours (including Simulation Rotations). Successful completion of both the theory and clinical components of the course with a letter grade of âC+â (2.3) or higher is necessary to pass the course. This course has an additional $750 Fee to cover clinical expenses.

title: Psychosocial Aspects of Occupation I
description: Study of psychosocial aspects of human occupation and disability. Includes review of DSM V; key theoretical perspectives, orientation to psychosocial practice settings and professional roles, and introduction to the occupational therapy intervention process for individuals with psychosocial dysfunction - 2 lecture hours. Prerequisite: Acceptance into the accelerated MS in OT program (Exam average policy in effect for this course.)

title: Medical-Surgical Nursing III Lab
description: Clinical component for NURS 4100 - 90 clinical hours. Successful completion of both the theory and clinical components of the course with a letter grade of âC+â (2.3) or higher is necessary to pass the course.

title: OT Fundamental Skills for Assessment and Intervention
description: Core assessments and basic transfer skills are taught and practiced. The focus is on basic skill development in preparation for fieldwork and entry level practice across lifespan - 1 lecture hour.

title: Professional Identity Seminar I
description: Prerequisite: Admission to MSOT Program

title: Psychosocial Aspects of Occupation II
description: Specialized concepts and skills for occupational therapy assessment and intervention with clients experiencing psychosocial dysfunction and/or serious mental illness. Study of established and emerging practice areas and future trends, including recovery principles and psychiatric rehabilitation. Includes an examination of psychosocial issues across practice areas - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: OT 3015 / OTL 3015 . (Exam average policy in effect for this course.)

title: Foundations of Occupational Therapy
description: This course provides an introduction to the profession of OT including its history, professional framework, regulatory entities and organizations, theories and practice models. Students will gain exposure to the OTPF and learn how to apply it within evidence-based and client-centered practice. The lab component of this course introduces students to skills and techniques used across practice settings including activity analysis, clinical documentation, and facilitation of occupation-focused groups - 3 lecture hours.

title: Professional Identity II: The Resilient Practitioner
description: Second in a five-semester series, this course will introduce students to the evolving construct of resilience and its role in supporting well-being as future occupational therapy practitioners. Students will gain understanding of challenges unique to occupational therapy practice and the transition from student to practitioner roles. The course will focus on acquiring and practicing evidence-based strategies to address these challenges and experience resilience and thriving in both academic and future clinical settings - 1 lecture hour. Prerequisites: OT 4015 / OTL 4015 or OT 6015 / OTL 6015 and OT 6151 .

title: Occupations of Adults and Seniors I
description: First in a three-semester series of courses focusing on occupational therapy practice with adults and seniors experiencing physical disabilities. Content covered includes orthopedics, spinal cord injury, cardiopulmonary disorders and low vision. Application of integrated theories and clinical reasoning for occupation-based intervention. Consideration of OT domain and process, client factors, and client performance skills and patterns as defined by the Occupational Therapy Practice Framework. Includes introduction to use of standardized and non-standardized functional assessments, intervention planning, and documentation specific to this population - 3 lecture hours. May be taught face-to-face or in a hybrid format. Prerequisites: OT 3012 / OTL 3012 and OT 3014 / OTL 3014 . (Exam average policy in effect for this course.)

title: Functional Neuroscience in Occupational Therapy
description: Study of the structure and function of the human central and peripheral nervous system. The course includes advanced clinical neuroanatomy and neurophysiology content in the context of understanding the effects of neurodevelopmental disorders, aging, trauma, and disease on the functions of the nervous system as related to occupational therapy practice - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: BIO 2500 / BIO 2505 and BIO 2600 / BIO 2605 .

title: Psychosocial Foundations of Occupational Therapy
description: First in a two-semester series focusing on the study of psychosocial aspects of human occupation. Focus on services provided to all individuals to enhance mental health promotion and prevention for all in a variety of contexts. Includes review of key theoretical perspectives, orientation to psychosocial frames of reference, and an introduction to creating a therapeutic relationship through active listening, motivational interviewing, and occupation centered practice. Development and application of interview, assessment, evaluation group facilitation, and goal setting within mental health promotion and prevention - 2 lecture hours.

title: Functional Neuroscience in Occupational Therapy
description: Study of the structure and function of the human central and peripheral nervous system. The course includes advanced clinical neuroanatomy and neurophysiology content in the context of understanding the effects of neurodevelopmental disorders, aging, trauma, and disease on the functions of the nervous system as related to occupational therapy practice - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: BIO 2500 / BIO 2505 and BIO 2600 / BIO 2605 .

title: Human Movement Analysis
description: Study of kinesiology and biomechanics related to human motion and occupation, and principles of joint and muscle action related to activity, function, and dysfunction of the moving body. Study and application of principles of activity/task analysis and the relationship to functional performance. Includes basic assessment skills following a biomechanical model - 2 lecture hours. Prerequisites: Concurrent enrollment in OT 4012 / OTL 4012 .

title: Occupations of Adults and Seniors I
description: First in a three-semester series of courses focusing on occupational therapy practice with adults and seniors experiencing physical disabilities. Content covered includes OT for medical and orthopedic conditions, and other OT-specialty practice areas such as industrial rehabilitation, low vision, hand therapy and driverâs rehabilitation. Application of integrated theories and clinical reasoning for occupation-based interventions. Consideration of OT domain and process, client factors, and client performance skills and patterns as defined by the Occupational Therapy Practice Framework. Includes an introduction to the use of standardized and non-standardized functional assessments, intervention planning, and documentation specific to this population - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 4011, OT 4012 / OTL 4012 , OT 4014 / OTL 4014 , and OT 4028 .

title: Culture, Diversity and Ethics in Occupational Therapy
description: An exploration of cultural and diversity issues in occupational therapy. Consideration of larger social and global issues related to democracy and equityâs influence on health and delivery of healthcare services. Designed to foster cultural competency and humility, social responsibility, and advocacy skills as critical competencies for occupational therapists - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 4012 / OTL 4012 or OT 6012 / OTL 6012 .

title: OT Fundamental Skills for Assessment and Intervention
description: Core assessments and basic transfer skills are taught and practiced. The focus is on basic skill development in preparation for fieldwork and entry level practice across lifespan - 1 lecture hour.

title: Occupations in Mental Health and Wellness
description: Second in a two-semester series, building on the foundational psychosocial skills gained in the first course. Application of occupation-centered assessments and intensive interventions in psychosocial settings. Includes advocacy, education, and group facilitation as key modes of OT intervention within psychosocial and behavioral health practice settings. Students apply psychosocial theories, assessments and interventions to meet the unique needs of individuals, groups, and populations experiencing psychosocial impacts on their occupations. Explores the historical, political and attitudinal influences on participation in occupation - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 4015 / OTL 4015 .

title: Professional Identity I: The Reflective Practitioner
description: This introductory course in a five-semester series will engage students in developing their professional identity through engagement in reflective practices to promote self-awareness and skills in occupational balance, self-care, and professional communication. This course will also provide students with an evidence-informed understanding of the behaviors that underlie success in both the academic program and professional practice. Students will build a professional identity development plan and engage in reflective journaling to document their growth - 1 lecture hour. Prerequisites: Admission in the OTD program.

title: Professional Identity I: The Reflective Practitioner
description: This introductory course in a five-semester series will engage students in developing their professional identity through engagement in reflective practices to promote self-awareness and skills in occupational balance, self-care, and professional communication. This course will also provide students with an evidence-informed understanding of the behaviors that underlie success in both the academic program and professional practice. Students will build a professional identity development plan and engage in reflective journaling to document their growth - 1 lecture hour. Prerequisites: Admission in the OTD program.

title: Professional Identity II: The Resilient Practitioner
description: Second in a five-semester series, this course will introduce students to the evolving construct of resilience and its role in supporting well-being as future occupational therapy practitioners. Students will gain understanding of challenges unique to occupational therapy practice and the transition from student to practitioner roles. The course will focus on acquiring and practicing evidence-based strategies to address these challenges and experience resilience and thriving in both academic and future clinical settings - 1 lecture hour. Prerequisites: OT 4015 / OTL 4015 or OT 6015 / OTL 6015 and OT 6151 .

title: Research in Occupational Therapy I: Evidence-Based OT Practitioner
description: Introduction to the research process and its application to evidence based practice. This course will guide students through the process of effectively interpreting and critiquing research evidence to inform practice decisions. Course content includes exploration of traditions, methods, ethics, outcome measures, and statistical analyses applicable to OT research. Students will apply knowledge and skills to conduct and disseminate the results of a critically appraised topic that addresses a clinical question posed by local practitioners - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: Acceptance into the accelerated MS in OT.

title: Teaching Assistant: Occupational Therapy
description: Opportunities to assist OT faculty in conducting research or teaching of OT classes. Course may be taken for 1-3 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. Prerequisites: must have previously completed course with âAâ (4.0) or âBâ (3.0) grade, and consent of the instructor. Contract required.

title: Occupations of Children and Adolescents I
description: Occupations of the child from infancy through childhood and adolescence. Developmental theories and typical child development are reviewed. Approaches to working with families and pediatric service delivery systems are introduced. Pediatric conditions treated by OT's in varied practice settings are studied - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 5012/OTL 5012 and OT 5014/OTL 5014. (Exam average policy in effect for this course.)

title: Independent Study: Occupational Therapy
description: Directed reading, research, and/or a project of special interest to a student. Course may be taken for 1-3 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. Contract required.

title: Culture and Ethics in Occupational Therapy
description: An exploration of cultural and ethical issues in occupational therapy, including the AOTA Code of Ethics - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: OT 5012/OTL 5012.

title: Professional Identity Seminar II
description: Prerequisite: Acceptance into the accelerated MS in OT program.

title: Educational Principles and Practices in Occupational Therapy
description: Introduction to teaching and learning theories used in occupational therapy practice and education. Practice developing educational interventions for clinical, community, and staff in-service teaching. Introduction to academic teaching methods and fieldwork supervision - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: OT 3012 / OTL 3012 .

title: Occupational Therapy Community Program Development
description: Introduction to theoretical and conceptual models supporting community-based occupational therapy practice. Development of skills needed to design, implement, fund, and evaluate innovative occupation-based programs in community settings focusing on wellness and health promotion - 3 lecture hours. Community service hours are required. Prerequisite: OT 3115 / OT 3115 or OT 5115/OTL 5115, OT 4116/ OTL 4116 or OT 5116 / OTL 5116 , and OT 4117/OTL 4117 or OT 5117 / OTL 5117 .

title: Research in Occupational Therapy I: Evidence-Based OT Practitioner
description: First in a series of four courses. Introduction to the research process and its application to evidence based practice. This course will guide students through the process of effectively interpreting and critiquing research evidence to inform practice decisions. Course content includes exploration of traditions, methods, ethics, outcome measures, and statistical analyses applicable to OT research. Students will apply knowledge and skills to conduct and disseminate the results of a critically appraised topic that addresses a clinical question posed by local practitioners - 3 lecture hours.

title: Health Policy, Leadership and Management in Occupational Therapy
description: This course is designed to provide students with an introduction to health policy, leadership, and management knowledge and skills relevant for entry-level administration and management in occupational therapy. Social, economic and political forces and policies impacting health care in general and OT services in particular will be examined. Reimbursement trends and issues will be studied in detail, with particular emphasis on Medicare, Medicaid, and managed care systems. Other pertinent professional issues to be covered in the course content include departmental time and fiscal management, administrative documentation and communication, program assessment, accreditation and licensure, supervision, professional ethics, and standards of OT practice.  - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: Acceptance into the accelerated MS in OT program.

title: Integrative Capstone Experience in Occupational Therapy III
description: Third in a series of three courses. Provides structure and support for the presentation of the capstone project and/or publication in a variety of academic and professional venues, including the creation of an academic poster. Prerequisite: OT 5111.

title: Occupations of Children and Adolescents II
description: Advanced concepts and skills for Occupational Therapy assessment and intervention with children and their families. Focus on frames of reference and evidence-based practice. Study of established and emerging practice areas and future trends - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: OT 5016 / OTL 5016 . (Exam average policy in effect for this course.)

title: Occupations of Adults and Seniors III
description: Third in a three semester series of courses focusing on occupational therapy practice with adults and seniors experiencing physical disabilities. Consideration of OT domain and process, client factors and client performance skills and patterns as defined by the Occupational Therapy Practice Framework. Content covered includes acute care, intensive care, primary care and community and role emergent settings â 2 lecture hours. Prerequisite: OT 4117/OTL 4117 or OT 5117 / OTL 5117 (Exam average policy in effect for this course.)

title: Occupations Adults and Seniors II
description: Second in a three semester series of courses focusing on occupational therapy practice with adults and seniors experiencing physical disabilities. Content covered includes healthy aging, fall prevention, and neurorehabilitation. Builds on concepts introduced in the first semester course. Synthesis, application, and evaluation of integrated theories and clinical reasoning for occupation-based intervention. Consideration of OT domain and process, client factors, and client performance skills and patterns as defined by the Occupational Therapy Practice Framework. Includes use of standardized and non-standardized functional assessments, intervention planning, and documentation - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 3117/OTL 3117 and OT 3018/OTL 3018. (Exam average policy in effect for this course.)

title: Assistive Technology in Occupational Therapy
description: Introduction to and overview of assistive technology as used by occupational therapists to facilitate function in people with and without disabilities â 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: OT 5012/OTL 5012.

title: Level II Fieldwork Placement 1
description: A minimum of 480 hours (12 weeks) full-time experience in a community agency or health care setting with supervision by a registered occupational therapist. Development of skills in patient/client occupational therapy assessment and intervention processes. Prerequisites: Approval of Academic Fieldwork Coordinator, completion of all required courses.

title: Evidence Based Practitioner
description: Introduction to the research process and its application to evidence based practice. This course will guide students through the process of effectively interpreting and critiquing research evidence to inform practice decisions. Course content includes exploration of traditions, methods, ethics, outcome measures, and statistical analyses applicable to OT research. Students will apply knowledge and skills to conduct and disseminate the results of a critically appraised topic that addresses a clinical question posed by local practitioners - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: Acceptance into the accelerated MS in OT program.

title: Level II Fieldwork Placement 3
description: A minimum of 480 hours (12 weeks) full-time experience in a community agency or health care setting with supervision by a registered occupational therapist. Development of skills in patient/client occupational therapy assessment and intervention processes. Prerequisites: Approval of Academic Fieldwork Coordinator, completion of all required courses. May be repeated for credit.

title: Professional Identity Seminar III
description: Prerequisite: Acceptance into the accelerated MS in OT program.

title: Level II Fieldwork Placement 2
description: A minimum of 480 hours (12 weeks) full-time experience in a community agency or health care setting with supervision by a registered occupational therapist. Development of skills in patient/client occupational therapy assessment and intervention processes. Prerequisites: Approval of Academic Fieldwork Coordinator, completion of all required courses.

title: Professional Identity V: The Prepared OT Professional
description: The final course in the professional identity series focuses on equipping students for their transition into the workforce and successful entry into professional practice. Students will create a comprehensive job search strategy, including résumé and interview preparation, and design a personalized plan for preparing for the licensing exam and obtaining licensure. The course will also guide students in developing a long-term professional development plan to support lifelong learning, career growth, and contributions to the occupational therapy profession. Emphasis will be placed on readiness for independent practice and a commitment to maintaining reflective practice, resilience, advocacy, and engagement throughout their careers. 1 lecture hour. Pre-requisite: OT 6154

title: Autism Spectrum Disorders - Child
description: This course will explore the etiology, diagnostic criteria, evaluation and treatment of children with Autism Spectrum Disorders. Designed to benefit both Occupational Therapy and Special Education students, special emphasis will be placed on the wide range of evidence-based interventions and treatments for children with ASD.

title: Introduction to Hand Therapy and Splinting
description: This course will provide an introduction to the practice area of hand therapy and hands-on lab experience in splint design and fabrication. Materials will include both the use of plaster as well as a variety of low temperature plastics. The course will include splinting for specific diagnosis, choosing the appropriate splint, and materials for joint contracture as well as for progressive mobilization. Static progressive and dynamic splinting will be covered. Emphasis will be on clinical reasoning, quality splint construction, and selection of a splint designed to increase participation in occupation. Prerequisites: OT 4017 or OT 5017. This course has an additional $35 Fee to cover course materials.

title: Wellness Movement
description: This course will explore movement wellness as the latest paradigm change in healthcare, and introduce students to a variety of wellness movement approaches. We will discuss wellness movement programs around the country that have been successful, and examine the evidence in support of wellness movement as a therapeutic modality. Wear loose clothing in which you can move comfortably.

title: Self Regulation/Social Success
description: This one unit course will provide occupational therapy students with knowledge and clinical skills to improve the functioning of children and adults with self-regulation difficulties. The course will use lecture, treatment videos, demonstrations, and lab practice to improve studentsâ ability to understand sensory processing, integration, and how it affects modulation. In addition, executive functions that impact self-regulation will be discussed and students will have an opportunity to work with tools that impact levels of alertness, social interaction and optimal functioning across settings.

title: Teaching Handwriting
description: This course will provide the student with in-depth knowledge of how to teach handwriting in a fun and efficient manner using 'Handwriting Without Tears' program for teaching print through cursive in grades kindergarten to 5th. It will also address the motor, sensory, and perceptual systems that affect pencil grip and classroom performance as it relates to handwriting. Lastly, we address some remediation strategies. In addition to one unit of University credit, students will receive a certificate of attendance from HWT for the HWT Kindergarten-5th course.

title: Fall Risk Reduction in Occupational Therapy
description: This course will provide students with knowledge of multifactorial fall risks and provide hands on experience conducting psychometric tests to assess fall risk. In addition, students learn to identify intrinsic and extrinsic risk factors for falls and consider ways to decrease falls.

title: Low Vision Rehabilitation
description: This course will provide an introduction to eye diseases, visual functions, psychosocial effects of low vision, and evaluations that are used in vision rehabilitation. It will introduce interventions such as visual aids, environmental modifications, visual skills, and community resources. Students will gain hands-on experience with low vision assistive devices; understand the impact of vision loss on participation, independence, safety, and psychosocial well-being; demonstrate knowledge of evaluation tools for assessing various aspects of visual function, including visual acuity, contrast sensitivity, and visual field; and explore use of high-tech and low-tech assistive devices that enhance participation in vision-dependent activities.

title: Introduction to Sensory Integration
description: A sensory integration frame of reference is the most common approach used for OT assessment and treatment of children with Autism Spectrum Disorder as well as other learning and behavioral disorders affecting their performance of school related and other daily life skills. Students will learn basic principles of sensory integration, identify indicators for sensory integration interventions, and have an opportunity to use some clinical sensory integration equipment. Prerequisites: OT 5016 / OTL 5016 .

title: Professional Issues in Occupational Therapy
description: Introduction to management, leadership, and health policy in occupational therapy. Also focuses on professional identity development and preparation for professional practice â 4 lecture hours. Prerequisites: Successful completion of all courses in the first and second year of MSOT program, or permission of the Chair. This course has an additional $25 Fee to cover the OT Knowledge Exam.

title: Health Care in Costa Rica
description: The focus of this course is on learning about the differences and similarities between Costa Rica and United States health belief and health care systems, through experiential learning. Students will provide volunteer service at hospitals, elderly centers, shelters and orphanages. This course is open to Occupational Therapy students only. Instructor's permission required.

title: OT Special Topics
description: Selected topics that provide the student with additional breadth or depth in the field of occupational therapy. Topics vary by semester.

title: Abilities Care Approach in Occupational Therapy
description: The Abilities Care ApproachÂ® is a program used to enhance the occupational performance of clients with dementia. Students will utilize a habilitative framework to develop Individualized Activity Prescriptions and modified caregiving approaches based on the clientâs Allen Cognitive Level (ACL). Students will explore teaching methods used to instruct families, staff and caregivers to facilitate occupational performance in clients with dementia. Students will create a Life Story Board and explore how this tool is used to enhance communication and meaningful participation in occupations for clients with dementia. Prerequisite: Enrollment in second or third year of OT Professional Program.

title: Interprofessional Communication in the Health Profession
description: This one unit course will include working through multiple case studies of conditions commonly seen in acute rehabilitation setting. Through guided discussions and collaborative 3D activities (debriefing, defusing and discovering), OT and nursing students will learn to appreciate the professional differences and similarities, and develop a common language to strengthen interprofessional communication in healthcare settings.

title: Sensory Modulation in Occupational Therapy
description: OT provides an introduction to sensory modulation assessment, intervention, and program development in mental health, brain injury, and geriatric treatment settings. Relevant theories and concepts will be presented, emphasizing evidence-based practice, neuroscience, trauma-informed care, cultural competency, and recovery principles. Prerequisites: OT 3015 / OTL 3015 and OT 3115 / OTL 3115 .

title: Occupational Science: The Science of Everyday Living
description: Occupational science is an interdisciplinary academic discipline that focuses on the study of humans as occupational beings. This course will encourage critical discussion of core concepts in occupational science. Topics may include occupational justice, identity, temporality, cultural-social-historical-political context and the form, function and meaning of occupation. Each student will be expected to reflect upon and challenge various concepts in occupational science and apply these concepts to occupational therapy practice. Prerequisites: OT 3012 and OT 3015 .

title: Healing & Mindfulness
description: Provides a historical, theoretical, evidence-based, and experiential introduction to the use of mindfulness as a therapeutic modality. Practical strategies for incorporating mindfulness into health-related professional roles and settings with varied populations will be presented. Students will explore and experience a variety of mindfulness activities, reflect on their individual and collective experience, and deepen their understanding of the healing power of mindfulness.

title: Introduction to Driving Rehabilitation
description: Occupational therapists provide a unique skill-set for evaluating, rehabilitating, and habilitating driving skills. In this course, students will learn about the role of occupational therapy in addressing driving as an occupation across the lifespan from teens to seniors. Content will include identifying fitness-to-drive through observation of ADLs and I-ADLs, selection and administration of evidenced-based assessment tools, and application of theoretical models. Students will apply clinical reasoning to make decisions about fitness-to-drive and intervention plans. Hands-on experience will include assessment of person-vehicle fit, operation of adaptive driving equipment, and selection of adaptive driving equipment. Prerequisites: OT 3117/OTL 3117; instructor consent required. Meets two Saturdays.

title: Physiology and Perception
description: This course will cover the physiology of sensation & perception from the sensory receptors to the cortex. Topics to be covered will include how the brain uses environmental cues and physiology to perceive the World around us. Particular attention will be paid to cultural differences in perception, as well as the effects that age and injury have on perception.

title: Palliative and End of Life Care
description: This course will present an overview of issues pertinent to end-of-life care. It explores cultural aspects of death and dying, palliative care in all settings, and grief management of those left behind.

title: Experiencing Resilience as an Occupational Therapy Practitioner
description: This course will introduce students to the evolving construct of resilience and its role in supporting well-being as future occupational therapy practitioners. Students will gain understanding of challenges unique to occupational therapy practice and the transition from student to practitioner roles. The course will focus on acquiring and practicing evidence-based strategies to address these challenges and experience resilience and thriving in both academic and future clinical settings. Prerequisites: OT 3012 or OT 5012

title: International Experience in Occupational Therapy
description: Course may include travel for international experience in occupational therapy. Additional prerequisites may vary.

title: Teaching Assistant: Occupational Therapy
description: Opportunities to assist OT faculty in conducting research or teaching of OT classes - 3-9 hours. Prerequisites: Instructor permission, class standing, must have previously completed course with âAâ (4.0) or âBâ (3.0) grade.

title: International Level I Fieldwork in Occupational Therapy
description: International Level I OT fieldwork experience in psychosocial, child & adolescent, and/or adults and seniors in a selected country as part of a structured international study abroad course. Includes site visits and observations in clinical and community settings with patients and/or clients who are or could be the recipients of occupational therapy services. Requires a minimum of 40 hours on site, plus pre-travel meetings during the semester preceding the trip.

title: Occupational Science
description: Occupational science is an interdisciplinary academic discipline that focuses on the study of humans as occupational beings. This course will encourage critical discussion of core concepts in occupational science. Topics may include occupational justice, identity, temporality, cultural-social-historical-political context and the form, function and meaning of occupation. Each student will be expected to reflect upon and challenge various concepts in occupational science and apply these concepts to themselves as an occupational being and to occupational therapy practice - 1 lecture hour.

title: The Psychobiology of Aging
description: This course will review biological, evolutionary, psychological and social theories of aging, discuss physical and psychological changes associated with aging, and consider the evidence for various anti-aging therapies. We will look at what factors may help us age successfully and what treatments may reverse some of the effects of aging. Weekend intensive course.

title: Occupational Therapy Treatment for Pediatric Feeding Difficulties
description: A primary area of practice for occupational therapists is supporting safe and appropriate oral feeding for children and addressing feeding difficulties. This course will focus on basic swallowing physiology, typical development of oral feeding skills, and strategies to improve oral feeding success for infant and pediatric clients. Students will have the opportunity to use information learned in this course, for several interactive case studies, to better solidify learning. Prerequisites: OT 5016 (with a passing grade) and OT 5116 (may be taken concurrently), or consent by instructor. Special Topic Course for OT majors only.

title: Independent Study: Occupational Therapy
description: Directed reading, research, and/or a project of special interest to a student. Course may be taken for 1-3 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. Contract required.

title: Foundations of Occupational Therapy
description: This course provides an introduction to the profession of OT including its history, professional framework, regulatory entities and organizations, theories and practice models. Students will gain exposure to the OTPF and learn how to apply it within evidence-based and client-centered practice. The lab component of this course introduces students to skills and techniques used across practice settings including activity analysis, clinical documentation, and facilitation of occupation-focused groups - 3 lecture hours.

title: Psychosocial Foundations of Occupational Therapy
description: First in a two-semester series focusing on the study of psychosocial aspects of human occupation. Focus on services provided to all individuals to enhance mental health promotion and prevention for all in a variety of contexts. Includes review of key theoretical perspectives, orientation to psychosocial frames of reference, and an introduction to creating a therapeutic relationship through active listening, motivational interviewing, and occupation centered practice. Development and application of interview, assessment, evaluation group facilitation, and goal setting within mental health promotion and prevention - 2 lecture hours.

title: Human Movement Analysis
description: Study of kinesiology and biomechanics related to human motion and occupation, and principles of joint and muscle action related to activity, function, and dysfunction of the moving body. Study and application of principles of activity/task analysis and the relationship to functional performance. Includes basic assessment skills following a biomechanical model - 2 lecture hours. Prerequisites: Concurrent enrollment in OT 6012 / OTL 6012 .

title: Functional Neuroscience in Occupational Therapy
description: Study of the structure and function of the human central and peripheral nervous system. The course includes advanced clinical neuroanatomy and neurophysiology content in the context of understanding the effects of neurodevelopmental disorders, aging, trauma, and disease on the functions of the nervous system as related to occupational therapy practice - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: BIO 2500 / BIO 2505 and BIO 2600 / BIO 2605 .

title: Culture, Diversity and Ethics in Occupational Therapy
description: An exploration of cultural and diversity issues in occupational therapy. Consideration of larger social and global issues related to democracy and equityâs influence on health and delivery of healthcare services. Designed to foster cultural competency and humility, social responsibility, and advocacy skills as critical competencies for occupational therapists - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 4012 / OTL 4012 or OT 6012 / OTL 6012 .

title: OT Fundamental Skills for Assessment and Intervention
description: Core assessments and basic transfer skills are taught and practiced. The focus is on basic skill development in preparation for fieldwork and entry level practice across lifespan - 1 lecture hour. Prerequisites: OT 6014 / OTL 6014 or OT 4014 / OTL 4014 and completion of or concurrent enrollment in OT 6027 / OTL 6027 or OT 4027 / OTL 4027 .

title: Occupations of Adults and Seniors I
description: First in a three-semester series of courses focusing on occupational therapy practice with adults and seniors experiencing physical disabilities. Content covered includes OT for medical and orthopedic conditions, and other OT-specialty practice areas such as industrial rehabilitation, low vision, hand therapy and driver’s rehabilitation. Application of integrated theories and clinical reasoning for occupation-based interventions. Consideration of OT domain and process, client factors, and client performance skills and patterns as defined by the Occupational Therapy Practice Framework. Includes an introduction to the use of standardized and non-standardized functional assessments, intervention planning, and documentation specific to this population - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 6012 / OTL 6012 ,  or OT 4012 / OTL 4012 , OT 6014 / OTL 6014 or OT 4014 / OTL 4014 , and completion of or concurrent enrollment in OT 6028 / OTL 6028 or OT 4028 / OTL 4028 .

title: Sensory Modulation Disorders in Children
description: The course Interventions for Sensory Modulation Disorders (SMD) in Children will explore and explain the concept of Sensory Modulation leading to an understanding of the associated sensory processing disorders. Core concepts for understanding SMD will be applied to developing and applying occupational therapy interventions to improve quality of life in individuals with SMD. Clinical reasoning will be applied through multiple case studies. Learning activities will include hands-on application and analysis of intervention techniques. Completion of OT 5016 / OTL 5016 and completion or concurrent enrollment in OT 5116 / OTL 5116 . Permission from Department Chair required.

title: Occupations of Populations
description: In this course, students will explore the impact of diverse sociocultural, political, economic and historical factors on the occupations of populations. This course prepares students to develop programmatic interventions addressing occupational participation for populations to promote population health, social and occupational justice, and social inclusion - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 4012 / OTL 4012 or OT 6012 / OTL 6012 and OT 4015 / OTL 4015 or OT 6015 / OTL 6015

title: Management and Leadership in Occupational Therapy
description: Development of management and leadership skills for OT practice and an in depth exploration of management and leadership roles for OTs in a variety of practice settings. Includes the application of ethical reasoning and advocacy within formal or informal roles of leadership - 2 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 6153

title: Educational Principles and Practices in Occupational Therapy
description: In-depth exploration and application of teaching and learning theories and strategies used in occupational therapy. This course will introduce students to best practices in teaching and learning in clinical, community-based, and academic settings, and students will have the opportunity to develop and implement an educational module in partnership with DUC faculty or a community partner - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 4012 / OTL 4012 or OT 6012 / OTL 6012 .

title: OT Program Development for Population-Level Practice
description: An immersive experiential course in which students partner with community organizations to design, implement, and evaluate innovative occupation-based programs and develop a grant proposal to fund these programs. This process includes application of theoretical and conceptual models supporting community-based occupational therapy practice- 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 6030 and OT 6104 .

title: Occupations in Mental Health and Wellness
description: Second in a two-semester series, building on the foundational psychosocial skills gained in the first course. Application of occupation-centered assessments and intensive interventions in psychosocial settings. Includes advocacy, education, and group facilitation as key modes of OT intervention within psychosocial and behavioral health practice settings. Students apply psychosocial theories, assessments and interventions to meet the unique needs of individuals, groups, and populations experiencing psychosocial impacts on their occupations. Explores the historical, political and attitudinal influences on participation in occupation - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 6015 / OTL 6015 .

title: Research in Occupational Therapy III: Research Study Implementation
description: Third in a series of four courses. Students work in small groups under faculty direction to implement a research study, collect and analyze data. Students are required to draft a submission to a professional conference. The course includes a formal oral dissemination presentation - 2 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 6122 .

title: Research in Occupational Therapy II: Research Proposal Development
description: Second in a series of four courses. This course introduces students to the process of OT as a scholar and knowledge generator. Students work in small groups to develop and complete a research proposal for a faculty-directed research study. This course is designed to provide formal structure and support to develop a written research proposal. In this course, students will: research a specific topic; conduct and write a literature review, compose a problem statement and research question, design research methodology, and submit an Institutional Review Board (IRB) application. The course concludes with oral dissemination of proposal progress to solicit formative feedback from peers and faculty - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: OT 6121 .

title: Assistive Technology in Occupational Therapy
description: Assistive Technology (AT) applications throughout the lifespan, ranging from low to high tech will be presented, discussed and examined through an evidence based lens. AT will be studied through the concepts of universal design and social models of disability. Occupational therapy clinical reasoning inclusive of AT will be emphasized including the role and application of activity analysis and assessment results interpretation. The interprofessional and collaborative aspects of AT practice will be emphasized throughout the course and connections with community AT experts - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 4012 / OTL 4012 or OT 6012 / OTL 6012 .

title: Research in Occupational Therapy I: Evidence-Based OT Practitioner
description: First in a series of four courses. Introduction to the research process and its application to evidence based practice. This course will guide students through the process of effectively interpreting and critiquing research evidence to inform practice decisions. Course content includes exploration of traditions, methods, ethics, outcome measures, and statistical analyses applicable to OT research. Students will apply knowledge and skills to conduct and disseminate the results of a critically appraised topic that addresses a clinical question posed by local practitioners - 3 lecture hours.

title: Research in Occupational Therapy IV: Research Dissemination
description: Fourth in a series of four courses. Students work in small groups under faculty direction to write a peer reviewed manuscript, design and present an academic poster and professional dissemination presentation - 2 lecture units. Prerequisites: OT 6123 .

title: Occupations of Children & Adolescents I
description: First in a two-semester series of courses focusing on occupational therapy for children and adolescents. Content includes study of development of occupations and the interaction of activity and disability from birth through 18 years of age; child, family and strengths-based clinical reasoning; theoretical frameworks and application to occupation; evaluation process, assessment selection, administration, scoring and interpretation of criterion and norm-referenced results; interprofessional collaboration in early intervention and school-based services - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 4012 / OTL 4012 or OT 6012 / OTL 6012 , and OT 4014 or OT 6014 .

title: Occupations of Adults and Seniors II
description: Second in a three-semester series of courses focusing on occupational therapy practice with adults and seniors experiencing physical disabilities. Content covered includes healthy aging and neurorehabilitation. Builds on concepts introduced in the first semester course. Includes synthesis, application, and evaluation of integrated theories and clinical reasoning for occupation-based interventions. Considers the OT domain and process, client factors, and client performance skills and patterns as defined by the Occupational Therapy Practice Framework. Includes use of standardized and non-standardized functional assessments, intervention planning, and documentation - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 4027 / OTL 4027 or OT 6027 / OTL 6027 .

title: Occupations of Adults and Seniors III
description: Third in a three-semester series of courses focusing on occupational therapy practice with adults and seniors experiencing physical disabilities. Application of OT domain and process, client factors and client performance skills and patterns as defined by the Occupational Therapy Practice Framework, and clinical reasoning across the spectrum of practice settings in acute care, intensive care, rehabilitation and community through the use of electronic health records. Includes innovative roles of OT in primary care and other role emergent practices - 2 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 6127 / OTL 6127 .

title: Professional Identity I: The Reflective Practitioner
description: This introductory course in a five-semester series will engage students in developing their professional identity through engagement in reflective practices to promote self-awareness and skills in occupational balance, self-care, and professional communication. This course will also provide students with an evidence-informed understanding of the behaviors that underlie success in both the academic program and professional practice. Students will build a professional identity development plan and engage in reflective journaling to document their growth - 1 lecture hour. Prerequisites: Admission in the OTD program.

title: Professional Identity III: OT as Change Agent
description: The third course in a five-semester series on professional identity focuses on acquiring knowledge about healthcare systems, policy, and advocacy as essential components of developing the role of advocate within the professional identity of an occupational therapist. Students will examine policy and regulatory issues impacting the health and well-being of individuals and populations, as well as the systems and funding mechanisms within healthcare, education, and community-based organizations. This course provides foundational knowledge to empower students to engage in effective advocacy as a critical part of occupational therapy practice- 2 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 6152 .

title: Professional Identity IV: Becoming an Engaged Practitioner
description: The fourth course in the professional identity series focuses on preparing students for the transition to fieldwork and doctoral capstone experiences. Students will explore the challenges they may encounter in maintaining their professional identity as occupation-based and client-centered practitioners. Emphasis is placed on strategies for navigating complex practice environments and upholding the values of the profession during fieldwork and capstone experiences. Students will refine their professional communication skills, deepen their understanding of client-centered and occupation-based care, and develop a framework for addressing ethical and practical challenges in real-world settings- 1 lecture hour. Prerequisites: OT 6153 .

title: International Experience in Occupational Therapy
description: Course may include travel for international experience in occupational therapy. Additional prerequisites may vary.

title: Introduction to Hand Therapy and Splinting
description: This course will provide an introduction to the practice area of hand therapy and hands-on lab experience in splint design and fabrication. Materials will include both the use of plaster as well as a variety of low temperature plastics. The course will include splinting for specific diagnosis, choosing the appropriate splint, and materials for joint contracture as well as for progressive mobilization. Static progressive and dynamic splinting will be covered. Emphasis will be on clinical reasoning, quality splint construction, and selection of a splint designed to increase participation in occupation. Prerequisites: OT 4017 or OT 5017. This course has an additional $35 Fee to cover course materials.

title: Doctoral Capstone in Occupational Therapy II
description: Second in a series of four courses. Continue development of individual doctoral capstone including establishment of a memorandum of understanding with the identified community partner defining responsibilities of all parties, and documentations that outline individualized specific objectives for the capstone project, plans for supervision or mentoring, and formal evaluation mechanism of the student’s performance during the doctoral capstone experience. Prerequisites: OT 6410 . Students on alternative curriculum plan must have approval from the Program Director.

title: Palliative and End of Life Care
description: This course will present an overview of issues pertinent to end-of-life care. It explores cultural aspects of death and dying, palliative care in all settings, and grief management of those left behind.

title: Doctoral Capstone in Occupational Therapy I
description: First in a series of four courses. Introduction to the individual doctoral capstone process with exploration to identify a student's specific area of focus for the doctoral capstone project. Student identifies and makes contact with community setting/partner and mentor who has expertise consistent with the studentâs area of focus in the doctoral capstone project. Prerequisites: OT 4012 / OTL 4012 or OT 6012 / OTL 6012 .

title: Sensory Modulation Disorders in Children
description: The course Interventions for Sensory Modulation Disorders (SMD) in Children will explore and explain the concept of Sensory Modulation leading to an understanding of the associated sensory processing disorders. Core concepts for understanding SMD will be applied to developing and applying occupational therapy interventions to improve quality of life in individuals with SMD. Clinical reasoning will be applied through multiple case studies. Learning activities will include hands-on application and analysis of intervention techniques. Completion of OT 5016 / OTL 5016 and completion or concurrent enrollment in OT 5116 / OTL 5116 . Permission from Department Chair required.

title: Occupations of Children and Adolescents II
description: Second in a two-semester series focusing on occupational therapy for children, adolescents. Application of advanced concepts and clinical reasoning for occupational therapy assessment and intervention with children and their families including administering assessments, developing, implementing and documenting effective intervention techniques. Focus on frames of reference and evidence-based practice across the spectrum of pediatric clients and practice settings. Study of established and emerging practice areas and future trends - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisites: OT 6016 / OTL 6016

title: Doctoral Capstone Experience I
description: First in a series of two courses. Completion of a 6-week (240 hours) doctoral capstone experience for a total of 14 weeks (series 1 & 2) to implement the individual doctoral capstone project in a community setting as identified by the student. Prerequisites: OT 6430 and OT 6532 .

title: Doctoral Capstone in Occupational Therapy III
description: Third in a series of four courses. Each student develops a complete and scholarly proposal for the doctoral capstone project and experience. Prerequisites: OT 6420 . Students on alternative curriculum plan must have approval from the Program Director.

title: Professional Identity II: The Resilient Practitioner
description: Second in a five-semester series, this course will introduce students to the evolving construct of resilience and its role in supporting well-being as future occupational therapy practitioners. Students will gain understanding of challenges unique to occupational therapy practice and the transition from student to practitioner roles. The course will focus on acquiring and practicing evidence-based strategies to address these challenges and experience resilience and thriving in both academic and future clinical settings - 1 lecture hour. Prerequisites: OT 4015 / OTL 4015 or OT 6015 / OTL 6015 and OT 6151 .

title: Doctoral Capstone in Occupational Therapy IV
description: Last in a series of four courses. Dissemination of the completed individual doctoral capstone project that relates to the doctoral capstone experience. Prerequisites: OT 6432 or current enrollment.

title: OT Level II Fieldwork 3
description: A minimum of 480 hours (12 weeks) full-time experience in a community agency or health care setting with supervision by a registered occupational therapist. Development of skills in client-centered occupational therapy assessment and intervention processes. Students choose one of the three practice areas: Behavioral Health, Pediatrics or Physical Rehabilitation. Prerequisites: Completion of all required courses and approval of OTD Academic Fieldwork Coordinator.

title: Doctoral Capstone Experience II
description: First in a series of two courses. Completion of a 6-week (240 hours) doctoral capstone experience for a total of 14 weeks (series 1 & 2) to implement the individual doctoral capstone project in a community setting as identified by the student. Prerequisites: OT 6430 .

title: Professional Identity V: The Prepared OT Professional
description: The final course in the professional identity series focuses on equipping students for their transition into the workforce and successful entry into professional practice. Students will create a comprehensive job search strategy, including résumé and interview preparation, and design a personalized plan for preparing for the licensing exam and obtaining licensure. The course will also guide students in developing a long-term professional development plan to support lifelong learning, career growth, and contributions to the occupational therapy profession. Emphasis will be placed on readiness for independent practice and a commitment to maintaining reflective practice, resilience, advocacy, and engagement throughout their careers. 1 lecture hour. Pre-requisite: OT 6154

title: Foundations in Occupational Therapy Lab
description: Lab component for OT 3012 . Introductory skills and techniques needed for clinical OT practice, including task and activity analysis, therapeutic media, an introduction to clinical documentation, and application of the Occupational Therapy Practice Framework - 2 lab hours.

title: OT Level II Fieldwork 1
description: A minimum of 480 hours (12 weeks) full-time experience in a community agency or health care setting with supervision by a registered occupational therapist. Development of skills in client-centered occupational therapy assessment and intervention processes. Students choose one of the three practice areas: Behavioral Health, Pediatrics or Physical Rehabilitation. Prerequisites: Completion of all required courses and approval of OTD Academic Fieldwork Coordinator.

title: OT Fundamental Skills for Assessment and Intervention Lab
description: Lab component for OT 4028 - 3 lab hours.

title: Psychosocial Aspects of Occupation I Lab
description: Lab component for OT 3015 . Practice developing therapeutic communication, interviewing, evaluation, treatment planning, and group leadership skills - 2 lab hours.

title: Occupations of Children and Adolescents I Lab
description: Lab component for OT 5016 . Overview of occupation-based pediatric assessment tools and procedures. Practice with activity analysis, task modification, observation, and interviewing skills - 2 lab hours.

title: Occupations of Adults and Seniors I Lab
description: Lab component for OT 3117. Practice with case analysis, assessment, treatment planning, adaptive equipment, and intervention - 2 lab hours.

title: Psychosocial Foundations of Occupational Therapy Lab
description: Lab component for OT 4015 - 3 lab hours.

title: Human Movement Analysis Lab
description: Lab component for OT 4014 - 3 lab hours.

title: Foundations of Occupational Therapy Lab
description: Lab component for OT 4012 - 3 lab hours.

title: Human Movement Analysis Lab
description: Lab component for OT 3014 . Activities designed to increase knowledge of joint structure and function and muscle action; Introduction to functional principles of joint range of motion - 2 lab hours.

title: Occupations of Adults and Seniors I Lab
description: Lab component for OT 4027 - 3 lab hours.

title: Occupations in Mental Health and Wellness Lab
description: Lab component for OT 6115 - 3 lab hours

title: OT Level II Fieldwork 2
description: A minimum of 480 hours (12 weeks) full-time experience in a community agency or health care setting with supervision by a registered occupational therapist. Development of skills in client-centered occupational therapy assessment and intervention processes. Students choose one of the three practice areas: Behavioral Health, Pediatrics or Physical Rehabilitation. Prerequisites: Completion of all required courses and approval of OTD Academic Fieldwork Coordinator.

title: OT Fundamental Skills for Assessment and Intervention Lab
description: Lab component for OT 4028 - 3 lab hours.

title: Psychosocial Foundations of Occupational Therapy Lab
description: Lab component for OT 6015 - 3 lab hours.

title: Occupations of Children and Adolescents I Lab
description: Lab component for OT 5016 . Overview of occupation-based pediatric assessment tools and procedures. Practice with activity analysis, task modification, observation, and interviewing skills - 2 lab hours.

title: Psychosocial Aspects of Occupation II Lab
description: Lab component for OT 3115 . Assessments, developing, implementing and documenting effective intervention techniques using clinical reasoning skills - 2 lab hours (includes community practice laboratory) and Level I Fieldwork - 40 fieldwork hours.

title: Occupations of Children and Adolescents II Lab
description: Lab component for OT 5116 . Assessments and developing, implementing and documenting effective intervention techniques using clinical reasoning skills. Includes community practice laboratory and Level I Fieldwork - 4 lab hours.

title: Foundations of Occupational Therapy Lab
description: Lab component for OT 6012 - 3 lab hours.

title: Occupations of Adults and Seniors I Lab
description: Lab component for OT 6027 - 3 lab hours.

title: Occupations of Children and Adolescents I Lab
description: Lab component for OT 6016 - 3 lab hours.

title: OT Fundamental Skills for Assessment and Intervention Lab
description: Lab component for OT 6028 - 3 lab hours.

title: Current Topics in the PA Profession
description: This course will provide an introduction and an overview of the PA profession for the PA student. It will provide information regarding the development of the PA profession and will explore in depth the current status of the PA profession regulatory requirements of practice, and a discussion regarding cultural competence and implicit bias - 7 week session. Fall I - Phase I.

title: Occupations of Children and Adolescents II Lab
description: Lab component for OT 6116 - 6 lab hours

title: Occupations of Adults and Seniors II Lab
description: Lab component for OT 6127 - 6 lab hours

title: Occupations of Adults and Seniors II Lab
description: Lab component for OT 5117 . Assessments and developing, implementing and documenting effective intervention techniques using clinical reasoning skills. Includes an introduction to assistive technology, therapeutic modalities, adaptive equipment, and splint fabrication. Includes community practice laboratory and Level I Fieldwork - 4 lab hours.

title: Clinical Assessment
description: The cornerstone of what physician assistants do is to obtain a medical history and perform a physical examination. As a profession we have gained recognition for our ability to do this in an extremely organized and thorough manner, many times discovering aspects or physical findings not previously uncovered. These lecture/laboratory courses are designed to present and teach the techniques for performing and documenting a complete and competent history and physical examination and to begin to interpret both historical and physical findings as applicable to the physiologic and/or disease state of a patient. Lectures will focus on history taking skills including communication skills and the normal physiologic findings for each system of the body in addition to discussing abnormal findings indicative of disease states. The laboratory sessions will focus on practicing and mastering the skills needed to competently perform both the medical interview and a thorough head to toe physical examination, as well as documentation techniques - 15 week session. Fall I - Phase I. Successful completion of both the theory and lab components of the course is required to pass the course.

title: Foundations of Clinical Medicine
description: The Foundations of Clinical Medicine course is designed to provide the first year physician assistant student with a baseline level of knowledge of the basic sciences (biochemistry, human anatomy and physiology, pathology, genetics, immunology and medical microbiology, and laboratory medicine) to enhance the PA studentâs study of health and disease. The course will combine in person lectures, recorded lectures, and in-class activities - 15 week session. Fall I - Phase I.

title: Medical Ethics and Professionalism
description: This course is an in depth study of the concepts of healthcare ethics. The course of study analyzes the fundamentals of ethical principles as they relate to healthcare, and examines the core values and beliefs of medical professionalism. Methods of ethical analysis and a review of current case studies will be used in discussions of ethical dilemmas faced by healthcare personnel in areas such as medical errors, informed consent, and care of the dying patient. The Physician Assistant student will explore the personal values, professional standards, and institutional guidelines that define the roles and responsibilities of a healthcare practitioner. This course will include both lectures and small group activities which will focus on the development of an understanding of the philosophical and practical principles related to professional ethics - 15 week session. Fall I - Phase I.

title: Introduction to Pharmacology and Infectious Disease Principles
description: This course is intended to provide the PA student with an understanding of the basic pharmacological concepts of pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, drug metabolism and drug interactions. It will also include descriptions of the pathogens that can cause infections of various organ systems, their clinical syndromes, treatment and prevention, and define nosocomial infections in terms of morbidity, mortality, and their associated costs to the general population and the healthcare system â 6 week session. Fall I - Phase I.

title: Clinical Pharmacology I
description: This course is the first of a two-course sequence (Spring, Summer) in clinical pharmacology. During this course sequence, students will receive systems-based education on patient evaluation, diagnosis, and management across the life span. Clinical Pharmacology I addresses the principles of: routes of administration, pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, toxicology, drug classes, and disease management. The systems that will be included in semester 2 include: Dermatologic, Eyes, Ears, Nose, and Throat, Pulmonary, Hematologic, Cardiovascular, Gastrointestinal, Infectious Diseases and Endocrine - 16 week session. Spring I - Phase I.

title: Human Movement Analysis Lab
description: Lab component for OT 6014 - 3 lab hours.

title: Introduction to Evidence-Based Medicine and Health Policy
description: This course is intended for students to acquire and develop both the knowledge and the skills for incorporating evidence-based recommendations, the peer-reviewed literature, and the clinical guidelines to clinical and health policy decision-making. Students will acquire knowledge and develop skills to determine the validity, the meaning, and the applicability of the evidence base to clinical practice. Students will evaluate research design, critically appraise research methodologies, and select and interpret appropriate statistical analyses. Students will also build on their knowledge of key healthcare and public health policies relevant to clinical practice, including those related to health care financing and current efforts in health reform - 15 week session. Fall I - Phase I.

title: Medical Spanish for the PA
description: This course will provide an introduction and an overview of Spanish. It will provide cultural understanding and situated medical terms. This course is a great fit for those who are just starting to learn Spanish or are heritage speakers among the PA students. It is designed to help individuals communicate more effectively with Spanish speaking patients, specifically those from migrant communities in Central America and Mexico, which make up the majority in California. Throughout the course, you will learn how to greet patients, engage in brief conversations, gather preliminary patient information, and check patients into the hospital. You will also learn how to communicate effectively at pediatrician and gynecologist appointments, as well as other general health subjects. In addition to language proficiency, this course will also help you gain a deeper understanding and appreciation of Central American and Mexican culture and the differences in health perceptions among Spanish-speaking patients. You will become familiar with culturally situated Spanish and medical terms, as well as basic grammar proficiency. Finally, this course emphasizes the importance of empathy and caring listening when working with English learners. By becoming a more effective communicator, you can help improve the health outcomes of Spanish-speaking patients in your care. We will use immersive Spanish through music and theater. This approach is a fun and engaging way to learn a new language while experiencing different cultural expressions. By incorporating music and theater into your language learning, you will have the opportunity to practice your language skills in a more natural and authentic way. This approach will help you improve your listening and speaking skills, as well as your cultural knowledge and appreciation - 1 lecture hour. Elective course for MSPAS 4th semester students.  Prerequisite: Enrollment in MSPAS program.

title: Clinical Medicine II
description: This course is the second of a two-course sequence (Spring, Summer) in clinical medicine. During this course sequence, students will receive systems-based education on patient evaluation, diagnosis, and management across the life span. The pathophysiologic basis of disease, (including genetics and molecular mechanisms of disease), generating differential diagnoses, ordering and interpreting diagnostic studies will be covered. Additionally, the clinical medicine series will challenge students to apply their knowledge through problem-based case scenarios to develop problem solving and medical decision-making skills. The systems that will be included in semester 3 include: Urology/Nephrology, Women’s Health, Neurology, Musculoskeletal/Rheumatology, Psychiatry- 12 week session. Summer I - Phase I. Successful completion of both the theory and lab components of the course is required to pass the course.

title: Occupations in Mental Health and Wellness Lab
description: Lab component for OT 6115 - 3 lab hours

title: Clinical Pharmacology II
description: This course is the second of a two-course sequence (Spring, Summer) in clinical pharmacology. During this course sequence, students will receive systems-based education on patient evaluation, diagnosis, and management across the life span.
