Clinical Pharmacology II addresses the principles of: routes of administration, pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, toxicology, drug classes, and disease management. The systems that will be included in semester 3 include: Urology/Nephrology, Womenâs Health, Neurology, Musculoskeletal/Rheumatology, Psychiatry, and Infectious Diseases - 12 week session. Summer I - Phase I.

title: Health Care for Special Populations
description: This course explores the medical needs and challenges of specific patient groups including pediatric, geriatric, LGBTQIA, disabled people, and other underserved groups. Students will explore a variety of health needs and health systems issues that are important in the care of specific patient populations. Students will adapt their patient assessment techniques to the developmental, functional, and educational level of diverse patient populations. Fall II - Phase I.

title: Emergency Medicine/Surgery/Skills
description: This course uses an integrated approach to provide principles of care for patients in the hospital setting. Emphasis is placed on the diagnosis and management of acute and emergent illness and pre- and post- operative care of surgical patients. This course also serves as an introduction to common clinical procedures in current physician assistant practice. Students will build upon knowledge developed in previous semesters and gain a critical thinking framework from which they will continue building their cognitive and procedural abilities throughout their education and careers. Fall II Phase I. Successful completion of both the theory and lab components of the course is required to pass the course.

title: Summative Course
description: The purpose of this course is to draw on the medical knowledge, and clinical and interpersonal skills that have been acquired throughout the curriculum that culminate in summative program evaluations. These summative evaluations must be completed satisfactorily in order to pass the course and progress to graduation. The elements of the summative evaluations consist of medical knowledge, clinical skills, professionalism, and communication and interpersonal skills. In addition, students will attend lectures that will help them prepare for the Physician Assistant National Certifying Examination (PANCE), and will present professional case presentations. Fall III - Phase III. Successful completion of both the theory and lab components of the course is required to pass the course.

title: Professional Practice Seminar
description: The purpose of this course is to prepare students for the transition from the didactic to clinical year. The course contains a self-assessment of medical knowledge, overview of the clinical policies and procedures, as well as program expectations while on clinical rotations. There will also be instruction on laws and regulations regarding the PA and the profession, as well as instruction on documentation of care, and billing and coding. Fall II - Phase I.

title: Clinical Applications I
description: This course is the first of a three-course sequence (Spring, Summer, Fall) in clinical medicine application that will challenge students to integrate their didactic learning with patient care experiences and simulated patient exercises. Coursework is designed to provide the student with opportunities to apply their knowledge and skills. Small group tutorials will utilize case-based learning strategies to emphasize integration of material presented during the semester. Students will perform focused patient evaluations, honing their clinical decision making and medical documentation skills - 16 week session. Spring I - Phase I.

title: Clinical Medicine I
description: This course is the first of a two-course sequence (Spring, Summer) in clinical medicine. During this course sequence, students will receive systems-based education on patient evaluation, diagnosis, and management across the life span. The pathophysiologic basis of disease, (including genetics and molecular mechanisms of disease), generating differential diagnoses, ordering and interpreting diagnostic studies will be covered. Additionally, the clinical medicine series will challenge students to apply their knowledge through problem-based case scenarios to develop problem solving and medical decision-making skills. The systems that will be covered include: Dermatologic, Eyes, Ears, Nose, and Throat, Pulmonary, Hematologic, Cardiovascular, Gastrointestinal, and Endocrine - 16 week session. Spring I - Phase I. Successful completion of both the theory and lab components of the course is required to pass the course.

title: Clinical Assessment Lab
description: Lab component for PA 5111 . Successful completion of both the theory and lab components of the course is required to pass the course.

title: Anatomy & Physiology I
description: This course is the first of a two-course sequence (Spring, Summer) in anatomy and physiology (A&P). This course is designed to provide general principles of anatomy and physiology to assist in developing spatial relationship skills with an emphasis on clinically relevant anatomy. A comprehensive review of physiologic functions of the major organs systems will also be covered. The PA student will learn the skills needed to recognize normal anatomy and physiology and anatomic and physiologic variations. The systems that will be included in semester 2 include: Dermatologic, Eyes, Ears, Nose, and Throat, Pulmonary, Hematologic, Cardiovascular, Gastrointestinal, and Endocrine - 16 week session. Spring I - Phase I.

title: Clinical Medicine Lab I
description: Lab component for PA 5280 . Spring I - Phase I. Successful completion of both the theory and lab components of the course is required to pass the course.

title: Anatomy & Physiology II
description: This course is the second of a two-course sequence (Spring, Summer) in anatomy and physiology (A&P). title: Clinical Applications III
description: This course is the third of a three-course sequence (Spring, Summer, Fall) in clinical medicine application that will challenge students to integrate their didactic learning with patient care experiences and simulated patient exercises. Coursework is designed to provide the student with opportunities to apply their knowledge and skills. Small group tutorials will utilize case-based learning strategies to emphasize integration of material presented during the semester. Students will perform focused patient evaluations, honing their clinical decision making and medical documentation skills. Fall II - Phase I.

title: Clinical Practice Experience Rotation II
description: Supervised Clinical Practice Experience Rotations are each a five-week required clinical rotation for second year PA students which focus on the role of the physician assistant in each of the following clinical settings: family medicine/internal medicine, women’s health, pediatrics, behavioral medicine, emergency medicine, surgery and  and two electives. The student will actively engage in applying the medical knowledge and clinical skills gained during the didactic year while continuing to develop clinical reasoning through an evidence-based approach to the evaluation and management of patients in ambulatory and in-patient hospital care settings. Students will also work to incorporate health promotion and disease prevention into patient care and advocate for healthy lifestyles - 5 week session. Spring II - Phase II.

title: Capstone
description: A cumulative and integrative scholarly and/or applied project that demonstrates a synthesis of concepts, methods, and experiences gained in the didactic portion of the program. The research project results in a scholarly project that includes a literature review, development of a research question, analysis of publicly available data, and interpretation and synthesis of the research project to address a clinical gap. In addition to class time, students will work independently with the instructor and other PA faculty to address project-specific student needs. Fall II - Phase II.

title: Behavioral Medicine
description: This course focuses on psychosocial issues of health and provides an overview of behavioral, psychological, and social sciences related to health and illness. Students receive instruction on providing culturally-sensitive care to individuals from multiple ethnic and socioeconomic backgrounds. This course aims to expand the professional physician assistantâs psychosocial considerations during patient assessment and treatment. Fall II - Phase I.

description: Additional activity credit classes are available for members of the University's intercollegiate sports program. Athletes may receive lower division activity credit on a credit/no credit basis up to a total of 8 units. These classes are more competitive and focus on building skills and enhancing the student's knowledge of individual sports, which come from practicing or playing with a team at Dominican University of California.

title: Aikido
description: A course designed to introduce the beginner to Aikido. The course will cover fundamental principles of taking care of oneself by learning how to fall properly, and learning forwards and backwards rolls. The students will then learn basic throws, proper posture, good centering, and etiquette. Aikido is a lifelong practice and this class is designed to help students discover the art.

title: Yoga
description: This class is offered as a general introduction to yoga and includes definitions of Yoga, the comfortable seat, warming the joints, breath awareness, standing postures, forward folds, lunges and lunge-squats, abdominals, sun salutations, balance poses, backbends, side stretches, twists, seated postures, mudras (sealing the energy), bandhas (body locks), introduction to breath work, centering practice, sequencing, counter posing, meditation practice, and Sanskrit nomenclature.

title: Golf
description: Designed to be an introduction to the fundamentals of the game of golf that intermediate golfers will benefit from too. The class offers golf swing instruction with an emphasis on 'keeping it simple.' Grip, stance and club selection will be covered as well as 'playing the game' strategies. Putting, chipping, pitching, trouble shots, warm up exercises, stretching and nutrition specific for golf will be taught. Rules and etiquette of the game will be reviewed as well.

title: Beg West Coast Swing Dance
description: This class is designed to cover the fundamentals of 'Swing', and includes starter and advanced steps, sugar push basic and variations, left side pass basic and variations, underarm turn basic and variations, whip and variations, closed position basics, throw-out basics, tuck and turn basics, spins and turn techniques, musicality, body isolation techniques, social skills, connection techniques, and lead and follow communications. Swing is a versatile couples dance with lots of room for individual expression. Unlike many other dances, West Coast Swing can be danced to a variety of music: big band, blues, pop, rock, soul, country, and funk.

title: West Coast Swing II
description: The course provides a general refresher of skills covered in West Coast Swing Dance I. West Coast Swing II introduces students to more intricate patterns and footwork variations. In addition to social dancing, students will also learn the ins and outs of competitive swing dancing and have the opportunity to compete at a local venue. Time allowing, students will also be introduced to the practice of 1) tandem swing, in which one leader dances with two followers, and 2) the skill of participating in dance jams, where couples are highlighted one by one in a free form format.

title: Low Impact Aerobics
description: Improve your cardio respiratory functioning and reaction time, expand your mind (aerobics has been proven to grow gray matter) and have fun This class is designed to get you moving safely to music while protecting your joints, as one foot is always on the floor. No previous aerobic exercise or dance experience is required for this beginner level class. You will learn basic steps and movements that are combined to create the routines, and before you know it, you will have completed an hour of cardio exercise. Each class will end with a cool down, including stretches for the major muscle groups.

title: Turbo Kick
description: Students will learn Turbo 101 material which introduces basic punches, kicks, and combinations. The class will then cover an entire Turbo Kick Round that blends shadowboxing with dance moves and sports drills. Each class will cover the 11 sections of a TK Round: warm-up, punches, kicks, punches & kicks combo, turbo, recovery, finale, finesse, legs, abs, and cool-down. A course which allows students of all fitness levels to participate.

title: Foundations in Fitness
description: This class is designed to help you jump-start your personal fitness program, or expand your current one. The first part of each class will include a short lecture/discussion on one or more components of fitness. During the remainder of the class, the focus will be on how to perform key exercises and use the equipment correctly for each major muscle group. By the end of the session, you'll have the tools and practice to design a safe and comprehensive fitness program.

title: Hip Hop Hustle
description: Students will learn hip hop dance through a series of 3-4 Hip Hop Hustle Rounds over the course of the semester. The class will cover the basics of HHH dance signature moves including the Dorothy, Scrub, Chest Pop, Snake, Butterfly, Slide, Creep, Stomp, Matrix, Rodeo, Fire Thrower, Body Roll, Toe Trippinâ, Pogo, Swing Kick, etc . Each class will cover the 6 sections of a Hip Hop Hustle: warm-up, Block 1, Block 2, Block 3, The Show, abs, and cool down. Prior dance experience is not required. A course which allows students of all fitness levels to participate.

title: Ethics
description: An introduction to ethical theory in Western philosophy followed by an investigation of a range of contemporary ethical problems drawn from private life, public policy, law, medicine, and business.

title: Physical Education, Movement, Fitness and Well-Being
description: This course studies motor development from birth through adolescence; sensory-motor integration and learning; stress management, relaxation, and physical fitness; role of nutrition and sleep; influence of media on fitness; games, dance and activities for the physically challenged. Fall semesters.

title: Enviromental Ethics
description: An investigation of philosophical perspectives on humanityâs relation to nature, combined with an exploration of contemporary environmental issues and related political decision-making.

title: The Power of Words: Culture, Conditioning
description: Mundane and colloquial verbal expressions can embody harmful histories and power dynamics. Our words and narratives represent elements of larger social systems, perpetuate cultural trends, and signify historical change over time. This course explores liberation theories and practical strategies such as constructive listening and counter-storytelling that can create inclusion and belonging. Connecting social analysis with ethnographic observations in the local community, students will identify empowering narratives to promote cross-cultural understanding and social equity across many contexts. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: "Gentefied": Lessons in Community Engagement
description: The Netflix series "Gentefied" illustrates many principles that are key to mutually-beneficial community engagement. Working together to address social issues requires practicing cultural humility with diverse people and communities. Students will examine social identities and related power imbalances and the local Marin county context, especially in relationship to the Canal, a predominantly Latinx immigrant community in San Rafael. This course uses the platforms Moodle, Zoom, Slack, and Netflix. This course is a blend of three synchronous sessions and asynchronous learning. Students need reliable internet access. Recommended for new students and returning students.

title: Ethics of Housing and Homelessness
description: Homelessness in the United States has been on the rise since the 1980s and for many individuals and families, housing costs are too high. In this course, students will analyze the problem of homelessness and lack of affordable housing in light of theories of justice. Community engagement will provide lived context for student learning. Students will gain an understanding of the demographics of homelessness and the policies that have created housing segregation, shortage, and insecurity, and the resulting problem of homelessness. Drawing on theories of justice, students will examine and envision alternative ways to structure society that ensure all have safe and secure housing. This course only satisfies the CORE Examined Life requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: Leadership in Coaching
description: Leadership in Coaching provides coaching experience for students interested in pursuing a variety of roles in the coaching profession. A knowledge of sports or desire to coach sports is not necessary to succeed in this class. The majority of the class takes place in the field, actively leading local youth with Dominicanâs non-profit after-school sports program here on campus. In addition, the course includes guest speakers such as Dominican varsity coaches and facilitators through CoachingCorps and Positive Coaching Alliance. The goal of the course is to learn methods and strategies of leading, motivating, and coaching individuals and teams. Students will then apply these principles in an actual coaching setting during the Allen Sports Club. Attendance is required one day a week, either Monday, Tuesday, or Wednesday, from 3-5pm each week on the same selected day.

title: Ethics, Leadership & Meaning
description: An evaluation of the ethical implications of various theories of leadership and decision making within the context of personal meaning. Issues such as individual rights, collective responsibilities, formation of character, integrity, caring relationships and the common good will be addressed as the students explore their own leadership role in various organizational settings and as citizens in a democratic society.

title: Social Movements and Cultural Response
description: This course examines how humans respond to social injusticeâthrough social movements, cultural response, and personal action. Using a sociological lens, we will analyze how social movements serve as collective responses to structural imbalances. Through community engagement, students will be guided to recognize diverse experiences within the American culture, and understand how our local community members are impacted and respond to social forces. Students will also explore cultural resistance and movement building strategies including the use of art, music, and embodied practices that empower people to address oppressive and inequitable social conditions. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: Ethics of Citizenship and Immigration
description: What does it mean to be a U.S. citizen? Who should and should not be allowed to become one? Why? Alongside divergent philosophical approaches to human rights, human nature, and ethics in general, the course will analyze the ethical and broader philosophical background of the origins and subsequent development of the rights and responsibilities of U.S. citizens, both in theory and in practice. Simultaneously, the course will consider ethical responses to immigration, asylum, and human trafficking, especially framed in terms of human rights and the legacies of historical inequities, such as slavery. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: Ethics from the Margins
description: This applied ethics course uses the framework of ethics from the margins, an approach that puts the experience, knowledge, and voices of vulnerable people and communities at the moral center. Students analyze and act on contemporary social justice issues through examination of oppressive myths, power dynamics, and structures. Through presence and relationship-building in community, students gain first-hand knowledge from people impacted by the issues under study. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: Independent Study
description: Permission of instructor required. Faculty supervision. Fall, Spring Semesters. Online.

title: Independent Study
description: Permission of instructor required. Faculty supervision. Fall, Spring Semesters. Online.

title: Ethics and Society
description: This course will explore the ethical complexities of information and communication technology in relation to humankind. By combining real-world inquiry with creative speculation, students will probe everyday ethical dilemmas they face as digital consumers, creators, and coders, as well as relevant policy. Students explore themes such as privacy, intellectual property, social justice, free speech, artificial intelligence, and social media. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: Environmental Ethics
description: The purpose of this course is to introduce the field of environmental philosophy and ethics, to encourage philosophical and ethical reflection on environmental issues, and to stimulate healthy attitudes and action in regards to our natural environment. To do this, we will examine various environmental ethics and eco-philosophies and how they apply to various environmental issues.

title: Self, Community and Ethical Action
description: This course bridges ethical theory and ideas to action through service learning with community partner organizations, emphasizing social justice issues, their root causes, systemic solutions and the dynamic relationship between these collective concerns and our individual values and choices. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: Ethical Being, Being Ethical
description: This course offers an introduction to and a critical engagement with a variety of perspectives within moral/ethical philosophy throughout history and across the world, including an investigation into a range of practical domains -- private life, public policy, law, medicine, and business-- in which we are called to act upon our moral/ethical values. Of particular concern will be the question of how we cultivate lives of ethical awareness and action.

title: Ethics of New Technologies
description: An analysis of the ethical implications of cutting-edge technologies, ranging from those relating to bioethics (e.g., cloning) and computing (e.g., artificial intelligence), to the military (e.g., drone warfare) and industry (e.g., automation), with particular attention to social impact (e.g., access, marginalization, equity).

title: Education for Democracy
description: If knowledge is power, education should be liberating no matter oneâs circumstances. Equally, a democracy can only thrive when people are equipped to question and challenge the status quo. Through the examination of the connection between education, democracy, and the concept of freedom, students develop critical awareness of social issues such as segregation and mass incarceration. Learning from and with community leaders, we gain skills and knowledge to advocate for a more just, inclusive society. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: Ethics, Leadership and Meaning
description: An evaluation of the ethical implications of various theories of leadership and decision making within the context of personal meaning. Issues such as individual rights, collective responsibilities, formation of character, integrity, caring relationships and the common good will be addressed as the students explore their own leadership role in various organizational settings and as citizens in a democratic society.

title: Feminist Disability Ethics in Literature
description: This course examines nineteenth to twenty-first century American literature that prompts debate about ethical issues regarding gender and disability. Applying a feminist methodology that includes intersectional analysis and an ethics of care, students will analyze narrative plots to form ethical arguments regarding sexism and ableism.

title: Environmental Ethics
description: The purpose of this course is to introduce the field of environmental philosophy and ethics, to encourage philosophical and ethical reflection on environmental issues, and to stimulate healthy attitudes and action in regards to our natural environment. To do this, we will examine various environmental ethics and eco-philosophies and how they apply to various environmental issues.

title: Healthcare Ethics and Equity
description: An introduction to ethical theory in Western philosophy followed by an investigation of the communal, institutional, social policy, and global dimensions of healthcare. Through community engagement, students practice civic professionalism and gain first-hand exposure to the ways in which existing social structures impact access and equity, especially for vulnerable populations. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

description: An analysis of the ethical implications of cutting-edge technologies, ranging from those relating to bioethics (e.g., cloning) and computing (e.g., artificial intelligence), to the military (e.g., drone warfare) and industry (e.g., automation), with particular attention to social impact (e.g., access, marginalization, equity).

title: Ethical Being, Being Ethical
description: This course offers an introduction to and a critical engagement with a variety of perspectives within moral/ethical philosophy throughout history and across the world, including an investigation into a range of practical domains -- private life, public policy, law, medicine, and business-- in which we are called to act upon our moral/ethical values. Of particular concern will be the question of how we cultivate lives of ethical awareness and action.

title: Human Being, Being Human
description: Inquiry into the enduring questions of human nature including the meaning and purpose of human life, the questions of its spiritual origin and destiny, its capacities for good and evil, the scope and limits of its freedom, the nature of knowing, and the question of immortality, drawing upon a range of Western thought. Particular attention is given to the tension between classical religious and philosophical views and those stemming from modern human sciences such as psychology, sociology, and sociobiology.

title: Othering and Belonging
description: Examines forces that marginalize those who are different ("the Other") from the empowered majority in society. Historical examples along with current policies and discourses that perpetuate oppression will be contrasted with the power of lesser heard stories and the strength of vulnerable communities whose perspectives are vital to counter fears and dehumanization while creating more equitable structures for all. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: Classic and Contemporary Philosophy
description: A survey and critical analysis of some of the most profound and influential perspectives throughout the history of philosophical thought. Topics covered include epistemology, metaphysics, philosophy of mind, ethics, political philosophy, philosophy of religion, and aesthetics.

title: Othering and Belonging
description: Examines forces that marginalize those who are different ("the Other") from the empowered majority in society. Historical examples along with current policies and discourses that perpetuate oppression will be contrasted with the power of lesser heard stories and the strength of vulnerable communities whose perspectives are vital to counter fears and dehumanization while creating more equitable structures for all. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: Gender: Ethics & Politics
description: A survey and critical analysis of contemporary scholarship on the ethical and political implications of gender. The course will explore the distinctions between the various forms of feminism, such as liberal, socialist, radical, lesbian, and postmodern, as well as studies on the nature of masculinity. It will address such concepts and topics as gender socialization, gender in relation to race and class, inequality, oppression, violence, work, families, mothering and fathering, intimacy and sexuality, health and medicine, and ethics.

title: Just Learning: Education as the Practice of Freedom
description: Toni Morrison wrote that 'that if you are free, you need to free somebody else. If you have some power, then your job is to empower somebody else.â Just Learning students cultivate critical consciousness, the ability to intervene in reality in order to change it. Combining knowledge from their own lived experience with an analysis of the larger social contexts, Just Learning students empower themselves and build capacity to question injustice, advocate, and take informed action. This course uses the platforms Moodle, Medium, Slack, and Zoom and is a blend of three synchronous sessions and asynchronous learning. Students need reliable internet access. Recommended for returning students or those planning to major in Social Justice or a related major.

title: Self, Community and Ethical Action
description: This course bridges ethical theory and ideas to action through service learning with community partner organizations, emphasizing social justice issues, their root causes, systemic solutions and the dynamic relationship between these collective concerns and our individual values and choices. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: Independent Study
description: The privilege of independent study is possible for students who are prepared to undertake it based on student need or interest with approval of the Chair of the department, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. The study must be in an area not covered by a regularly scheduled course. Course may be taken for 1-3 units. The maximum number of units that can be earned in one semester by independent study is 3; a total of up to six units of independent study may be applied to the degree.

title: Capstone
description: Research-based culminating project guided by a faculty mentor. Prerequisite: CASC 3405

title: Teaching Assistant
description: The privilege of serving as a teaching assistant is available to students who are interested with the approval of the Department Chair, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. A contract between the student and the instructor to determine goals, specifics on project contents/materials, and evaluation process will be required at time of request. Course may be taken for 1-3 units.

title: Earth Science
description: An introduction to earth science emphasizing physical processes that affect the world and the way humans live on it. This course investigates the nature and origin of the earth and earth materials, the forces that create change on land and in the oceans, and how we have come to understand these processes. We study the causes of change (floods, earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, and climate change), the impact those changes have on the world around us, and what can be done to mitigate their hazards. The class ends with study of the oceans and atmosphere, and the particular challenges facing California. This provides an opportunity for students to see how an understanding of scientific principles informs good citizenship - 3 lecture hours. Recommended for non-Biological Sciences or non-Chemistry majors. PHSC 2200 / PHSC 2205 meet the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirements. PHSC 2200 / PHSC 2205 are designed to meet all requirements for the Earth Science portion of the California Multiple Subject Teaching Credential when taken in combination with BIO 1400 / BIO 1405 and PHYS 1000 / PHYS 1005 .

title: Bay Area Rocks: Geology of Northern California
description: This class investigates the unique ecosystems of the Bay Area and Northern California. It explores how the geology and physical environment of California have and continue to shape the way people live here. Topics include natural disasters, resource use and allocation, water rights, development limitations and energy generation - 3 lecture hours. PHSC 3210 / PHSC 3215 meet the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirements.

title: Virtuosity: Cultivating Moral & Political Character
description: What does it mean to be morally virtuous? This course will explore a range of philosophical and scientific responses to that question, paying particular attention to the notion that moral virtue ought to be understood as a kind of intellectual and practical expertise, or, in other words, as virtuosity.

title: Independent Study:Physical Sciences
description: Directed reading on specialized topics or development of a set of special laboratory skills. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. PHSC 4999 also available for upper division credit with approval. Contract required.

title: Conceptual Physics Lab
description: Lab component of PHYS 1000 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $25 Fee to cover lab materials. PHYS 1000 / PHYS 1005 meet the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirements. PHYS 1000 / PHYS 1005 are designed to meet all requirements for the Physical Science portion of the California Multiple Subject Teaching Credential when taken in combination with BIO 1400 / BIO 1405 and PHSC 2200 / PHSC 2205 .

title: Bay Area Rocks: Geology of Northern California Lab
description: Lab component of PHSC 3210 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $25 Fee to cover on-campus lab materials. PHSC 3210 / PHSC 3215 meet the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirements.

title: Independent Study: Physical Sciences
description: Directed reading on specialized topics or development of a set of special laboratory skills. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Course may be repeated for credit. PHSC 2999 also available for lower division credit with approval. Contract required.

title: Conceptual Physics
description: An introductory physical science course, this course emphasizes a conceptual and intuitive understanding of physical science principles related to common physical phenomenon. Students build scientific knowledge and critical and creative thinking skills through participation in demonstrations and discussions. Topics will include: laws of motion; conservation of energy and momentum; light, sound and wave properties; atomic structure; properties of matter, heat, electricity and magnetism; and an introduction to atomic and nuclear physics - 3 lecture hours. Recommended for non-Biological Sciences or non-Chemistry majors. PHYS 1000 / PHYS 1005 meet the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirements. PHYS 1000 / PHYS 1005 are designed to meet all requirements for the Physical Science portion of the California Multiple Subject Teaching Credential when taken in combination with BIO 1400 / BIO 1405 and PHSC 2200 / PHSC 2205 .

title: General Physics II
description: This is part two of a two-part introductory physics course that covers sound, electricity, magnetism and modern physics - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: PHYS 2000 / PHYS 2005 . Recommended only for students for whom this is a required course and those interested in a pre-health professional program.

title: General Physics I Lab
description: Lab component of PHYS 2000 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $25 Fee to cover lab materials. PHYS 2000 / PHYS 2005 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: Internship: Philosophy
description: Students gain practical, hands-on training in their particular area of career or volunteer interest by working with an organization of their choosing, in consultation with their academic advisor and the Career and Internship office. After meeting with the academic advisor, students meet with the Career and Internship office to fill out the necessary paperwork and, then, after securing all necessary signatures, submit an add/drop form along with the internship paperwork to the Registrar. This course cannot be accessed via the self-service registration cart. 1-3 units.

title: General Physics II Lab
description: Lab component of PHYS 2100 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $25 Fee to cover lab materials.

title: Earth Science Lab
description: Lab component of PHSC 2200 - 3 lab hours. This course has an additional $25 Fee to cover lab materials. PHSC 2200 / PHSC 2205 meets the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirements. PHSC 2200 / PHSC 2205 are designed to meet all requirements for the Earth Science portion of the California Multiple Subject Teaching Credential when taken in combination with BIO 1400 / BIO 1405 and PHYS 1000 / PHYS 1005 .

title: Independent Study: Physics
description: Directed reading on specialized topics or development of a set of special laboratory skills. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Prerequisite: consent of the Program Chair. Contract required. Course may be repeated for credit.

title: California for All I
description: California for All examines political processes, policies, and social structures in relation to issues of equity, inclusion, and opportunity. Students will explore themes of education equity, healthy futures, and economic opportunities while serving with community partners whose missions address the opportunity gap, access to healthcare, and social mobility. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: Teaching Assistant: Physics
description: This course provides teaching experience at the undergraduate level. This course allows students to gain experience working with students both in a classroom setting and on an individual basis. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. 1 unit equals 45 hours. Prerequisites: a grade of 'B' or higher, an overall GPA of 3.0 or higher, and consent of the instructor. Contract required. Course may be repeated for credit.

title: American Politics Through Film
description: The phrase "Lights, Camera, Action" is normally associated with Hollywood and film making; however, many people do, in fact, get their ideas about politicians and the political system from the movies. There is a cause and effect relationship between what people see and what people believe. When the "lights" go out and the "camera" rolls, how much do films tell us about the political system in the United States? Through an examination of key political concepts, political institutions, and historical events students will develop an understanding of the nature of political leadership, power, institutions, and theories of decision making as expressed through popular films.

title: World Politics through Science Fiction
description: This course covers the advanced theories of international relations and global politics issues in the 21st Century through the science-fictions franchises (Star Trek, Star Wars, etc). Students apply theories and concepts to select films and television shows to appreciate their relevance to modern day world politics. (International Politics and Policy Designated)

title: General Physics I
description: This is part one of a two-part introductory physics course that covers properties of matter, mechanics, and heat - 3 lecture hours. Prerequisite: MATH 1400 . Not recommended for non-science majors. Recommended only for students for whom this is a required course and those interested in a pre-health professional program. PHYS 2000 / PHYS 2005 are CORE Equivalent Courses which may be substituted for the Science for Global Citizens CORE requirement.

title: Introduction to World Politics
description: This is an introductory course to the political science subfield of international relations (IR). Students learn about the different theories of IR, realism, liberalism, Marxism, constructivism, and feminism, in order to analyze various global political economy and security issues. The course starts out with a brief review of history, and relevant methodologies for the study of the modern world system, its institutions, as well as individual, group, and state behavior. Students learn application of theory to practice through various activities.

title: History and Politics of the Middle East
description: This course introduces students to contemporary politics in the Middle East. The course is designed to answer the following questions: What explains the dynamics of this region known as the Middle East? Why is political development so difficult to achieve in the Middle East? What are the factors contributing to the various conflicts in the region? What are the future prospects for peace in the Middle East? In order to answer these questions, students review the relevant social science theories and methodologies. First part of the course examines the role of religion, nationalism and the different political systems as well as governments found in the region to better understand the 21st Century Middle East. The second part covers the various conflicts, the Gulf Region, and U.S. involvement in the Middle East to assess the possibility for long-lasting democracy, prosperity, and peace. The course culminates with a research project to be presented at an undergraduate academic conference. (International Politics and Policy Designated, Research Designated)

title: Religion and Politics
description: Healthy patriotism and sincere faith can transform into violent racial-ethnic supremacy. Why? This course explores a range of possibilities at the intersection of the religious and the political. Through social scientific analysis of religions, including civil religion the course will trace the roots and development of religion and politics in the U.S.

title: History and Politics in Film
description: This course examines the relationship between politics and film along with the influence films have on culture, ideas, beliefs, and perceptions of history, politics and the political system. Does film hide the multiple interpretations of historical events and the political process? Does the need to present a story visually impact how people consume history and politics? Has film created a taste for history as entertainment or sensation and distorted accuracy and complexity? Students will critically review and analyze films and explore the way images of political theories and concepts are depicted. The course will have alternating themes exploring historical events, power, justice, ideology, the nature of political leadership, theories of decision making, electoral politics, domestic and international political institutions and political processes.

title: Virtuosity: Cultivating Moral & Political Character
description: What does it mean to be morally virtuous? This course will explore a range of philosophical and scientific responses to that question, paying particular attention to the notion that moral virtue ought to be understood as a kind of intellectual and practical expertise, or, in other words, as virtuosity. (Political Philosophy Designated)

title: Gender: Ethics & Politics
description: A survey and critical analysis of contemporary scholarship on the ethical and political implications of gender. The course will explore the distinctions between the various forms of feminism, such as liberal, socialist, radical, lesbian, and postmodern, as well as studies on the nature of masculinity. It will address such concepts and topics as gender socialization, gender in relation to race and class, inequality, oppression, violence, work, families, mothering and fathering, intimacy and sexuality, health and medicine, and ethics. (Political Philosophy Designated)

title: Classic and Contemporary Philosophy
description: A survey and critical analysis of some of the most profound and influential perspectives throughout the history of philosophical thought. Topics covered include epistemology, metaphysics, philosophy of mind, ethics, political philosophy, philosophy of religion, and aesthetics. (Political Philosophy Designated)

title: Bugs and Mickey Go to War: WWII Censorship and Propaganda
description: World War II is well-known for technological innovation, fascism, global diplomacy, and military conflicts. However, it also serves as an important historical example for censorship and propaganda. Cartoon characters, including those from Disney, Looney Tunes, and even Dr. Suess, were at the center in this effort. This course carefully studies censorship and propaganda across the globe during the war and asks students to critically analyze the propaganda pieces within their historical context. This course uses the platforms Moodle and Zoom, and is a blend of synchronous and asynchronous learning. Students need reliable internet access. Recommended for new and returning students.

title: American Presidency
description: Examines the institution of the presidency. Leadership, decision-making, and personal styles are used to evaluate the past, present, and future of presidential politics. Special attention to the constitutional foundation, presidential elections, relations with the press, the public, and other branches of government. The presidentâs role in domestic and foreign policy-making is explored using case studies from the modern presidency.

title: Philosophy and Science of Being Human
description: A philosophical and scientific inquiry into the nature of human being. The concept of the soul provides a thematic thread that links our selection of readings. For many, the soul is the essential locus of human existence and the most important facet of our being, yet it also has proven to be the least explicable. Our challenge in this course is to consider a range of inquiries into the nature of human being by philosophers and scientists and consider what they can tell us about the human capacity for consciousness, discovery, and expression. (Political Philosophy Designated)

title: State and Local Politics and Policy
description: Politics and policy making at the state, county, and city levels, with particular emphasis on the features unique to California and the Bay Area. Students engage with the community to address local policy problems to cultivate active civic leadership, teambuilding, and cooperation in the resolution of contemporary political problems. (American Politics and Society Designated)

title: Congress
description: Considers the role of Congress in the political system with a focus on the legislative process and behavior of legislators. A close examination of the nature of representation and an historical look into how Congress evolved is included in order to gain a better understanding of the contemporary Congress and its members.

title: American Politics, Policy & Society
description: This course analyzes American political thought, governing institutions, the political process, and political culture, from the founding of the Constitution to the present. Political participation, the concepts and theories associated with the foundations of American Government and democracy, issues and problems affecting contemporary society and the policy making process is explored. Social science methodology, specifically the methodology used by political scientists will be utilized throughout the course to study changes that have occurred throughout the development of American politics. Students participate in a "town hall" style forum to explore selected policy issues, propose solutions, and debate concerns and questions about the future of American politics.

title: Research Methods in Social Science
description: The study of social science requires attention to development of advanced research, critical thinking, argumentation, and writing skills. This course will challenge the student to reflect upon and utilize various research methods, writing formats, and styles of argumentation and critique within the context of the various sub-fields of the study of social science.

title: The American Legal System
description: We believe that our US society is governed by the 'rule of law.' This sounds simple, but in fact we have separate federal and state court judiciaries, different procedures for criminal and civil offenses, and we are subject to a patchwork of constitutional, statutory, administrative and case law requirements. This course takes the deep dive intended to provide a full understanding of how 'the rule of law' truly works in America. (American Politics and Society Designated, Law and Society Designated)

title: Political Parties and Interest Groups
description: Study of the decline of parties and the rise of interest groups in the American political system and an analysis of the role of each in the policy making process. The strategies interest groups use to influence the political process are covered along with the concept of political pluralism. Close attention is given to party in the government, party in the electorate, and party organization. Special attention is given to elections and political behavior. (American Politics and Society Designated)

title: Constitutional Criminal Procedure
description: This course addresses various constitutional criminal procedure issues such as 4th Amendment unreasonable searches and seizures, 5th Amendment compulsory self-incrimination and double jeopardy, and 6th Amendment trial by jury and assistance of counsel. In addition to exploring the development of the Supreme Courtâs treatment of constitutional criminal procedure, the course will also venture into an analysis of broader social, economic, and political issues within the criminal justice system. (Law and Society Designated)

title: Campaigns, Elections and Political Behavior
description: This course considers the role elections play in U.S. politics using historical and modern-day examples of campaigns and elections. Elections allow people an opportunity to participate in their government. But exactly what does this exercise in political choice entail? When and how do campaigns matter? Do campaigns persuade voters? How do voters interact with and influence campaigns and elections? How do campaigns determine strategy? These questions among others will be explored in order to study the dynamics of presidential and congressional campaigns, the process of running for office, and the role of the media, interest groups, and political parties in elections. In addition, social science research is used to explore the connection between elections and governing, the publicâs role, and the implications for democracy. (American Politics and Society Designated)

title: Constitutional Law: Powers, Rights & Liberties
description: This course addresses constitutional issues decided by the U.S. Supreme Court. We will investigate, analyze, and critique theories of constitutional interpretation, as well as various issues involving federalism, inherent governmental powers, freedom of speech and press, freedom of religion, economic liberty, gun rights, equal protection, voting, sexual privacy/autonomy, and abortion. (Law and Society Designated)

title: Model United Nations
description: This course is designed to prepare students for participation in a Model United Nations (MUN) conference. Coursework will entail a study of the United Nations system, the role of the United Nations in regional, international, and global diplomacy, current issues in world politics, and the policy issues of specific countries. (International Politics and Policy Designated)

title: American Foreign Policy & National Security
description: This course covers the history of the American foreign policy machine. The role of the President, foreign policy bureaucracy in relationship to Congress and public opinion is explored throughout. The first part of the course covers the foreign policy analysis and process from the birth of the United States through the Cold War. The second part of the course reviews U.S. foreign policy from the end of the Cold War to present day with a focus on the formulation, organization, and implementation of U.S. national security policy through an examination of contemporary case studies. Topics include challenges posed by rogue and failed states, peacekeeping, terrorism, arms control, military alliances, and the role of international institutions. The course culminates with a research project to be presented at an undergraduate academic conference. (International Politics and Policy Designated, Research Designated)

title: Tech Law: Law for the 21st Century
description: The rise of the Internet, wireless communications, and social media has dramatically altered how we live in the Digital Age. This course explores a wide variety of fast-breaking and cutting-edge legal tech issues, including but not limited to cyber risks and online liabilities, Information technology and intellectual property (patent, copyright, trademark and trade secrets). Rights and responsibilities will be explored, as well as freedom of speech and privacy in the Digital Age with a focus on risks and liabilities encountered on the information highway. Students will learn how to navigate in the tech world personally and on behalf of businesses and they will become aware of potential danger spots and how to address them from a legal standpoint. (Law and Society Designated)

title: Politics of Developing Countries
description: This course focuses on the common problems facing developing countries and is designed to answer the following questions: What is development? What factors contribute to development? Why are some countries more successful in achieving development? What are some major problems that keep some countries from developing? How does colonialism account for development? Is economic development necessary for political development? Is political development necessary for economic development? How does the developed world contribute to the success or failure of lesser-developed countries? In order to answer these questions, the first part of the course introduces the approaches, methodologies, and theories used to understand development. The second part covers the myriad of issues facing the developing world with examples from Asia, Latin America, Middle East and Africa to search for solutions. The course culminates with a research project to be presented at an undergraduate academic conference. (International Politics and Policy Designated, Research Designated)

title: International Political Economy
description: This course examines how the "international system" works, focusing upon patterns that exist in economic and political behavior. Students will explore the links between localized events and larger global trends in the history and contemporary dynamics of international relations, including not only the activities of governments but also of businesses and non-governmental organizations. In particular, the course addresses issues of trade and monetary policies. MNCâs, environment, money and finance. (International Politics and Policy Designated)

title: Women in Politics
description: This course explores the role of women in politics around the world. Special focus is applied to women in executive and legislative leadership roles in government. Students will employ relevant social science theories and methods to examine the institutional, structural, and cultural differences in electoral systems for select countries. The comparative analysis educates on the success and challenges that women face in reaching high office. The course culminates with a research project to be presented at an undergraduate academic conference. (International Politics and Policy Designated, Research Designated)

title: Politics of the Middle East
description: The course on Politics in the Middle East is designed to answer the following questions: What explains the dynamics of this region known as the Middle East? Why is political development so difficult to achieve in the Middle East? What are the factors contributing to the Arab-Israeli conflict? What are the future prospects for war and peace in the Middle East? The course is divided into two parts. The first part reviews the role of religion and nationalism and examines the different political systems and governments found in the region to better understand the 21st Century Middle East. The second part covers the Arab-Israeli conflict, the Gulf Region, and U.S. involvement in the Middle East to assess the possibility for long-lasting democracy, prosperity, and peace.

title: Ethnicity, Nationalism & Conflict
description: This course introduces the relevant theories, approaches, and methodologies for the study of ethnicity, nation, nationhood and nationalism, including primordialism, instrumentalism, and constructivism. Students also learn about the various factors that contribute ethno-religious conflicts to understand why some groups around the world fight each other while others coexist in peace. The course culminates with a research project to be presented at an undergraduate academic conference. (International Politics and Policy Designated, Research Designated)

title: Politics and the Media
description: Analysis of the ideas and concepts associated with how the media covers political information and how political actors use the media. Concepts including agenda setting, priming, and framing are examined. Special attention is given the role of the media during elections. (American Politics and Society Designated)

title: Power, Order, & Justice
description: Students will explore historical and contemporary problems faced by political actors, governmental institutions, and the citizenry using the fundamental concepts for the study of politics.

title: Comparative Politics
description: This course is in the theories and concepts of comparative politics as well as the methodologies applied to understand social science phenomena in different regions of the world. Students learn about the political and economic systems, institutions, structures of government, as well as group and human behavior in specific countries through a process of comparison. The course covers theories of democracy, fascism, capitalism, and communism. Students will study five different countries according to regime types, including advanced industrialized democracies, authoritarian communist and post-communist states. The course culminates with a research project to be presented at an undergraduate academic conference. (International Politics and Policy Designated, Research Designated)

title: International Law
description: A survey and analysis of the theoretical framework, institutional structure, and history of the international legal system. Pays particular attention to: nation-states; international organizations; individuals; treaties; human rights; nationality; territory and jurisdiction; law of the sea, airspace, outer space, and celestial bodies; economics; the environment; crime; diplomacy; security; war and peace. (International Politics and Policy Designated, Law and Society Designated)

title: Politics of Europe
description: This course introduces students to the diversity of political institutions and practices that exist in Europe. Focus is primarily on the European Union, as well as the political systems of select countries in the region. Ultimately, the course strives to familiarize students to the historical, structural, and societal explanations behind the political institutions, policies, and behaviors found in Europe. (International Politics and Policy Designated)

title: Environmental History and Law
description: This course focuses on an historic overview of the environmental movement along with the study of the principal environmental federal and state statutes, as well as rights provided under the common law and the Constitution. The origins of the American idea of "common space;" the struggle between Native Americans and Europeans over land; origins and development of the National Park system; conflicts between industry and environmentalists; and womenâs groups in urban beautification is considered. Current legal trends in environmental justice, takings, and protection for endangered species is examined. (Law and Society Designated)

title: Political Psychology
description: In this course, we will cover key foundations of psychological phenomena related to political ideology on both an individual and global level. Topics covered include political identity, ideology, moral reasoning, voting behavior, international relations, and the role of culture. This course is designed as a curated overview of Political Psychology as its own discipline, familiarizing students with some of the debates in the field and introducing them to material not encountered elsewhere, opening up new lines of inquiry and providing inspiration for future research. Prerequisite: PSY 1100

title: Debate Theory into Practice I
description: A debate course devoted to the development of advanced debate skills. May be repeated for credit.

title: Debate Theory into Practice III
description: A debate course devoted to the development of advanced debate skills. May be repeated for credit.

title: Debate Theory into Practice IV
description: A debate course devoted to the development of advanced debate skills. May be repeated for credit.

title: Thesis Continuation
description: If after taking POL 4911 or IS 4911 a student has not completed the senior project, s/he enrolls in this course in order to complete. The reader must be consulted prior to enrolling to make sure s/he will be available during the semester in which the student wishes to complete. Please note that most readers are unavailable during the summer. Also note, students who do not complete the project during thesis continuation are required to re-enroll in POL 4911or IS 4911 for 4 units at the full course rate. This course has a flat fee. Check with Business Services for the current fee

title: Introduction to Psychology
description: Basic methods and concepts of psychology, focusing on research findings and theoretical viewpoints. Topics include theories of personality, psychological development, personality disorders, social psychology, cognition, motivation and emotion, perception and learning, biological and socio-cultural bases of behavior. Prerequisite for most upper division psychology courses.

title: Civic Action Fellowship
description: In this course, Civic Action Fellows learn through a semester of civically-engaged service with a specific community partner. Open for enrollment only to students with an Americorps Agreement in place.

title: Independent Study
description: The privilege of independent study is possible for students who are prepared to undertake it based on student need or interest with approval of the Chair of the department, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. The study must be in an area not covered by a regularly scheduled course. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. The maximum number of units that can be earned in one semester by independent study is 4; a total of up to six units of independent study may be applied to the degree.

title: Freshman Seminar: Foundations of Critical Thinking in Psychology
description: Review and practice critical thinking skills necessary to evaluate psychological information and skills needed to write in psychology. Topics include evaluating contrasting viewpoints, formulating research hypotheses and developing literature reviews. Introduction to the use of the digital portfolio within the psychology major and selection of academic works to include in the portfolio.

title: Psychology of Dreams
description: Exploration of both research and theoretical models of sleep and dreaming; application of dream theory to aid students in understanding their own dreams.

title: Debate Theory into Practice II
description: A debate course devoted to the development of advanced debate skills. May be repeated for credit.

title: Effective Communication
description: Practical, hands-on training in the essentials of interpersonal communication, including active listening, outgoing messages, and de-escalating conflict.

title: Human Response to Natural Disaster
description: An interdisciplinary overview of disasters and the economic, social and psychological impact that such events have on human populations.

title: Psychology of Death & Dying
description: Introduction to models of grief and mourning, psychological understanding of the dying process and funeral rites, and an exploration of personal attitudes toward death and dying.

title: Psychology of Prejudice
description: A review of theories and research findings addressing the complex issue of prejudice.

title: Psychology of Money
description: Explores the principles and psychodynamics of our relationship with money from historical, psychological, cultural, and philosophical perspectives. Disentangling emotional from financial issues in order to achieve an optimum balance of emotional well-being and financial success.

title: Creating Your Future
description: Strategies for participating fully in creating the life of your dreams: ways of living a more meaningful and deeply fulfilling life. Processes for exploring what you want and value, including practices for generating these results.

title: Psychology of Personal Development
description: Application of developmental models of psychology to understanding oneâs own growth throughout the lifespan.

title: Stress Management
description: Provides understanding of a variety of relaxation techniques; students develop a personal stress management plan.

title: Positive Psychology
description: An introduction to the scientific study of optimal human functioning. Topics include happiness, flow, signature strengths, optimism, characteristics of healthy relationships. Emphasis is on the application of positive psychology research findings to real-life experiences.

title: Language Development
description: We will explore the development of language in humans, from infancy to adulthood. We will also discuss techniques for fostering language development in children, bilingual development and the benefits of bilingualism, and comparisons between human language and animal communication.

title: Health Campaigns and Persuasion
description: Discussion of theory and research on design, execution, and evaluation of effective, as well as ineffective, health campaigns and public service announcements. Topics include source, message, and audience characteristics that determine success; effective message design; and other strategies for health behavior change, such as cognitive behavioral therapy and implementation intentions. Instruction includes lectures, video, activities. Students will design, create, and present a media campaign focused on a health behavior of their choosing; they will then propose a study to assess its potential effectiveness.

title: Environment and Behavior
description: An overview of the field of Environmental Psychology with an emphasis on the topics of personal space and territoriality, environmental stressors such as crowding, noise and high temperatures, and the restorative aspects of natural environments.

title: Psychology of Happiness
description: An introduction to psychological research on happiness, optimism and signature strengths. Includes an overview of positive psychology. Emphasis is on the application of research findings to enhancing happiness and relationships.

title: Social Influence
description: An introduction to classic social psychology topics such as persuasion, conformity, and obedience, with special emphasis on compliance tactics used in advertising and sales.

title: Psychology of Happiness
description: This course reviews various models of creativity and explores the psychological characteristics of creative individuals. We explore the measurement of creativity, the role of education in enhancing or discouraging creativity, links between creativity and mental illness and we use various techniques and exercises to build our own creative repertoire.

title: Why We Age
description: Explores the biological causes of aging.Â  Introduces evolutionary, genetic and cellular theories of why organisms grow old and die.Â  Reviews the effects of aging on the mind and brain andÂ considers various attempts at preventing and delaying the aging process.

title: Awakening Intuition
description: Throughout history, people have accessed their intuition to make important discoveries and contributions in science, art, literature, music, business, and all fields of endeavor.Â  Intuition is an invaluable skill that can be used by all to help us improve the quality of our lives.Â  In these courses you will learn how to recognize and develop your intuition in order to guide you in variousÂ  areas of your life (relationships, career, health and well-being, spirituality, decision-making, problem solving, financial prosperity, sports, creativity, etc.).Â

title: Psychology of Evolution
description: What is the connection between Psychology and Evolution?Â  This issue will be introduced, along with how it relates to natural selection and Darwin's theory of Evolution.Â  Topics will include how genes, the environment, and heredity combine to affect behaviorÂ in both animals and humans.Â

title: Healing Relationships
description: Explores the principles, systems, and psychodynamics of our primary relationships, focusing on ways to understand and enhance the well-being of our relationships.

title: Temperament & Child Behavior
description: An examination of inborn traits and how temperament affects behavior and development throughout childhood. Interactions between temperament and the environment are explored in terms of "goodness of fit" in creating positive relationships.

title: Addiction: Recovery issues
description: Designed to provide students with an introduction to addiction, including the nature of addiction, its history, biology, inter/intra personal, and social aspects.

title: Managing Anxiety
description: The physiology of anxiety and factors which perpetuate it. Students learn specific techniques for handling feelings of anxiety, and discussion topics include phobias, test anxiety, and obsessive-compulsive behaviors.

title: Psychology and Law
description: Exploration of the interface between law and psychology and the role of mental health issues in the legal system. Topics vary and may include jury selection, domestic relations, sexual harassment, sexual offenders, competency to stand trial, etc.

title: Behavior modification
description: A brief history of the beginnings of Behaviorism will be presented, introducing Classical and Operant conditioning. This will be followed by lecture and discussion of Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy and its application in the classroom, especially in dealing with issues of teaching in a special education setting.

title: Animal Cognition
description: An introduction to cognitive abilities of non-human species. A review of various examples of intelligent behavior in other species, including spatial cognition and navigation, time perception, problem solving, tool use, culture and language. We will look at empirical evidence for intelligence in the behavior of both captive and wild animals. (Note: PSY 3041 meets Group 1 requirement).

title: Psychology of Homicide
description: This entirely on-line course is an overview of some of the psychological theories of why people commit homicide. We will consider the role of various factors â developmental, genetic, neurological, social, cultural, etc. in acts of murder. We will review some of the research into what makes a murder and discuss the interaction of nature and nurture in creating killers. This course will be composed of a set of recorded lectures and films that the student can go through on their own pace.

title: Suicide: Prevention & Intervention
description: Using both lecture and classroom discussion, this course will examine subtle and overt warning signs of suicidal behavior. Appropriate levels of intervention will be paired with the degree of threat so that students will learn how to safely intervene. A variety of perspectives on this sensitive topic will be thoughtfully explored, with careful attention to a broad range of cultural issues.

title: Infancy
description: Human growth and development from conception to two years, exploring the biological, cognitive, emotional, linguistic, motor, perceptual, and social dimensions of development in infancy. Emphasis on innovative methods of assessing cognitive, social-emotional, and linguistic development in infants, and theories of developmental change. Additional topics include resiliency in infancy and cross-cultural approaches to rearing infants.

title: Hormones and Behavior
description: Examination of how hormones influence human behavior. Behaviors to be considered include sex and reproduction, cognition, sleep and arousal, response to stress and aggression. The role of hormones during development and throughout the lifespan will also be considered. (Note: PSY 3047 meets Group 1 requirement).

title: Visual & Auditory Illusions
description: A primer of visual processing, this course introduces the reasons and theories for why we sometimes don't always see what is actually there. Mechanisms responsible for visual illusions ranging from physiological to environmental/contextual to cultural will be discussed. Several examples of illusions will be presented, both with their explanations and how to create them.

title: Cross Cultural Psychology
description: The comparative study of cultural effects on human psychology. Examines psychological diversity and the ways in which particular human activities (behavior, family life, education, social experience, etc.) are influenced by social and cultural factors.

title: Psychology of Sport Injury
description: This course will examine the psychological factors involved with sports-related injuries. Theory and research will be discussed as well as responses to injury and applied strategies for helping athletes through the recovery process. We will learn how mental training skills such as motivation, goal setting, relaxation, and imagery can help empower athletes, provide them with a sense of control over their recovery, and aid in the healing process.

title: Advanced Experimental Design
description: Complex experimental data analysis leading to the interpretation of causal research designs. Students will use SPSS to analyze data. Topics include Between and Within - groups ANOVA, analytical comparisons, and factorial designs exploring interactions among variables. Prerequisites PSY 3187 and PSY 3191 .

title: Psychology of Emotion
description: This course explores the psychological and evolutionary basis of emotions. We will consider the role of emotions in everyday life, the evolutionary history and universality of emotions, brain areas involved in emotions and various psychological theories of emotions.

title: Psychology of Travel
description: This class will explore the psychological aspects of travel, including travel motivations (escapism, social needs, personal growth and challenge), factors related to destination choice, and both positive and negative outcomes (restorative benefits, self-esteem, culture shock). Additionally, related topics like cognitive mapping, and cross-cultural differences in social customs will be explored.

title: Science Communication
description: Science is touted as the solution to all the worldâs problems, but one problem that scientists canât seem to solve is: how do you tell people about your research? Scientific journals donât suffice, science blogs are only a little better, popular media has wide reach but is fraught with issues. This course addresses how to effectively, engagingly, and accurately communicate scientific findings to a variety of audiences.

title: Psychology of Mentoring
description: This course explores the psychological, social, and developmental aspects of mentoring relationships. We will discuss benefits of mentorship to both mentors and protÃ©gÃ©s and consider these benefits for individuals across the lifespan including youth and college mentoring, career mentoring, and intergenerational partnerships. Students in this course will develop an understanding of theoretical perspectives on mentoring as well as consider implications for building high quality mentoring programming and improving their own mentoring relationships.

title: Psychology of Hope
description: This course explores the psychological construct of hope and what it means to be hopeful. Calling upon research from the field of positive psychology, as well as using an interdisciplinary lens to capture definitions of hope from fields such as nursing, theology, and political science, we will discuss hope from multiple perspectives and learn practical strategies for becoming more hopeful.

title: Social Media, Networks & Social Support
description: This course explores the role social media and online social networking play in our interpersonal relationships and well-being. We consider theory and empirical findings to investigate the costs and benefits of using social media for seeking social support, developing a sense of self, and connecting with information across the lifespan.

title: Psychology of Pregnancy
description: This course will explore the experience of pregnancy from both psychological and systems theory perspectives. We will examine the history and the psychology of pregnancy and birth practices in America. We will discuss issues such as high risk pregnancy, still birth, prematurity, postpartum depression, cultural issues and expectations, among others. This course will require students to think critically about the societal complexities of pregnancy and the transition to parenthood

title: Psychology of Fantasy Football
description: This class will examine the psychology behind the worldâs most popular fantasy sports gameâfantasy football, which has roughly 60 million active participants worldwide. Topics covered include peopleâs motivation to play fantasy football, cognitive biases that affect decision-making (e.g., which players to draft or play), and research-based strategies to help players avoid psychological pitfalls. Instruction will include lectures, readings, discussions, and activities. Students will be required to create and manage a fantasy football team during the semester.

title: Special Topics Organizational Psychology
description: Explore the foundations, applications, and key principles of organizational psychology. Topics covered include: careers in organizational psychology; assessment; group dynamics; leadership; and organizational culture.
