Class will include experiential activities, lecture, discussion, and opportunities for practice.

title: Madness in the Movies
description: This course will explore the portrayal and treatment of persons with mental illness in the mass media, particularly in film. Discussion will focus on the perpetuation of stereotypes and stigmatization of marginalized populations commonly seen in the movies.

title: Psychology of Emotional Development
description: This course includes a brief summary of the neurobiology of emotions and considers inborn temperament vs. family environment and life events in the development of emotional states. Topics include: ranges of emotions from indifference to flooding; how emotions can drive behavior; ways emotions such as rage or melancholy can be modified; how positive emotions can be enhanced and developed over a lifetime. This is an interactive lecture and discussion course, drawing examples from the instructor's clinical cases and the student's own observation and experience.

title: Foundations of Social Work
description: A general social work course with an introduction to history, theory, fields of practice working with individual families, groups and organizations, values and skills of the profession. This course defines the concept of human diversity, social chance and social systems. This course is designed to establish the skills, knowledge base and values for those interested in social work and related fields.

title: Body Image and Stigma
description: This course will examine the impact of weight stigma on mental health. Topics covered include sizeism and size based discrimination, diet culture, eating disorders, Health at Every Size (HAES), and the body positivity movement.

title: Psychology of Romantic Relationships
description: The broad spectrum of theories of romantic partnerships, including many of the components that predict relationship satisfaction and success. Includes how we view ourselves and our partners when in relationships, as well as how we view the relationship as a whole, as seen through the lenses of the varied stages of courtship.

title: Psychology of Monsters
description: Psychology of Monsters reviews what we know about the causes and consequences of conditions that in the popular imagination are considered monstrosities. We will consider the physiological roots of myths about zombies, werewolves, vampires, and other monsters, and their role in history, literature and popular culture.

title: Neuropsychology
description: This course covers the basics of neuropsychological functioning. Topics will include: an overview of the nervous system, cognitive and behavioral effects of various types of brain damage, neurological bases of mental disorders and neurological testing.

title: Theories of Personality:History & Systems II
description: The historical evolution of psychology and the various positions and theories which fall under the heading of "personality." Emphasis on primary source readings and on comparison and contrast among various viewpoints in the growth of the discipline. Theorists include Watson, Skinner, Sullivan, Allport, Bowlby, Rogers, humanists, and cognitive theorists. Prerequisite: PSY 1100 and PSY 3111 (or by permission of the instructor).

title: Culture and Psychology
description: This course aims to introduce students to how culture influences human psychology. We will explore the similarities and differences between (and within) cultures as well as the underlying psychological mechanisms. Students will learn different approaches to studying the impact of culture on human behavior and explain the role of culture in many areas of psychology (e.g., social, cognitive, etc.). Overall, students will be able to acquire an understanding of psychological studies of culture.

title: Counseling and Communication
description: Development of individual and group counseling skills, such as effective listening, responsible self-expression, building trust, sensitivity to individual value systems. Various theoretical approaches to counseling and communication are presented through readings, films, tapes, and experiential exercises.

title: Theories of Personality: History and Systems
description: The historical evolution of psychology and the various positions and theories which fall under the heading of "personality". Emphasis on primary source readings and on comparison and contrast among various viewpoints in the growth of the discipline. Prerequisite: PSY 1100 (or by permission of the instructor)

title: Adult Development & Aging
description: Human growth and development from early to late adulthood, including aging, death and dying. Includes social, biological, moral, familial, vocational, sexual, religious, and personal processes as they appear and are given significance within the developmental process. (NOTE: Meets Group II requirement.)

title: Child & Adolescent Development
description: Human growth and development, from prenatal development through adolescence, focusing on biological, cognitive, and social-emotional processes within the context of family, culture and society. This course combines theory and research with practical applications. Part of the curriculum for this course will include outside-of-class activities that may include (but not be limited to) the following: projects, analysis of readings and films, and/or participation in online discussion forums. All students must have access to the Moodle course-organization program.

title: Psychological Meaning of Everyday Objects
description: This course will look at the many material objects with which we are surrounded in our daily life and these objects influence our sense of self, our gender identity and our place in the world. We will learn to 'read' the meaning of items such as clothing, toys, household contents, technological devices, decorative arts, automobiles and many others. How are these objects reflective of human needs and aspirations? What aspects of our personality and our culture cause us to choose and/or create particular items? This will be and interesting and active class.

title: Lifespan Development
description: Human development over the lifespan, from prenatal development through adolescence, adulthood, including aging, death and dying. Emphasis on theory and research regarding developmental milestones in the cognitive, social and emotional realms. (Psychology majors are encouraged to take either PSY 3120 or PSY 3121 instead of this course).

title: Family Dynamics
description: A systems approach to marriage and family relationships focusing on psychological processes and communication systems which promote or restrict intimacy and health.

title: Abnormal Psychology
description: The psychology of mental illness: psychoses, depression, anxiety, personality and many other disorders will be explored using texts, readings, films, and case histories. Part of the curriculum for this course will include outside-of-class activities that may include (but not be limited to) the following: projects, analysis of readings and films, and/or participation in online discussion forums. All students must have access to the Moodle course-organization program. (NOTE: Meets Group II requirement.)

title: Behavioral Health
description: This course will provide students with a background on the issues of community health and the cultural norms that may effect health behavior. Using a bio-psychosocial perspective we will examine biological, psychological, social, cultural, and policy influences on health behavior and on one's overall physical and mental health.

title: Social Psychology
description: Social influences on motives, attitudes, and behavior. Topics include attitude formation and change, propaganda, aggression, altruism, prejudice, mob psychology, affiliation, friendship, and love. Lectures, films, and activities. (NOTE: Meets Group II requirement.)

title: Small Group Interaction
description: Analysis of small group dynamics: structure, process, roles, alliances, shared assumptions, group communication, and leadership. Lectures, demonstrations, and practical group experience.

title: Child & Adolescent Development. - Non-PSY Majors
description: Human growth and development, from prenatal development through adolescence, focusing on biological, cognitive, and social-emotional processes within the context of family, culture and society. This course combines theory and research with practical applications.

title: Organizational Psychology
description: The application of principles of industrial and social psychology to organizational settings. Topics covered include: motivation of workers; group decision-making; leadership styles; selection of personnel; career management and organizational development. Experiential exercises combined with lecture, discussion, and demonstration.

title: Cross-Cultural Communication
description: Examination of ethnic, racial, cultural, socio-economic, and sexual diversity as they influence and determine effective and appropriate communication strategies and processes in counseling or management practices.

title: Psychology of Learning
description: An historical look at how the school of Behaviorism changed and impacted early psychology, including a review of the research on classical conditioning conducted by Pavlov and the work on operant conditioning conducted by Skinner. Applications of this research in everyday contexts and clinical settings will be examined. Prerequisite PSY 1100 . (NOTE: Meets Group I requirement.)

title: Psychological Testing & Assess
description: Principles of psychological test construction: norms, reliability, validity, item analysis; ethical issues in psychological testing; survey of intelligence, aptitude, achievement, personality, interest, and clinical measures.

title: Sensation and Perception
description: Analysis and comparison of the several theoretical approaches explaining sensation and perceptual processing. Topics include the physiological correlates of perception, cognitive influences on perception, perceptual illusions, perception of space and form, and how the modalities are integrated in forming our perceptions. Prerequisite PSY 1100 . (NOTE: Meets Group I requirement.)

title: Junior Seminar
description: Analysis of research, fieldwork, career, and graduate school opportunities in psychology. Course design includes lecture, discussion, role playing/experiential exercises, and interviewing assignments. Prerequisite PSY 1100 . Limited to junior psychology majors.

title: Sophomore Seminar: Psychology of Career Choice
description: The role of skills, interests, and values in establishing successful career goals. Course design includes goal setting, networking, behavioral contracting, and establishing support systems. This course is designed for sophomore psychology majors and non-majors who are interested in choosing a career consistent with their personality, talents and values.

title: Statistics for Health & Behavioral Sciences
description: Descriptive and inferential statistics as they are applied to a variety of research designs in the health and behavioral sciences. Topics include data summary and presentation, measures of central tendency and variability, correlation and regression, probability, sampling and hypothesis testing and using a computer software package to analyze data sets. Prerequisite - MATH 1210 or equivalent with a grade of "C" (2.0) or better, or appropriate placement test score. Adult Degree Completion students: MATH 1350 or equivalent with a grade of "C" (2.0) or better, or appropriate placement test score.

title: Research Methods
description: Methods of investigation in the behavioral sciences. Includes experimental, observational, survey, and unobtrusive methodologies. The course focuses on the major steps in the research process, research design, and ethics. Prerequisite: PSY 3187 .

title: STA: Social/Psychology: People of the Mediterranean Area
description: **Study Travel Course** This course will focus on the social, cultural, and psychological characteristics of those who live around the Mediterranean Sea. We will explore the collective experiences and individual differences in religious and cultural practices, politics, social customs, dietary and culinary preferences, child rearing and family practices, and housing.

title: Mediation, Negotiation
description: The practical use of effective communication, negotiation, and mediation skills to improve interpersonal relations and to intervene in resolving conflicts. The focus is on developing skills in dealing effectively and ethically with a wide range of conflict situations.

title: Natural Disasters: Reaction to Risk
description: The social, economic, political and psychological impact of natural hazards on human populations; topics include the natural forces which cause such disasters, areas that are most at-risk from certain hazards, risk perception, disaster preparedness, coping and recovery.

title: Advances in Positive Psychology
description: The scientific study of optimal human functioning and the strengths and virtues that enable individuals and communities to thrive. Includes research on positive emotions, happiness, compassion, and gratitude as well as practical techniques.

title: Intro to Cognitive Processes
description: Explores the psychological processes involved in pattern recognition, attention, memory, human learning, problem solving, language development, verbal communication and decision making. Emphasis is placed on the process of conducting research to support theory, and how new evidence leads to modifications in old theory. Prerequisite PSY 1100 . (NOTE: Meets Group I requirement.)

title: Environmental Psychology
description: The impact of the natural or physical environment on behavior and health, including topics such as crowding, privacy, territoriality and personal space.

title: Health Psychology
description: Focuses on the psychological influences that affect our physical health and susceptibility to illness; an analysis of the complex interactions between mind and body.

title: Human Sexuality
description: A survey of the biological, psychological, and social aspects of sexuality, including topics such as gender identity, sexual orientation, reproduction, sexually transmitted diseases, sex in the media, etc.

title: Sports Psychology
description: This course explores the application of psychology to the understanding of sports. Other topics covered include the Psychology of Play and Stress Management. The use of various strategies to enhance peak performance in sports will be extrapolated into other areas such as business, art, education, dance, teaching, relationships and basic life skills.

title: Physiological Psychology
description: The biological foundations of human behavior and psychological processes; the physiological basis of emotional disturbance, language, hemispheric specialization of the brain, emotion, aggression, hunger, sleep, and dreaming. Prerequisite: PSY 1100 . (NOTE: Meets Group I requirement.)

title: Psychopharmacology
description: Examines the effects of psychoactive drugs on neuro-chemical, mental, and behavioral processes.Â  Major depressant, stimulant, narcotic, and hallucinogenic drugs will be studied, as will drugs used to treat mental disorders such as anxiety, depression, phobias, and schizophrenia. Prerequisite: PSY 1100 . (NOTE: Meets Group I requirement.)

title: Forensic Psychology
description: This course explores the various facets of the field of forensic psychology, including models, practices and procedures. Psychological constructs in forensic psychology will be reviewed, including developmental, behavioral, cognitive, social, and biological models of understanding human behavior. The course will cover landmark legal cases relevant to the practice of forensic psychology, potential careers in psychology including expert testimony, legal consultation, and forensic assessment in the criminal justice system. The course also reviews ethical principles and dilemmas in the practice of forensic psychology, correctional psychology and working in the juvenile justice system. Relevant case law will be covered to provide a historical perspective of the increasing important role of forensic psychology in our country.

title: Media Psychology: TV & Society
description: The influence of television on social trends, attitudes, behavior, and psychological well-being is explored through an analysis of historical trends and milestones in programming as well as theory and research from the fields of Media Studies and Social Psychology. Topics include portrayal of minority groups, impact of media sex and violence, advertising, and implications of the new trend in 'Reality TV.'

title: Ecopsychology
description: Ecopsychology is an introductory course examining an individual's relationship to nature. This subfield of psychology explores how the individual is influenced by the environment and how one's psychological orientation influences interaction with the environment. The course is offered thematically (with topics including, but not limited to: water, animals, food, place, wildness, the city/wilderness, interdependency, ecofeminism) and is offered in lecture and experimental formats.

title: Comparative Psychology
description: Lecture and Discussion of Darwinâs theory of Evolution and Natural Selection, how it applies to Psychology, and how it applies to the study of behavior. The course will focus on the interaction between genes and the environment in influencing the behavior of animals, and comparing this to the behavior of humans. Topics include DNA, Mendelian genetics, natural selection, adaptation and evolution. Prerequisite: PSY 1100 . (NOTE: Meets Group I requirement.)

title: Psychological Issues of Social Work
description: This course will familiarize students to social work practice which is multi-level engagement to working in systems to effect change. This course will introduce students to the Code of Ethics and core values that govern social work practice. Students will learn how diversity and social justice are key constructs to social change.  Students will learn about the various practice areas, tasks , functions and mandates that social workers perform and follow. Students will also learn about various modalities and interventions that social workers utilize to support their client populations.

title: Psychology of Motivation and Emotion
description: Course examines the psychology of emotion and motivation from a multidisciplinary perspective, highlighting biological, developmental, cultural, social, evolutionary, and cognitive influences. Course will review research and theorizing on psychological and physiological aspects of motivation and emotion in human behavior. There is a particular focus on social-cognitive processes and how situational factors trigger various emotions and corresponding motives that then drive behavior. Topics covered include, but are not limited to, passion and love, substance use and addiction, aggression, stress, and the influence of emotion on cognition and behavior. Instruction includes lectures, video, activities. Prerequisite: PSY 1100 (NOTE: Meets Group I Requirement)

title: Senior Seminar
description: Support student's transition to post-graduation life by refining their digital portfolio and presence; discussing conference and publication options for the senior thesis; and reviewing student values and skills to optimize life after college in multiple domains, including financial, professional (resume, etc.), personal and community.

title: Field Placement
description: Application, development, and refinement of assessment, counseling, communication and consulting skills. Seminar and supervised fieldwork in an agency setting appropriate to the studentâs ability and interest. Two semesters of field placement are highly recommended for students who wish to be adequately prepared for careers in psychology and/or intend to apply to graduate school. Prerequisite: PSY 1100 , PSY 3182 .

title: Teaching Assistantship
description: Teaching assistants in Psychology assist instructors by meeting with students, assisting in grading, creating assignments, and helping with classroom activities. The privilege of serving as a teaching assistant is available to students who are interested with the approval of the Department Chair, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. A contract between the student and the instructor to determine goals, specifics on project contents/materials, and evaluation process will be required at time of request. Course may be taken for 1-4 units.

title: Research Assistantship
description: Research Assistant opportunities in psychology partner a student with new or ongoing faculty research projects with approval from the Department Chair, the instructor concerned and the Dean of the School. Students become involved in either library research, data collection, analysis, presentation or other aspects of faculty scholarly research. Course may be taken for 1-4 units.

title: Internship: Psychology
description: Internship opportunities in psychology include, but are not limited to: local human service agencies, businesses and organizations, hospitals, non-profits, and various research sites. This experience is managed by both the campus Career and Internship Services Department and a psychology department faculty member. Course may be taken for 1-4 units.

title: Psychology of Women
description: Analysis of the psychological realities of womenâs lives organized around critical issues and events in womenâs experience from infancy to adulthood and aging.

title: Political Psychology
description: In this course, we will cover key foundations of psychological phenomena related to political ideology on both an individual and global level. Topics covered include political identity, ideology, moral reasoning, voting behavior, international relations, and the role of culture. This course is designed as a curated overview of Political Psychology as its own discipline, familiarizing students with some of the debates in the field and introducing them to material not encountered elsewhere, opening up new lines of inquiry and providing inspiration for future research. Prerequisite: PSY 1100

title: Independent Study
description: The privilege of independent study is possible for students who are prepared to undertake it based on student need or interest with approval of the Chair of the department, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. The study must be in an area not covered by a regularly scheduled course. Course may be taken for 1-3 units. The maximum number of units that can be earned in one semester by independent study is 3; a total of up to six units of independent study may be applied to the degree.

title: Directed Research II
description: Supervised completion of the senior thesis project, with an emphasis on data collection, statistical analysis, interpretation of research findings, and completion of the thesis manuscript in proper American Psychological Association format. Class structure includes lectures, class discussion, individual appointments, oral and poster presentations of thesis findings. Note: no incomplete grades are given for PSY 4998 . Prerequisite: PSY 4997 .

title: Directed Research I
description: Focuses on writing a review of the psychological literature relevant to the studentâs senior thesis research topic, and designing an appropriate and ethical research design for the thesis project. Class structure includes lectures, discussion, group exercises and support, individual appointments and behavioral contracting. Note: no incomplete grades are given for PSY 4997 . Prerequisites: PSY 1100 , PSY 3187 , PSY 3191 .

title: Evolutionary Psychology
description: Explores the biological basis of animal behavior through the natural selection of specific traits, and compares this to the behavior of humans. Topics include physiological, genetic, developmental, and environmental mechanisms controlling behavior in various animal species (including humans) from an evolutionary perspective. Prerequisite: PSY 1100 . (NOTE: Meets Group I requirement.)

title: Jewish and Christian Origins
description: Survey of the early history, scriptures, beliefs, and cultures of Judaism and then of Christianity intertwined with it, with special attention to the development of concepts of justice in socio-political context.

title: Independent Study
description: The privilege of independent study is possible for students who are prepared to undertake it based on student need or interest with approval of the Chair of the department, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. The study must be in an area not covered by a regularly scheduled course. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. The maximum number of units that can be earned in one semester by independent study is 4; a total of up to 4 units of independent study may be applied to the degree.

title: Dance and Spiritual Expression
description: Drawing on the wisdom of Kimerer LaMothe, Friedrich Nietzsche, and other scholars, philosophers, and dancers, this course demonstrates the power of dance to open human beings to knowledge and experience that we would not otherwise have. In case studies drawn from around the world, we see dancing bodies as instruments of devotion, and as vehicles for traveling through different realms of reality. We see moving bodies as catalysts for spiritual change, and as vessels for religious meaning. We see women and men finding in dance a medium for expressing and becoming their highest ideals. The course is divided into three interrelated sections: "Dance in the Worldâs Religions" â "Dance in the Modern West: Religious Impulses and Aspects" â "Dance, Philosophy, and the Study of Religion."

title: Religion and Politics
description: Healthy patriotism and sincere faith can transform into violent racial-ethnic supremacy. Why? This course explores a range of possibilities at the intersection of the religious and the political. Through social scientific analysis of religions, including civil religion the course will trace the roots and development of religion and politics in the U.S.

title: The Power of Compassion and Forgiveness
description: Using an interdisciplinary framework, this course draws from research that highlights the magnitude of the life-affirming dimensions and powerful consequences of compassion and forgiveness. As recent research from various fields demonstrates, and as wisdom traditions have traditionally attested, the benefits of compassion and forgiveness include personal and spiritual transformation, as well as enhanced health, well-being, and creativity. The ultimate objective of this course is to deepen our understanding of the power of compassion and forgiveness and, ideally, to improve our own life and the lives of other.

title: Masculinity, Religion, and Violence
description: Whether in acts of religiously-motivated terrorism, mass shootings, domestic violence, or sexual violence, why are the vast majority of perpetrators men? This course will analyze how religion(s) can contribute to those problems, while investigating how the same religion(s) and other sources might provide resources for constructing healthy masculinities.

title: Religion and Social Theory
description: Working from the classics, Durkheim, Marx, Weber, Mead, Parsons and Bellah, we will examine religious consciousness within a general theory of human action. Historical and comparative frameworks will be used to identify the role religious expression and activity has played in society and social organization. We will explore the major themes of secularization, modernization, religion and politics, civil religion, religious movements, rise of fundamentalism, and civility and religion. Examples of contemporary religious activity will be drawn from cross-cultural and cross-national perspectives.

title: Religion & Social Movements
description: This course examines the important role of religion in movements for social change. Primary focus will be on movements for economic and racial justice in the United States. Students will explore principles of nonviolence, religious art and culture, theological concepts, and faith communities in relation to social movements. Practical experience will be gained through engagement with community organizations that address structural oppression and advocate for social justice.

title: Women, Religion and Sexuality
description: A cross-cultural study of the impact of traditional religious teachings about sex on societyâs perception of womenâs role and status and on womenâs own self-perception, with particular attention to the historical origins of female subordination.

title: The Rhetoric of Belief
description: An investigation of God-language in the speeches and writings of outstanding politicians, novelists, poets, philosophers, and religious activists from around the world, this Honors seminar aims at deepening our skills as communicators, sharpening our capacity for critical analysis, and understanding the complex relationship between language and belief.

title: Liberation Theologies
description: This course surveys the ideas and practices behind various religion-based social justice projects emerging from and in solidarity with the poor of Latin America, North American feminists, and the African American Christian community. Bridging theory and practice, students work with marginalized groups in our local community. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: Art and the Sacred
description: Analyzes ways that religions and the arts illuminate each other while providing insight into human experiences, including alienation and injustice. May include community engagement.

title: Contemporary Catholic Thought
description: A survey of Catholic intellectual response since the Second Vatican Council to the issues, questions, and concerns of the contemporary world. Topics covered will include: war and peace, social, economic, and environmental justice, ecumenism and collegiality, and sexuality.

title: Ecology and Religion
description: A comparative study of the beliefs and values of religions, wisdom traditions, and indigenous lifeways from around the world pertaining to the interrelationships of humans, Earth, cosmos, and the sacred. Particular attention is paid to the ways these can inform the task of developing and sustaining an ecologically sound relationship to the Earth. May include engagement with community partners, such as work in a garden or local farm.

title: Life and Death
description: Surveys approaches of various religions to the beginning and end of physical human life, along with their attempts to find hope in the midst of human suffering. Specifically designed to help those preparing for healthcare, mental health, and social service professions better understand the perspectives of those they will serve. May include community engagement.

title: AAPI Cultures and Religions
description: This course will explore how religions, spirituality, and cultural traditions ground and provide strength in the daily lives of different generations of Asian American Pacific Islanders (AAPI), whether they are immigrants, migrants, assimilated, marginalized, or organized. Through publications of AAPI scholars, community engagement in AAPI-serving religious, cultural, social, and political non-profit organizations, and discussions with AAPI religious experts and civil rights advocates, we will (re)consider trauma-informed, context-appropriate theories and practices of social justice from the depth and breadth of AAPI experiences.

title: Prophets, Psalms, and Social Justice
description: Early in human civilization scribes, prophets, psalmists and philosophers determined that the world was not just. Cross culturally and across religious perspective a common language and a series of ethical images and motifs develop to express the human longing for order and justice in daily life. We will explore the common roots of Western religion and ethics around the human urge to improve the lot of the 'other.' We will study the classical references and texts in the Hebrew Bible including the Torah literature and the multiple voices of the prophets with emphasis upon Amos, Hosea, Micah and Isaiah. We will discuss the implications and applications of these texts to our leaders, our society and to ourselves.

title: Buddhism and Beyond
description: A survey of major philosophical and religious teachings originating in Asia in their historical and cultural contexts, examining how each conceives the nature of reality, the goals of human life, and the way to those goals.

title: Meditation and Mindful Action
description: Analyses diverse beliefs about and practices of meditation, contemplation, including various forms of mysticism, especially as combined with the pursuit of mindful service toward others. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: Social Justice: Jewish, Christian, and Muslim Foundations
description: Who are you? Who should you be? Who should each of us be for each other? With significant similarities, even among differences, both within themselves and between each other, Judaism, Christianity, and Islam convey the fundamental equality of humans as beings made in the image of God. Building on that foundation, this course will explore some of the various ways that these religions have argued for our fundamental responsibility to care for each other, our natural environment, and the socio-economically marginalized among us.

title: Myth, Symbol and Ritual
description: Using an interdisciplinary framework this course stress that myth, symbol and ritual are of crucial significance to all human societies, are intricately interdependent as phrenoma of religious expression, and are vital to the study and to the understanding of religion and humankind. In this course, we will engage in a cross-cultural examination of how diverse peoples establish their religious worlds by narrating stories and symbolically acting out their deepest aspirations and beliefs.

title: Jewish, Christian, & Muslim Foundations
description: Who are you? Who should you be? Who should each of us be for each other? With significant similarities, even among differences, both within themselves and between each other, Judaism, Christianity, and Islam convey the fundamental equality of humans as beings made in the image of God. Building on that foundation, this course will explore some of the various ways that these religions have argued for our fundamental responsibility to care for each other, our natural environment, and the socio-economically marginalized among us.

title: Capstone
description: Research-based culminating project guided by a faculty mentor. Prerequisite: CASC 3405

title: Religion and Globalization
description: With close attention to diversity and social justice concerns, this course considers the historical dimensions of globalization (empire, trade, population migration) and the impact of globalized connections and movements on different facets of religious traditions, as well as the resurgence and impact of various religions on an increasingly globalized world.

title: Faith-based Social Teachings
description: A study of the literature of social teachings drawn from various religions and an investigation of the ways in which people of different faiths have attempted to change the world for the better. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: Teaching Assistant
description: The privilege of serving as a teaching assistant is available to students who are interested with the approval of the Department Chair, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. A contract between the student and the instructor to determine goals, specifics on project contents/materials, and evaluation process will be required at time of request. Course may be taken for 1-3 units.

title: The World's Religions
description: A study of humankind's ultimate and enduring questionsÂ­ the nature of reality and the meaning and end of human lifeÂ­ against the background of its multiform spiritual heritage. The course will survey the world's religious traditions (Judaism, Christianity, Islam, Hinduism, Buddhism, Confucianism, and Taoism), accenting both their commonalities and their distinctiveness.

title: Internship: Religion
description: Students gain practical, hands-on training in their particular area of career or volunteer interest by working with an organization of their choosing, in consultation with their academic advisor and the Career and Internship office. Student have interned with churches, non-profit organizations, retreat centers, and camps. After meeting with the academic advisor, students meet with the Career and Internship office to fill out the necessary paperwork and, then, after securing all necessary signatures, submit an add/drop form along with the internship paperwork to the Registrar. This course cannot be accessed via the self-service registration cart. 1-3 units.

title: Independent Study
description: The privilege of independent study is possible for students who are prepared to undertake it based on student need or interest with approval of the Chair of the department, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. The study must be in an area not covered by a regularly scheduled course. Course may be taken for 1-3 units. The maximum number of units that can be earned in one semester by independent study is 3; a total of up to six units of independent study may be applied to the degree.

title: Beginning Spanish
description: Speaking, listening to, practice in speaking, listening, reading, and writing Spanish. Study of Hispanic culture. Class will implement project based learning with field work in the community and adding the project to studentsâ digital portfolio.

title: Intermediate Spanish for Business
description: Continuation of SPAN 1100 : further practice in speaking, listening, reading, and writing Spanish. Grammar review, oral practice, composition, and special emphasis on vocabulary, readings, and activities geared toward the language of business, business planning, and entrepreneurship, communications and international studies in the context of the United States Hispanic community. Assignments may include professional documents, community service work and projects, and a research project. Prerequisite: SPAN 1100 or equivalent, or permission of languages director.

title: Survey of Spain and Latin America
description: An advanced course designed to study the cultural heritage that has shaped nations of Latin America from 1850 to the present. Readings and discussion of 20th-century literary trends, including film, music, and art from Spain and Latin America. Topics vary but may include the Generation of 1898 in Spain, modernism, magical realism, or Latin-American women writers. The class is conducted entirely in Spanish. Prerequisite: SPAN 3201 or equivalent.

title: Intermediate Spanish for Health Professions
description: Continuation of SPAN 1100 : further practice in speaking, listening, reading, and writing Spanish. Grammar review, oral practice, composition, and special emphasis on vocabulary needed by medical personnel, counselors, psychology majors, public health and OT professionals.. Topics include greetings with cultural and medical protocols, getting to know the patient, assisting in taking preliminary medical histories, and assisting in patient assessment and treatment and successful cross-cultural communication. Prerequisite: SPAN 1100 or equivalent, or permission of languages director.

title: Ethics: Jewish, Christian, and Muslim Applications
description: An analysis of the dominant ethical schools of thought explicit and implicit in Judaism, Christianity, and Islam, with attention to both the similarities between those religions and to the diversity within each religion. Contemporary examples will serve to illustrate how much adherents of all three religions have struggled to live out their ideals, both in terms of personal ethics and social ethics.

title: Independent Study
description: The privilege of independent study is possible for students who are prepared to undertake it based on student need or interest with approval of the Chair of the department, the instructor concerned, and the Dean of the School. The study must be in an area not covered by a regularly scheduled course. Course may be taken for 1-4 units. The maximum number of units that can be earned in one semester by independent study is 4; a total of up to four units of independent study may be applied to the degree.

title: STA: Spanish Immersion
description: **Study Travel Course** Course will provide an opportunity for students to travel to a Spanish-speaking country, such as Spain or Mexico, for the purpose of a language and cultural immersion. Students will meet together as a class to cover such topics as cultural differences, learning brief histories about the area being visited, as well as other relevant information prior to departure on the trip.

title: Latino Families in the United States
description: This course will examine the cultural, social, political, economic, educational and health experiences of Latino families in the United States. Through readings, and examinations of media and discourse, we will discuss the family experience and transnational ties among diverse Latino families. We will discuss the roles of social norms, family values and perspectives, and structures in shaping everyday family life. Through a semester-long project, students will gain a critical understanding of agency and ethical considerations as Latino families navigate family dynamics and contemporary issues shaped by race, gender, sexuality, language, religion, socioeconomic status, and immigration status. This course will examine the cultural, social, political, economic, educational and health experiences of Latino families in the United States. Through readings, and examinations of media and discourse, we will discuss the family experience and transnational ties among diverse Latino families. We will discuss the roles of social norms, family values and perspectives, and structures in shaping everyday family life. Through a semester-long project, students will gain a critical understanding of agency and ethical considerations as Latino families navigate family dynamics and contemporary issues shaped by race, gender, sexuality, language, religion, socioeconomic status, and immigration status.

title: Religion in American History
description: This course is an exploration of 500 years of American history from pre-Columbian Native American culture and spirituality and its continuing impact today; the Christian-Catholic roots of the American southwest and California of the 15th and 16th century; the Christian-Protestant roots of the Anglo-European migrations of the 17th and 18th century; the role of American Civil Religion and Secularism/Atheism that emerged from the American Revolution, and the diversity of world religions such as Hinduism, Buddhism, and Islam that exist in the United States today. Students participate in a community-based learning project.

title: Introduction to Sociology
description: Overview of the principles and basic concepts of sociology as a social science. Topics that will be examined include the study of groups, collective behavior, socialization, culture, social institutions, class and caste systems, stratification, community, social roles, social planning, and social control. This course only satisfies the CORE requirement when offered as a service learning course.

title: Research Methods in Social Science
description: The study of social science requires attention to development of advanced research, critical thinking, argumentation, and writing skills. This course will challenge the student to reflect upon and utilize various research methods, writing formats, and styles of argumentation and critique within the context of the various sub-fields of the study of social science.

title: Contemporary Families
description: The analysis of the historical and social forces shaping family structure, power relations based on gender and age, family interaction, and their effects on individual members. Racial, ethnic, and class differences in families and contemporary diversity.

title: Racial and Ethnic Issues
description: Focuses on the historical development and structure of racial and ethnic inequality in the United States. Analyzes the experience of, and the relationships between, African-Americans, Native-Americans, Asian-Americans, Latinx, as well as Caucasians. Addresses contemporary issues including white privilege, access to education and jobs, and land rights.

title: Advanced Composition and Conversation
description: An advanced course designed to increase practice in writing with an emphasis on sentence and paragraph structure. Attention will be given to narration, description, exposition, and dialogue as well as to other forms of written. Prerequisite: SPAN 2111 , 2113, or equivalent, or permission of languages director.

title: Theoretical Images of Crime, Deviance and Social Control
description: This course provides a detailed overview of ten major theoretical traditions for understanding crime and deviance, as well as the policy implications and social control methods recommended by each theoretical perspective. Some attention will be given to empirical studies to see how well the theories meet the test of material reality.

title: Gender Issues
description: An examination of the various psychological, social, and political issues related to gender from multiple theoretical perspectives. Topics include, but are not limited to the differences between men and women, the development of masculine and feminine sex roles, and the social construction of gender appropriate behavior.

title: Senior Capstone
description: The senior capstone course provides students an opportunity to develop a culminating research project during their senior year. Students develop a research project that demonstrates the ability to synthesize, analyze, and evaluate major questions within the discipline. Students will also explore career options and postgraduate goals.
